steps.body: The heart got the opposite treatment. Egyptians believed the heart was the seat of thought, feeling, and the person's true self. So it was usually left right inside the body, protected. The organ they trusted to do the thinking stayed put, while the organ that actually does the thinking was discarded.

steps.prompt: Why was the heart left inside the body?
options: It was too fragile to remove
Egyptians believed it was the seat of thought and self
It was needed to pump blood after death
steps.explain: Egyptians treated the heart as the center of thought, feeling, and identity. Since it held the self, it was kept in place rather than removed.

steps.title: Other organs got their own jars
steps.body: Several other organs were not discarded but stored. The lungs, liver, stomach, and intestines were removed and placed in containers known as canopic jars. The body itself was then dried out with a salt called natron. The brain was the odd one out: not kept in the body, not saved in a jar, just gone.

steps.prompt: Order the fate of these organs from 'guarded most' to 'thrown away'.
items: Heart
Lungs and liver
Brain

glosses.Heart: Kept in the body as the seat of self
glosses.Lungs and liver: Removed but saved in canopic jars

steps.prompt: Why did embalmers preserve the heart but not the brain?
options: The heart was easier to reach
They believed the heart, not the brain, did the thinking
The brain rotted too slowly to bother with
steps.explain: They believed the heart was the seat of thought and the self. The brain seemed like useless mush, so it was discarded while the heart was protected.

steps.title: That's mummified anatomy.
recap: Brain pulled out the nose and thrown away.
Heart kept inside as the seat of self.
Other organs stored in canopic jars.

subject: HISTORY
title: Athenian Democracy Excluded Almost Everyone
blurb: The 'birthplace of democracy' barred women, foreigners, and the enslaved. Only a minority of men could vote.

steps.title: Democracy with the doors mostly shut
steps.sub: Athens gets credit for inventing democracy. It also locked out women, foreigners, and the enslaved. The people allowed to vote were a small slice of the city.

steps.prompt: Roughly what share of people living in Athens could vote?
options: Nearly everyone living there
Only a minority, all of them men
All adults, men and women alike
steps.explain: Only a minority, and all of them men. Women, enslaved people, and foreign residents were excluded, so the voting body was a small fraction of the population.

steps.title: Only adult male citizens
steps.body: In Athenian democracy, the people who could attend the assembly and vote were adult male citizens. That is the whole list. Women were excluded from voting and from formal politics entirely. Half the free population was shut out before you counted anyone else.

steps.prompt: Who was allowed to vote in Athens?
options: All adults who lived in the city
Only adult male citizens
Any man who owned property, citizen or not
steps.explain: Only adult male citizens. Women were excluded from voting and formal politics, which alone removed half the free population from the process.

steps.title: Whole groups locked out
steps.body: Two more large groups had no vote. Enslaved people made up a big share of Athens and had no political rights at all. Foreign residents, called metics, could live and work in the city for years but still could not vote. Citizenship was restricted and generally required Athenian parentage, so you could not simply move in and earn it.

steps.prompt: What were foreign residents of Athens called, and could they vote?
options: Metics, and no they could not vote
Metics, and yes after a few years
Citizens, and yes automatically
steps.explain: Metics. They could live and work in Athens but could not vote. Citizenship was restricted and generally tied to Athenian parentage, so residence alone did not grant it.

steps.prompt: Order these groups from 'could vote' to 'no political rights'.
items: Adult male citizens
Metics (foreign residents)
Enslaved people

glosses.Adult male citizens: The only group that could vote
glosses.Metics (foreign residents): Lived and worked there, but no vote

steps.prompt: Which group was shut out of voting first, before counting metics or the enslaved?
options: Wealthy landowners
Women
Soldiers
steps.explain: Women. They were excluded from voting and formal politics entirely, which removed about half the free population before any other group was counted.

steps.title: That's Athenian democracy.
recap: Only adult male citizens could vote.
Women, the enslaved, and metics were excluded.
The voters were a minority of the city.

subject: HISTORY
title: The Hanging Gardens May Never Have Existed
blurb: It is the only one of the Seven Wonders with no confirmed ruins. Some scholars think it stood in a different city, or not at all.

steps.title: A wonder with no ruins
steps.sub: Six of the Seven Wonders left traces, or at least a known site. The Hanging Gardens left neither. We are not even sure they were real.

steps.prompt: Of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World, how many have a confirmed location?
options: All seven
Six of the seven
Only three
steps.explain: Six of the seven have known sites or surviving remains. The Hanging Gardens are the lone exception: no ruins, and no agreed-upon location.

steps.title: No stones, no site
steps.body: Babylon sat in Mesopotamia, in what is now Iraq. Archaeologists have dug there for over a century. They found palaces, walls, and gates. They have not found anything that can be confirmed as the Hanging Gardens. For a structure said to be massive and famous, that absence is strange.

steps.prompt: What is the main physical problem with the Hanging Gardens story?
options: The site of Babylon has never been found
No confirmed remains of the gardens have been dug up at Babylon
The gardens were made of materials that cannot survive
steps.explain: Babylon itself is well known and heavily excavated. The problem is narrower: nothing found there can be confirmed as the gardens.

steps.title: The witnesses came late
steps.body: Our vivid descriptions of terraced, watered gardens come from Greek and Roman writers, working well after the fact. The Babylonians themselves left detailed records. King Nebuchadnezzar II, the ruler usually credited, boasted in writing about his building projects. None of those surviving records mention a hanging garden. The people who should have bragged about it stayed silent.

steps.prompt: Why do some historians doubt the gardens stood at Babylon?
options: Greek writers said they were elsewhere
Babylon's own surviving records never mention them
The climate could not support plants
steps.explain: The descriptions are all from later outsiders. Babylon's own records, including Nebuchadnezzar's building accounts, say nothing about the gardens.

steps.title: Maybe the wrong city
steps.body: One leading theory comes from scholar Stephanie Dalley. She argues the gardens were real, but built at Nineveh by the Assyrian king Sennacherib, not at Babylon at all. Nineveh had the engineering and the water systems to match the legend. If she is right, the most famous garden in history has been filed under the wrong city for two thousand years.

steps.prompt: Where do our detailed descriptions of the gardens actually come from?
options: Babylonian palace records
Later Greek and Roman writers
Carvings on the gardens themselves
steps.explain: Later Greek and Roman writers, not contemporary Babylonian sources. That gap between the legend and the local record is the heart of the mystery.

steps.title: That's the Hanging Gardens.
recap: The only Wonder with no confirmed site.
Babylon's own records never mention it.
One theory puts it at Nineveh instead.

subject: HISTORY
title: Ancient People Knew Earth Was Round
blurb: Educated Greeks worked out the planet was a sphere over 2,000 years ago. The idea that medieval people thought it was flat is itself a myth.

steps.title: Old knowledge, new myth
steps.sub: People did not first learn Earth was round in 1492. Greek scholars had it figured out well over two thousand years ago.

steps.prompt: When did educated people first conclude the Earth is a sphere?
options: When Columbus sailed in 1492
In ancient Greece, over 2,000 years ago
After the first photos from space
steps.explain: Ancient Greek scholars argued for a spherical Earth more than two thousand years ago. Columbus arrived very late to a settled question.

steps.title: The Greeks worked it out
steps.body: By the 4th century BC, Aristotle was making the case in writing. He pointed to two things you can check yourself. During a lunar eclipse, the Earth casts a curved shadow on the Moon. And as you travel north or south, different stars rise into view. Both only make sense if the ground beneath you curves. This was reasoning, not guessing.

steps.prompt: What did Aristotle point to as evidence the Earth is round?
options: The curved shadow the Earth casts on the Moon
A photograph taken from a mountain
The shape of the Sun at noon
steps.explain: The round shadow on the Moon during a lunar eclipse was one of his arguments. A sphere always casts a round shadow, whatever its angle.

steps.title: They even measured it
steps.body: They did not stop at shape. Around the 3rd century BC, Eratosthenes, a Greek scholar working in Alexandria, estimated the Earth's circumference using shadows cast at different places. The point is not how exact he was. The point is that the question by then was how big, not whether it was a ball at all.

steps.prompt: What did Eratosthenes try to do?
options: Prove the Earth was flat
Estimate the Earth's circumference
Sail around the Earth
steps.explain: He estimated the planet's circumference. By his time the spherical shape was assumed, and the open question was its size.

steps.title: The Middle Ages knew too
steps.body: Here is the twist. The idea that medieval Europeans thought the Earth was flat is a modern invention. Educated people through the Middle Ages generally accepted a round Earth, inheriting it from the Greeks. The flat-earth Middle Ages is a story we told later, not something those people believed.

steps.prompt: Did educated medieval Europeans believe the Earth was flat?
options: Yes, until Columbus proved otherwise
No, they generally accepted it was round
Only sailors knew it was round
steps.explain: No. They generally accepted a round Earth, carried forward from Greek scholars. The flat-earth belief is a myth attached to them later.

steps.title: That's the round Earth.
recap: Greeks argued for a sphere over 2,000 years ago.
Aristotle used eclipses and shifting stars.
The flat-earth Middle Ages is itself a myth.

subject: HISTORY
title: Hieroglyphs Were Unreadable for 1,400 Years
blurb: For over a millennium, nobody could read ancient Egyptian writing. One stone with the same text in three scripts finally broke it open.

steps.title: A silent civilization
steps.sub: Egypt left walls covered in writing. For over a thousand years, nobody alive could read a word of it.

steps.prompt: Why was a single stone slab the key to reading hieroglyphs?
options: It was the only hieroglyph text that survived
It repeated the same text in a script scholars could already read
It contained a dictionary of every symbol
steps.explain: It carried the same decree in three scripts, including Greek, which scholars could still read. A known version next to the unknown one gave them a way in.

steps.title: The knowledge died out
steps.body: Ancient Egyptian writing fell out of use, and the skill of reading it was lost. For roughly fourteen centuries, the carvings on temples and tombs were just shapes. People could see them everywhere and understand none of them. A whole civilization's records had gone silent.

steps.prompt: What was the state of hieroglyphs before the 19th century?
options: They had been destroyed and lost
They survived but no one could read them
Only priests could read them
steps.explain: The writing survived all around Egypt. What was lost was the ability to read it, for around fourteen hundred years.

steps.title: Three scripts, one message
steps.body: In 1799, French soldiers in Egypt found a slab near the town of Rosetta. It carried the same decree three times: in hieroglyphs, in a later Egyptian script called Demotic, and in ancient Greek. Scholars could still read Greek. So for the first time they had a known translation sitting beside the mystery script.

steps.prompt: Which of the three scripts could scholars already read?
options: Hieroglyphic
Demotic
Ancient Greek
steps.explain: Ancient Greek was still readable. Matching it against the other two scripts is what made the stone a usable key.

steps.title: Cracking the code
steps.body: The breakthrough is credited to Jean-Francois Champollion, who announced his decipherment in 1822, building on earlier work by Thomas Young. The key insight was that hieroglyphs are not just pictures. Many signs stand for sounds. Once you treat them partly as an alphabet of sounds, the writing starts to speak.

steps.prompt: What made the Rosetta Stone so useful for decipherment?
options: It listed every hieroglyph and its meaning
It repeated one text in three scripts, one of them readable
It was the oldest writing ever found
steps.explain: The same text appeared in three scripts, and Greek was still readable. That known version beside the unknown ones was the way in.

steps.title: That's the Rosetta Stone.
recap: Hieroglyphs went unread for about 1,400 years.
One stone held the same text in three scripts.
Champollion cracked it in 1822.

subject: HISTORY
title: Ancient Rome Was a Million-Person Megacity
blurb: At its height Rome held around a million people. No European city would reach that size again for many centuries.

steps.title: A city ahead of its time
steps.sub: Around a million people lived in ancient Rome. For the cities that came after, that scale would be out of reach for a very long time.

steps.prompt: Roughly how many people lived in Rome at its peak?
options: About 100,000
About one million
About ten million
steps.explain: Estimates put it around one million. That was an enormous city for the ancient world, and a size later European cities would not see again for centuries.

steps.title: A million people
steps.body: At its height, around the 1st to 2nd century AD, Rome is estimated to have held about a million people. We cannot count them exactly. These are scholarly estimates, not a modern census. But the figure is striking. After Rome declined, no European city is thought to have reached that size again until London in the 1800s.

steps.prompt: Which European city is usually cited as the next to reach about a million?
options: Paris in the 1500s
London in the 1800s
Athens in the 700s
steps.explain: London in the 19th century. The long gap shows just how unusual Rome's scale was for its time.

steps.title: Feeding a million mouths
steps.body: A city that size cannot feed itself from nearby fields. Rome leaned on imported grain, much of it shipped from Egypt and North Africa. Water came in through a network of aqueducts. And to house everyone, Romans built up: crowded multi-story apartment blocks called insulae. The megacity ran on logistics, not magic.

steps.prompt: How did Rome feed a population that large?
options: Farms inside the city walls
Grain imported from places like Egypt and North Africa
Each family grew its own food
steps.explain: Imported grain, much of it from Egypt and North Africa. A city of a million could not survive on local farmland alone.

steps.prompt: Roughly how large was Rome at its peak?
options: About a million people
About fifty thousand people
About five million people
steps.explain: Around a million, by scholarly estimate. That scale would not return to a European city until London in the 1800s.

steps.title: That's ancient Rome.
recap: Around a million people at its peak.
Unmatched in Europe until 1800s London.
Fed by imported grain and aqueducts.

subject: HISTORY
title: Julius Caesar Wasn't Born by C-Section
blurb: The name link is folk etymology. Such surgery then killed the mother, and Caesar's mother lived well into his adulthood.

steps.title: A neat story that breaks
steps.sub: Caesar, cesarean. The words look like a match. But the history does not hold up.

steps.prompt: Is the cesarean section named after Julius Caesar's birth?
options: Yes, he was the first born that way
Probably not; the link is likely a folk story
Yes, Roman doctors named it for him
steps.explain: Probably not. It is a tidy-sounding link, but the evidence points the other way. The neat name match is a folk etymology, not history.

steps.title: His mother lived
steps.body: Here is the clinching detail. In the ancient world, cutting a baby from the womb was effectively a death sentence for the mother. There was no way to keep her alive through it. Yet Caesar's mother, Aurelia, is recorded living well into his adulthood. If she survived for years, she did not die on a surgical table at his birth.

steps.prompt: Why is it unlikely Caesar was born by cesarean?
options: Romans had no surgical tools
His mother lived for years afterward, and the surgery then was fatal
Caesar was born outside Rome
steps.explain: The surgery then killed the mother. Aurelia lived into Caesar's adulthood, so the procedure almost certainly did not happen at his birth.

steps.title: Where the word came from
steps.body: So why the matching names? The likeliest source is a Latin verb meaning to cut, such as caedere. The procedure is named for the cutting, not for the man. The resemblance to Caesar's name is a coincidence that later got dressed up as a fact.

steps.prompt: What is the more likely source of the word cesarean?
options: Caesar's family name
A Latin verb meaning to cut
The name of a Roman doctor
steps.explain: A Latin verb meaning to cut, such as caedere. The procedure is named for the act of cutting, not for Julius Caesar.

steps.title: What the Romans actually did
steps.body: Romans did perform such cuts, but the situation was grim. The procedure was used on women who had died or were dying, as a last attempt to save the infant. It was not a way to deliver a healthy child to a healthy mother. That alone makes the Caesar legend hard to fit.

steps.prompt: What fact most directly contradicts the Caesar birth story?
options: Caesar was born in winter
His mother lived into his adulthood
Romans banned the surgery
steps.explain: Aurelia lived for years after his birth. Since the surgery then was fatal to the mother, her survival breaks the story.

steps.title: That's the Caesar myth.
recap: The name link is folk etymology.
His mother lived well past his birth.
Cesarean likely comes from a word meaning to cut.

subject: HISTORY
title: Cleopatra Lived Closer to Us Than to the Pyramids
blurb: The Great Pyramid was already ancient in Cleopatra's day. Egypt's history runs absurdly deep.

steps.title: How old is old?
steps.sub: We file the pyramids and Cleopatra in the same drawer marked 'ancient Egypt.' But the gap between them is enormous. She was a latecomer.

steps.prompt: Cleopatra lived closer in time to which of these?
options: The building of the Great Pyramid
The first Moon landing
They are about equally far from her
steps.explain: The Moon landing. The Great Pyramid went up around 2,500 years before Cleopatra. We are only about 2,000 years after her. The pyramids were her ancient history, more distant than we are from her.

steps.title: The pyramid came first by a mile
steps.body: The Great Pyramid of Giza was finished around 2560 BC, for the pharaoh Khufu. Cleopatra was born around 69 BC. That is a gap of roughly 2,500 years. For her, the pyramids were not recent monuments. They were already weathered relics from a barely imaginable past, as far back from her as classical Rome is from us.

steps.prompt: About how long before Cleopatra was the Great Pyramid built?
options: Around 500 years
Around 2,500 years
They were near contemporaries
steps.explain: Around 2,500 years. The pyramid dates to about 2560 BC and Cleopatra to about 69 to 30 BC. That gulf is why the monuments were ancient even to her.

steps.title: She was the end, not the middle
steps.body: Cleopatra VII was the last active ruler of the Ptolemaic line, the dynasty that took Egypt after Alexander the Great. The Ptolemies were Greek in origin, not the pyramid builders' descendants. By her death around 30 BC, Egyptian civilization already stretched back roughly three thousand years. She sits near the very end of that story, not in its heart.

steps.prompt: Where does Cleopatra sit in the span of ancient Egypt?
options: Near the beginning, around the pyramid age
Near the very end of a roughly three-thousand-year story
Right in the middle
steps.explain: Near the very end. She was the last active Ptolemaic ruler, and Egyptian civilization already ran back about three thousand years before her. The deep past was behind her, not ahead.

steps.prompt: Put these in order, oldest first.
items: Great Pyramid built
Cleopatra rules Egypt
First Moon landing

steps.prompt: Earlier: roughly how big was the gap between the Great Pyramid and Cleopatra?
options: About 2,500 years
About 250 years
steps.explain: About 2,500 years. That is larger than the roughly 2,000 years between Cleopatra and us, which is why she lived closer to our time than to the pyramids.

steps.title: Deep time, Egyptian style.
recap: The Great Pyramid predates Cleopatra by about 2,500 years.
We are only about 2,000 years after Cleopatra.
She lived near the end of Egypt's long story, not the start.

subject: HISTORY
title: Romans Didn't Wear Togas Every Day
blurb: The toga was heavy, awkward formalwear. For daily life, Romans reached for a simple tunic.

steps.title: Not the daily uniform
steps.sub: Picture a Roman and you picture a toga. But that drape was the suit-and-tie of its day, saved for formal moments. Ordinary life looked plainer.

steps.prompt: What did most Romans actually wear on a normal day?
options: A toga, always
A simple tunic
Nothing much, given the climate
steps.explain: A simple tunic. The tunic was the everyday workhorse garment. The toga was reserved formalwear, too bulky and fussy for ordinary tasks.

steps.title: The tunic did the daily work
steps.body: The basic Roman garment was the tunic, often made of wool. It was simple, practical, and easy to move in. Men, women, workers, and slaves all wore versions of it. For farming, trading, cooking, or building, you wanted clothing that let you move. The tunic did that. The toga did not.

steps.prompt: Why was the tunic the everyday choice?
options: It was simple and easy to move in
It was the most expensive option
Only citizens were allowed to wear it
steps.explain: It was simple and practical. A plain tunic let people work and move freely, which is exactly what daily life demanded.

steps.title: The toga was heavy, formal, and a hassle
steps.body: A toga was a large piece of wool cloth, draped without any pins or buttons. Arranging it took care and often help, and keeping it in place limited how you moved. It signaled status and was tied to Roman male citizens. You wore it for formal and public occasions, not for hauling goods across town. Augustus even pushed citizens to wear it in the Forum, which suggests they often did not bother.

steps.prompt: What made the toga impractical for daily wear?
options: It was see-through
It was bulky, hard to arrange, and restricted movement
It was illegal outside the home
steps.explain: It was a large drape of wool with no fasteners. Arranging it took effort and often help, and wearing it limited movement, so it suited formal occasions, not work.

steps.prompt: Earlier: what was the common everyday Roman garment?
options: The toga
The tunic
steps.explain: The tunic. It was simple and easy to move in, which is why it, not the formal toga, was the daily standard.

steps.title: Tunic for work, toga for show.
recap: The tunic was the everyday garment for most Romans.
The toga was heavy, fastener-free, and hard to arrange.
It was formal, status-marking wear, not daily clothing.

subject: HISTORY
title: Ancient Doctors Used Honey on Wounds, and It Worked
blurb: Long before germ theory, healers packed wounds with honey. We now know it fights bacteria.

steps.title: An old remedy that holds up
steps.sub: Most ancient cures were guesswork, and plenty were harmful. But honey on a wound was a rare hit. Modern science backs it.

steps.prompt: Why might honey help a wound rather than rot it?
options: It is sterile sugar water that feeds the skin
It creates conditions bacteria struggle to grow in
It has no real effect; it just felt soothing
steps.explain: It makes a hostile environment for bacteria. Honey is concentrated sugar and acidic, and it pulls water out of microbes. That is why an ancient habit turned out to be sound.

steps.title: Healers used it for thousands of years
steps.body: Putting honey on wounds is very old. Ancient Egyptian medical texts, including the Edwin Smith and Ebers papyri, describe honey as a wound dressing. Healers had no idea germs existed. They simply noticed that honey-dressed wounds tended to do better, and the practice stuck across many cultures.

steps.prompt: How did ancient healers know to use honey?
options: They understood it killed bacteria
They noticed honey-dressed wounds tended to do better
They were told by physicians studying germs
steps.explain: By observation. They had no germ theory. They simply saw that wounds treated with honey fared better, so they kept doing it.

steps.title: Why it actually fights bacteria
steps.body: Honey works on several fronts. It is mostly sugar with very little free water, so by osmosis it draws water out of bacteria and starves them. It is also acidic, which many bacteria dislike. And when diluted, many honeys release small amounts of hydrogen peroxide, a mild antiseptic. Together these make honey genuinely antibacterial.

steps.prompt: Which is one reason honey inhibits bacteria?
options: Its high water content drowns them
It is so concentrated it draws water out of bacterial cells
It raises the wound's temperature
steps.explain: Honey holds very little free water. By osmosis it pulls water out of bacteria, which starves and inhibits them. Its acidity and trace hydrogen peroxide add to the effect.

steps.title: Still used in hospitals today
steps.body: This is not just a historical curiosity. Medical-grade honey, prepared and sterilized for clinical use, is part of modern wound care. So an instinct from thousands of years ago, formed with no understanding of microbes, lines up with what we now know about bacteria.

steps.prompt: Is honey still used in medicine?
options: No, it was disproven
Yes, medical-grade honey is used in wound care
steps.explain: Yes. Specially prepared medical-grade honey is used in modern wound care, confirming the ancient practice had real merit.

steps.prompt: Earlier: what is one reason honey resists bacteria?
options: It draws water out of bacterial cells
It feeds bacteria so they leave the wound alone
steps.explain: It draws water out of bacterial cells by osmosis, since it is so concentrated. That, plus its acidity and trace hydrogen peroxide, makes it antibacterial.

steps.title: An ancient habit, vindicated.
recap: Healers dressed wounds with honey for thousands of years.
It is concentrated, acidic, and mildly peroxide-producing, so it fights bacteria.
Medical-grade honey is still used in wound care today.

subject: HISTORY
title: The Berlin Wall Fell by Mistake
blurb: A spokesman fumbled a press conference, said the border was open now, and crowds did the rest.

steps.title: Nobody planned that night
steps.sub: The Berlin Wall fell on 9 November 1989. Not by decree, not by storming. It opened because a tired official botched a press conference.

steps.prompt: How did the Berlin Wall actually open on that night in 1989?
options: A formal government order to tear it down
A garbled announcement that sent crowds to the gates
An armed uprising that overran the guards
steps.explain: A garbled announcement. An official misread new travel rules at a press conference, said the border was open now, and crowds rushed the checkpoints. No grand plan, no battle.

steps.title: A wall up since 1961
steps.body: East Germany built the Berlin Wall in 1961 to stop its citizens fleeing to the West. For decades it split the city, guarded and deadly to cross. By 1989 East Germany was under huge pressure and decided to loosen its strict travel rules. That decision, badly communicated, is what cracked the wall open.

steps.prompt: Why was the Berlin Wall built in the first place?
options: To stop East Germans fleeing to the West
To defend against a Western invasion
To mark a trade boundary
steps.explain: To stop East Germans leaving for the West. East Germany put it up in 1961, which is why its sudden opening in 1989 was so momentous.

steps.title: The press conference slip
steps.body: On 9 November 1989, official Gunter Schabowski presented new travel rules at a press conference. He had been handed the regulations shortly before and was not fully briefed. Asked when they took effect, he shuffled his papers and said, in effect, immediately. That was not the plan. The rules were not meant to take effect that night that way, but the words were already out.

steps.prompt: What was the key mistake at the press conference?
options: He announced the wall would be rebuilt
Unbriefed, he said the new rules took effect immediately
He refused to answer any questions
steps.explain: He was not fully briefed and, asked about timing, said the rules took effect immediately. That was not the intended rollout, but the announcement could not be taken back.

steps.title: The crowd finished the job
steps.body: The news spread fast. Huge crowds gathered at the checkpoints demanding to pass. The guards had no orders for this and no instruction to use force. Outnumbered and unsure, they finally raised the barriers. People streamed through, and the wall as a barrier was finished. The opening came from confusion and crowd pressure, not a signed plan.

steps.prompt: Why did the border guards open the checkpoints?
options: They received a direct order to open them
Overwhelmed by crowds and with no orders to use force, they gave way
The crowds broke through by force
steps.explain: They were overwhelmed by the crowds and had no orders to use force. Unsure what to do, they raised the barriers and let people through.

steps.prompt: Put the night of 9 November 1989 in order.
items: East Germany drafts looser travel rules
Schabowski announces them, unbriefed, as immediate
Crowds rush to the checkpoints
Overwhelmed guards open the barriers
People stream through the wall

steps.prompt: Earlier: what did Schabowski say about when the new rules applied?
options: Immediately
Next month
steps.explain: Immediately. He was not fully briefed, and that off-the-cuff answer is what sent crowds to the checkpoints that very night.

steps.title: History by accident.
recap: The wall stood from 1961 to divide East and West Berlin.
An unbriefed official said new travel rules applied immediately.
Crowds gathered, guards gave way, and the wall opened on 9 November 1989.

subject: HISTORY
title: Napoleon Wasn't Short
blurb: He was average height for his time. The tiny-tyrant image is mostly propaganda and a measurement mix-up.

steps.title: The myth of the tiny tyrant
steps.sub: Everyone 'knows' Napoleon was tiny. He was not. He stood about average for his day. The short story is mostly spin and bad math.

steps.prompt: Compared with other Frenchmen of his time, how tall was Napoleon?
options: Unusually short
About average
Unusually tall
steps.explain: About average. Converted to modern units he stood around 5 feet 6 or 7 inches, typical for a French man of that era. The shortness is a myth.

steps.title: The inch that wasn't an inch
steps.body: Napoleon's height was recorded as about 5 feet 2, but in French units. The French inch of the time was longer than the English inch. Convert it properly and he comes out around 5 feet 6 or 7 in English measure, which was roughly average for a French man then. The famous '5 foot 2' is a units error, not a fact about his body.

steps.prompt: Why does the '5 foot 2' figure mislead?
options: It was a typo in his records
It was measured in French inches, which were longer than English inches
He was measured wearing boots
steps.explain: It was in French inches, which were longer than English ones. Convert correctly and he stands around 5 feet 6 or 7, about average for the time.

steps.title: Propaganda did the rest
steps.body: British cartoonists, James Gillray among them, drew Napoleon as a ranting little man. Mockery sells, and the image stuck. It did not help that he was often seen beside his Imperial Guard, who were picked for their height. Standing next to giants, an average man looks small. The picture in your head is enemy spin plus a trick of comparison.

steps.prompt: Why did Napoleon sometimes look short in person?
options: He stooped from poor health
He was often beside his Imperial Guard, chosen for height
He wore unusually flat shoes
steps.explain: He was frequently surrounded by his Guard, who were selected for their height. Next to taller men, an average-height man reads as small.

steps.title: The nickname misled too
steps.body: Napoleon was called 'le petit caporal,' the little corporal. It sounds like a jab at his size, but it was an affectionate nickname from his troops, not a measurement. Read literally by later audiences, it quietly reinforced the short legend.

steps.prompt: What did 'the little corporal' really signal?
options: That he was physically short
An affectionate nickname from his troops
His low military rank
steps.explain: It was an affectionate nickname from his soldiers, not a comment on his height. Taken literally later, it helped cement the myth.

steps.prompt: Earlier: why was Napoleon's recorded height misread as short?
options: It was measured in longer French inches
His doctors rounded the number down
steps.explain: It was measured in French inches, which were longer than English inches. Converted correctly, he was about average height for his era.

steps.title: Average, not tiny.
recap: His height was recorded in longer French inches, so '5 foot 2' misleads.
Converted, he stood around 5 feet 6 or 7, about average then.
British propaganda, his tall Guard, and a nickname did the rest.

subject: HISTORY
title: Nobody Called the Titanic Unsinkable
blurb: The famous boast that the ship could not sink grew mostly after she went down, not before.

steps.title: The boast came after the wreck
steps.sub: Everyone knows they called the Titanic unsinkable, then it sank. The first half of that story is mostly wrong.

steps.prompt: Before the voyage, how did the White Star Line mostly describe the ship?
options: With a bold guarantee that it could never sink
With cautious pride in its size, luxury, and safety design
By warning that icebergs made it risky
steps.explain: Cautious pride. The marketing sold scale and luxury, and pointed to clever safety features. A flat promise that the ship could never sink would have been a strange thing for any company to print. The absolute boast is mostly a later invention.

steps.title: The real pitch was 'practically'
steps.body: The Titanic had watertight compartments that could be sealed off if the hull was breached. A 1911 trade journal called the design 'practically unsinkable'. Notice that word: practically. It means almost, not never. The official sales material leaned on size and luxury more than on any safety guarantee. The careful, hedged claim is what existed before the voyage.

steps.prompt: What word did the 1911 trade journal actually use about the design?
options: 'Absolutely' unsinkable
'Practically' unsinkable
'Guaranteed' unsinkable
steps.explain: 'Practically'. That single hedge changes everything. It claims the design was very strong, not that sinking was impossible. Legend dropped the qualifier and turned a careful engineering boast into a reckless promise.

steps.title: The legend grew from the irony
steps.body: Once the ship went down on its first voyage, the story needed a villain, and arrogance made a good one. 'They said it couldn't sink' is a perfect moral: pride punished. So the qualified, technical claim got flattened into a confident boast and repeated until it felt like fact. The disaster created the legend, not the other way around.

steps.prompt: Why did the 'unsinkable' boast spread so widely after the sinking?
options: Old advertisements were finally discovered
The irony of pride punished made a satisfying story to retell
The shipbuilder confessed to it
steps.explain: The irony was irresistible. A ship that supposedly could not sink, sinking on its maiden voyage, is a clean moral tale. People kept telling it because it was a good story, and the hedge got lost in the retelling.

steps.prompt: Put the story of the claim in order.
items: Builders design watertight compartments for safety
A trade journal calls the design 'practically unsinkable'
The ship sinks on its maiden voyage
Legend flattens 'practically' into a flat boast

glosses.Builders design watertight compartments: Real engineering, real reputation for safety.
glosses.A trade journal calls the design 'practi: A hedged claim, not a guarantee.
glosses.Legend flattens 'practically' into a fla: The myth is born after the fact.

steps.prompt: What did the qualifier 'practically' actually mean in the original claim?
options: Sinking was impossible
The design was very strong, but not a guarantee against sinking
The ship was still being tested
steps.explain: Very strong, not guaranteed. 'Practically unsinkable' praises a robust design while leaving room for failure. Stripping that word out is exactly how a measured claim became a myth of arrogance.

steps.title: Mind the hedge.
recap: The pre-voyage claim was 'practically', not 'never'.
Official marketing sold luxury and size, not a guarantee.
The absolute boast grew after the disaster.

subject: HISTORY
title: The First Programmers Were Women
blurb: When early computers needed programming, that job was handed to women, because at the time it looked like low-status clerical work.

steps.title: Coding started as women's work
steps.sub: The people who first made early computers actually run were mostly women. The reason why says a lot about status.

steps.prompt: In the 1940s, which computing job was treated as the prestigious one?
options: Writing the programs
Building the hardware
Both were valued equally
steps.explain: Building the hardware. Engineering the machine looked like the real achievement. Telling it what to do was seen as fiddly clerical work, so it was handed off, often to women. The status ranking was backwards from how we'd rank it now.

steps.title: Six women ran ENIAC
steps.body: ENIAC was one of the first electronic general-purpose computers, finished in the 1940s. The men who designed it got the credit for the machine. The job of programming it fell to a team of six women. They had no manuals. They figured out how to set up the machine by studying its wiring, then physically setting switches and plugging in cables to route the calculation by hand.

steps.prompt: How did ENIAC's programmers set up a calculation?
options: By typing code on a keyboard
By setting switches and plugging in cables by hand
By speaking commands to the machine
steps.explain: By hand, with switches and cables. There was no screen and no keyboard for code. Programming meant physically rewiring the machine for each new problem. It was demanding technical work that the era mislabeled as clerical.

steps.title: Status followed money, not skill
steps.body: Programming was low-status partly because it was new and partly because it looked like typing or filing, jobs already coded as women's work. But as software grew central and pay rose, the field's status climbed with it. Over later decades, programming in many countries shifted toward being male-dominated. The work didn't get harder. The prestige and the paycheck got bigger.

steps.prompt: What changed as programming gained status and pay over later decades?
options: It stayed mostly women throughout
The field shifted toward being male-dominated
It was banned for non-engineers
steps.explain: It shifted male. As the work became prestigious and well paid, the demographics changed. That tells you the early gender split was about perceived status, not about who was capable of doing it.

steps.prompt: Put the history of programming's status in order.
items: Hardware is the prestige job; programming looks clerical
Women are assigned to program early machines like ENIAC
Software becomes central and pay rises
The field shifts toward male-dominated

glosses.The field shifts toward male-dominated: Status pulled the demographics with it.

steps.prompt: Why was programming first handed to women in the 1940s?
options: It paid the most
It was seen as low-status clerical work
Only women understood the math
steps.explain: It looked low-status. Programming resembled typing and filing, jobs already treated as women's work, so it carried little prestige. The assignment reflected the era's status ranking, not the difficulty of the task.

steps.title: Status is not skill.
recap: Six women programmed ENIAC by hand.
Coding was first seen as low-status clerical work.
As pay rose, the field shifted male.

subject: HISTORY
title: The Crash Didn't Cause the Depression
blurb: The 1929 stock crash kicked things off, but bank failures and a shrinking money supply did the deep damage.

steps.title: The crash was the spark, not the fire
steps.sub: We blame October 1929 for the whole decade. The crash lit the match, but other failures kept it burning.

steps.prompt: In 1929, how many Americans owned stocks?
options: Almost everyone
Only a minority
Exactly half
steps.explain: Only a minority. Most people held no stocks at all, so the crash alone couldn't ruin the whole country directly. If a falling market were the entire story, the damage would have stopped at the people who owned shares. It didn't, so something else carried it.

steps.title: The banks were the real wound
steps.body: In the early 1930s, thousands of banks failed. Back then deposits were not insured, so when a bank collapsed, ordinary people's savings simply vanished. Fear spread: savers rushed to pull cash out, which pushed even healthy banks under. This reached far more households than the stock market ever did, because almost everyone used a bank.

steps.prompt: Why did bank failures hurt so many ordinary people in the early 1930s?
options: Everyone owned bank stock
Deposits were not insured, so failed banks erased people's savings
Banks were required to invest in the stock market
steps.explain: No insurance. When a bank went under, the money in it was gone, with no government backstop to make depositors whole. Since nearly everyone banked, this spread the pain far wider than the crash did.

steps.title: The money supply shrank
steps.body: As banks failed and people hoarded cash, the amount of money moving through the economy shrank hard. Less money meant less lending, less spending, and falling prices. Many economists argue the Federal Reserve made this worse by not stepping in to support the banking system. A downturn that might have been ordinary turned into a decade-long depression because the money itself dried up.

steps.prompt: What did the shrinking money supply do to the economy?
options: Boosted lending and spending
Cut lending and spending, deepening the downturn
Had no real effect
steps.explain: It choked it. Less money in circulation meant less lending and less spending, and prices fell. That feedback loop is what many economists blame for turning a slump into a depression.

steps.prompt: Put the chain from crash to depression in order.
items: The stock market crashes in 1929
Banks fail and uninsured savings vanish
The money supply shrinks as cash is hoarded
Lending and spending collapse into a deep depression

glosses.The stock market crashes in 1929: The spark, but it hit a minority directly.
glosses.The money supply shrinks as cash is hoar: Less money moving through the economy.

steps.prompt: Why couldn't the 1929 crash alone explain the whole Depression?
options: Stocks recovered the next day
Most Americans owned no stocks, so the crash hit only a minority directly
The crash was actually small
steps.explain: Few owned stocks. The crash directly ruined only the minority who held shares. The damage that reached everyone came from failing banks and a shrinking money supply, not from falling stock prices alone.

steps.title: Triggers aren't causes.
recap: The crash directly hit only stockholders, a minority.
Uninsured bank failures wiped out savings widely.
A shrinking money supply turned a slump into a depression.

subject: HISTORY
title: One Man Stopped Nuclear War in 1983
blurb: A Soviet officer saw his early-warning system report incoming US missiles, judged it a glitch, and did not pass the alarm up the chain.

steps.title: The night the alarm was wrong
steps.sub: A computer told a Soviet officer that American missiles were on the way. He had minutes to decide whether to believe it.

steps.prompt: The warning system showed only a handful of incoming missiles. Why might that detail look suspicious?
options: A real surprise attack would likely be massive, not a few missiles
A few missiles is normal and expected
The number of missiles tells you nothing
steps.explain: A real first strike would be overwhelming. The point of a surprise attack is to destroy the enemy's ability to fire back, which takes many missiles at once. A trickle of five made no military sense, and that oddity is exactly what made Petrov doubt the screen.

steps.title: The screen said attack
steps.body: On 26 September 1983, Stanislav Petrov was the duty officer at a Soviet early-warning center. The system flashed a launch: one US missile inbound, then several more. His job was to report it up the chain, where it could trigger a Soviet nuclear response. He had only minutes. The machine was telling him the war had started.

steps.prompt: What was Petrov's job when the alarm fired?
options: To launch missiles himself
To report the warning up the chain, where it could trigger a response
To repair the satellite
steps.explain: To report it upward. He didn't push a button to fire, but passing the alarm along could set a nuclear response in motion. His choice was whether to trust the system enough to start that chain.

steps.title: He trusted judgment over the machine
steps.body: Petrov decided it was a false alarm and reported a malfunction. His logic: a genuine US attack would come as a massive launch, not five lonely missiles. He was right. The system had been fooled by sunlight glinting off high clouds, which the satellite read as rocket flares. One person's reasoning, against the confident output of a machine, kept the alarm from climbing the ladder.

steps.prompt: What had actually fooled the satellite system?
options: A real missile test
Sunlight reflecting off high-altitude clouds
A radar jamming attack
steps.explain: Sunlight on high clouds. The satellite mistook those bright reflections for the flares of launching missiles. The threat was never real, which is exactly why Petrov's skepticism, not the machine's certainty, was the safe call.

steps.prompt: Put the 1983 incident in order.
items: The early-warning system reports incoming US missiles
Petrov notices the launch is too small to be a real first strike
He reports it as a malfunction, not an attack
The cause is later traced to sunlight on high clouds

steps.prompt: Why did the small number of missiles make Petrov doubt the warning?
options: Small launches are easy to shoot down
A real first strike would be massive, so a few missiles made no sense
The system always exaggerates
steps.explain: It made no military sense. A surprise attack aims to knock out retaliation, which needs overwhelming numbers. Five missiles looked nothing like that, and spotting the mismatch is what let him call the glitch correctly.

steps.title: Question the alarm.
recap: In 1983 a Soviet system falsely reported US missiles.
Petrov judged it a glitch and didn't escalate.
Sunlight on clouds had fooled the satellite.

subject: HISTORY
title: Many Country Borders Were Drawn in Europe
blurb: A lot of modern borders in Africa and the Middle East were sketched by colonial officials, often ignoring the people who lived there.

steps.title: Borders drawn far from home
steps.sub: Look at a map of Africa and you'll see oddly straight lines. They weren't drawn by the people who lived under them.

steps.prompt: Why do some African and Middle Eastern borders run in dead-straight lines?
options: They follow rivers and mountain ranges
They were drawn on maps by officials, not along local divisions
Straight borders are easier to defend
steps.explain: They were drawn on paper. Natural borders follow rivers, coasts, and ridges, which are never perfectly straight. A ruler-straight line is the fingerprint of someone drawing on a map from a distance, paying little attention to who actually lived on each side.

steps.title: Carved up at a conference
steps.body: At the Berlin Conference of 1884 to 1885, European powers set rules for dividing Africa among themselves. They split territory to suit their own claims, often using map lines rather than the real boundaries between peoples. In the Middle East, deals like the Sykes-Picot Agreement of 1916 saw Britain and France quietly carve the region into spheres of influence. The people living there were not consulted.

steps.prompt: What did European powers mainly use to divide Africa at the Berlin Conference?
options: Existing boundaries between local peoples
Map lines that suited their own claims
Votes from local populations
steps.explain: Map lines for their own benefit. The goal was to settle European claims, not to respect communities on the ground. That's why the borders so often cut across the real human geography of the continent.

steps.title: The lines outlived the empires
steps.body: When colonies won independence, mostly in the mid-20th century, most kept the borders they had inherited. Those lines sometimes split a single ethnic group across several countries, or forced rival groups together inside one. New states largely chose to keep the inherited borders anyway, fearing that redrawing them would spark endless conflict. So colonial map lines became permanent national borders.

steps.prompt: Why did newly independent states mostly keep their inherited colonial borders?
options: The old borders matched local divisions perfectly
Redrawing them risked sparking endless conflict
Europe forced them to at gunpoint
steps.explain: Redrawing was too risky. If every group claimed new lines, the result could be constant border wars. Keeping the flawed inherited borders was the lesser evil, which is how colonial lines became locked in.

steps.prompt: Put the story of these borders in order.
items: European powers divide territory at conferences and secret deals
Borders are drawn on maps, ignoring who lives where
Colonies gain independence in the mid-20th century
New states keep the inherited borders to avoid conflict

steps.prompt: What does a dead-straight national border usually tell you?
options: It follows a major river
It was drawn on a map rather than along local divisions
It marks an ancient kingdom
steps.explain: Drawn on a map. Real geography and human settlement rarely line up in straight lines. A ruler-straight border is the trace of officials dividing land from afar, not a boundary that grew from the people living there.

steps.title: Lines have authors.
recap: European powers divided Africa and the Middle East on maps.
Borders ignored the people who actually lived there.
Independent states kept the lines to avoid new wars.

subject: HISTORY
title: The Spanish Flu Wasn't Spanish
blurb: The 1918 flu got its name from wartime censorship, not from where it started.

steps.title: A name born from silence.
steps.sub: The deadliest flu of the 20th century is called Spanish. It almost certainly did not start there. The name is an accident of war.

steps.prompt: Why might a disease get named after a country it did not start in?
options: That country reported on it openly while others hid it
That country had the best doctors
That country asked for the honor
steps.explain: Open reporting. During World War I, warring nations censored bad news. Spain was neutral and printed the truth, so the flu looked like a Spanish problem when it was everywhere.

steps.title: War made everyone go quiet
steps.body: It was 1918, the last year of World War I. Countries at war controlled their newspapers to protect morale. Reports of a fast, deadly flu sweeping through troops and cities were played down or buried. Admitting your army was sick was a gift to the enemy. So the press in fighting nations stayed largely silent about the outbreak.

steps.prompt: Why did warring nations downplay the flu in 1918?
options: They did not think it was serious
Censorship protected morale and hid weakness from the enemy
No newspapers existed yet
steps.explain: Censorship. Wartime governments suppressed bad news to keep morale up and avoid showing weakness, so the spreading flu went underreported in their press.

steps.title: Neutral Spain told the truth
steps.body: Spain stayed out of the war. With no wartime censorship pressing on its papers, Spanish journalists covered the flu openly, including when it struck their own country. To readers elsewhere, Spain looked like the place drowning in the disease. The label Spanish flu stuck, even though historians still cannot pin down where the pandemic actually began.

steps.prompt: What made Spain report on the flu so freely?
options: It was where the flu definitely began
It was neutral, so its press was not under wartime censorship
Its government wanted the blame
steps.explain: Neutrality. Spain was not at war, so its newspapers were not muzzled by wartime censorship and could cover the outbreak plainly. That openness, not origin, earned it the name.

steps.prompt: Earlier: why did the fighting nations keep quiet about the flu?
options: Wartime censorship to protect morale
They had cured it already
steps.explain: Wartime censorship. The contrast between silent warring nations and open, neutral Spain is exactly what produced the misleading name.

steps.title: Named by the messenger.
recap: Warring nations censored news of the 1918 flu.
Neutral Spain reported it openly, so the name stuck.
Where the pandemic truly began is still unknown.

subject: HISTORY
title: Banning Alcohol Made Drinking Worse
blurb: Prohibition didn't dry out America. It handed the liquor trade to criminals.

steps.title: The cure that spread the disease.
steps.sub: America banned alcohol to make people healthier and safer. The ban did the opposite in several ways at once.

steps.prompt: If alcohol becomes illegal but people still want it, who is most likely to supply it?
options: Licensed, regulated businesses
Criminal operations willing to break the law
Nobody, demand simply vanishes
steps.explain: Criminals. A ban does not erase demand. It just removes legal sellers and hands a profitable market to people willing to break the law, with no rules on quality or safety.

steps.title: A gift to organized crime
steps.body: Prohibition began in 1920 under the 18th Amendment. People still wanted to drink, so a huge illegal market opened overnight. Criminal gangs rushed in to make, smuggle, and sell liquor through secret bars called speakeasies. The money was enormous, and it bankrolled the rise of organized crime, including figures like Al Capone. The law created the very underworld it claimed to fight.

steps.prompt: What did Prohibition do to organized crime?
options: Starved it of money
Fueled its growth by handing it the liquor market
Had no effect on it
steps.explain: It fueled it. With legal sellers gone but demand intact, gangs took over the trade, and the profits powered the rise of organized crime.

steps.title: Riskier liquor, harder drinks
steps.body: Illegal booze had no quality control. Some of it was contaminated or cut with dangerous substances, and tainted liquor sickened and killed people. Smuggling also rewarded strength. Hauling weak, bulky beer was a poor use of risk, so bootleggers favored high-proof spirits that packed more alcohol into less space. The ban nudged drinkers toward harder, more dangerous stuff.

steps.prompt: Why did bootleggers favor strong spirits over beer?
options: People stopped liking beer
Spirits packed more alcohol into less space, making the smuggling risk pay off
Beer was actually legal
steps.explain: Concentration. Smuggling was risky, so it paid to move the most alcohol per trip. High-proof spirits beat bulky beer, pushing drinkers toward harder liquor.

steps.prompt: Put these in order.
items: Prohibition takes effect
Speakeasies and bootleggers boom
Prohibition is repealed

steps.prompt: Earlier: what was the effect of banning alcohol on crime?
options: It fueled organized crime
It eliminated crime
steps.explain: It fueled it. Demand stayed, legal supply vanished, and criminal gangs grew rich filling the gap, which is the core backfire of Prohibition.

steps.title: Ban it and watch it warp.
recap: Prohibition handed the liquor trade to organized crime.
Unregulated booze poisoned drinkers, and smuggling favored hard spirits.
It started in 1920 and was repealed in 1933.

subject: HISTORY
title: Apollo Flew on Less Power Than Your Phone
blurb: The computer that landed humans on the Moon was almost unimaginably weak by today's standards.

steps.title: Tiny brain, giant leap.
steps.sub: We picture moon rockets needing supercomputers. The truth is the opposite. The flight computer was feeble by any modern measure.

steps.prompt: Compared to a modern smartphone, how powerful was the Apollo flight computer?
options: Far more powerful
About the same
Far less powerful, by a huge margin
steps.explain: Far less powerful. The Apollo Guidance Computer had memory measured in tens of kilobytes and ran around a megahertz. A modern phone beats it by many orders of magnitude on both counts.

steps.title: Kilobytes, not gigabytes
steps.body: Apollo 11 reached the Moon in 1969 using the Apollo Guidance Computer. Its memory was measured in tens of kilobytes, and it ran at a clock speed of roughly one megahertz. A modern smartphone carries gigabytes of memory and processors clocked in gigahertz. The phone in your pocket outclasses that flight computer by many orders of magnitude. It is not even close.

steps.prompt: Roughly how much memory did the Apollo Guidance Computer have?
options: Tens of kilobytes
Gigabytes, like a phone
No memory at all
steps.explain: Tens of kilobytes. That is a vanishingly small amount next to a phone's gigabytes, which is why the comparison is so striking.

steps.title: People made up the difference
steps.body: If the computer was that weak, how did Apollo work? Through human skill and careful engineering, backed by a small army on the ground. The flight computer handled focused guidance tasks, not everything. During the Apollo 11 landing it even threw alarm codes, and the crew kept going with help from mission control. Apollo was a triumph of people and planning more than raw computing.

steps.prompt: How did Apollo succeed with such a weak computer?
options: The computer was secretly very powerful
Human skill, engineering, and ground support carried the load
It did not really need to navigate
steps.explain: People and planning. The computer did focused work, while crew skill, careful engineering, and mission control filled the gaps, even handling alarms during the landing.

steps.prompt: Earlier: how does the Apollo computer compare to a modern phone?
options: Far weaker, by many orders of magnitude
Slightly stronger
steps.explain: Far weaker. With tens of kilobytes of memory and around a megahertz of speed, it is dwarfed by a modern smartphone many times over.

steps.title: Small machine, huge feat.
recap: The Apollo computer had tens of kilobytes of memory and ran near a megahertz.
A modern phone outclasses it by many orders of magnitude.
Human skill and ground support, not raw computing, made Apollo work.

subject: HISTORY
title: Gandhi Beat an Empire With Salt
blurb: A long walk to make illegal salt was a calculated act of protest that shook British rule in India.

steps.title: The most ordinary weapon.
steps.sub: In 1930 Gandhi walked to the sea to make salt. It sounds small. It was one of the sharpest protests of the century.

steps.prompt: Why pick salt, of all things, to defy an empire?
options: Salt was rare and valuable
Everyone needs salt, so the unfair law touched everyone
Salt was secretly a weapon
steps.explain: Universality. The British taxed and monopolized salt, a thing every person uses daily. An unjust tax on a basic necessity was easy for everyone to feel and to rally behind.

steps.title: An unfair law on a basic need
steps.body: Under British rule, the colonial government held a monopoly on salt and taxed it. Indians were barred from making or selling their own, even though it sat in their own seawater. Gandhi saw the genius in targeting it. Salt was used by everyone, rich and poor alike. A protest over salt was a protest everyone could understand and join, which made it powerful.

steps.prompt: Why was the salt law a smart target for protest?
options: Salt was a luxury only the rich cared about
Salt was a basic necessity everyone used, so the unfairness was universal
Nobody actually used salt
steps.explain: It was universal. Because everyone used salt, an unfair tax on it angered everyone, giving the protest broad, shared appeal.

steps.title: A walk built to be watched
steps.body: Gandhi marched roughly 240 miles to the coastal village of Dandi, gathering followers and press as he went. At the sea he scooped up salt from the water, openly breaking the law. This was nonviolent civil disobedience, his satyagraha, staged for attention. The slow walk gave newspapers time to cover it, and the images of peaceful defiance exposed the harshness of colonial rule worldwide.

steps.prompt: What did the length and pace of the march achieve?
options: It tired everyone out and killed interest
It gave the press time to cover it and built attention along the way
It kept the protest secret
steps.explain: Attention. The long, slow march let crowds and newspapers gather, turning a single act into a sustained, widely watched story of peaceful defiance.

steps.prompt: Put these in order.
items: March begins at Sabarmati Ashram
Gandhi reaches the coast at Dandi
Gandhi makes salt and breaks the law
India gains independence

steps.prompt: Earlier: why was salt the chosen target?
options: Everyone used it, so the unfair law touched everyone
It was rare and expensive
steps.explain: Everyone used it. A tax on a daily necessity was felt by all, which is exactly what made the salt protest so broadly powerful.

steps.title: Small act, large blow.
recap: The 1930 Salt March protested Britain's salt monopoly and tax.
Salt was chosen because everyone used it, making the unfairness universal.
The slow, public march drew the press and galvanized the movement.

subject: HISTORY
title: The New Deal Didn't End the Depression
blurb: The New Deal eased the pain, but the economy fully recovered only with the spending of the 1940s.

steps.title: The rescue that didn't finish the job.
steps.sub: The story says the New Deal beat the Great Depression. The timeline tells a more complicated tale.

steps.prompt: When did the US economy truly recover from the Great Depression?
options: Soon after the New Deal began in 1933
During the wartime spending of the 1940s
It never recovered
steps.explain: The 1940s. The New Deal eased suffering, but unemployment stayed high through the 1930s. Full recovery came with the enormous government spending of World War II.

steps.title: Relief, but not a cure
steps.body: The Great Depression hit after the 1929 crash. Starting in 1933, President Roosevelt's New Deal poured out relief programs, jobs, and reforms. They softened real misery and reshaped the economy for the long run. But they did not fully end the Depression. Unemployment stayed high through much of the 1930s, and a sharp downturn in 1937 to 1938 showed how fragile the recovery still was.

steps.prompt: What does the 1937 to 1938 downturn tell us about the New Deal?
options: The Depression was fully over by then
Recovery was still fragile and unemployment was still high
The New Deal had not started yet
steps.explain: Recovery was fragile. The mid-decade slump and persistent high unemployment show the New Deal eased the pain without finishing off the Depression.

steps.title: The war spent the slump away
steps.body: What finally pulled the economy out was the 1940s. As the US geared up for and entered World War II after Pearl Harbor in late 1941, the government spent on a massive scale. Factories ran flat out, and military demand soaked up workers. Unemployment fell sharply. Economists still argue over the exact split, but huge wartime spending, more than the New Deal alone, drove the full recovery.

steps.prompt: What drove the full economic recovery in the 1940s?
options: A drop in government spending
Massive wartime government spending and military demand
The repeal of all New Deal programs
steps.explain: Wartime spending. Gearing up for World War II flooded the economy with spending and demand, putting people back to work and finishing the recovery.

steps.prompt: Put these in order.
items: Stock market crash
New Deal begins
Roosevelt recession
Wartime spending drives recovery

steps.prompt: Earlier: did the New Deal fully end the Depression?
options: No, unemployment stayed high through the 1930s
Yes, it ended it within a year
steps.explain: No. It provided relief and reform, but high unemployment persisted, and full recovery waited for the wartime spending of the 1940s.

steps.title: Aid first, recovery later.
recap: The New Deal eased the Depression but did not end it.
Unemployment stayed high, with a sharp slump in 1937 to 1938.
Massive wartime spending in the 1940s drove the full recovery.

subject: HISTORY
title: Why a Single Resource Redrew the 20th Century
blurb: When the world switched from coal to oil, it also switched which countries mattered and which wars could be fought.

steps.title: One fuel, a new map
steps.sub: For a century, coal ran the world. Then ships, armies, and economies switched to oil. The switch changed which countries held power and which could go to war.

steps.prompt: Why might a navy switch its warships from coal to oil before World War I?
options: Oil was cheaper everywhere
Oil ships were faster, traveled farther, and refueled more easily
Coal had run out
steps.explain: Performance, not price. Oil-fired ships ran faster, covered more distance, and refueled far more easily than ships that needed crews to shovel coal. That edge was worth depending on a fuel many countries did not have at home.

steps.title: Coal was the old power, and it was everywhere
steps.body: Through the 1800s and early 1900s, coal ran everything. Factories, trains, and warships all burned it. Coal was also spread widely across industrial Europe, so the countries with industry usually had fuel under their own ground. Power and fuel sat in the same place. That kept the strategic map fairly simple.

steps.prompt: What made coal convenient for industrial Europe?
options: It was found widely across the industrial countries themselves
It only existed in the Middle East
It burned without producing smoke
steps.explain: It was local. Coal lay under much of industrial Europe, so the countries with factories usually had their own fuel. Power and fuel sat together, which kept dependence low.

steps.title: Oil broke that link
steps.body: Oil gave better speed, range, and easy refueling, so navies and then armies switched. But oil was not spread evenly. Big reserves sat in a few regions, like the Middle East, the United States, the Caucasus, and Latin America. Now a powerful country might have no oil at home. Fuel and power came apart. Suddenly far-off oil regions mattered enormously, and securing supply became a reason for alliances and wars.

steps.prompt: Why did the shift to oil change which countries mattered?
options: Oil was spread evenly, so everyone gained equally
Oil sat in only a few regions, so powerful countries now depended on far-off supply
Oil made coal regions richer
steps.explain: Uneven supply. Unlike coal, oil was concentrated in a few regions. An industrial power could lack it entirely, so distant oil lands gained outsized importance and became worth fighting over.

steps.prompt: Order how oil reshaped the strategic map.
items: Coal runs industry, and fuel sits where the power is
Navies switch to oil for speed and range
Power and fuel come apart, since oil sits in a few regions
Distant oil regions become strategic prizes

glosses.Coal runs industry, and fuel sits where: Industrial Europe had its own coal.
glosses.Power and fuel come apart, since oil sit: Many powers had no oil at home.
glosses.Distant oil regions become strategic pri: Securing supply drives alliances and wars.

steps.prompt: Earlier: what was the main reason navies switched warships from coal to oil?
options: Oil was always cheaper
Oil ships had better speed, range, and refueling
Coal was banned
steps.explain: Better performance. Oil-fired ships ran faster, went farther, and refueled more easily. That advantage was worth depending on a fuel that often had to come from somewhere else.

steps.title: Fuel is power.
recap: Coal sat where industrial power already was.
Oil gave a real edge but sat in a few regions.
That split made distant oil lands strategic.

subject: HISTORY
title: Radio Was the First Mass-Manipulation Machine
blurb: Before TV or the internet, radio let one voice reach millions at once. Dictators noticed fast.

steps.title: A voice in every home
steps.sub: Print could spread ideas, but you had to read it, and you read it alone. Radio put a live human voice into millions of homes at the same instant. That was new, and it was powerful.

steps.prompt: What could radio do that newspapers could not?
options: Reach people who could not read, with a live voice, all at the same moment
Be stored and reread later
Carry photographs
steps.explain: A live voice to everyone at once. Print needs a reader and is read at different times. Radio delivered a speaker's actual voice, tone and all, to a whole audience in the same instant, even to people who could not read.

steps.title: One voice, millions of ears, all at once
steps.body: Before radio, mass communication meant print. You read a paper or pamphlet, by yourself, whenever you got to it. Radio changed the shape of that. A single speaker could be heard live, in millions of homes, at the same moment. It did not need the listener to read, and it carried tone and feeling that flat text could not. One person could now address a nation directly.

steps.prompt: What was different about how radio reached its audience?
options: Each person read it at a different time
It reached the whole audience live, at the same moment, by voice
It only reached people who already owned newspapers
steps.explain: Live and simultaneous. Print is read alone at scattered times. Radio hit everyone at once with a speaking voice, which made it feel personal and shared at the same time.

steps.title: Leaders saw the lever and pulled it
steps.body: A direct line into every home is a powerful tool, and governments grabbed it. In Germany after 1933, the Nazi regime used radio hard for propaganda and pushed a cheap receiver so more people could tune in. Not all radio use was sinister. In the United States, President Roosevelt used calm radio talks, the 'fireside chats,' to reassure the public. Same technology, very different ends. The point is that radio handed whoever controlled it a direct channel to millions of minds.

steps.prompt: Why was radio attractive to a government that wanted to shape opinion?
options: It was hard for anyone to hear
It gave a direct, emotional channel into millions of homes at once
It could only be used for music
steps.explain: Direct access. Radio let a leader speak straight into homes, with tone and emotion, reaching huge numbers instantly. Whoever controlled the broadcast controlled a powerful channel to the public.

steps.prompt: Order how radio became a mass tool.
items: Mass communication means printed text, read alone
Radio spreads, putting a live voice in homes
One speaker can reach millions at the same moment
Governments seize the channel to shape opinion

glosses.Mass communication means printed text, r: You had to read, at your own time.
glosses.Radio spreads, putting a live voice in h: A speaking voice, not just print.

steps.prompt: Earlier: what could radio do that a newspaper could not?
options: Be reread weeks later
Deliver a live voice to a whole audience at the same moment, even to non-readers
Print in color
steps.explain: Live voice, all at once. Print is read alone at different times. Radio carried a speaker's actual voice to everyone simultaneously, reaching people who could not read, which is exactly what made it so persuasive.

steps.title: The medium gave the power.
recap: Radio put one live voice in millions of homes at once.
It reached non-readers and carried tone print could not.
Governments, good and bad, used that direct channel.

subject: HISTORY
title: The Fridge Reshaped Where People Live
blurb: Cheap cooling let food travel and let hot regions fill up. It quietly redrew the population map.

steps.title: Cold changed the map
steps.sub: It sounds dull, but cheap cooling moved food and people. It let food travel far and let hot places fill with residents. Where humans live today is partly a refrigeration story.

steps.prompt: Before mechanical refrigeration, why was fresh meat or milk hard to sell far from where it was produced?
options: People did not like fresh food
It spoiled quickly, so it could not travel or sit for long
Roads did not exist yet
steps.explain: Spoilage. Without reliable cooling, perishable food went bad fast. You could salt or dry it, but fresh meat, milk, and produce could not survive a long trip or a long wait. Refrigeration removed that limit.

steps.title: Cooling let food travel
steps.body: Before machines, keeping food cold meant ice, cellars, salting, or drying. Fresh food spoiled fast, so it stayed local. Mechanical refrigeration changed that. Refrigerated rail cars, then trucks and ships, let meat, dairy, and produce travel long distances and keep for longer. A city no longer had to grow or raise everything nearby. Food could come from far away and still arrive fresh.

steps.prompt: How did refrigeration change where a city's food could come from?
options: Food still had to be produced nearby
Fresh food could now travel from far away and still arrive good
Cities stopped eating fresh food
steps.explain: It widened the range. Refrigerated transport let perishable food survive long trips, so a city could be fed from distant farms and regions instead of only its immediate surroundings.

steps.title: The same trick cooled people, not just food
steps.body: Air conditioning uses the same basic principle as a fridge, moving heat out of a space with a refrigerant. As it spread through the 20th century, hot and humid regions became far more comfortable to live and work in year round. In the United States, warm states across the South and West grew strongly in the later 1900s. Cheap cooling helped make those places attractive. So one technology moved food in and people too.

steps.prompt: How did air conditioning affect where people chose to live?
options: It made cold regions colder
It made hot, humid regions more livable, helping their populations grow
It had no effect on population
steps.explain: It made heat bearable. By cooling homes and workplaces, air conditioning made hot regions comfortable year round, which helped draw and keep large populations in places that were once harder to settle.

steps.prompt: Order how cooling reshaped the map.
items: Fresh food spoils fast, so it stays local
Refrigerated transport lets food travel far
Air conditioning makes hot regions livable
Population shifts toward warm regions

glosses.Population shifts toward warm regions: Warm states grow in the later 1900s.

steps.prompt: Earlier: before mechanical refrigeration, why did fresh perishable food stay local?
options: It spoiled quickly, so it could not survive long trips
People preferred local food for taste
There were no farms far from cities
steps.explain: Spoilage limited it. Without reliable cooling, fresh meat, milk, and produce went bad before they could travel far. Refrigerated transport removed that barrier and let food come from a distance.

steps.title: Cold moved the world.
recap: Refrigeration let perishable food travel and keep.
Air conditioning made hot regions livable year round.
Together they shifted where people and food go.

subject: HISTORY
title: A Policy, Not a Drought, Caused History's Deadliest Famine
blurb: The Great Leap Forward famine came from forced policy and faked numbers, not nature.

steps.title: When the cause was the plan
steps.sub: Famines feel like weather. This one was mostly policy. China's Great Leap Forward killed on the order of tens of millions, and the main driver was decisions, not drought.

steps.prompt: What is usually blamed for a famine, and what does this case challenge?
options: Bad weather and crop failure; here the bigger cause was policy
Too much rain; here it was too little
Overpopulation alone; here it was disease
steps.explain: We blame nature, but here policy did most of the damage. Drought and bad harvests can cause famine, yet historians hold that the Great Leap famine was driven mainly by the campaign's own decisions, not by the weather.

steps.title: The plan reorganized farming by force
steps.body: Starting in 1958, under Mao Zedong, China launched the Great Leap Forward. It forced farmers into large collectives and set huge, unrealistic targets for grain and industry. Rural labor was also pulled away from fields toward projects like small backyard steel furnaces. Reorganizing all of agriculture this fast, by command, disrupted the very work of growing food. The structure that fed the country was torn up on purpose.

steps.prompt: What did the Great Leap Forward do to farming?
options: Left it untouched
Forced collectivization, set unrealistic targets, and pulled labor away from fields
Gave farmers more freedom to plant what they wanted
steps.explain: It reorganized farming by force. Collectives, impossible targets, and labor diverted to other projects disrupted food production directly. The damage started with the policy, not the sky.

steps.title: Faked numbers made it deadly
steps.body: Here is the cruel part. Under pressure to show success, local officials reported harvests far bigger than what actually came in. The government then took grain based on those inflated figures. So the state hauled away food the villages did not really have, leaving people with nothing. The lie about the harvest turned a bad situation into mass starvation. The result was one of the deadliest famines in recorded history, on the order of tens of millions of deaths.

steps.prompt: Why did faked harvest numbers make the famine worse?
