options: They made farmers plant more
The state took grain based on inflated figures, stripping villages of food they did not have
They had no real effect
steps.explain: The state collected against a lie. Officials reported harvests bigger than reality, so the government seized grain the villages never actually grew, leaving them to starve. The false numbers turned shortage into catastrophe.

steps.prompt: Order how the famine unfolded.
items: The Great Leap Forward forces collectivized farming
Unrealistic targets pressure local officials
Officials report harvests bigger than reality
The state seizes grain villages do not have
Mass starvation follows

glosses.Mass starvation follows: Among the deadliest famines on record.

steps.prompt: Earlier: what do historians point to as the main driver of this famine?
options: Drought and natural disaster alone
Policy decisions like forced collectivization and faked harvest numbers
A foreign blockade
steps.explain: Policy, not nature. Weather played some part, but historians hold that the scale came from the campaign itself: forced collectivization, impossible targets, and false harvest reports that led the state to take food people needed.

steps.title: A disaster by decision.
recap: The Great Leap Forward forced collectivized farming from 1958.
Faked harvest numbers led the state to seize food people lacked.
Policy, not drought, drove the famine's deadly scale.

subject: HISTORY
title: The Suburbs Were Deliberately Built
blurb: Postwar suburbs were not an organic dream. Highways, mortgages, and policy built them, and often excluded whole groups.

steps.title: Built, not grown
steps.sub: The postwar suburb looks like something people just naturally wanted. In fact it was engineered, by highways, cheap mortgages, and policy. And the same machinery left many groups out.

steps.prompt: Why did so many American families move to the suburbs after World War II?
options: Suburbs simply appeared because people preferred them
Government-backed loans and new highways made suburban homes affordable and reachable
Cities banned new housing
steps.explain: Policy made it possible. Government-backed mortgages made buying a suburban house affordable, and new highways made commuting from there practical. The 'natural' move was steered by deliberate programs.

steps.title: Mortgages and highways did the building
steps.body: After World War II, two policy levers created the suburbs. First, government-backed mortgages, through the Federal Housing Administration and the GI Bill for veterans, made buying a house affordable for many families. Second, huge highway construction, including the Interstate system authorized in 1956, made it practical to live outside the city and drive in. Builders mass-produced whole towns, like the Levittowns. The suburb was a product of policy, not just taste.

steps.prompt: Which two policy levers drove postwar suburban growth?
options: Higher city taxes and rent control
Government-backed mortgages and large-scale highway building
Factory closures and farm subsidies
steps.explain: Cheap loans and roads. Government-backed mortgages made buying affordable, and new highways made commuting practical. Together they made the suburbs both reachable and within reach financially.

steps.title: The same machinery shut people out
steps.body: These benefits were not shared equally. Through redlining, maps and lending rules rated some neighborhoods, especially those with Black and minority residents, as risky, so loans were denied or limited there. Racially restrictive covenants also blocked many Black Americans and others from buying in many suburbs. So the policies that handed cheap homes and growing wealth to some families deliberately excluded others. That helped create racial gaps in homeownership and wealth that lasted for generations.

steps.prompt: What was redlining?
options: A road-painting program for new highways
Rating neighborhoods for lending so loans were denied or limited, often where Black and minority residents lived
A discount on suburban homes for everyone
steps.explain: It was discriminatory lending. Maps and rules marked some neighborhoods, especially minority ones, as risky, choking off mortgages there. That excluded many families from the wealth-building the suburbs offered others.

steps.prompt: Order how the suburbs were engineered.
items: Government backs cheap mortgages after the war
New highways make commuting practical
Builders mass-produce suburban towns
Redlining and covenants exclude many minority families

glosses.Government backs cheap mortgages after t: FHA loans and the GI Bill.

steps.prompt: Earlier: what really drove families to the suburbs after the war?
options: Suburbs grew naturally with no policy behind them
Government-backed loans and new highways made them affordable and reachable
Cities outlawed apartments
steps.explain: Deliberate policy. Cheap government-backed mortgages and major highway building made suburban homes both affordable and easy to commute from. The move looked natural but was actively engineered.

steps.title: Policy shapes the map.
recap: Cheap mortgages and highways built the postwar suburbs.
Redlining and covenants shut out many minority families.
The result was a lasting racial wealth gap.

subject: HISTORY
title: One Tiny Pill Rewired Society
blurb: The contraceptive pill reshaped careers, families, and economies faster than almost any technology of its century.

steps.title: A pill, not a law
steps.sub: No army moved. No border changed. A small daily pill quietly reorganized work, marriage, and money across a generation.

steps.prompt: Beyond family size, what did reliable contraception most directly change?
options: Nothing much outside the home
When women could invest in school and careers
Mainly the price of medicine
steps.explain: It changed timing. Once a woman could reliably delay children, she could commit years to a degree or a career without that plan being upended. Control over timing turned out to be control over a life path.

steps.title: Control over timing, not just numbers
steps.body: The pill was approved in the United States in 1960. For the first time, many women could reliably decide whether and when to have children, using hormones to prevent ovulation. The deep change was not just smaller families. It was timing. You could plan around a degree or a job instead of planning around uncertainty. A decision that used to be left to chance became something you could schedule.

steps.prompt: What was the deeper shift the pill enabled?
options: Cheaper healthcare overall
Reliable control over the timing of children
An end to all family planning debate
steps.explain: Timing. Smaller families were one effect, but the bigger one was predictability. When you can choose when, you can plan everything else around it, from school to work to marriage.

steps.title: The career math changed
steps.body: Long training, like law, medicine, or graduate study, only pays off if you can finish it. Before reliable contraception, that bet was risky for women. Economists Claudia Goldin and Lawrence Katz tied the spread of the pill to a jump in women entering higher education and professional fields. Court rulings widened access: Griswold v. Connecticut in 1965 for married couples, and Eisenstadt v. Baird in 1972 for unmarried people. The pill changed the odds on a long-term bet, so more women took it.

steps.prompt: Why did reliable contraception push more women into long professional training?
options: It paid for tuition directly
It made finishing a long program a safer bet
It was required for college admission
steps.explain: It de-risked the bet. A multi-year degree only pays off if you complete it. Reliable timing made that completion far more predictable, so the investment made sense for many more women.

steps.prompt: Put the pill's spread in the United States in order.
items: The pill is approved in 1960
Griswold v. Connecticut allows it for married couples in 1965
Eisenstadt v. Baird extends access to unmarried people in 1972
Women's enrollment in professional training climbs

glosses.The pill is approved in 1960: FDA clears the first oral contraceptive.

steps.prompt: In what year was the pill first approved in the United States?
steps.explain: 1960. That approval marked the start of reliable, woman-controlled contraception, and the social and economic shifts followed over the next two decades.

steps.title: Small object, large rewiring.
recap: The pill was approved in 1960.
Its power was control over timing, not just family size.
That changed the math on careers and schooling.

subject: HISTORY
title: A Metal Box Created Globalization
blurb: The standardized shipping container slashed cargo costs so dramatically it made the modern global economy physically possible.

steps.title: The boring box that won
steps.sub: It has no engine and no moving parts. Yet the steel shipping container may have shaped global trade more than any ship that carries it.

steps.prompt: Before containers, what was the most expensive part of ocean shipping?
options: Fuel for the long voyage
The labor of loading and unloading at the docks
Insurance against storms
steps.explain: The dockwork. Cargo was handled piece by piece by gangs of workers, which was slow and costly. A ship could sit in port for days. The voyage itself was often the cheap part. The box attacked the expensive part.

steps.title: The old way: handle everything twice
steps.body: Before containers, ocean cargo moved as break-bulk: barrels, sacks, and crates loaded one item at a time by dockworkers. It was slow, needed lots of labor, and ships idled in port while it happened. Every transfer between truck, warehouse, and ship meant unpacking and repacking. The cost of moving goods was dominated by all that handling, not the sailing.

steps.prompt: What made break-bulk shipping so expensive?
options: Loading and unloading goods one piece at a time
The ships were extremely slow
Goods spoiled on every trip
steps.explain: The piece-by-piece handling. Loading and unloading by hand needed large crews and kept ships stuck in port. That labor, repeated at every transfer, drove the cost.

steps.title: Pack once, move anywhere
steps.body: In 1956, trucking businessman Malcom McLean sent containers from Newark to Houston on a converted tanker, the Ideal X. The idea was simple: pack goods once into a standard steel box, then move the whole box between ship, truck, and train without unpacking it. Cranes replaced crews. Ports cleared in hours, not days. Sizes were standardized worldwide, commonly 20 and 40 feet. Cheap shipping then made it practical to build factories on one continent and sell on another.

steps.prompt: What is the core advantage of a standard container?
options: It is waterproof in storms
It moves between ship, truck, and train without unpacking
It holds more than any other method
steps.explain: No unpacking between modes. You load goods once and the sealed box travels by ship, truck, and rail. Cranes move it fast, which removes the costly hand-handling at every transfer.

steps.prompt: Put the container revolution in order.
items: Cargo moves slowly as hand-loaded break-bulk
McLean ships the first containers in 1956
Container sizes are standardized worldwide
Shipping costs fall and global supply chains grow

glosses.McLean ships the first containers in 195: The Ideal X carries the first boxes.
glosses.Container sizes are standardized worldwi: Boxes fit any ship, truck, or train.

steps.prompt: Why did break-bulk shipping cost so much before containers?
options: Fuel was extremely expensive
Goods were handled one piece at a time by hand
Ports charged very high docking fees
steps.explain: Hand-handling. Loading and unloading each item by crews was slow and labor-heavy, and it happened at every transfer. The container removed that step, which is where the savings came from.

steps.title: The cost was on the dock.
recap: Old shipping was slow, hand-loaded break-bulk.
McLean's 1956 container packed goods once for any mode.
Cheaper transport made global supply chains practical.

subject: HISTORY
title: America Rebuilt Its Enemies on Purpose
blurb: The Marshall Plan poured money into former rivals, not just as charity, but as a strategy to create trading partners and block communism.

steps.title: Charity with a strategy
steps.sub: After the war, the United States sent billions to rebuild Europe, including countries it had just fought. Generous, yes. But also calculated.

steps.prompt: Why would a victor pour money into rebuilding former enemies?
options: Pure guilt over the war
To create stable trading partners and block a rival ideology
Because the law required it
steps.explain: Self-interest dressed as aid. A prosperous Europe would buy American goods and resist communism. Rebuilding former enemies was less about forgiveness than about building customers and allies that served U.S. interests.

steps.title: What the plan actually was
steps.body: Secretary of State George C. Marshall laid out the idea in a 1947 speech at Harvard. Starting in 1948, the United States sent billions of dollars to help Western Europe recover from World War II. The official name was the European Recovery Program. Aid even went to former enemies like West Germany and Italy. It ran for about four years. This was not scattered handouts. It was a coordinated push to get whole economies running again.

steps.prompt: Who was the Marshall Plan named after?
options: A U.S. president
U.S. Secretary of State George C. Marshall
A European prime minister
steps.explain: Secretary of State George C. Marshall, who outlined it in a 1947 Harvard speech. The name stuck to the program he proposed, formally the European Recovery Program.

steps.title: Two goals behind the generosity
steps.body: The plan served clear U.S. interests. First, a recovered Europe would be a stable trading partner and a market for American goods. Sell to people who can afford to buy. Second, hardship feeds extremism. By improving conditions in Western Europe, the United States aimed to reduce the appeal of communism. The Soviet Union and the Eastern European countries under its influence did not take part. The aid line became part of the Cold War line.

steps.prompt: Which was a U.S. goal of the Marshall Plan?
options: To weaken its own economy
To reduce the appeal of communism in Western Europe
To fund the Soviet Union's recovery
steps.explain: Blunting communism. Poverty makes radical ideas attractive, so improving Western European conditions was a way to keep those countries in the U.S. camp. The Soviet bloc did not participate.

steps.prompt: Put the Marshall Plan's arc in order.
items: World War II leaves Europe's economies in ruins
Marshall proposes the aid plan in a 1947 speech
Aid begins flowing to Western Europe in 1948
The Soviet bloc declines and stays out

glosses.Marshall proposes the aid plan in a 1947: The idea goes public at Harvard.

steps.prompt: Beyond goodwill, why did the U.S. rebuild former enemies?
options: To create trading partners and block communism
Only to apologize for the war
To prepare them for another war
steps.explain: Trading partners and a wall against communism. A prosperous, friendly Europe bought American goods and resisted Soviet influence. The generosity also served clear strategic interests.

steps.title: Generous and self-interested.
recap: The Marshall Plan rebuilt Western Europe from 1948.
It aided former enemies like West Germany.
Goals: trading partners and a check on communism.

subject: HISTORY
title: The 'End of History' Didn't Happen
blurb: After the Cold War, many declared liberal democracy had permanently won. The following decades dismantled that confident prediction.

steps.title: We won, now what?
steps.sub: As the Soviet bloc fell, a bold claim spread: the big argument about how to govern was over, and democracy had won for good.

steps.prompt: What did 'the end of history' actually claim was ending?
options: That events would stop happening
The big ideological contest over how to govern
The study of history in schools
steps.explain: The ideological contest. Not events, which kept coming. The claim was that the great debate over which system should govern had effectively been settled in favor of liberal democracy. History as drama continued. History as argument, supposedly, did not.

steps.title: The claim, and what it meant
steps.body: In 1989, political scientist Francis Fukuyama published an essay, 'The End of History?', expanded into a 1992 book. The timing mattered: the Cold War was ending and the Soviet bloc was collapsing. His point was not that events would stop. It was that liberal democracy might be the final, enduring form of government, because the big ideological rivals had lost. He drew on Hegel's idea that history moves toward an endpoint.

steps.prompt: Who made the 'end of history' argument?
options: Francis Fukuyama
Karl Marx
George Marshall
steps.explain: Francis Fukuyama, in a 1989 essay and a 1992 book. He wrote as the Cold War wound down, arguing liberal democracy might be government's final form.

steps.title: Why the decades pushed back
steps.body: The prediction looked shaky as time passed. China rose as a major power that is wealthy but not liberal democratic, breaking the link between getting rich and turning democratic. And many established democracies showed signs of democratic backsliding, where elected leaders erode checks and norms. The big argument about how to govern clearly was not settled. A confident forecast made at a moment of triumph aged badly.

steps.prompt: Which later development challenged the thesis?
options: The invention of the internet
China rising as a wealthy non-liberal power
The end of the Cold War itself
steps.explain: China's rise. A country can grow rich and powerful without becoming a liberal democracy, which breaks the assumed link. Combined with democratic backsliding elsewhere, it showed the argument was far from over.

steps.prompt: Put the idea's life in order.
items: The Cold War winds down in the late 1980s
Fukuyama publishes 'The End of History?' in 1989
He expands it into a book in 1992
Later decades challenge the thesis

glosses.He expands it into a book in 1992: The argument gets a full treatment.
glosses.Later decades challenge the thesis: Events refuse to settle the argument.

steps.prompt: The phrase 'end of history' meant the end of what?
options: All future events
The big ideological argument over how to govern
The Cold War only
steps.explain: The ideological argument. Events kept happening. The claim was that the debate over which system should rule had been settled for liberal democracy, a claim later decades undercut.

steps.title: Forecasts age fast.
recap: Fukuyama argued liberal democracy had won for good.
He meant the ideological argument, not events, was over.
China's rise and backsliding undercut the claim.

subject: HISTORY
title: The 1918 Flu Wasn't Spanish
blurb: It got the name only because neutral Spain reported it freely while wartime censors elsewhere hid it. Its true origin is still debated.

steps.title: Named by accident
steps.sub: The deadliest flu of the century carries Spain's name. Spain was not where it started. It was just the country honest enough to talk about it.

steps.prompt: Why might a disease get named after a country it didn't come from?
options: That country reported it openly while others stayed quiet
It always means the disease started there
Scientists picked the name at random
steps.explain: Open reporting. If one country talks about an outbreak while everyone else hides it, the disease looks like it lives there. Visibility, not origin, drove the name. Spain got blamed for being honest.

steps.title: A war, and a press blackout
steps.body: The pandemic hit in 1918 and 1919, during and just after World War I. The countries at war censored bad news to protect morale, so reports of a spreading sickness among their soldiers and people were held back. Spain was neutral. Its press was not under the same wartime censorship, so Spanish papers reported the outbreak openly. From the outside, that made it look like Spain was the center of the disease.

steps.prompt: Why did Spanish papers report the flu freely when others did not?
options: Spain had better doctors
Spain was neutral and not under the same wartime censorship
The flu was only in Spain
steps.explain: Neutrality. Spain was not at war, so its press was not muzzled to protect morale. The combatant nations censored the news, so only Spain's reporting was visible, and the name stuck to Spain.

steps.title: Where it really started: still unsettled
steps.body: So if not Spain, where? The honest answer is that the true origin is still debated. Researchers have proposed several sites, including locations in the United States and France, but there is no firm consensus. What is clear is that it was caused by an influenza virus and killed many millions worldwide. The 'Spanish' label is a wartime artifact of who reported the news, not a finding about the source.

steps.prompt: What is known about the pandemic's true origin?
options: It definitely began in Spain
It remains uncertain and debated
It clearly began in China
steps.explain: Still debated. Several origins have been proposed, including sites in the United States and France, but none is settled. The name reflects reporting, not a confirmed source.

steps.prompt: Put the naming story in order.
items: World War I censors hold back news of the outbreak
Neutral Spain's press reports it openly
The disease looks centered in Spain from outside
The 'Spanish flu' name sticks despite an unknown origin

steps.prompt: Why is it called the 'Spanish flu'?
options: Spain reported it openly while wartime censors hid it elsewhere
It was proven to have started in Spain
A Spanish scientist first described it
steps.explain: Open reporting in neutral Spain. Other countries censored the news for morale, so the outbreak only looked Spanish. The name reflects who talked, not where it began.

steps.title: The honest country got blamed.
recap: Wartime censors hid the 1918 flu's spread.
Neutral Spain reported it, so the name stuck.
Its true origin is still debated.

subject: HISTORY
title: The Titanic Met the Rules
blurb: The lifeboat shortage was legal. That was the real scandal.

steps.title: Not enough boats, but not against the law
steps.sub: The Titanic carried too few lifeboats for the people aboard. It still passed inspection. Here is how both can be true.

steps.prompt: How did the Titanic's lifeboat count compare to what the law required?
options: It carried fewer than required and broke the rules
It carried exactly the required minimum
It carried more than the law required
steps.explain: It carried more than required. The rules set a low floor, and the ship cleared it with room to spare. The problem was the floor, not the ship's compliance.

steps.title: The rules were tied to tonnage, and frozen in time
steps.body: British lifeboat rules came from the Board of Trade and were last set in 1894. They scaled by ship size, but the top bracket simply said: ships over 10,000 tons must carry 16 lifeboats. There was no higher rung. So a giant ship and a merely large one faced the same minimum.

steps.prompt: Why did the rules not demand more boats for a bigger ship?
options: Bigger ships were considered unsinkable by law
The rules topped out at 10,000 tons and were set in 1894
The Titanic was registered in a country with no rules
steps.explain: The bracket capped at 10,000 tons. Once a ship passed that mark, the requirement stopped rising. The rules had not been updated since 1894, long before ships this size existed.

steps.title: The ship outgrew the law by miles
steps.body: The Titanic weighed over 46,000 tons, more than four times the bracket's ceiling. The required 16 boats were meant for a ship a fraction of its size. The Titanic actually carried 20, exceeding the minimum, yet still had room for only about a third of everyone aboard at full capacity.

steps.prompt: How many lifeboats did the Titanic carry?
options: 16, the bare legal minimum
20, more than the law required
46, one per thousand tons
steps.explain: Twenty. That beat the 16 required, which is exactly why no inspector could fault it. The law, not the owner, set the trap.

steps.prompt: When were those lifeboat rules last updated before 1912?
steps.explain: 1894. Eighteen years of shipbuilding had raced ahead while the rule sat still. Regulation lagging behind technology is the lesson that outlasts the ship.

steps.title: Legal can still be lethal
recap: The Titanic carried 20 boats, more than the law's 16.
The rules capped at 10,000 tons and dated to 1894.
The ship was over 46,000 tons, far past the bracket.

subject: HISTORY
title: The Berlin Wall Went Up in a Night
blurb: People went to sleep in one city and woke up in two.

steps.title: A border drawn while the city slept
steps.sub: The Berlin Wall feels like it was always there. It was not. It began on a single night and cut through streets, tram lines, and families.

steps.prompt: What went up first on the night the border was sealed?
options: A finished concrete wall
Barbed wire and fencing
A row of guard towers
steps.explain: Barbed wire came first. A concrete wall takes time to build. To seal a border in hours, you string wire and post guards, then pour concrete later.

steps.title: One weekend, one barrier
steps.body: Just after midnight on Sunday, 13 August 1961, East German forces began closing the border with West Berlin. By morning the crossing points were shut and barbed wire ran through the city. Germans call it Barbed Wire Sunday. The solid wall came in the days and years after.

steps.prompt: What is the night of 13 August 1961 called?
options: Barbed Wire Sunday
The Long Weekend
Checkpoint Night
steps.explain: Barbed Wire Sunday. The name fits the method: wire strung overnight, not concrete poured for months. The wall was the second act.

steps.title: It was built to keep people in
steps.body: East Germany was losing people. Millions had fled west through Berlin, the last open gap in the Iron Curtain. The wall's official story was defense against the West. Its real job was to stop its own citizens from leaving. Doors, windows, and families ended up split mid-street.

steps.prompt: What was the wall's real purpose?
options: To defend East Berlin from invasion
To stop East Germans from fleeing west
To mark an agreed national border
steps.explain: To stop people leaving. The defense framing was cover. Berlin was the last easy escape route west, and the wall closed it.

steps.prompt: What was strung up first, before any concrete?
options: Barbed wire
A concrete slab wall
Electric fencing
steps.explain: Barbed wire. Speed was the point. You cannot pour a wall overnight, but you can fence a city by sunrise.

steps.title: Built fast, felt permanent
recap: The border was sealed overnight on 13 August 1961.
Barbed wire came first; concrete came later.
Its real purpose was keeping East Germans in.

subject: HISTORY
title: Your Phone Beats the Moon Computer
blurb: The computer that landed on the Moon held less than one photo's worth of memory.

steps.title: Tiny computer, enormous job
steps.sub: The Apollo Guidance Computer flew humans to the Moon and back. By today's standards its memory is almost nothing. The interesting part is why that was enough.

steps.prompt: Roughly how much working memory (RAM) did the Apollo Guidance Computer have?
options: A few kilobytes
A few hundred megabytes
A few gigabytes
steps.explain: A few kilobytes. It was built in the 1960s from hand-wired magnetic cores, not silicon chips. Megabytes and gigabytes were decades away.

steps.title: Kilobytes, not gigabytes
steps.body: The Apollo Guidance Computer had about 4 kilobytes of erasable memory for working calculations and about 72 kilobytes of fixed memory for its programs. A single modern phone photo is several megabytes, thousands of times larger. The computer that steered a Moon landing would not hold one of your pictures.

steps.prompt: How big was the Apollo computer's working memory?
options: About 4 kilobytes
About 4 megabytes
About 4 gigabytes
steps.explain: About 4 kilobytes. The whole machine worked in kilobytes. Your phone carries millions of times more and mostly idles.

steps.title: The programs were woven by hand
steps.body: The fixed programs were stored in core rope memory. Wires threaded through tiny rings: through a ring meant a one, around it meant a zero. Workers wove the code by hand, so a mistake meant unpicking the wire. The software was literally sewn into the machine.

steps.prompt: How were the Apollo computer's programs stored?
options: On a hard drive
In core rope memory woven by hand
On punch cards fed in during flight
steps.explain: Core rope memory, woven by hand. The path of each wire encoded a one or a zero. The program was a physical object, not a file you could edit on the fly.

steps.prompt: In what unit was the Apollo computer's memory measured?
options: Kilobytes
Gigabytes
Terabytes
steps.explain: Kilobytes. That is the headline: a kilobyte-scale machine did a job we now associate with vast computing power. Clever engineering beat raw size.

steps.title: Small machine, giant leap
recap: The Apollo computer had about 4 KB of working memory.
Its programs lived in hand-woven core rope memory.
A single phone photo dwarfs its total storage.

subject: HISTORY
title: Banning Alcohol Made Crime Boom
blurb: America banned drinking and got gangsters instead.

steps.title: A ban that fed the thing it fought
steps.sub: Prohibition was meant to clean up America. It mostly handed a huge new market to criminals while people kept drinking.

steps.prompt: When a popular product is banned but still wanted, what usually happens?
options: Demand quietly disappears
A black market forms to supply it
Prices fall to almost nothing
steps.explain: A black market forms. Demand does not vanish on command. If people still want something, someone illegal will sell it, usually at a markup.

steps.title: The ban, and the loophole in human behavior
steps.body: Nationwide Prohibition ran from 1920 to 1933 under the 18th Amendment and the Volstead Act, which banned making, selling, and transporting alcohol. It did not ban wanting a drink. Speakeasies, bootleggers, and home stills filled the gap, and many Americans simply kept drinking.

steps.prompt: What did the Volstead Act and 18th Amendment actually ban?
options: Drinking alcohol in your own home
Making, selling, and transporting alcohol
Importing alcohol only
steps.explain: Making, selling, and transporting it. Notice what is missing: demand. Banning supply while leaving demand intact is what created the black market.

steps.title: The money went to organized crime
steps.body: A banned product that millions still want is a goldmine for whoever will sell it. Organized crime took over the trade. Al Capone became the era's most famous gangster, running bootlegging in Chicago. The ban did not dry out the country; it funded gangs.

steps.prompt: Who profited most from Prohibition?
options: Temperance campaigners
Organized crime, like Al Capone's operation
Government tax collectors
steps.explain: Organized crime. With legal sellers gone but buyers staying, the whole market flowed to whoever would break the law to supply it.

steps.prompt: Put Prohibition's arc in order.
items: 18th Amendment and Volstead Act take effect (1920)
Bootlegging and speakeasies spread
Organized crime grows rich
21st Amendment repeals Prohibition (1933)

glosses.21st Amendment repeals Prohibition (1933: The only amendment ever repealed by another.

steps.prompt: Which law put the alcohol ban into effect alongside the 18th Amendment?
options: The Volstead Act
The Sherman Act
The Capone Act
steps.explain: The Volstead Act. It spelled out how the 18th Amendment would be enforced. The amendment set the rule; the act ran it.

steps.title: Demand wins
recap: Prohibition ran 1920 to 1933 under the 18th Amendment.
It banned supply, not demand, so a black market grew.
Organized crime, like Al Capone's, got rich.

subject: HISTORY
title: Penicillin Was a Happy Accident
blurb: A messy lab and a stray mold spore launched modern medicine.

steps.title: The drug nobody set out to make
steps.sub: The first antibiotic was not the product of a careful plan. It grew in a dish someone forgot to clean up.

steps.prompt: How was penicillin first discovered?
options: By deliberately testing molds against bacteria
By accident, when mold contaminated a culture dish
By isolating it from a sick patient's blood
steps.explain: By accident. Mold drifted onto a forgotten dish of bacteria. The lucky part was noticing what the contamination had done, not planning it.

steps.title: A dish left out, a mold blown in
steps.body: In 1928, Alexander Fleming returned from holiday to a messy London lab. One dish of Staphylococcus bacteria had been left out and a mold had grown on it. Fleming noticed a clear ring around the mold where the bacteria had died. The mold was killing them.

steps.prompt: What did Fleming notice on the contaminated dish?
options: The mold made the bacteria grow faster
A clear ring where bacteria had died near the mold
Nothing unusual until years later
steps.explain: A clear ring where bacteria had died. That gap was the clue: the mold was releasing something that killed bacteria around it.

steps.title: He named it, but could not make it useful
steps.body: The mold was a Penicillium, so Fleming named the substance penicillin. But he could not purify enough to turn it into a usable drug. That took other scientists, Howard Florey and Ernst Chain, years later, to make penicillin into a medicine that saved lives.

steps.prompt: Did Fleming turn penicillin into a usable drug himself?
options: Yes, he had it in pharmacies within a year
No, others purified it into a medicine years later
No, it was never made into a real drug
steps.explain: No. Fleming found and named it, but Florey and Chain did the hard work of making it into a usable medicine years afterward.

steps.prompt: Order the path from accident to medicine.
items: Fleming leaves a bacteria dish out (1928)
Mold contaminates the dish and kills nearby bacteria
Fleming names the substance penicillin
Florey and Chain turn it into a usable drug

glosses.Fleming names the substance penicillin: Discovery, but not yet a drug.
glosses.Florey and Chain turn it into a usable d: Years of work make it medicine.

steps.prompt: What gave penicillin its name?
options: The Penicillium mold it came from
The patient it first cured
The Latin word for cure
steps.explain: The Penicillium mold. Fleming named the substance after the mold that produced it. The accident named the cure.

steps.title: Luck, plus a sharp eye
recap: In 1928 Fleming found mold killing bacteria on a forgotten dish.
He named the substance penicillin, after the Penicillium mold.
Florey and Chain later made it into a usable medicine.

subject: HISTORY
title: The Crash Didn't Cause the Depression
blurb: The 1929 crash was a trigger, not the cause. Bank failures and bad policy turned a slump into a decade-long collapse.

steps.title: One bad month did not do this
steps.sub: Stocks crashed in October 1929. But a crash is a fast drop in prices, and prices drop often without a decade of ruin following. The Depression came from what happened next.

steps.prompt: Why would a stock crash turn into ten years of mass unemployment?
options: Everyone owned stocks, so everyone went broke at once
The crash shook the banking system, and bank failures wrecked the wider economy
Crashes always cause depressions automatically
steps.explain: Most people did not own stock in 1929, so the crash alone could not bankrupt the country. The damage spread through banks. When banks collapsed, ordinary savings and credit vanished too.

steps.title: A trigger, not the engine
steps.body: The crash was a trigger. It frightened people and wiped out paper wealth, but only a minority owned stock. A trigger starts something; it is not the force that keeps it going. The real engine of the Depression was the banking system breaking down in the years after. That is where everyday savings and loans lived.

steps.prompt: Why was the crash only a trigger?
options: It happened too quickly to matter
Only a minority owned stock, so the crash alone could not ruin the whole economy
Stock prices recovered the next week
steps.explain: A trigger starts a chain but is not the main force. Since most people held no stock, the crash by itself reached few wallets. The collapse needed another mechanism to spread.

steps.title: Banks were the engine
steps.body: In the early 1930s thousands of banks failed. There was no federal deposit insurance yet, so when a bank died, people's savings died with it. Frightened depositors rushed to pull their money out, which pushed more banks under. As banks vanished, the money supply shrank and lending froze. Bad policy made it worse: the Federal Reserve let money tighten instead of loosening it. Deposit insurance, the FDIC, only arrived in 1933.

steps.prompt: Why did bank failures hit ordinary savings so hard in the early 1930s?
options: Banks kept all money in stocks
There was no federal deposit insurance yet, so a failed bank took savings with it
The government seized the deposits
steps.explain: Without insurance, a failed bank meant lost savings, plain and simple. That is why a run on one bank could spread fear and pull down others. The FDIC, created in 1933, was the fix.

steps.prompt: Order how a slump became a decade-long collapse:
items: Stock market crashes in October 1929
Fear spreads and depositors pull cash from banks
Banks fail and savings vanish, with no insurance
Money supply shrinks and lending freezes
Spending and jobs collapse across the economy

steps.prompt: Earlier: why call the 1929 crash a trigger rather than the cause?
options: It was not actually a real crash
Only a minority owned stock, so it could not ruin the whole economy by itself
It happened on a weekend
steps.explain: A trigger starts a chain reaction; the banking collapse was the chain. Because few people owned stock, the crash needed the banks to fail before it could become a national disaster.

steps.title: Look past the crash
recap: The 1929 crash triggered the Depression but did not cause it.
Bank failures with no deposit insurance destroyed savings and credit.
A shrinking money supply and bad policy turned a slump into a decade.

subject: HISTORY
title: The World Nearly Ended in 13 Days
blurb: In 1962 the US and USSR stood at the edge of nuclear war. One Soviet officer's refusal may have stopped it.

steps.title: Thirteen days on the edge
steps.sub: In October 1962 the United States and the Soviet Union came as close to nuclear war as the world has ever been. It hinged not just on presidents, but on one officer in a submarine.

steps.prompt: What set off the crisis?
options: A Soviet attack on a US city
The US finding Soviet nuclear missiles being installed in Cuba
Cuba declaring war on the US
steps.explain: The trigger was discovery, not attack. US spy planes photographed Soviet missile sites under construction in Cuba, about 90 miles from Florida. Nuclear weapons that close were the whole problem.

steps.title: Missiles 90 miles away
steps.body: Spy planes caught Soviet missile sites being built in Cuba, roughly 90 miles from Florida. President Kennedy ordered a naval blockade, which the US called a quarantine, to stop more Soviet ships from reaching the island. For about thirteen days neither side backed down, and a single misstep could have started a nuclear exchange. This was the standoff most people picture.

steps.prompt: How did Kennedy respond to the missiles in Cuba?
options: He launched an immediate invasion
He ordered a naval blockade, called a quarantine, around Cuba
He ignored it to avoid escalation
steps.explain: He chose a blockade over an attack. A quarantine pressured the Soviets and bought time without firing first. An invasion or air strike risked triggering the war everyone feared.

steps.title: One officer said no
steps.body: On October 27, a Soviet submarine near the blockade was being hammered by US depth charges. Cut off from radio and fearing war had begun, its captain wanted to fire a nuclear torpedo. But launching it needed agreement from more than one officer aboard. Vasili Arkhipov refused. His no stopped the launch. The crisis ended days later when the Soviets agreed to pull the missiles, and the US secretly agreed to remove its own missiles from Turkey.

steps.prompt: Why did Arkhipov's refusal matter so much?
options: He was the only person who knew about the missiles
Launching the nuclear torpedo needed more than one officer to agree, and he said no
He personally controlled the entire Soviet navy
steps.explain: The launch required agreement from more than one officer aboard. Because Arkhipov refused, the torpedo could not be fired. A single dissent broke the chain to a nuclear strike.

steps.prompt: Order the crisis from start to finish:
items: US spy planes photograph Soviet missile sites in Cuba
Kennedy orders a naval blockade of Cuba
A Soviet submarine is pressured with depth charges on October 27
Arkhipov refuses to approve the nuclear torpedo
The Soviets agree to remove the missiles and the crisis ends

steps.prompt: Earlier: how did Kennedy respond to the Soviet missiles?
options: An immediate nuclear strike on Cuba
A naval blockade, called a quarantine, around the island
A formal apology to the Soviets
steps.explain: He picked a blockade over an attack. The quarantine applied pressure and bought time without firing the first shot, which kept a path open to a peaceful end.

steps.title: Closer than you think
recap: The 1962 crisis began when the US found Soviet missiles in Cuba.
Kennedy chose a naval blockade instead of an attack.
Vasili Arkhipov's refusal helped stop a nuclear launch at sea.

subject: HISTORY
title: A Walk to Make Salt Shook an Empire
blurb: Gandhi's 240-mile march to gather free seawater salt was a planned, theatrical strike at British rule.

steps.title: Why walk 240 miles for salt?
steps.sub: In 1930 Gandhi walked for weeks to the sea to pick up a pinch of salt. It sounds small. It was one of the sharpest blows ever struck against the British Empire.

steps.prompt: Why choose salt, of all things, to protest British rule?
options: Salt was rare and expensive to find
Everyone needs salt, yet the British taxed it and banned Indians from making their own
Salt had no real meaning, it was random
steps.explain: Salt was perfect because everyone needs it. The British held a monopoly, taxed it, and made it illegal for Indians to produce their own. That turned a basic need into a symbol of unjust rule.

steps.title: The injustice everyone could feel
steps.body: British law gave the colonial government a monopoly on salt. It taxed salt and made it illegal for Indians to gather or sell their own, even though they could scoop it free from the sea. Salt touches everyone, rich and poor alike. Gandhi picked it because the unfairness needed no explaining. Anyone could understand a law that taxed the sea itself.

steps.prompt: What made the salt law such a powerful target?
options: Salt was a luxury only the rich used
Everyone needs salt, yet it was taxed and Indians were banned from making their own
The British did not actually enforce it
steps.explain: Because salt is universal, the tax and ban touched every Indian. An injustice everyone feels is an injustice everyone can rally against, which is exactly what Gandhi wanted.

steps.title: Planned political theater
steps.body: The march was no spontaneous outburst. Gandhi planned it carefully. He set out from his Sabarmati ashram and walked about 240 miles over roughly 24 days to the coastal village of Dandi, gathering crowds and press the whole way. At Dandi in April 1930 he bent down and picked up natural salt from the shore, openly breaking the law. That single image triggered mass civil disobedience across India.

steps.prompt: How was the march itself designed to work?
options: As a secret operation no one would notice
As planned, public theater that drew crowds and press over a long, visible walk
As a quick raid on a salt factory
steps.explain: The long, visible walk was the point. Each day on the road built attention and crowds, so the lawbreaking at Dandi landed as a national event rather than a quiet gesture.

steps.prompt: Order the Salt March:
items: British law makes Indian salt-making illegal and taxes salt
Gandhi sets out from Sabarmati toward the coast
Crowds and press grow over about 24 days of walking
Gandhi reaches Dandi and picks up sea salt, breaking the law
Mass civil disobedience spreads across India

steps.prompt: Earlier: why was salt the ideal target for protest?
options: It was rare and few people used it
Everyone needs it, yet the British taxed it and banned Indians from making their own
It was the only thing the British exported
steps.explain: Salt is universal, so the tax and ban hit every Indian. A grievance everyone shares makes the clearest rallying point, which is why Gandhi chose it.

steps.title: Small act, huge blow
recap: The British monopolized and taxed salt and banned Indians from making it.
Gandhi's planned 240-mile march turned that into national theater.
Picking up salt at Dandi sparked mass civil disobedience.

subject: HISTORY
title: Women's Vote Came Country by Country
blurb: There was no single day women got the vote. New Zealand led in 1893; Switzerland waited until 1971.

steps.title: Not one moment, but many
steps.sub: People often picture a single day when 'women got the vote.' There wasn't one. It happened country by country, spread across nearly a century.

steps.prompt: Which was the first self-governing country to give women the national vote?
options: The United States
The United Kingdom
New Zealand
steps.explain: New Zealand led in 1893, well before larger powers. Being first had nothing to do with size. A small country can move sooner than empires weighed down by their own politics.

steps.title: A slow, uneven spread
steps.body: There was no global moment. New Zealand granted women the national vote in 1893. The United Kingdom gave some women the vote in 1918 and equal terms in 1928. The United States ratified the 19th Amendment in 1920. Each country moved on its own timeline, and many extended the vote in steps, to some women first and all women later.

steps.prompt: How did women's suffrage actually arrive worldwide?
options: In a single year by international treaty
Country by country, often in gradual steps, over many decades
Everywhere at once after World War I
steps.explain: It spread unevenly, nation by nation, sometimes in stages. There was no treaty or shared date, which is why the dates range across nearly a century.

steps.title: Wealthy did not mean early
steps.body: Being rich or democratic did not guarantee acting early. Switzerland, a stable and wealthy democracy, only gave women the federal vote in 1971. One Swiss canton, Appenzell Innerrhoden, held out on local votes until a court forced it in 1990. Saudi Arabia first let women vote in municipal elections in 2015. The order surprises people because progress was not a tidy ranking of how modern a country looked.

steps.prompt: When did Switzerland grant women the federal vote?
steps.explain: Switzerland waited until 1971 at the federal level, far later than many assume. Wealth and stability did not mean moving early, which is the whole surprise of the timeline.

steps.prompt: Order these by year women won the national vote:
items: New Zealand (1893)
United States (1920)
Switzerland (1971)
Saudi Arabia, municipal elections (2015)

steps.prompt: Earlier: which country was first to grant women the national vote?
options: Switzerland
New Zealand
The United States
steps.explain: New Zealand led in 1893. It shows that being first was not about size or wealth, since a small country acted decades ahead of larger powers.

steps.title: A century, not a day
recap: There was no single moment women got the vote.
New Zealand led in 1893; the US ratified in 1920.
Switzerland waited until 1971, proving wealth did not mean early.

subject: HISTORY
title: Chernobyl Wasn't a Nuclear Bomb
blurb: The reactor blew from steam and a flawed design, not a nuclear blast. Most harm came from the invisible aftermath.

steps.title: It was not a mushroom cloud
steps.sub: People imagine Chernobyl as a nuclear bomb going off. It wasn't. A power reactor cannot detonate that way. What actually happened was different, and in some ways worse.

steps.prompt: Could the Chernobyl reactor have exploded like a nuclear bomb?
options: Yes, all nuclear material works the same way
No, reactor fuel is not built to detonate like a bomb
Yes, but only because it was Soviet-made
steps.explain: No. A bomb needs highly enriched fuel arranged for an instant chain reaction. Reactor fuel is not enriched or arranged that way, so it physically cannot produce a nuclear detonation.

steps.title: Steam, not a bomb
steps.body: On April 26, 1986, reactor 4 at Chernobyl ran a botched safety test at low power. The reactor had a design flaw that let its power surge dangerously under those conditions. Power spiked, water flashed to steam, and the buildup blew the reactor apart, likely helped by a hydrogen reaction. This was a steam and pressure explosion, not a nuclear blast. A reactor cannot go off like a bomb.

steps.prompt: What kind of explosion destroyed reactor 4?
options: A nuclear detonation like a bomb
A steam and pressure explosion after a power surge
A chemical bomb planted at the plant
steps.explain: It was a steam and pressure explosion from a runaway power surge. The fuel could not detonate like a bomb, so the destructive force came from steam and the blown-apart reactor, not a nuclear blast.

steps.title: The invisible danger
steps.body: The blast killed only a small number of people directly, from the explosion and acute radiation sickness in the following weeks. The bigger danger was invisible. The explosion and fire flung radioactive material into the air, and it spread across a wide area and parts of Europe. Much of the lasting harm came from this contamination and exposure over time, not from the explosion itself.

steps.prompt: Where did much of the wider harm from Chernobyl come from?
options: The force of the explosion flattening cities
Radioactive material spreading and exposing people over time
A second reactor exploding days later
steps.explain: The explosion killed relatively few directly. The wider harm came from radioactive material released into the air and spread over a large area, an invisible danger that lingered.

steps.prompt: Order the Chernobyl disaster:
items: A safety test is run improperly at low power
The flawed reactor's power surges out of control
Steam pressure blows reactor 4 apart
Radioactive material is released into the air
Contamination spreads across a wide area over time

glosses.Steam pressure blows reactor 4 apart: The explosion, not a nuclear blast

steps.prompt: Earlier: why couldn't the reactor explode like a nuclear bomb?
options: It was too small
Reactor fuel is not enriched or arranged to detonate like a bomb
The Soviets removed the nuclear material first
steps.explain: A bomb needs highly enriched fuel set up for an instant chain reaction. Reactor fuel is neither, so the worst it could do was a steam and pressure explosion, not a nuclear detonation.

steps.title: Worse in a quieter way
recap: Chernobyl was a steam explosion from a flawed reactor, not a nuclear bomb.
A botched safety test triggered an uncontrolled power surge.
Most lasting harm came from radioactive fallout, not the blast.

subject: HISTORY
title: India Was Split in Five Weeks
blurb: A British lawyer who had never set foot in India drew the border in about five weeks. Millions were displaced almost overnight.

steps.title: A border drawn by a stranger
steps.sub: When British India split in 1947, the line between India and Pakistan was drawn by a lawyer who had never visited the country. He had about five weeks. The result uprooted millions.

steps.prompt: How long did the man in charge have to draw the border between India and Pakistan?
options: Several years of careful surveying
About five weeks
A full decade with local councils
steps.explain: About five weeks. The British were rushing to leave, so the boundary was settled at speed rather than through long, careful work on the ground.

steps.title: The man with the pen
steps.body: The border was drawn under Cyril Radcliffe, a British lawyer. He had never been to India before. He was handed maps, census numbers, and a deadline of roughly five weeks. He chaired the commissions that split the two big provinces, Punjab and Bengal, where Hindus, Muslims, and Sikhs lived mixed together. There was no clean line to find.

steps.prompt: What stands out about Cyril Radcliffe's qualifications for the job?
options: He had lived in India for decades
He was a lawyer who had never been to India
He was a cartographer who knew the land
steps.explain: He was a lawyer who had never been to India. The choice was partly meant to signal neutrality, but it also meant the man drawing the line had no first-hand knowledge of the places he was dividing.

steps.title: Millions on the move
steps.body: When the line was published in August 1947, people woke up on the wrong side of a new border. The partition set off one of the largest mass migrations in history, with millions crossing in both directions. Punjab and Bengal, the very provinces Radcliffe split, saw the worst of it. Violence followed, and it is estimated to have killed hundreds of thousands.

steps.prompt: What was the human scale of the partition?
options: A quiet handover with few people affected
One of the largest mass migrations in history
A slow shift over many decades
steps.explain: One of the largest mass migrations in history. Millions crossed the new borders within months, and the speed of the split left little time to plan or protect anyone.

steps.prompt: Put the partition in order.
items: Britain decides to leave India quickly
Radcliffe is given about five weeks to draw the line
The boundary is published in August 1947
Millions migrate and violence breaks out

glosses.Radcliffe is given about five weeks to d: A lawyer with no India experience
glosses.The boundary is published in August 1947: People learn which side they are on

steps.prompt: Which two provinces did the border cut through?
options: Punjab and Bengal
Kashmir and Goa
Gujarat and Kerala
steps.explain: Punjab and Bengal. Both held mixed populations, so any line through them left huge numbers of people stranded on the wrong side.

steps.title: That's the partition of India.
recap: A British lawyer drew the border in about five weeks.
He had never been to India.
The split triggered one of history's largest migrations.

subject: HISTORY
title: TV Picked a President in 1960
blurb: Radio listeners thought Nixon won the debate. TV viewers saw a tired, sweating man lose to a tanned Kennedy. Looks won.

steps.title: Same debate, two verdicts
steps.sub: In 1960, Kennedy and Nixon held the first televised presidential debate. People who listened on radio and people who watched on TV came away with different winners. The difference was how the men looked.

steps.prompt: Radio listeners and TV viewers disagreed on who won. What most likely caused the split?
options: They heard different answers
Appearance swayed the TV audience
The radio feed was edited
steps.explain: Appearance swayed the TV audience. The words were the same on both. Only the viewers saw the candidates, and what they saw changed their judgment.

steps.title: Two audiences, two winners
steps.body: The debate aired in September 1960, the first of its kind on television. It is widely reported that radio listeners tended to give the edge to Nixon, while TV viewers favored Kennedy. Same questions, same answers. The only difference was that one audience could see the candidates and the other could not.

steps.prompt: Which audience tended to favor Nixon?
options: Television viewers
Radio listeners
Newspaper readers
steps.explain: Radio listeners. With no picture, they judged on voice and argument alone, and on that basis Nixon held up better than he did on screen.

steps.title: The camera was not kind
steps.body: Nixon was recovering from illness. On camera he looked pale and tired, and he sweated under the hot studio lights. Kennedy looked tanned, rested, and calm. To the TV audience, one man looked like a leader and the other looked unwell. Kennedy won the election and became president in 1961.

steps.prompt: Why did Nixon come across poorly on television?
options: He refused to answer questions
He was ill, pale, and sweating on camera
His microphone failed
steps.explain: He was ill, pale, and sweating. Television showed all of it, while Kennedy looked tanned and composed beside him. The visual contrast did the damage.

steps.prompt: Order the night and what followed.
items: The first televised debate airs in 1960
Radio listeners lean toward Nixon
TV viewers lean toward Kennedy
Kennedy wins and takes office in 1961

steps.prompt: Which audience favored Kennedy, and why?
options: Radio listeners, because of his voice
TV viewers, because of how he looked
Both equally, because of his answers
steps.explain: TV viewers, because of how he looked. They saw a rested, tanned Kennedy next to a sick-looking Nixon, and appearance tipped their judgment.

steps.title: That's the 1960 debate.
recap: It was the first televised presidential debate.
Radio favored Nixon; TV favored Kennedy.
How the candidates looked changed the verdict.

subject: HISTORY
title: Rosa Parks Wasn't Just Tired
blurb: The story says her feet hurt. In fact she was a trained activist and longtime NAACP secretary who knew exactly what she was doing.

steps.title: Not an accident
steps.sub: The popular story is that Rosa Parks was just a tired woman who would not stand up. The real Rosa Parks was a seasoned activist who understood exactly what her stand would set off.

steps.prompt: Before this lesson, what does the common story say about why Parks stayed seated?
options: She was a trained organizer making a stand
She was simply too tired to move
She did not understand the rules
steps.explain: The common story says she was simply too tired. It is a tidy tale, but it hides who Parks actually was and the work behind the moment.

steps.title: An organizer, not a bystander
steps.body: Rosa Parks was the secretary of the Montgomery NAACP chapter. She had years of activism behind her and had attended civil rights training, including a session at the Highlander Folk School. She later said she was not physically tired. She was tired of giving in. Her refusal in December 1955 was a deliberate act, not a fluke of fatigue.

steps.prompt: What role did Parks hold in the movement?
options: She had no organizing experience
She was secretary of the Montgomery NAACP
She was a national NAACP president
steps.explain: She was secretary of the Montgomery NAACP. She was an experienced organizer who knew the local movement, which is why her stand could be turned into something larger.

steps.title: One seat, a year of pressure
steps.body: Her arrest helped spark the Montgomery Bus Boycott. Black residents stopped riding the buses and kept it up for more than a year, over 380 days. A young pastor named Martin Luther King Jr. emerged as a leader during the campaign. The boycott ended after the Supreme Court acted against bus segregation in Montgomery.

steps.prompt: Roughly how long did the Montgomery Bus Boycott last?
options: A few days
More than a year
About a decade
steps.explain: More than a year, over 380 days. That sustained pressure, not a single moment, is what forced change and made the protest impossible to ignore.

steps.prompt: Order the events.
items: Parks trains and organizes with the NAACP
She refuses to give up her seat in 1955
The bus boycott begins and runs over a year
The Supreme Court acts against bus segregation

steps.prompt: How did Parks herself describe being tired?
options: Tired from a long day of work
Tired of giving in
Too tired to think clearly
steps.explain: Tired of giving in. Her own words push back on the exhaustion story and point to a deliberate stand by someone who had trained for this.

steps.title: That's the real Rosa Parks.
recap: She was a trained NAACP organizer, not just a tired rider.
Her 1955 stand was deliberate.
It sparked a boycott that lasted over a year.

subject: HISTORY
title: One Scientist May Have Saved a Billion
blurb: Norman Borlaug bred high-yield wheat that quietly headed off mass famine. He is often called the man who saved a billion lives, and almost nobody can name him.

steps.title: The famous man you have never heard of
steps.sub: Norman Borlaug bred better wheat. That work is credited with feeding hundreds of millions and possibly preventing famine on a massive scale. He won a Nobel Prize, and most people cannot name him.

steps.prompt: How did one agronomist help prevent mass famine?
options: By inventing a new fertilizer alone
By breeding wheat that produced far more grain
By banning the export of food
steps.explain: By breeding wheat that produced far more grain. More food per acre meant the same land could feed many more people, which is what held off famine.

steps.title: Better wheat, more food
steps.body: Borlaug was an American agronomist who developed high-yield, disease-resistant wheat. His varieties were adopted widely, including in Mexico, India, and Pakistan, where they sharply raised crop yields. This was the heart of what is called the Green Revolution. He is often credited with helping prevent mass famine, and is sometimes said to have saved around a billion lives. That number is an estimate.

steps.prompt: What did Borlaug actually develop?
options: A vaccine for crop pests
High-yield, disease-resistant wheat
A new irrigation pump
steps.explain: High-yield, disease-resistant wheat. The plants themselves produced more grain and resisted disease, so farmers harvested far more from the same fields.

steps.title: Seeds plus a system
steps.body: The seed alone was not the whole story. The Green Revolution paired improved varieties with fertilizer, irrigation, and modern methods. Borlaug won the Nobel Peace Prize in 1970 for this work. The revolution also drew criticism, since the high yields leaned heavily on fertilizer and water and raised lasting environmental concerns.

steps.prompt: What did the high yields of the Green Revolution depend on?
options: Seeds and nothing else
Improved seeds plus fertilizer, irrigation, and modern methods
Cooler weather across the region
steps.explain: Improved seeds plus fertilizer, irrigation, and modern methods. The seed needed the whole system around it, which is also why critics point to its reliance on inputs.

steps.prompt: Order Borlaug's impact.
items: Borlaug breeds high-yield, disease-resistant wheat
The wheat spreads to Mexico, India, and Pakistan
Yields rise and famine is held off
He wins the Nobel Peace Prize in 1970

glosses.Yields rise and famine is held off: More food from the same land

steps.prompt: What prize did Borlaug receive for his work?
options: The Nobel Prize in Chemistry
The Nobel Peace Prize
No major prize at all
steps.explain: The Nobel Peace Prize, in 1970. The committee tied feeding the hungry to peace, recognizing that preventing famine is itself a force for stability.

steps.title: That's Norman Borlaug.
recap: He bred high-yield, disease-resistant wheat.
It is credited with helping prevent mass famine.
He won the 1970 Nobel Peace Prize.

subject: HISTORY
title: The Cold War Was Fought by Proxy
blurb: The US and the Soviet Union never fought each other directly. They waged it through other countries' wars, spies, sports, and the space race.

steps.title: A war without a battlefield
steps.sub: For decades the US and the Soviet Union were locked in conflict. They never once fought each other directly. Instead they fought through proxy wars, spies, sports, and a race to space.

steps.prompt: Why might two superpowers avoid fighting each other directly?
options: Neither had a real military
Both had nuclear weapons and feared destruction
They were close allies
steps.explain: Both had nuclear weapons and feared destruction. A direct war risked wiping out both sides, so the rivalry was pushed into safer, indirect channels.

steps.title: Fighting everywhere but head-on
steps.body: The Cold War was the rivalry between the United States and the Soviet Union after World War II. The two never fought each other in a direct, declared war. Both held nuclear weapons, and the threat of mutual destruction made direct conflict too dangerous. So they competed indirectly, backing opposite sides in other countries' wars.

steps.prompt: Why did the two superpowers avoid direct war?
options: They had signed a peace treaty
Nuclear weapons made direct war too dangerous
Neither could afford an army
steps.explain: Nuclear weapons made direct war too dangerous. A head-on fight risked destroying both, so they channeled the rivalry into proxy conflicts instead.

steps.title: Proxies, spies, and space
steps.body: Instead of fighting directly, each side backed opposing forces in proxy wars, including the Korean War and the Vietnam War. They also raced in weapons, espionage, and even space. The Soviets launched Sputnik, the first satellite, in 1957. The US landed astronauts on the Moon in 1969. The whole rivalry is generally considered to have ended around 1991, when the Soviet Union dissolved.

steps.prompt: Which of these were proxy wars of the Cold War?
options: The Korean War and the Vietnam War
World War I and World War II
The American Civil War
steps.explain: The Korean War and the Vietnam War. The superpowers backed opposing sides in each, fighting their rivalry through other nations rather than against each other.

steps.prompt: Order these Cold War milestones.
items: The Soviets launch Sputnik in 1957
The US lands astronauts on the Moon in 1969
The Soviet Union dissolves around 1991

steps.prompt: Did the US and the Soviet Union ever fight each other in a direct, declared war?
options: Yes, several times
No, never directly
Yes, once at the very end
steps.explain: No, never directly. Nuclear weapons made that too dangerous, so the conflict played out through proxy wars, espionage, and competition like the space race.

steps.title: That's the Cold War.
recap: The superpowers never fought each other directly.
Nuclear fear pushed the rivalry into proxy wars.
It ran from after WWII to about 1991.

subject: HISTORY
title: The Dust Bowl Was Human-Made
blurb: The 1930s black blizzards were not just bad luck. Plowing up the prairie's deep roots left the soil free to blow away.

steps.title: When the sky turned black
steps.sub: In the 1930s, walls of dust rolled across the American plains. The drought was real, but the dust was something people helped make.

steps.prompt: Drought hit the Plains before. Why did the 1930s produce such monster dust storms?
options: The land had been stripped of its deep-rooted grass and plowed for crops
A volcano nearby filled the air with ash
The soil there had always been loose sand
steps.explain: The native prairie grass had deep roots that held the soil down. Once that grass was plowed up for farming, a drought could lift the bare topsoil straight into the sky.

steps.title: The grass was the anchor
steps.body: The Southern Plains were once covered in native grasses with deep, tangled roots. Those roots gripped the soil and held moisture, even through dry spells. In the decades before the 1930s, farmers plowed up huge stretches of this grassland to plant wheat. The anchor was gone. The soil was now held in place only by the crops on top of it.

steps.prompt: What did the native prairie grass do that crops did not?
options: It grew taller than wheat
Its deep roots held the soil in place
It needed no rain at all
steps.explain: The deep root systems anchored the soil. Wheat roots were shallow by comparison, so once the crops failed, nothing held the dirt down.

steps.title: Then the rain stopped
steps.body: In the 1930s a severe drought settled over the region. Crops withered and died, leaving bare, dry topsoil with nothing to hold it. Strong plains winds picked it up and carried it for miles. The huge dark clouds of dust were called black blizzards. On April 14, 1935, one storm was so vast the day was named Black Sunday.

steps.prompt: Why did the drought turn into airborne dust instead of just dead crops?
options: The bare topsoil had nothing holding it, so wind carried it away
The rain washed the soil into rivers
Farmers burned the fields on purpose
steps.explain: With the grass gone and the crops dead, the dry topsoil was exposed and loose. Plains winds lifted it into the black blizzards.

steps.prompt: Put the road to the Dust Bowl in order.
items: Deep-rooted prairie grass holds the soil
Farmers plow the grassland to plant wheat
A long drought kills the crops
Bare topsoil blows away as dust storms

steps.prompt: What made the Dust Bowl partly human-made rather than pure bad luck?
options: People plowed up the deep-rooted grass that held the soil
People built too many cities on the Plains
People drained all the rivers
steps.explain: Plowing up the deep-rooted prairie removed the soil's anchor. When drought came, the exposed dirt simply blew away.

steps.title: That's the Dust Bowl.
recap: Deep-rooted prairie grass once held the soil.
Plowing for wheat removed that anchor.
Drought plus bare soil made the black blizzards.

subject: HISTORY
title: Summer Once Meant Polio Panic
blurb: Before the vaccine, polio outbreaks peaked in summer. Parents closed pools and feared every fever.

steps.title: The dreaded season
steps.sub: For decades, summer brought a quiet panic. Polio struck mostly in warm months, and no one knew who would be next.

steps.prompt: Why did parents in the 1940s especially fear summer?
options: Polio outbreaks peaked in the summer months
Schools were closed and children got bored
Heat itself caused the disease
steps.explain: Polio cases surged in summer. Parents learned to dread the season and the public places where they feared the virus spread.

steps.title: A virus that crippled children
steps.body: Polio is caused by a virus. Most people who caught it had mild symptoms or none at all. But in a fraction of cases the virus attacked the nerves and caused paralysis, sometimes for life. It hit children hard enough that it was long called infantile paralysis. The worst cases could not breathe on their own and were placed in a machine called the iron lung.

steps.prompt: What was the iron lung used for?
options: To help severely affected patients breathe
To sterilize swimming pools
To deliver the vaccine
steps.explain: The iron lung mechanically assisted breathing for patients whose paralysis affected the muscles they needed to breathe.

steps.title: Closed pools and watched fevers
steps.body: Because no one knew exactly how to stop it, communities reacted to summer outbreaks with fear. Swimming pools, movie theaters, and other crowded places were sometimes shut down. Parents watched their children for any fever. The dread lasted until 1955, when Jonas Salk's injected vaccine was announced as effective. Albert Sabin's oral vaccine followed, and polio cases collapsed.

steps.prompt: What finally ended the yearly polio panic?
options: Effective vaccines, starting with Salk's in 1955
Keeping pools closed permanently
A new kind of iron lung
steps.explain: Vaccination did it. Salk's vaccine was announced as effective in 1955, Sabin's oral vaccine followed, and cases dropped sharply.

steps.prompt: Order the polio story.
items: Summer outbreaks paralyze thousands of children
Communities close pools and watch for fevers
Salk's vaccine is announced effective in 1955
Cases collapse and polio is eliminated in the US

steps.prompt: Why was polio historically called infantile paralysis?
options: It hit children hard, sometimes paralyzing them
Only newborns could catch it
It came from baby formula
steps.explain: It struck children especially hard, and in some cases paralyzed them, which is where the old name came from.

steps.title: That's polio summer.
recap: Polio outbreaks peaked in summer.
Fear closed pools and watched every fever.
Salk's 1955 vaccine ended the panic.

subject: HISTORY
title: Dozens of Nations Were Born in a Decade
blurb: More than 30 African countries gained independence in the 1960s, redrawing the world map at remarkable speed.

steps.title: A map redrawn fast
steps.sub: In a single decade, the world map filled with new nations. Africa alone added more than thirty independent countries in the 1960s.

steps.prompt: Roughly how many African countries gained independence in the 1960s?
options: About 3
About 10
More than 30
steps.explain: More than thirty. The pace was so fast that 1960 alone, with around seventeen new nations, is called the Year of Africa.

steps.title: The Year of Africa
steps.body: After World War II, the European powers that ran most of Africa were weakened and stretched thin. Colony after colony pushed for and won independence. This process is called decolonization. It crested in 1960, when around seventeen African countries became independent in a single year. By the end of the decade, more than thirty new nations had appeared.

steps.prompt: Why is 1960 called the Year of Africa?
options: Around seventeen African countries became independent that year
It was the first year anyone visited Africa
All of Africa united into one country
steps.explain: About seventeen African nations gained independence in 1960 alone, a burst large enough to earn the year its name.

steps.title: But the lines stayed
steps.body: The new nations were free, but many kept the borders the colonial powers had drawn. Those lines were often set for European convenience, not for the people on the ground. They cut through ethnic and language groups, putting rivals inside one country and splitting communities across several. Independence came fast. The inherited map did not change with it, and some of those borders strained the new states for decades.

steps.prompt: What problem did many new nations inherit along with independence?
options: Borders drawn by colonial powers that ignored ethnic groups
A complete lack of any cities
A shared single language for the whole continent
steps.explain: They kept colonial borders that often cut across ethnic and language lines, which created lasting tension within the new states.

steps.prompt: Roughly how many African nations became independent across the 1960s?
options: More than 30
About 5
Exactly 100
steps.explain: More than thirty across the decade, with the single year 1960 supplying around seventeen of them.

steps.title: That's the independence wave.
recap: More than 30 African nations went independent in the 1960s.
1960 alone added around 17, the Year of Africa.
Many kept colonial borders that ignored ethnic lines.

subject: HISTORY
title: Ford Didn't Invent the Assembly Line
blurb: He perfected and popularized the moving line, borrowing the idea from meatpacking plants that ran it in reverse.

steps.title: Credit where it's due
steps.sub: Henry Ford is famous for the assembly line. But he did not invent it. He borrowed the idea and made it work at a scale no one had seen.

steps.prompt: Where did the moving-line idea partly come from?
options: Meatpacking plants that moved carcasses past workers
Ancient Roman shipyards
Ford dreamed it up from scratch
steps.explain: Meatpacking plants moved carcasses along a line while workers each removed a part. Ford saw the idea and ran it in the opposite direction: building up instead of cutting apart.

steps.title: The disassembly line
steps.body: In meatpacking plants, carcasses hung from a moving line that carried them past worker after worker. Each person made one cut or removed one part. It was, in effect, a disassembly line. Ford and his engineers took that flow and reversed it. Instead of taking an animal apart, they would carry a car along a line and have each worker add one piece.

steps.prompt: How was the meatpacking line related to Ford's assembly line?
options: It ran in reverse: workers removed parts instead of adding them
It built cars before Ford did
It had nothing to do with it
steps.explain: Meatpacking ran a disassembly line, taking carcasses apart. Ford reversed the flow to assemble a product instead.

steps.title: What Ford actually did
steps.body: Ford's real achievement was scale and refinement. Building the Model T, his moving line cut the time to assemble a car dramatically. This only worked because of interchangeable parts, perfected before Ford, so any piece fit any car. In 1914 he also introduced a five-dollar daily wage. Faster, cheaper production helped push the price of the Model T down over the years.

steps.prompt: What made the moving assembly line possible in the first place?
options: Interchangeable parts, so any piece fit any car
Each car being built by a single craftsman
Hand-fitting every part to one specific car
steps.explain: Interchangeable parts were the prerequisite. If parts had to be hand-fitted to each car, a fast moving line could not work.

steps.prompt: Order the path to Ford's line.
items: Interchangeable parts make pieces fit any car
Meatpacking plants run moving disassembly lines
Ford reverses the flow to assemble cars
Model T assembly time drops dramatically

steps.prompt: What did Ford really contribute to the assembly line?
options: He refined it and ran it at huge scale, building on borrowed ideas
He invented it entirely on his own
He was the first to use any machines at all
steps.explain: He did not invent the line. He adapted the meatpacking idea, relied on interchangeable parts, and scaled it up to build the Model T cheaply.

steps.title: That's the assembly line.
recap: Ford perfected the line, he did not invent it.
The idea came partly from meatpacking, run in reverse.
Interchangeable parts made the moving line work.

subject: HISTORY
title: The New Deal Was an Alphabet Soup
blurb: FDR's response to the Depression spawned dozens of agencies known by their initials, and a few like the FDIC still shape your bank today.

steps.title: So many initials
steps.sub: To fight the Great Depression, the government created agency after agency. People joked it was an alphabet soup of three-letter names.

steps.prompt: Why was the New Deal nicknamed an alphabet soup?
options: It created many agencies known by their initials
It handed out free soup to the unemployed
Its laws were written in code
steps.explain: It launched dozens of new agencies, each known by its initials, like the CCC, the WPA, and the TVA. The cluster of letters earned the nickname.

steps.title: Letters everywhere
steps.body: When Franklin Roosevelt became president in 1933, the country was deep in the Great Depression. His New Deal answered with a flood of new agencies. The CCC put young men to work on conservation. The WPA employed millions on public works. The TVA built dams and brought electricity to the Tennessee Valley. Each was known by its initials, so people called the whole thing an alphabet soup.

steps.prompt: What did the TVA do?
options: Built dams and supplied electricity in the Tennessee Valley
Insured bank deposits
Regulated the stock market
