steps.explain: The Tennessee Valley Authority built dams and delivered electric power to a poor region. Different letters, different jobs.

steps.title: Some letters stuck
steps.body: Many of these agencies were temporary and closed once the crisis eased. But a few became permanent fixtures. The FDIC still insures the money in your bank account today, so a bank failure does not wipe out your savings. The SEC still polices financial markets. The Social Security Act of 1935 created the retirement benefits system millions still rely on. Some alphabet letters never left.

steps.prompt: Which New Deal creation still protects your bank deposits today?
options: The FDIC
The CCC
The WPA
steps.explain: The FDIC insures bank deposits, so a bank's failure does not erase your savings. It is a New Deal agency that is still working today.

steps.prompt: What event was the New Deal a response to?
options: The Great Depression
World War I
The 2008 financial crisis
steps.explain: The New Deal was Roosevelt's response to the Great Depression, the economic collapse of the 1930s.

steps.title: That's the alphabet soup.
recap: The New Deal answered the Depression with dozens of agencies.
Each was known by its initials: CCC, WPA, TVA.
A few, like the FDIC, still shape your bank today.

subject: HEALTH & THE BODY
title: Why You Get Goosebumps
blurb: Tiny muscles pull your body hair upright. In furry ancestors that trapped heat and looked bigger. In you it does almost nothing.

steps.title: Bumps with a backstory
steps.sub: Cold air or a chilling song, and your skin pebbles up. The bumps don't warm you and don't scare anyone. They're a reflex built for a body you no longer have.

steps.prompt: What is actually happening on your skin when you get goosebumps?
options: Pores opening to release sweat
Tiny muscles pulling each hair upright
Blood vessels bursting near the surface
steps.explain: Hairs standing up. Tiny muscles at the base of each hair contract and yank it upright, and the pulled skin forms a bump. The bumps are the side effect. The point was always the raised hair.

steps.title: Tiny muscles, raised hairs
steps.body: Each hair has a tiny muscle at its base, the arrector pili. When you get cold or feel a strong emotion, your sympathetic nervous system fires and these muscles contract automatically. They tug the hairs upright and bunch the skin around them into bumps. You don't choose it. It's a reflex, the same one that makes a scared cat's fur stand on end.

steps.prompt: What creates the bump itself?
options: Tiny muscles contracting and pulling hairs upright
Skin swelling with extra blood
Sweat pushing the skin out
steps.explain: Muscle contraction. The arrector pili muscle pulls a hair up, and the gathered skin around it forms the bump. It's mechanical, not swelling or sweat.

steps.title: A reflex for fur you don't have
steps.body: In furry mammals, raised hair does real jobs. It traps a layer of air for warmth, and a puffed-up coat makes the animal look bigger to a threat. Our ancestors had thick body hair, so the reflex paid off. We kept the wiring but lost the fur. So the same muscles still fire, but with thin hair they barely warm you or impress anyone. That leftover, useless-in-us reflex is called vestigial.

steps.prompt: Why don't goosebumps do much for humans?
options: Our muscles are too weak
We have little body hair, so raising it barely warms us or makes us look bigger
Our nervous system can't trigger them
steps.explain: We lost the fur. The reflex still raises our hairs, but thin hair traps little air and adds little bulk. The wiring outlived the coat it was built for.

steps.prompt: In furry animals, what did raised hair actually do?
options: Cooled them down
Trapped warm air and made them look bigger to threats
Helped them swim
steps.explain: Warmth and bulk. A raised coat traps insulating air and puffs the animal up to look larger. Those payoffs are why the reflex exists, even though it does little for us now.

steps.title: An echo on your skin.
recap: Tiny arrector pili muscles pull each hair upright.
In furry ancestors that meant warmth and looking bigger.
With little hair left, it's a vestigial reflex in us.

subject: HEALTH & THE BODY
title: Your Stomach Growls Even When You're Full
blurb: That rumble isn't a hunger alarm. It's your gut sweeping out leftovers, and it runs whether you're hungry or not.

steps.title: The rumble lies
steps.sub: Your stomach growls and you think 'I'm starving.' But it also growls right after a big meal, quietly. The sound isn't a hunger signal. It's plumbing.

steps.prompt: What does a growling stomach actually mean?
options: Your body is out of food and demanding more
Muscles in your gut are moving gas and fluid around
Your stomach acid is too high
steps.explain: It's movement. The growl is gas and fluid sloshing as your gut muscles squeeze. That happens during digestion and between meals alike. It can line up with hunger, but the sound itself is just the gut working.

steps.title: The sound is moving gas and fluid
steps.body: Your digestive tract is a long muscular tube that squeezes to push contents along. As it contracts, it shoves gas and fluid through, and that sloshing makes the rumble. The medical name for it is borborygmus. It's loudest when the gut is fairly empty, because there's no food to muffle the noise. So a loud growl often means empty, not desperate.

steps.prompt: Why is the growl often louder when your gut is empty?
options: Your stomach makes extra acid
There's no food to muffle the gas and fluid moving inside
Empty muscles squeeze harder
steps.explain: Nothing to muffle it. A full gut dampens the sound of moving gas and fluid. An empty one lets it ring out. Louder means emptier, not necessarily hungrier.

steps.title: A cleaning crew that runs on a timer
steps.body: Between meals, your gut runs a recurring wave of contractions called the migrating motor complex. Think of it as a cleanup sweep that pushes out leftover food, bacteria, and debris to keep the tract clear. It fires on a cycle whether you feel hungry or not. That's why a full stomach can still gurgle: the sweep is doing housekeeping, not begging for lunch.

steps.prompt: When does the gut's cleanup sweep run?
options: Only when you're hungry
On a cycle between meals, hungry or not
Only right after eating
steps.explain: On a cycle, regardless of hunger. The migrating motor complex sweeps out leftovers between meals whether you feel hungry or full. That's why the growl isn't a reliable hunger alarm.

steps.prompt: Your stomach growls an hour after a full lunch. Best explanation?
options: You're already starving again
The gut's cleanup sweep is moving gas and fluid through
Something is wrong with your digestion
steps.explain: It's the sweep. The migrating motor complex runs between meals to clear leftovers, moving gas and fluid that make the sound. It runs whether you're hungry or full.

steps.title: Not an alarm, just housekeeping.
recap: The growl is gas and fluid moving as gut muscles squeeze.
A between-meal sweep clears leftovers, hungry or not.
It's louder when empty because nothing muffles it.

subject: HEALTH & THE BODY
title: The Only Organ That Grows Back
blurb: Surgeons can remove most of a liver and it rebuilds itself to full size in months.

steps.title: An organ that grows back
steps.sub: Cut out most of a kidney or a lung and it stays gone. Cut out most of a liver and the body rebuilds it. The liver is the standout among your organs.

steps.prompt: A surgeon removes a large chunk of a healthy liver. What happens to what is left behind?
options: It stays the smaller size forever
It grows back toward the original size
It slowly shuts down without the missing part
steps.explain: It grows back. The remaining liver tissue expands until the organ returns to roughly its original mass. This is why a surgeon can safely remove a major portion in the first place.

steps.title: It rebuilds its mass
steps.body: The liver can lose a large fraction of itself and recover. A common figure is that up to about two-thirds can be removed, and the rest will regrow to the original mass over weeks to months. It does not copy back the exact original shape. It restores the working bulk, which is what your body actually needs.

steps.prompt: When the liver regrows, what does it restore?
options: Its exact original shape and outline
Its original working mass, over weeks to months
Only the outer surface, not the inside
steps.explain: It restores the mass. The body cares about having enough functioning liver tissue, so regrowth rebuilds the bulk and function rather than recreating the precise original shape.

steps.title: Ordinary cells do the work
steps.body: This is not a hidden pool of stem cells. The regrowth is driven mainly by the liver's own working cells, the hepatocytes, which normally just do not divide much. After a loss, they start dividing to replace what is gone. When the mass is back, the dividing stops. That on and off control is part of what makes the liver special.

steps.prompt: What mainly drives liver regrowth?
options: A reserve of stem cells kept for emergencies
The liver's own working cells starting to divide
New cells arriving from the bone marrow
steps.explain: The liver's own hepatocytes. They usually divide very little, but after a loss they multiply to rebuild the mass, then stop. No special stem-cell reserve is needed.

steps.title: Why living donation works
steps.body: This regrowth is what makes living-donor liver transplants possible. A healthy person can give away part of their liver. Their remaining piece regrows, and the piece placed in the recipient regrows too. Two people end up with working livers from one. Few organs let you do anything close to that.

steps.prompt: A friend says the liver regrows using a secret stash of stem cells. Based on the lesson, is that right?
options: Yes, that is the main mechanism
No, it is mostly ordinary liver cells dividing
Yes, but only in transplant donors
steps.explain: No. The lesson said regrowth is driven mainly by the liver's own working cells dividing, not a stem-cell reserve. That is what lets the organ rebuild its mass after a major loss.

steps.title: Built to rebuild.
recap: A large share of the liver can be removed and the rest regrows.
It restores working mass over weeks to months, not exact shape.
Ordinary liver cells divide to do it, which makes living donation possible.

subject: HEALTH & THE BODY
title: Your Bones Are Alive and Rebuilding
blurb: Bones are not dead scaffolding. They are living tissue your body is constantly tearing down and rebuilding.

steps.title: Bones are not dead
steps.sub: A skeleton in a museum looks like dry scaffolding. Inside you, bone is living tissue with cells, blood, and nerves, and it never stops rebuilding itself.

steps.prompt: Think about the bone inside your body right now. Which is closest to the truth?
options: It is dead, hardened material like a fingernail tip
It is living tissue with its own cells and blood supply
It is alive only while you are still growing as a child
steps.explain: It is living tissue. Your bones hold living cells, blood vessels, and nerves, and stay active your whole life. The dry museum skeleton is what is left after the living part is gone.

steps.title: Living tissue, not scaffolding
steps.body: Bone is a living organ. It is woven through with living cells, blood vessels, and nerves. That is why a fracture hurts, bleeds, and can heal itself. Dead scaffolding could not do any of that. The hardness fools people, but hard does not mean dead.

steps.prompt: Which clue shows that bone is living tissue?
options: It is hard and rigid
A broken bone can bleed and heal itself
It keeps its shape after you die
steps.explain: Healing and bleeding give it away. Only living tissue with blood and cells can repair a fracture. Hardness alone says nothing about whether something is alive.

steps.title: A constant teardown and rebuild
steps.body: Bone is always under renovation. One crew of cells, the osteoclasts, breaks down old bone. Another crew, the osteoblasts, lays down fresh bone. This remodeling runs your whole life. Bit by bit your skeleton gets replaced, with full turnover often described as taking around a decade. The bones you have now are not the same material you had years ago.

steps.prompt: Which cells break down old bone during remodeling?
options: Osteoblasts
Osteoclasts
Red blood cells
steps.explain: Osteoclasts break it down. Osteoblasts build the new bone back up. The two crews working together let the skeleton replace itself over time.

steps.prompt: Put the bone remodeling cycle in order at one spot on a bone.
items: Osteoclasts break down a patch of old bone
The old bone is cleared away
Osteoblasts lay down fresh bone
New bone hardens with minerals

steps.prompt: Someone insists bones are just dead struts that hold you up. From the lesson, what is the best correction?
options: They are right, bone is dead once you stop growing
Bone is living tissue with cells and blood, constantly rebuilt
Bone is alive but never changes after childhood
steps.explain: Bone is living and active. The lesson showed it has cells and blood, heals when broken, and is torn down and rebuilt throughout life. It is the opposite of dead struts.

steps.title: A living frame.
recap: Bone is living tissue with cells, blood, and nerves.
Osteoclasts break it down, osteoblasts build it back.
Remodeling replaces your skeleton over roughly a decade.

subject: HEALTH & THE BODY
title: Your Blood Is Never Blue
blurb: Veins look blue through your skin, but the blood inside is always red.

steps.title: The blue blood myth
steps.sub: Look at the veins in your wrist. They read as blue, and many people think the blood inside is blue until it meets air. It never is.

steps.prompt: Blood that has dropped off its oxygen and is heading back toward the lungs. What color is it inside you?
options: Blue, until it touches air
Dark red
Colorless
steps.explain: Dark red. Oxygen-poor blood is a deeper, duller red than oxygen-rich blood, but it stays red the whole time. There is no blue stage inside your body.

steps.title: Red comes from iron
steps.body: Your red blood cells are packed with hemoglobin, a protein built around iron. That iron grabs oxygen, and it is what makes blood red. Loaded with oxygen, blood is bright red. After handing oxygen off, it is a darker red. Either way, red. There is no blue version inside you.

steps.prompt: What gives blood its red color?
options: Hemoglobin, an iron-containing protein
A blue pigment that turns red in air
Oxygen, which is itself red
steps.explain: Hemoglobin. Its iron binds oxygen and gives blood its color. Bright red when oxygen-loaded, dark red when not, but always red.

steps.title: Why veins look blue
steps.body: So why the blue lines under your skin? It is a light trick. Skin absorbs and scatters light unevenly. Red light reaches deeper into tissue, while more of the bluer light bounces back to your eye from nearer the surface. So the vein reads as blue or greenish even though the blood inside is plain dark red. Your skin does the coloring, not the blood.

steps.prompt: Why does a vein look blue through your skin?
options: The blood in it really is blue
Skin scatters light so more blue light reaches your eye from the vein
Veins are wrapped in a thin blue layer
steps.explain: It is how skin handles light. Red light goes deeper while bluer light reflects back, so the vein reads blue to your eye. The blood underneath stays dark red.

steps.prompt: You give blood and the sample drawn from your vein looks dark red, not blue. Is that a problem?
options: Yes, vein blood should come out blue
No, vein blood is oxygen-poor and dark red, which is normal
Yes, it should be bright red like in diagrams
steps.explain: Nothing is wrong. Vein blood is oxygen-poor, so it is dark red rather than bright red. The blue vein was always just a light effect, never the blood's real color.

steps.title: Always red.
recap: Hemoglobin's iron makes blood red, bright or dark.
Veins look blue because of how skin scatters light.
The blood inside is never actually blue.

subject: HEALTH & THE BODY
title: Your Brain Can't Feel Pain
blurb: The organ that creates pain has no pain sensors of its own, which is why awake brain surgery is possible.

steps.title: The painless organ
steps.sub: The brain is where pain is felt for your whole body. Yet the brain itself cannot feel pain. That gap is what makes awake brain surgery possible.

steps.prompt: Doctors sometimes operate on the brain while the patient is awake and talking. How can that not be agony?
options: The patient is given enough painkiller to block all feeling everywhere
Brain tissue itself has no pain sensors
The patient is simply told to ignore the pain
steps.explain: Brain tissue has no pain sensors. There is nothing in the brain itself to register a cut as painful, so a surgeon can work on it while the patient stays awake.

steps.title: No sensors in the tissue
steps.body: Pain starts at tiny sensors called nociceptors. They sit in skin, muscle, and organs and shout when something is wrong. The brain tissue itself has none of them. So the brain feels everyone else's pain but cannot feel its own. Touch it, cut it, and it does not hurt.

steps.prompt: Why can't brain tissue feel pain?
options: It is numbed by the spinal cord
It has no pain sensors of its own
It is too busy processing other signals
steps.explain: No sensors. Pain needs nociceptors to start the signal, and brain tissue does not have them. Without sensors, there is nothing to report pain.

steps.title: The wrapping does feel pain
steps.body: Getting to the brain still hurts, though. The scalp, the covering of the skull, the membranes wrapped around the brain, and the blood vessels all carry pain sensors. So surgeons numb those outer layers with local anesthetic. Once they are through to the brain itself, there is nothing left to numb.

steps.prompt: During awake brain surgery, what is the local anesthetic for?
options: To numb the brain tissue directly
To numb the scalp and the layers around the brain, which do feel pain
To put the patient fully to sleep
steps.explain: It numbs the outer layers. The scalp, skull covering, and membranes have pain sensors, so they must be numbed. The brain itself needs nothing, since it has no sensors.

steps.title: Why stay awake at all
steps.body: Keeping the patient awake is not just possible, it is useful. Near critical areas, the surgeon asks the patient to talk, count, or move while testing the tissue. If a spot disrupts speech or movement, the surgeon knows to avoid it. The painless brain lets doctors map it live, in real time.

steps.prompt: A headache makes it feel like the brain itself is hurting. Based on the lesson, is the brain tissue what hurts?
options: Yes, the brain tissue is firing pain signals
No, brain tissue has no pain sensors; the pain comes from other structures
Yes, but only the front of the brain can hurt
steps.explain: No. The lesson said brain tissue has no pain sensors. Headache pain comes from pain-sensitive structures like blood vessels, the membranes around the brain, and nearby muscles, not the brain tissue itself.

steps.title: Feels everything but itself.
recap: Brain tissue has no pain sensors, so it cannot feel pain.
The scalp and membranes do feel pain and get numbed.
An awake patient lets surgeons map speech and movement live.

subject: HEALTH & THE BODY
title: Spicy Is Not a Flavor
blurb: Heat from chili is not a taste. It is your pain and heat nerves reacting to a chemical.

steps.title: Spicy is not a taste
steps.sub: We call chili a flavor and lump it in with sweet and salty. But your taste buds are not what is reacting. Your pain and heat nerves are.

steps.prompt: When a chili pepper feels hot in your mouth, what is mainly responding?
options: Your taste buds detecting a spicy flavor
Your heat and pain nerves reacting to a chemical
Your tongue physically rising in temperature
steps.explain: Your heat and pain nerves. The chili contains a chemical that switches on the same nerves that warn you about real heat, so your brain reads it as burning even though nothing is actually hot.

steps.title: What a taste actually is
steps.body: Taste buds report a short list: sweet, salty, sour, bitter, and umami, the savory one. That is the whole menu of basic tastes. Spicy is not on it. So when you feel the heat of a pepper, that signal is not coming from your taste buds at all. Something else is doing the talking.

steps.prompt: Which of these is one of the basic tastes?
options: Spicy
Umami
Hot
steps.explain: Umami, the savory taste, is a basic taste. Spicy and hot are not. The basic tastes are sweet, salty, sour, bitter, and umami, and spice is not among them.

steps.title: A chemical that fakes heat
steps.body: The heat comes from a compound in chili called capsaicin. It latches onto receptors that normally fire for real heat and for pain. One of them, TRPV1, is the same sensor that reacts to a hot drink. Capsaicin flips it on without any actual temperature change. Your brain gets a burning alarm and believes it.

steps.prompt: How does capsaicin make your mouth feel like it is burning?
options: It raises the real temperature of your tongue
It switches on receptors that normally respond to heat and pain
It overloads your sweet taste buds
steps.explain: It triggers heat and pain receptors. Capsaicin activates the same sensors that fire for real heat, so the brain reads burning even though your tongue's temperature has not changed.

steps.title: Why milk beats water
steps.body: This also explains the kitchen trick. Capsaicin barely dissolves in water, so a glass of water just pushes it around and the burn stays. It does dissolve in fat. Dairy like milk carries the capsaicin away and eases the burning. Reaching for water after a hot bite is the slow way out.

steps.prompt: A friend says spicy is just another taste like salty. From the lesson, what is the correction?
options: They are right, spicy is a sixth basic taste
Spicy is not a taste; it is heat and pain nerves reacting to capsaicin
Spicy is a taste, but only on the back of the tongue
steps.explain: Spicy is not a taste. The lesson showed the basic tastes are sweet, salty, sour, bitter, and umami, and that chili heat comes from capsaicin triggering heat and pain nerves, not taste buds.

steps.title: A burn, not a taste.
recap: The basic tastes are sweet, salty, sour, bitter, and umami.
Capsaicin triggers heat and pain receptors, faking a burn.
It dissolves in fat, so milk beats water for relief.

subject: HEALTH & THE BODY
title: You Have Way More Than Five Senses
blurb: Sight, smell, taste, touch, hearing. And a whole lot more.

steps.title: Five is a guess, not a count
steps.sub: Close your eyes and touch your nose. How did your hand know where it was?

steps.prompt: With your eyes shut, you can still tell which way is up. Which sense does that?
options: Touch
A separate balance sense in your inner ear
Sight, working in the dark
steps.explain: Your inner ear has fluid-filled canals that detect motion and the pull of gravity. That is balance, and it is not one of the famous five. So the list was always incomplete.

steps.title: The classic five are just the obvious ones
steps.body: Sight, hearing, smell, taste, and touch all point outward at the world. They are easy to notice because each has a clear organ. That is why the old list stuck. But your body also senses itself, and those senses have no obvious organ on your face.

steps.prompt: Why did the classic five get all the attention?
options: They are the only real senses
Each points outward and has an obvious organ
They are the strongest senses
steps.explain: They face the outside world and each has a visible organ, like the eye or ear. The inward senses are harder to spot, so they got left off the famous list.

steps.title: You feel your own body without looking
steps.body: Proprioception is your sense of where your body parts are. It is why you can clap with your eyes closed or touch your nose in the dark. Sensors in your muscles and joints report position to your brain constantly. You also sense temperature, pain, and the state of your insides, like hunger and a full bladder.

steps.prompt: What lets you touch your nose with your eyes closed?
options: Proprioception, your sense of body position
Hearing
A lucky guess
steps.explain: Proprioception uses sensors in your muscles and joints to track where your limbs are. No vision needed, because the position data comes from inside your body.

steps.prompt: Which body part runs your sense of balance?
options: The brain stem alone
The inner ear
The eyes
steps.explain: Fluid-filled canals in the inner ear track motion and gravity. When they get confused, like on a boat, you feel dizzy or seasick.

steps.title: Senses, plural and then some
recap: The classic five all point outward and have obvious organs.
Balance comes from the inner ear, not the five.
Proprioception tracks where your body is without looking.

subject: HEALTH & THE BODY
title: You Can't Sneeze With Your Eyes Open
blurb: Your eyes slam shut every time. You don't get a vote.

steps.title: Try it. You can't.
steps.sub: Next time you sneeze, notice your eyes. They close before you can stop them.

steps.prompt: Why might your eyes shut when you sneeze?
options: To stop your eyes from popping out
It is an automatic reflex, no clear purpose required
Because the light gets too bright
steps.explain: The eye-closing is a reflex wired into the same nerve circuit as the sneeze. There is no proof it protects your eyes from popping out, which is a myth. It just happens automatically.

steps.title: A sneeze is a reflex you don't run
steps.body: A sneeze starts when something irritates the lining of your nose. Nerves send a signal to your brain stem, which fires off the whole sneeze sequence at once. You take a breath, your chest squeezes, and air blasts out. Closing your eyes is part of that automatic package. Your conscious brain is not in charge.

steps.prompt: What starts a sneeze?
options: A decision to sneeze
Irritation in the lining of your nose
Bright light only
steps.explain: Irritation in the nasal lining triggers nerve signals to the brain stem. The brain stem runs the rest automatically, which is why you cannot simply choose to start or stop it.

steps.title: The eyes are bundled into the reflex
steps.body: The eye-closing rides along with the sneeze because the nerves controlling both are linked in the brain stem. When the sneeze fires, the eyelids get the signal too. It is hard to override because it is not a thought. It is a built-in motor pattern, like a knee jerk.

steps.prompt: Why is it so hard to keep your eyes open during a sneeze?
options: The eyelids are physically locked
The eye-closing is part of the automatic reflex, not a thought
You are too distracted
steps.explain: Eye-closing is wired into the sneeze reflex in the brain stem. Because it is automatic, not deliberate, your conscious effort has little say over it.

steps.prompt: Is it true that sneezing with eyes open makes them pop out?
options: Yes, that is why they close
No, that is a myth
Only for some people
steps.explain: There is no evidence the reflex protects against eyes popping out. The pressure in a sneeze does not work that way. The eyes close simply because the reflex bundles them in.

steps.title: Not your call
recap: A sneeze is a reflex run by the brain stem, not by choice.
Eye-closing is bundled into that reflex.
The eyes-pop-out story is a myth.

subject: HEALTH & THE BODY
title: Your Gut Has Its Own Brain
blurb: Hundreds of millions of neurons, running your digestion on their own.

steps.title: A brain in your belly
steps.sub: Your gut keeps working even when your head is asleep. Something has to run it.

steps.prompt: Roughly how many neurons line your gut?
options: A few thousand
Hundreds of millions
More than your brain
steps.explain: The gut holds on the order of hundreds of millions of neurons. That is far fewer than the brain, but enough to run digestion largely on its own.

steps.title: A nervous system in your gut wall
steps.body: Inside the wall of your gut is a mesh of nerve cells called the enteric nervous system. It runs from your esophagus to your rectum and holds hundreds of millions of neurons. People call it the second brain because it is the only organ system that can work without orders from the head.

steps.prompt: What is the enteric nervous system?
options: A mesh of nerve cells in the gut wall
A second heart
Part of the spinal cord
steps.explain: It is a network of neurons built into the wall of the gut. It earns the second brain nickname because it can operate on its own, unlike other organ systems.

steps.title: It works without the head
steps.body: The gut brain controls the muscle waves that push food along and the timing of digestive juices. It can do this even when its link to the actual brain is cut. The two still talk constantly through the vagus nerve, but the gut does not wait for permission to do its basic job.

steps.prompt: What can the gut brain still do if its link to the head is cut?
options: Nothing at all
Run the basic muscle waves of digestion
Only feel pain
steps.explain: The enteric nervous system can drive the muscle contractions that move food along on its own. That independence is exactly what makes it brain-like.

steps.prompt: Put the gut's basic job in order.
items: Food enters the gut
Stretch is sensed in the gut wall
Enteric neurons fire
Muscle waves push food along

steps.prompt: Roughly how many neurons does the gut hold?
options: A few thousand
Hundreds of millions
Zero, it borrows the brain's
steps.explain: Hundreds of millions. That is a small fraction of the brain's count, but plenty to manage digestion without supervision.

steps.title: Two brains, one body
recap: The enteric nervous system lines your gut wall.
It holds hundreds of millions of neurons.
It can run digestion even when cut off from the head.

subject: HEALTH & THE BODY
title: Most of the Cells in You Aren't You
blurb: You carry about as many bacterial cells as human ones.

steps.title: You are a crowd
steps.sub: Trillions of bacteria live on and in you right now. You need them.

steps.prompt: Compared to your human cells, how many bacterial cells do you carry?
options: Almost none
Roughly the same number
Ten times more, for sure
steps.explain: Current estimates put bacterial cells at roughly the same order as human cells, give or take. The old line about ten times more has been walked back, but the count is still in the same ballpark as your own cells.

steps.title: A roughly even split
steps.body: Your body is home to trillions of bacteria, most of them in your gut. Recent estimates suggest the number of bacterial cells is roughly comparable to the number of your own human cells. So a large share of the cells you carry are not human at all. This community is called your microbiome.

steps.prompt: What is the microbiome?
options: A type of human cell
The community of bacteria living on and in you
A small organ near the gut
steps.explain: The microbiome is the whole community of microbes you host, mostly in the gut. Knowing the name helps because researchers study it as one big system.

steps.title: You would not do well without them
steps.body: These bacteria are not just along for the ride. Gut bacteria help break down food your own body cannot digest and produce certain vitamins. They also crowd out harmful microbes. Wipe them all out and your digestion and defenses suffer. You and your microbes are a working partnership.

steps.prompt: What do your gut bacteria do for you?
options: Nothing useful
Help digest food and make some vitamins
Only cause disease
steps.explain: Gut bacteria break down food you cannot digest alone and produce certain vitamins. That is why losing them all would hurt, not help.

steps.prompt: How does your bacterial cell count compare to your human cell count?
options: Far fewer bacteria
Roughly the same order
A thousand times more
steps.explain: Roughly the same order of magnitude. The old ten-to-one claim was an overestimate, but bacteria still rival your own cells in number.

steps.title: Mostly you, barely
recap: Bacterial cells in you are on the same order as human ones.
That whole community is your microbiome.
Gut bacteria help you digest food and make vitamins.

subject: HEALTH & THE BODY
title: The Tongue Taste Map Is a Lie
blurb: Sweet at the tip, bitter at the back? No. Your whole tongue tastes everything.

steps.title: The map in your textbook was wrong
steps.sub: You probably saw a tongue split into zones for each taste. Forget it.

steps.prompt: Put a drop of sugar on the back of your tongue. Can you taste sweet?
options: No, the back only does bitter
Yes, you can taste sweet there too
Only if you swallow it
steps.explain: You can taste sweet at the back, the sides, and the tip. The zoned map is wrong. Every taste-sensing region of the tongue can pick up every basic taste.

steps.title: Every region tastes every taste
steps.body: Your taste buds sit all over the tongue, and each one can detect all the basic tastes: sweet, sour, salty, bitter, and savory. There is no zone that handles only one taste. Sensitivity can vary a little from spot to spot, but no region is blind to a whole taste.

steps.prompt: Can the tip of your tongue taste bitter?
options: No, only the back can
Yes, taste buds everywhere detect all basic tastes
Only with help from the nose
steps.explain: Taste buds across the tongue can detect all the basic tastes. The tip is not limited to sweet, and the back is not limited to bitter.

steps.title: Where the myth came from
steps.body: The map traces back to an old study that was misread. The original work showed only small differences in sensitivity across the tongue, not separate zones. When it was redrawn for textbooks, those tiny differences became hard borders. The cartoon stuck for decades.

steps.prompt: How did the tongue map myth start?
options: A study showing strict separate zones
Small sensitivity differences got redrawn as hard zones
A marketing campaign
steps.explain: The original research found only minor differences in sensitivity. Textbooks turned those slight differences into rigid taste zones, which is how the false map spread.

steps.prompt: True or false: only the back of the tongue tastes bitter.
options: True
False, all regions detect all basic tastes
steps.explain: False. Taste buds across the tongue can pick up every basic taste, so bitter is not stuck at the back.

steps.title: Toss the map
recap: Every taste-sensing region detects every basic taste.
Sensitivity varies only slightly across the tongue.
The zoned map came from a misread study.

subject: HEALTH & THE BODY
title: Your Kidneys Filter Your Whole Blood Supply Constantly
blurb: Two fist-sized organs clean your entire blood volume dozens of times a day.

steps.title: Always on
steps.sub: You have around five liters of blood. Your kidneys work through all of it again and again, every single day, without you noticing.

steps.prompt: Roughly how many times a day do your kidneys filter your whole blood supply?
options: About once
A few times
Dozens of times
steps.explain: Dozens. Your kidneys process roughly your entire blood volume many times over each day. They are not a once-a-day cleanup. They run constantly.

steps.title: They filter blood, not urine
steps.body: Blood enters each kidney and passes through about a million tiny filters called nephrons. Each one strains the blood, pulling out water, waste, and excess salts. What gets removed becomes urine. The cleaned blood goes straight back into circulation. So the kidney's real job is cleaning blood. Urine is just the waste it sets aside.

steps.prompt: What is a nephron?
options: A tube that stores urine
A tiny filter unit inside the kidney
A blood vessel to the heart
steps.explain: A nephron is the kidney's tiny filtering unit. You have about a million per kidney, and together they strain waste from your blood.

steps.title: Most of what they filter, they keep
steps.body: The kidneys filter a huge volume of fluid each day, far more than you ever pee out. The reason is that they over-filter on purpose, then reclaim almost everything useful: nearly all the water, sugar, and salts get pulled back into the blood. Only a small fraction leaves as urine. This is how they fine-tune your water and salt balance, not just remove trash.

steps.prompt: Why do the kidneys filter far more fluid than you actually urinate?
options: They are inefficient and waste effort
They over-filter, then reclaim almost everything useful
Most of it evaporates inside the body
steps.explain: They over-filter on purpose. By straining a large volume and then reabsorbing nearly all the water and useful substances, they can finely control your balance of water and salt.

steps.prompt: Order the path of cleaning your blood.
items: Blood enters the kidney
Nephrons filter out water and waste
Useful water and salts are reabsorbed
Leftover waste becomes urine
Cleaned blood returns to circulation

steps.prompt: Earlier: what is the kidney's filtering unit called?
options: A nephron
A neuron
steps.explain: A nephron. Each kidney holds about a million of them, and they do the actual work of straining waste from your blood.

steps.title: Quiet, constant cleanup.
recap: Kidneys clean blood; urine is just the waste they set aside
Each kidney holds about a million tiny filters called nephrons
They over-filter, then reclaim nearly all the water and useful substances

subject: HEALTH & THE BODY
title: How Your Body Stays at One Temperature
blurb: Sweating, shivering, and flushing are all one fight to hold a steady core temperature.

steps.title: A thermostat made of flesh
steps.sub: Whether it is freezing or sweltering outside, your core stays near a fixed point. Your body works hard, in several ways, to keep it there.

steps.prompt: What is normal human body temperature, roughly?
options: About 30 degrees C
About 37 degrees C
About 42 degrees C
steps.explain: About 37 degrees C, or 98.6 F. It varies a little by person, time of day, and where you measure, but your body defends a value near there.

steps.title: A control center in the brain
steps.body: A small region near the base of your brain, the hypothalamus, acts as your thermostat. It reads temperature signals from your blood and your skin. When you drift too warm or too cold, it triggers responses to push you back toward the set point. You do not decide to do any of this. It runs automatically.

steps.prompt: Which part of the body acts as the temperature control center?
options: The heart
The hypothalamus in the brain
The skin
steps.explain: The hypothalamus. It senses your temperature and triggers the responses that pull you back toward the set point, all without conscious effort.

steps.title: Cooling down and warming up
steps.body: When you are too hot, you sweat, and as that sweat evaporates it carries heat away. Blood vessels near your skin widen, which is the flush you see, dumping heat to the air. When you are too cold, those vessels narrow to hold heat in, and you shiver. Shivering is rapid muscle twitching, and the work of those muscles generates heat.

steps.prompt: How does shivering warm you up?
options: It pulls warm blood up from your feet
Rapid muscle activity generates heat
It seals the pores to trap heat
steps.explain: Shivering is fast, involuntary muscle twitching. Muscles release heat when they work, so all that twitching warms you from the inside.

steps.title: Sweat cools by evaporating
steps.body: Sweat does not cool you just by being wet. It cools you as it evaporates. Turning liquid into vapor takes energy, and that energy is drawn as heat from your skin. This is why a hot, humid day feels worse: when the air is already full of moisture, sweat evaporates poorly, so it cools you less.

steps.prompt: Why is sweating less effective on a humid day?
options: You produce less sweat when it is humid
Sweat evaporates poorly into already-moist air
Humid air blocks your pores
steps.explain: Cooling comes from evaporation. When the air is already saturated with moisture, sweat cannot evaporate well, so it carries away less heat.

steps.prompt: Order what happens when you get too hot.
items: The hypothalamus senses you are overheating
Skin blood vessels widen to release heat
Sweat glands release sweat
Sweat evaporates and carries heat away
Your core temperature falls back toward normal

steps.prompt: Earlier: which organ runs your temperature control?
options: The hypothalamus
The liver
steps.explain: The hypothalamus. It senses temperature and triggers sweating, flushing, or shivering to hold your core near the set point.

steps.title: One set point, many tools.
recap: The hypothalamus is the thermostat, working automatically
Sweat cools by evaporating, not just by being wet
Shivering and narrowed skin vessels warm and conserve heat

subject: HEALTH & THE BODY
title: Your Appendix Isn't Useless After All
blurb: The classic leftover organ may be a safe house for your gut bacteria.

steps.title: The organ with a bad reputation
steps.sub: For a long time the appendix was written off as a useless evolutionary leftover. The newer view is more interesting than that.

steps.prompt: What is the most common claim you have heard about the appendix?
options: It helps you digest meat
It is a useless leftover with no function
It pumps blood to the gut
steps.explain: The 'useless leftover' idea is the famous one, and it is the misconception this lesson takes apart. The appendix may do real work after all.

steps.title: A possible safe house for good bacteria
steps.body: Your gut is full of helpful bacteria that aid digestion. The appendix is a small pouch off the start of the large intestine, and it holds a lot of immune tissue. A leading idea is that it acts as a reservoir, a safe house for good bacteria. After an illness like severe diarrhea flushes your gut, the appendix can help restock it.

steps.prompt: What is the leading proposed function of the appendix?
options: Storing extra digestive enzymes
Acting as a reservoir to restock good gut bacteria
Filtering the blood like a small kidney
steps.explain: The reservoir idea. By keeping a protected stash of helpful bacteria, the appendix may help repopulate the gut after illness clears it out.

steps.title: Useful does not mean essential
steps.body: Here is the catch. People who have their appendix removed do fine. So whatever the appendix does, you can live a normal life without it. 'Has a likely function' and 'you cannot live without it' are different claims. The appendix appears to be helpful but not essential, which is why surgeons remove an inflamed one without hesitation.

steps.prompt: If the appendix has a function, why is it safe to remove?
options: Removing it actually improves digestion
It is likely helpful but not essential, and people live fine without it
It grows back within a year
steps.explain: Helpful is not the same as essential. People without an appendix live normal lives, so an inflamed one can be removed safely.

steps.prompt: Earlier: what is the appendix thought to be a safe house for?
options: Good gut bacteria
Spare red blood cells
steps.explain: Good gut bacteria. The reservoir idea is that it can help restock your gut after an illness flushes it out.

steps.title: Not so useless.
recap: The 'useless leftover' label is outdated
It may be a reservoir that restocks good gut bacteria after illness
It is likely helpful but not essential, so removal is safe

subject: HEALTH & THE BODY
title: Your Biggest Organ Is the One You Can See
blurb: Skin is a real organ: it waterproofs you, blocks germs, and renews itself constantly.

steps.title: An organ in plain sight
steps.sub: We tend to picture organs as hidden things inside us. But your largest organ is the one wrapped around the outside of you.

steps.prompt: What is the largest organ in the human body?
options: The liver
The skin
The lungs
steps.explain: The skin. By surface area and by weight it is the body's largest organ. An organ is just a structure of tissues doing a shared job, and skin qualifies.

steps.title: A barrier that works both ways
steps.body: Skin is your frontline barrier. It keeps water and fluids in, so you do not dry out, and it keeps threats out, blocking most germs before they can reach your insides. It also shields you from physical knocks and from some of the sun's radiation. A break in the skin, like a cut, is dangerous mainly because it opens that barrier.

steps.prompt: Why is even a small cut a genuine risk?
options: It lets heat escape too quickly
It opens a break in the barrier that blocks germs
It drains too much blood pressure
steps.explain: Skin's main job is keeping fluids in and germs out. A cut breaches that barrier, giving germs a way past your frontline defense.

steps.title: It is always rebuilding itself
steps.body: The outer layer of your skin is constantly replacing itself. New cells form deeper down, get pushed toward the surface, fill with a tough protein, and die. The flat dead cells on the outside are what actually faces the world. They flake off all the time, and much of household dust is old skin. Underneath, fresh cells are always rising up to replace them.

steps.prompt: What is the very outermost layer of your skin made of?
options: Living, dividing cells
Flattened dead cells that flake off
A thin layer of fat
steps.explain: The surface is flattened dead cells. New cells form deeper, rise up, harden, and die, so the outer layer facing the world is already dead.

steps.prompt: Order the journey of a skin cell to the surface.
items: A new cell forms in the deeper layer
It is pushed upward by newer cells below
It fills with tough protein and dies
It reaches the surface as a flat dead cell
It flakes off and becomes dust

steps.prompt: Earlier: what is the body's largest organ?
options: The skin
The heart
steps.explain: The skin. It is the largest by both surface area and weight, and it serves as your frontline barrier.

steps.title: More than a wrapper.
recap: Skin is the body's largest organ
It keeps fluids in and blocks germs and physical harm
Its outer layer is dead cells, constantly shed and replaced

subject: HEALTH & THE BODY
title: What 'Muscle Memory' Actually Is
blurb: Your muscles don't remember anything. The skill lives in your brain and nerves.

steps.title: The memory is not in the muscle
steps.sub: Ride a bike after ten years off and it comes right back. We call that muscle memory. But the muscle is not where the memory is kept.

steps.prompt: When a skill becomes 'muscle memory,' where is that memory actually stored?
options: In the muscle fibers themselves
In the brain and nervous system
In the tendons connecting muscle to bone
steps.explain: In the brain and nervous system. Muscles only contract when nerves tell them to. The pattern, the skill, is learned and stored by your nervous system.

steps.title: Muscles only follow orders
steps.body: A muscle has no way to store a sequence. It can only do one thing: contract when a nerve signals it to. Every skilled movement, from typing to a tennis serve, is really a precise pattern of signals sent from your brain through your nerves to many muscles in the right order and timing. The intelligence is in the signaling, not the meat.

steps.prompt: What is the only thing a muscle does on its own?
options: Stores movement patterns
Contracts when a nerve signals it
Decides when to move
steps.explain: A muscle just contracts on command. It cannot store a sequence or decide anything. The pattern and the decision come from the nervous system.

steps.title: Practice rewires the brain
steps.body: When you practice, your brain refines the pathways that fire those signals. With repetition the pattern becomes faster, smoother, and more automatic, so you no longer think it through step by step. This is a form of learning in the nervous system. That is why a long-unused skill returns quickly: the wiring is still there, just rusty.

steps.prompt: Why does a skill like cycling come back fast after years off?
options: The muscles kept the memory the whole time
The brain's learned pathways are still there, just rusty
You relearn it completely from scratch each time
steps.explain: Practice rewires pathways in the nervous system. Those learned pathways persist, so the skill is rusty rather than gone and returns quickly.

steps.prompt: Earlier: what is the only thing a muscle does by itself?
options: Contracts when a nerve signals it
Remembers movement patterns
steps.explain: It only contracts on a nerve signal. Since it cannot store anything, the memory behind a skill must live in the brain and nerves.

steps.title: It is brain memory.
recap: Muscles only contract when nerves signal them
Skills are patterns of signals learned by the nervous system
Practice rewires brain pathways, so old skills return quickly

subject: HEALTH & THE BODY
title: Why Yawns Are Contagious
blurb: You read the word yawn and felt the urge. Here's what's actually happening.

steps.title: Catching a yawn
steps.sub: See someone yawn, and your own jaw starts to drop. You don't have to be tired. Something about watching another person triggers the reflex.

steps.prompt: A contagious yawn is mostly a sign of what?
options: How tired you are
Something social, tied to noticing other people
Low oxygen in the room
steps.explain: It's largely social. A spontaneous yawn can come from tiredness, but a caught yawn is set off by perceiving another person doing it, even just hearing or reading about it.

steps.title: It travels through attention to others
steps.body: Contagious yawning is triggered by watching, hearing, or even thinking about someone else yawn. Researchers link it to the brain systems we use to read and mirror other people. People who score higher on measures of empathy tend to catch yawns more readily, and you are more likely to catch a yawn from someone close to you than from a stranger.

steps.prompt: Whose yawn are you most likely to catch?
options: A stranger's
Someone you're close to
It makes no difference who it is
steps.explain: You catch yawns more readily from people close to you. The effect rides on the same machinery we use to attend to and mirror others, which is stronger for people we're bonded with.

steps.title: The yawn itself may cool the brain
steps.body: Why yawn at all? One leading idea is brain cooling. A big yawn pulls in cool air and stretches the jaw, increasing blood flow that may help lower the temperature of the brain. The brain works best in a narrow temperature range, so a yawn may act like a small reset. This is still debated, but it fits why yawns cluster around drowsy, overheated moments.

steps.prompt: According to the brain-cooling idea, what does a yawn do?
options: Forces extra oxygen into the blood
Helps lower brain temperature
Empties carbon dioxide from the lungs
steps.explain: The cooling hypothesis says the deep inhale and jaw stretch boost blood flow and help shed heat. The brain runs best in a tight temperature band, so cooling acts like a reset.

steps.prompt: Earlier: a caught yawn is best described as what kind of response?
options: A social one, triggered by perceiving others
A purely physical one, triggered by low oxygen
steps.explain: Social. Catching a yawn is set off by noticing another person yawn, and it tracks with empathy and closeness, not just how tired you are.

steps.title: Now try not to yawn.
recap: Caught yawns are triggered by perceiving others, not just tiredness
They track with empathy and are stronger between close people
One leading idea: yawning helps cool the brain

subject: HEALTH & THE BODY
title: Your Heart Runs on Its Own Electricity
blurb: Cut every nerve to a heart and it still beats. The spark comes from inside.

steps.title: A self-starting pump
steps.sub: Your brain doesn't tell your heart to beat. The heart makes its own electrical signal, which is why a transplanted heart beats even after its old nerves are gone.

steps.prompt: Where does the signal that starts each heartbeat come from?
options: The brain, down the spinal cord
A patch of cells inside the heart itself
The lungs, with each breath
steps.explain: From inside the heart. A small cluster of specialized cells fires on its own rhythm. The brain can speed it up or slow it down, but it does not create the beat.

steps.title: The pacemaker is built in
steps.body: Tucked in the upper right of the heart is the sinoatrial node, the heart's natural pacemaker. These cells generate an electrical pulse all on their own, over and over, without any outside command. That self-firing is why a heart removed for transplant can keep beating in solution, and why the donor heart beats once placed in someone new, even though its nerve connections were cut.

steps.prompt: Why does a transplanted heart still beat despite cut nerves?
options: Surgeons restart it with a permanent battery
Its own pacemaker cells generate the signal
The new body's brain wires into it during surgery
steps.explain: The sinoatrial node fires on its own. Because the beat originates inside the heart, severing the outside nerves does not stop it.

steps.title: The signal spreads in a set path
steps.body: Once the pacemaker fires, the pulse spreads through the heart in order. It first makes the upper chambers, the atria, contract. Then it reaches a relay called the atrioventricular node, which briefly delays the signal so the lower chambers fill. From there it races down conducting fibers and makes the larger lower chambers, the ventricles, squeeze and push blood out.

steps.prompt: Put one heartbeat's electrical path in order.
items: Sinoatrial node fires
Atria contract
Atrioventricular node delays the signal
Ventricles contract and push blood out

glosses.Atrioventricular node delays the signal: A pause so the lower chambers fill.
glosses.Ventricles contract and push blood out: Lower chambers do the heavy lifting.

steps.prompt: Earlier: what is the sinoatrial node?
options: The heart's natural pacemaker
A valve between the chambers
steps.explain: The heart's natural pacemaker. It generates the electrical pulse that starts each beat, which is why the heart does not need the brain to keep going.

steps.title: Beating to its own drum.
recap: The heart generates its own electrical signal and needs no brain command to beat
The sinoatrial node is the built-in pacemaker
The pulse spreads in order: atria, a delay, then the ventricles

subject: HEALTH & THE BODY
title: You're Constantly Falling Apart (and Rebuilding)
blurb: You shed millions of skin cells a day. A lot of household dust is you.

steps.title: Always shedding
steps.sub: Your outer skin is not a fixed shell. It sheds dead cells constantly and grows fresh ones underneath, so the surface you have today is not the one you had a month ago.

steps.prompt: Roughly how many skin cells do you shed in a day?
options: A few thousand
About a hundred thousand
Hundreds of millions
steps.explain: Hundreds of millions. The number is large because shedding happens across your whole body surface all day, every day. The cells are tiny, so the loss is invisible.

steps.title: The outer layer is mostly dead cells
steps.body: The outermost layer of your skin is made of flattened, dead cells packed with a tough protein called keratin. New cells are born deep in the skin, then get pushed up over weeks. By the time they reach the surface they have died and hardened into a protective barrier. That barrier wears off in tiny flakes all the time, which is the shedding you never notice.

steps.prompt: What are the cells at the very surface of your skin like?
options: Living cells that breathe air
Dead, flattened cells hardened with keratin
Cells that are about to divide
steps.explain: Dead and hardened with keratin. New cells form deep down, rise over weeks, die, and become a tough barrier that flakes off, which is why the surface keeps renewing.

steps.title: Much of household dust is you
steps.body: All those shed flakes have to go somewhere. A meaningful share of the dust in your home is made of dead human skin cells, mixed with fabric fibers, dirt, and other bits. It is a common claim that the majority of house dust is skin. That overstates it, but human skin is a real and steady contributor to the dust around you.

steps.prompt: What does the lesson say about the 'most of house dust is skin' claim?
options: It's exactly right
It overstates it, but skin is a real contributor
Skin is never found in dust
steps.explain: It overstates it. Dust is a mix of fibers, dirt, and other debris, but shed skin really is a steady part of it, which is why the myth has staying power.

steps.prompt: Earlier: about how many skin cells do you shed per day?
options: Hundreds of millions
A few thousand
steps.explain: Hundreds of millions. The loss is constant and spread over your whole body, so the cells flake off and feed into the dust around you without you noticing.

steps.title: A body in motion.
recap: You shed hundreds of millions of skin cells a day
The skin's surface is dead cells hardened with keratin, replaced from below
Shed skin is a real part of house dust, though not most of it

subject: HEALTH & THE BODY
title: Where Lost Fat Actually Goes
blurb: You don't burn fat into heat or muscle. You mostly breathe it out.

steps.title: The vanishing pounds
steps.sub: Lose weight and the fat seems to just disappear. It doesn't vanish, and it doesn't turn into muscle. It mostly leaves through your lungs.

steps.prompt: When you lose fat, where does most of its mass go?
options: It turns into energy or heat
It's converted into muscle
It leaves as carbon dioxide you exhale
steps.explain: You breathe most of it out. The common answers break a basic rule: matter is not destroyed and does not become muscle. The fat's atoms have to leave, and most exit as exhaled carbon dioxide.

steps.title: Fat is mostly carbon, hydrogen, oxygen
steps.body: A fat molecule is built almost entirely from carbon, hydrogen, and oxygen atoms. To get rid of fat, your body breaks those molecules apart and combines the atoms with oxygen you breathe in. This is just chemistry: the atoms in the fat have to end up somewhere, packaged into new molecules that can leave the body.

steps.prompt: What is a fat molecule mostly made of?
options: Carbon, hydrogen, and oxygen
Mostly water
Protein and minerals
steps.explain: Carbon, hydrogen, and oxygen. Those atoms can't disappear, so losing fat means repackaging them into molecules that exit the body.

steps.title: Out as carbon dioxide and water
steps.body: When fat is fully broken down, its atoms leave in two forms: carbon dioxide and water. The large majority of the lost mass leaves as carbon dioxide, which you exhale with every breath. The rest leaves as water, in your sweat, urine, and breath. To 'burn' fat you literally exhale most of it.

steps.prompt: Of the fat mass you lose, most leaves the body as what?
options: Sweat
Carbon dioxide you exhale
Heat
steps.explain: Carbon dioxide. When fat is broken down its atoms exit as carbon dioxide and water, and most of the mass goes out as the carbon dioxide you breathe off.

steps.prompt: Trace a fat molecule out of the body.
items: Stored fat molecule
Broken apart and combined with oxygen
Becomes carbon dioxide and water
Carbon dioxide is exhaled, water is shed

glosses.Carbon dioxide is exhaled, water is shed: Most mass leaves through the lungs.

steps.prompt: Earlier: most of the fat you lose leaves the body as what?
options: Exhaled carbon dioxide
Muscle
steps.explain: Exhaled carbon dioxide. Fat does not become muscle or simply vanish. Its atoms leave mainly through your breath, with a smaller part as water.

steps.title: Weight loss is an exhale.
recap: Fat is mostly carbon, hydrogen, and oxygen atoms
Breaking it down combines those atoms with oxygen you breathe
Most lost fat leaves as exhaled carbon dioxide, the rest as water

subject: HEALTH & THE BODY
title: A Calorie Isn't Always a Calorie
blurb: 100 calories of almonds and 100 of soda do not hit your body the same way.

steps.title: Same number, different effect
steps.sub: A calorie is a unit of energy, so on paper 100 is 100. But your body is not a furnace. What the food is made of changes how much you absorb and what happens next.

steps.prompt: Do 100 calories of almonds and 100 of soda affect the body identically?
options: Yes, a calorie is a calorie
No, the body handles them differently
Only if you eat them at the same time
steps.explain: No. The energy on the label is the same, but your gut absorbs them differently and they trigger different responses. The label number is a starting point, not the full story.

steps.title: You don't absorb every labeled calorie
steps.body: Food labels estimate the energy in food, but your body does not capture all of it. Whole foods like nuts come wrapped in fibrous cell walls that are hard to break down, so some of their fat and energy passes through undigested. Studies on almonds found people absorb noticeably fewer calories than the label lists. Liquid sugar, by contrast, is absorbed quickly and almost completely.

steps.prompt: Why might you absorb fewer calories from almonds than the label says?
options: Almonds have fewer calories than labeled
Their fibrous structure means some energy passes through undigested
Your body refuses to digest fat
steps.explain: Their tough, fibrous structure resists full breakdown, so some fat and energy leaves undigested. The label measures the food's energy, not how much you actually take up.

steps.title: Digesting food itself costs energy
steps.body: Processing food burns energy, an effect called the thermic effect of food. It is not the same for every nutrient. Protein takes far more energy to digest and process than fat or carbohydrate, so a chunk of protein's calories is spent just handling it. Different foods also affect fullness and blood sugar differently, which shapes how much you eat later.

steps.prompt: Which nutrient costs the most energy to digest and process?
options: Fat
Carbohydrate
Protein
steps.explain: Protein. The thermic effect of food is higher for protein than for fat or carbohydrate, so more of its calories are spent just on digestion.

steps.prompt: Earlier: why might 100 labeled calories of almonds give you less than 100?
options: Their fibrous structure lets some energy pass undigested
Almonds are labeled incorrectly by law
steps.explain: Their fibrous structure resists full breakdown, so some fat and energy passes through. The label counts the food's energy, not how much your gut actually absorbs.

steps.title: Beyond the label number.
recap: A calorie is an energy unit, but the body doesn't absorb every labeled calorie
Fibrous whole foods like nuts pass some energy through undigested
Digestion itself costs energy, and protein costs the most

subject: HEALTH & THE BODY
title: MSG Was Never the Villain
blurb: The 'Chinese restaurant syndrome' scare grew from a single 1968 letter.

steps.title: A panic with thin roots
steps.sub: For decades people blamed MSG for headaches and worse. The fear spread faster than the evidence, and the evidence never really caught up.

steps.prompt: What is MSG mostly made of?
options: A synthetic chemical found nowhere in nature
Sodium plus glutamate, an amino acid found in many foods
A type of sugar
steps.explain: It is monosodium glutamate: a sodium atom joined to glutamate, an amino acid your body already uses. Glutamate occurs naturally in foods like tomatoes, parmesan, and mushrooms.

steps.title: It is just salt and a savory amino acid
steps.body: MSG is monosodium glutamate. Glutamate is one of the most common amino acids in protein, and free glutamate is what gives the savory taste called umami. You eat it whenever you have tomatoes, aged cheese, or soy sauce. The version sold as a seasoning is the same molecule, made by fermentation. Your body cannot tell added glutamate from the glutamate in food.

steps.prompt: Why does MSG taste savory?
options: It contains glutamate, which produces the umami taste
It is high in sugar
It coats the tongue in sodium
steps.explain: Free glutamate is what the tongue reads as umami, the savory taste. That is the whole point of MSG, and it is the same glutamate found in foods like parmesan.

steps.title: The scare started with one letter
steps.body: In 1968 a letter in a medical journal described a writer's own symptoms after Chinese meals and floated MSG as one possible cause. The press ran with it and 'Chinese restaurant syndrome' was born. Later controlled studies, where people did not know if they got MSG, failed to find a consistent reaction at normal amounts. The fear outlived the science.

steps.prompt: Where did the MSG scare begin?
options: A large clinical trial
A 1968 letter to a medical journal
A government warning
steps.explain: It began with a 1968 letter describing one person's symptoms and guessing at causes. Anecdote, not a trial. Blind studies later did not back it up at normal intakes.

steps.prompt: Earlier: glutamate, the key part of MSG, occurs naturally in which of these?
options: Tomatoes and parmesan
Only in lab-made additives
Only in red meat
steps.explain: Tomatoes and parmesan are rich in free glutamate. That is why they taste savory, and it is the same molecule as the seasoning.

steps.title: Same molecule, less fear.
recap: MSG is sodium plus glutamate, an amino acid found in everyday foods
Glutamate gives the savory umami taste
The scare grew from a 1968 letter, not from solid studies

subject: HEALTH & THE BODY
title: 'Natural' Doesn't Mean Safe
blurb: Cyanide and death cap mushrooms are natural too. The word is marketing.

steps.title: A word that sells
steps.sub: 'Natural' on a label feels reassuring. But nature makes plenty of things that can hurt you, and the word itself promises nothing.

steps.prompt: Which of these is natural?
options: Cyanide
Arsenic
Death cap mushrooms
All of them
steps.explain: All of them. Cyanide forms in some plant seeds, arsenic is an element in the earth's crust, and death cap mushrooms grow in the wild. Natural and safe are not the same thing.

steps.title: Nature makes poisons too
steps.body: Many of the deadliest substances known are natural. Cyanide occurs in apple seeds and cassava. Arsenic is a natural element found in soil and water. Plenty of wild mushrooms can kill you, and the death cap is among the most lethal. Being made by nature says nothing about whether something is good for you.

steps.prompt: What does it tell you that a substance is 'natural'?
options: That it is safe to consume
Nothing reliable about its safety
That it has no side effects
steps.explain: Nothing reliable. Cyanide and death caps are natural and still deadly. Safety depends on the substance and the dose, not on whether nature made it.

steps.title: On labels, the word is barely defined
steps.body: On most food packaging, 'natural' is a loose marketing term with little legal meaning. It does not mean organic, healthy, pesticide free, or even minimally processed. To judge a product, read the ingredient list and the nutrition facts. Those are defined and regulated. The single word on the front is not.

steps.prompt: How should you judge a product instead of trusting 'natural'?
options: Trust the front of the package
Read the ingredient list and nutrition facts
Buy whichever costs more
steps.explain: The ingredient list and nutrition facts are defined and regulated, so they actually tell you what is inside. The word 'natural' on the front is loosely defined marketing.

steps.prompt: Earlier: are death cap mushrooms natural?
options: Yes, and still deadly
No, they are synthetic
steps.explain: Yes. They grow in the wild and are among the most lethal mushrooms. Natural, and dangerous, which is the whole point.

steps.title: Read past the front label.
recap: Cyanide, arsenic, and death cap mushrooms are all natural
'Natural' is loose marketing, not a safety guarantee
Judge food by its ingredients and nutrition facts

subject: HEALTH & THE BODY
title: You Already Own a Detox System
blurb: Your liver and kidneys clean your blood all day. No juice speeds them up.

steps.title: The cleanse you don't need to buy
steps.sub: Juice cleanses promise to flush out toxins. Your body already does that job, around the clock, for free.

steps.prompt: Which organs do most of the work of clearing waste from your blood?
options: The stomach and lungs
The liver and kidneys
The skin and hair
steps.explain: The liver and kidneys. The liver breaks down many harmful substances, and the kidneys filter waste out of your blood into urine. They run constantly, with no cleanse required.

steps.title: Your liver and kidneys never clock off
steps.body: Your liver chemically breaks down drugs, alcohol, and other waste so they can be removed. Your kidneys filter your blood and send waste out as urine. Your lungs clear carbon dioxide, and your gut and skin help too. This system runs every second whether you are eating salad or pizza. It does not wait for a cleanse.

steps.prompt: When does your liver and kidney waste clearing happen?
options: Only after a juice cleanse
Constantly, all the time
Only when you sleep
steps.explain: Constantly. These organs work every second to break down and remove waste, no special diet required. That is their normal job.

steps.title: No drink makes them work faster
steps.body: There is no good evidence that juice cleanses or detox products speed up your organs or remove some special backlog of 'toxins'. Sellers rarely even name which toxin they mean. You cannot rev up a healthy liver with juice. The useful version is simpler: drink enough water, eat reasonably, and skip the things that strain these organs, like excess alcohol.

steps.prompt: What does a juice cleanse do for your detox organs?
options: Speeds them up and clears a toxin backlog
Nothing that is well supported by evidence
Replaces the need for the kidneys
steps.explain: There is no solid evidence a cleanse speeds these organs up or clears a special backlog. A healthy liver and kidneys already handle waste on their own.

steps.prompt: Trace a waste product from your blood to the exit.
items: Waste builds up in the blood
The liver breaks down many substances
The kidneys filter the blood
Waste leaves the body as urine

steps.prompt: Earlier: which pair of organs does most of your waste clearing?
options: Liver and kidneys
Stomach and lungs
steps.explain: Liver and kidneys. The liver breaks waste down, the kidneys filter it out. They already do the job a cleanse claims to sell you.

steps.title: The system is built in.
recap: The liver and kidneys clear waste from your blood constantly
No juice or cleanse speeds them up or clears a 'backlog'
Water, decent food, and less alcohol help more than any detox product

subject: HEALTH & THE BODY
title: Brown Bread Isn't Always Whole Grain
blurb: Color can come from molasses, not grain. Check that 'whole' comes first.

steps.title: Brown is a color, not a promise
steps.sub: A loaf can look hearty and brown and still be mostly white flour. The shade tells you less than you think.

steps.prompt: What makes some 'wheat' bread brown if it isn't whole grain?
options: It must contain whole grain to be that color
Added molasses or caramel coloring
Longer baking time only
steps.explain: Color can be added. Molasses or caramel coloring can darken a loaf made largely from white flour, so brown does not prove whole grain.

steps.title: Whole grain keeps the whole kernel
steps.body: A grain kernel has three parts: the bran, the germ, and the starchy endosperm. White flour keeps only the endosperm and throws out the bran and germ, which hold most of the fiber and nutrients. Whole grain flour keeps all three. That is the real difference, and it is not something you can judge by color.

steps.prompt: What does whole grain flour keep that white flour removes?
options: The bran and germ
Only the endosperm
Added coloring
steps.explain: Whole grain keeps the bran and germ, where most of the fiber and nutrients sit. White flour strips them out and leaves the starchy endosperm.

steps.title: Read the first ingredient
steps.body: Words like 'wheat', 'multigrain', and 'made with whole grain' can appear on bread that is mostly white flour. The reliable check is the ingredient list. Look for the word 'whole', as in 'whole wheat flour', and look for it first. Ingredients are listed by weight, so the first one is the main one.

steps.prompt: How can you tell if bread is mostly whole grain?
options: It says 'wheat' on the front
'Whole' grain is the first ingredient listed
It is dark brown in color
steps.explain: Ingredients are listed by weight, so the first one is the main one. If a 'whole' grain leads the list, that is what the loaf is mostly made of.

steps.prompt: Earlier: why might a loaf be brown without being whole grain?
options: Added molasses or caramel coloring
It is impossible to be brown without whole grain
steps.explain: Color can be added with molasses or caramel coloring. So brown alone does not prove whole grain, which is why you check the ingredient list.

steps.title: Trust the list, not the color.
recap: Brown color can come from molasses or caramel coloring, not grain
Whole grain keeps the bran and germ; white flour removes them
Check that 'whole' grain is the first ingredient

subject: HEALTH & THE BODY
title: Sugar Doesn't Make Kids Hyper
blurb: Controlled studies find no link. The party does the rest.

steps.title: A belief almost everyone holds
steps.sub: Give a kid cake and watch them bounce off the walls. It feels obvious. The careful studies do not back it up.

steps.prompt: What do controlled studies generally find about sugar and children's behavior?
options: Sugar reliably makes kids hyperactive
No consistent effect on behavior
Sugar makes kids calmer
steps.explain: No consistent effect. When sugar intake is controlled and people do not know who got it, the expected burst of hyperactivity does not reliably show up.

steps.title: The studies don't show a sugar high
steps.body: Researchers have tested this many times. They give some children sugar and others a sugar free substitute, without telling anyone which is which. Across these blinded trials, children's behavior does not reliably differ. Reviews of this research reach the same conclusion: ordinary sugar does not cause hyperactivity in children, even in those said to be sensitive to it.

steps.prompt: What happens to behavior in blinded sugar studies?
options: It reliably gets more hyperactive
It does not reliably differ
It cannot be measured
steps.explain: Behavior does not reliably differ between the sugar and sugar free groups. That is the core finding once nobody knows who got the sugar.

steps.title: Expectation does the heavy lifting
steps.body: So why does the belief survive? Expectation. In one kind of study, parents were told their child had been given sugar when the child actually had none. Those parents still rated their child as more hyperactive. We tend to see what we expect. Add a loud party full of excited kids, and the chaos has plenty of causes that are not sugar.

steps.prompt: Why do parents still believe sugar makes kids hyper?
options: The studies were never done
Expectation makes them see hyperactivity they expect
Sugar works only on weekends
steps.explain: Expectation. Parents told their child had sugar rated them as more hyperactive even when there was none. We notice behavior that fits what we already believe.

steps.prompt: Earlier: in blinded studies, does sugar reliably change children's behavior?
options: No, it does not
Yes, it reliably causes hyperactivity
steps.explain: No. Once the test is blinded, behavior does not reliably differ. The belief comes from expectation and noisy settings, not from the sugar.

steps.title: Blame the party, not the cake.
recap: Controlled, blinded studies find no reliable sugar to hyperactivity link
Parents told their kids had sugar rated them as more hyper anyway
Expectation and exciting settings explain the apparent effect

subject: HEALTH & THE BODY
title: Fat-Free Often Means Sugar-Loaded
blurb: Strip out the fat and the food tastes like cardboard. So they pour in sugar to fix it.

steps.title: The swap nobody mentions
steps.sub: A label says fat-free, so it sounds like the healthier pick. But taking fat out creates a problem, and the fix often makes the food no better, sometimes worse.

steps.prompt: Strip the fat out of a yogurt. What happens to the taste?
options: It tastes the same
It tastes worse, because fat carries flavor and texture
It tastes sweeter on its own
steps.explain: It tastes worse. Fat carries flavor and gives food a rich, smooth feel in your mouth. Remove it and the food goes flat and thin, which is the whole reason the recipe gets changed.

steps.title: Fat is the flavor
steps.body: Fat does a lot of quiet work. It carries flavor compounds, coats your tongue, and gives food a creamy, satisfying texture. Take it out and the product tastes dull and watery. A maker selling fat-free food still needs it to taste good, so they have to put something back in to rescue it.

steps.prompt: Why does removing fat hurt the taste of a food?
options: Fat carries flavor and gives food its texture
Fat is the only sweet ingredient
Fat keeps the food cold
steps.explain: Fat carries flavor and creates that rich, smooth mouthfeel. Without it the food tastes flat, which forces the maker to add something to bring the appeal back.

steps.title: Sugar fills the gap
steps.body: The cheap fix is sugar. It makes the dull food taste good again, so fat-free products are often loaded with it. Here is the catch. Fat has about 9 calories per gram and sugar about 4, but they pile in so much sugar that the calorie count can land near the original, sometimes higher. Fat-free does not mean calorie-free, and it does not mean healthier.

steps.prompt: How can a fat-free product end up with as many calories as the full-fat one?
options: Fat has no calories to remove
So much sugar is added back that the calories climb again
The package is always bigger
steps.explain: Cutting fat removes calories, but the sugar poured in to fix the taste adds them right back. Enough of it can match or beat the original count, so the fat-free label tells you little about calories.

steps.prompt: Order how a food becomes fat-free but not better:
items: Maker removes the fat
The food now tastes flat and dull
Sugar is added to fix the taste
Calories climb back up

steps.prompt: Earlier: why does taking fat out make food taste worse?
options: Fat carries flavor and texture
Fat is the cheapest ingredient
Fat blocks the sugar
steps.explain: Fat carries flavor and gives food its rich texture. Lose it and the food falls flat, which is exactly why sugar gets added back in.

steps.title: Read the label, not the front
recap: Fat carries flavor and texture, so removing it makes food taste flat.
Sugar is the cheap fix, and fat-free products are often loaded with it.
Fat-free can match or beat the original calories, so compare the labels.

subject: HEALTH & THE BODY
title: Most 'Expiration' Dates Are Guesses
blurb: 'Best by' is about peak flavor, not safety. The food does not turn to poison at midnight.

steps.title: Midnight does nothing
steps.sub: You find something a day past its date and toss it. But that date was never a safety alarm. It was a guess about flavor, and treating it as a deadline wastes a stunning amount of food.

steps.prompt: What does a 'best by' date on a package mostly tell you?
