steps.explain: Current. Voltage only sets it up by providing the push. It is the current flowing through your chest that does the harm. Volts push, amps kill.

steps.title: That's the real danger.
recap: Voltage is the push, current is the flow.
Current through the chest is what stops the heart.
Higher voltage and wet skin both raise the current through you.

subject: HOW THINGS WORK
title: Your Septic Tank Is Alive
blurb: It's not a holding tank. It's a colony of bacteria digesting your waste.

steps.title: Your Septic Tank Is Alive
steps.sub: A septic tank is not a bucket that fills up and waits. It is a living system, and bacteria do most of the work.

steps.prompt: If a septic tank were just a sealed container, what would happen to it over time?
options: It would fill completely and need pumping every few weeks
It would stay empty on its own
It would never need any maintenance
steps.explain: It would fill fast and constantly overflow. It does not, because the tank is not just storing waste. Bacteria inside are breaking much of it down.

steps.title: The tank settles into layers
steps.body: Wastewater flows into the tank and sits still. Heavy solids sink to the bottom and form a sludge layer. Grease and light solids float to the top as a scum layer. The fairly clear liquid in the middle is what flows out to the drain field. The tank's calm gives everything time to separate.

steps.prompt: What flows out of the tank toward the drain field?
options: The heavy sludge from the bottom
The clearer liquid from the middle
The floating scum from the top
steps.explain: The middle liquid. Solids stay trapped as sludge and scum, so only the clarified liquid moves on. That separation protects the drain field from clogging.

steps.title: Bacteria do the digesting
steps.body: The bottom sludge is full of bacteria that live without oxygen. They digest the solids and break much of the waste down, which is why the sludge builds up slowly instead of filling the tank. They cannot eat everything, so leftover solids still accumulate over years. That is why a tank must be pumped out periodically, not because it simply filled, but because the bacteria leave a residue behind.

steps.prompt: Why does the sludge build up slowly instead of quickly filling the tank?
options: The tank leaks the solids out
Bacteria digest and break down much of the waste
The solids evaporate
steps.explain: Bacteria digest it. They live in the sludge and break the waste down, so only a slow residue is left. Without them the tank would fill far faster.

steps.prompt: Trace one flush through a septic system, in order.
items: Wastewater flows into the tank
Solids settle, grease floats
Bacteria digest the bottom sludge
Clarified liquid exits to the drain field
Tank is pumped out years later

glosses.Bacteria digest the bottom sludge: Microbes break much of it down.
glosses.Clarified liquid exits to the drain fiel: Middle liquid drains into the soil.

steps.prompt: Someone pours strong bleach and drain cleaner down the toilet for weeks. What's the likely harm to the septic system?
options: It kills off the bacteria that digest the waste
It makes the tank fill more slowly
It has no effect on the tank
steps.explain: It kills the bacteria. They are what break the waste down, so wiping them out lets solids build faster and the system works worse. The tank is alive, and it can be poisoned.

steps.title: That's a septic system.
recap: Waste separates into sludge, liquid, and scum.
Bacteria digest much of the bottom sludge.
Leftover residue is why the tank still needs pumping.

subject: HOW THINGS WORK
title: The Best Part of a Window Is the Gap
blurb: A double-glazed window insulates with the trapped gas, not the glass.

steps.title: The best part of a window is the gap
steps.sub: A double-glazed window has two panes with a sealed space between them. That empty-looking space is where the insulation actually happens.

steps.prompt: What stops most of the heat from escaping through a double-glazed window?
options: The thickness of the glass itself
The sealed layer of gas trapped between the panes
A special coating that reflects all heat back
steps.explain: The trapped gas. Glass is a fairly poor insulator on its own. The real barrier is the still layer of gas sealed between the two panes.

steps.title: Still gas is a poor heat conductor
steps.body: Heat moves easily through solids like glass. It moves much more slowly through a still pocket of gas. A double-glazed unit traps a thin layer of dry air, or often argon, in a sealed gap between two panes. That trapped layer is the part that resists heat flow. The glass mostly just holds the gas in place.

steps.prompt: Why does the sealed gap insulate better than the glass?
options: Gas conducts heat much more slowly than the solid glass does
Gas is colder than glass
The gap is a vacuum with nothing in it
steps.explain: Gas conducts heat slowly. A still layer of gas passes heat far less readily than solid glass, so the gap, not the panes, does the insulating.

steps.title: The gas has to stay still
steps.body: The gap only works if the gas barely moves. If the space is too wide, the gas circulates: warm gas rises on the warm side, sinks on the cold side, and carries heat across. That is convection. So the gap is kept narrow, and the unit is sealed so moist outside air cannot get in and fog it up.

steps.prompt: Why isn't a much wider gap automatically better?
options: Wider glass is too heavy to hang
A wide gap lets the gas circulate and carry heat across by convection
Wider gaps let in more light and more heat with it
steps.explain: Convection. In a wide gap the gas starts looping, warm side to cold side, moving heat the seal was meant to block. A narrow gap keeps it still.

steps.prompt: A double-glazed unit fails and the seal leaks. Cold weather, and it fogs up inside the gap. Why is it now a worse insulator?
options: The glass got thinner
Moist air leaked into the gap, so it no longer holds the dry, still gas layer
The fog blocks the sunlight that used to warm it
steps.explain: The seal is the whole point. Once outside air leaks in, the carefully trapped dry gas layer is gone, and the gap can no longer do its job.

steps.title: It's the gap doing the work.
recap: Still gas conducts heat far more slowly than glass
Double glazing seals a thin gas layer between two panes
Keep the gap narrow and sealed so the gas stays still

subject: HOW THINGS WORK
title: What Happens Inside an Engine Every Second
blurb: Suck, squeeze, bang, blow: four strokes turn fire into motion.

steps.title: What happens inside an engine every second
steps.sub: A petrol engine runs on a four-step cycle that repeats thousands of times a minute. The old shorthand is suck, squeeze, bang, blow.

steps.prompt: In the cylinder, what actually pushes the piston down to make power?
options: The spark plug shoving it
Burning fuel and air expanding hard against the piston
The crankshaft pulling it from below
steps.explain: The expanding burn. When the fuel-air mix ignites, the hot gases expand fast and slam the piston down. The spark only lights it; the expansion does the pushing.

steps.title: The piston goes up and down
steps.body: Inside each cylinder a piston slides up and down. One full move in either direction is a stroke. The piston is linked to the crankshaft, which turns that straight up-and-down motion into the rotation that eventually spins the wheels. A four-stroke engine needs four of these strokes to complete one power cycle.

steps.title: The four strokes
steps.body: Intake (suck): the piston drops and pulls in a mix of air and fuel. Compression (squeeze): the piston rises and squashes that mix into a small, dense charge. Power (bang): the spark plug fires, the mix burns and expands, and the blast drives the piston down. Exhaust (blow): the piston rises again and pushes the burnt gases out. Then it starts over.

steps.prompt: Which stroke is the one that actually produces power?
options: Intake, when fresh fuel comes in
Compression, when the mix is squeezed
Power, when the burning mix expands and pushes the piston
steps.explain: The power stroke. The other three set it up or clean up after. Only the power stroke turns burning fuel into a downward push on the piston.

steps.prompt: Put the four strokes in the order they happen.
items: Intake
Compression
Power
Exhaust

glosses.Intake: Piston drops, pulls in air and fuel
glosses.Power: Spark fires, the burn drives the piston down
glosses.Exhaust: Piston rises, pushes burnt gas out

steps.prompt: Squeezing the mix before the spark, the compression stroke, helps the engine. Why bother compressing it first?
options: A dense, squeezed charge burns harder and gives a stronger push
It cools the fuel down before it burns
It is only there to make the engine louder
steps.explain: Compression packs the charge tight, so when it burns it releases more force into the same space. A squeezed mix gives a much stronger power stroke than a loose one.

steps.title: That's the four-stroke cycle.
recap: Four strokes: intake, compression, power, exhaust
Only the power stroke makes force; the rest set it up
The cycle repeats thousands of times a minute

subject: HOW THINGS WORK
title: Diesel Engines Have No Spark Plugs
blurb: A diesel lights its fuel with pure compression heat, no spark.

steps.title: Diesel engines have no spark plugs
steps.sub: Petrol engines use a spark to light the fuel. Diesel engines have no spark plugs at all. They use heat instead.

steps.prompt: If a diesel engine has no spark plug, what sets the fuel alight?
options: A hidden electric coil
Air squeezed so hard it gets hot enough to ignite the fuel
Friction from the moving parts
steps.explain: Compression heat. Squeeze a gas hard enough and it gets very hot. A diesel compresses air until it is hot enough to ignite fuel on contact, no spark needed.

steps.title: Squeezing air heats it up
steps.body: Compress a gas and its temperature rises. A diesel takes this to an extreme. It pulls in plain air, with no fuel yet, and squeezes it far harder than a petrol engine does. By the end of that squeeze the air is hundreds of degrees hot, hot enough to set fuel alight by itself.

steps.prompt: Why does the air inside a diesel cylinder get so hot?
options: A spark plug heats it
It is compressed very hard, and squeezing a gas raises its temperature
The fuel is already burning as it comes in
steps.explain: The squeeze. Compressing a gas raises its temperature, and a diesel compresses air enough to make it hot enough to ignite fuel without a spark.

steps.title: Fuel goes in last
steps.body: Because the air ignites the fuel on contact, timing matters. A diesel squeezes the air first, then sprays fuel into that superheated air at the right moment. The fuel lights instantly. A petrol engine instead mixes fuel and air first, then triggers it with a spark when it chooses.

steps.prompt: In a diesel, when does the fuel enter the cylinder?
options: Mixed with the air before compression
Sprayed in after the air is compressed and hot
Only during the exhaust stroke
steps.explain: After compression. The air is squeezed and heated first, then fuel is sprayed into it, so it lights on contact instead of waiting for a spark.

steps.prompt: Same logic in reverse: a bicycle pump gets warm near the nozzle as you pump hard. Why?
options: You are compressing the air, and squeezing a gas heats it
The rubber hose is rubbing against itself
Cold air from outside is leaking in
steps.explain: Compression again. Forcing air into a small space heats it, the same effect a diesel pushes to the extreme to ignite its fuel.

steps.title: No spark required.
recap: Diesels have no spark plugs
They squeeze air until it is hot enough to ignite fuel
Fuel is sprayed in after the air is compressed and hot

subject: HOW THINGS WORK
title: Gears Don't Make Power, They Trade It
blurb: Low gear buys you force, high gear buys you speed. Never both.

steps.title: Gears don't make power, they trade it
steps.sub: A gearbox can give you turning force or speed at the wheel, but not both at once. It trades one for the other.

steps.prompt: You shift a bike into low gear to climb a steep hill. What does low gear give you?
options: More speed for the same effort
More turning force, but the wheel turns slower
Both more force and more speed at once
steps.explain: More force, less speed. Low gear multiplies your turning force so the climb feels easier, but the wheel turns slowly for each pedal stroke. You cannot get both.

steps.title: Force and speed pull against each other
steps.body: A gear pairs a small wheel with a large one. Drive the large gear from the small one and the output turns slower but with more turning force. Drive the small from the large and it turns faster but with less force. You are trading force for speed or speed for force. The engine's power stays the same; gears only split it differently.

steps.prompt: A gearbox gives you more turning force at the wheel. What do you give up?
options: Nothing, force is free
Speed: the wheel turns slower
Fuel: it burns twice as much
steps.explain: You give up speed. More force per turn means fewer turns for the same effort. Gears trade one for the other; they do not create extra power.

steps.title: Why cars need several gears
steps.body: An engine only makes useful power across a limited range of speeds. Low gears give lots of force to get a heavy car moving from a stop. High gears give speed once it is rolling, so the engine can stay in its good range while the wheels spin fast. You shift up as you speed up to keep trading force you no longer need for speed you now want.

steps.prompt: Pulling away from a stop and getting up to speed, which gears does a car use, in order?
items: First gear
Second gear
Third gear
Top gear

glosses.First gear: Most force, to start the car moving
glosses.Second gear: A bit more speed, a bit less force
glosses.Third gear: Picking up pace as force needs drop
glosses.Top gear: Most speed, least force, for cruising

steps.prompt: A cyclist on the flat shifts into a high gear. What are they trading away to gain speed?
options: Turning force: each pedal stroke pushes with less force
Nothing, high gear is simply better everywhere
Their balance
steps.explain: Turning force. High gear spins the wheel fast per stroke but with less force, which is why it feels brutal on a hill. On the flat that trade is worth it.

steps.title: Gears trade, they don't create.
recap: Low gear: more force, less speed
High gear: more speed, less force
Engine power is fixed; gears just divide it

subject: HOW THINGS WORK
title: A Turbo Runs on Exhaust You'd Otherwise Waste
blurb: A turbo recycles exhaust to cram in air. A supercharger steals engine power to do it.

steps.title: A turbo runs on exhaust you'd otherwise waste
steps.sub: Turbochargers and superchargers both force extra air into an engine for more power. The difference is where they get the energy to do it.

steps.prompt: Why does forcing more air into an engine make it more powerful?
options: More air alone is what burns
More air lets you burn more fuel, and more fuel burned means more power
The extra air cools the engine so it can rev higher
steps.explain: More air, more fuel. An engine makes power by burning fuel, and fuel needs air to burn. Pack in more air and you can burn more fuel each cycle, so each power stroke hits harder.

steps.title: Both pumps cram in extra air
steps.body: A normal engine just sucks in air as the piston drops. A turbocharger and a supercharger both do the same job: they use a spinning compressor to force more air into the cylinders than the engine could pull in on its own. More air packed in means more fuel can burn, which means more power from the same size engine.

steps.title: Where they get their energy
steps.body: A supercharger is driven by the engine itself, usually by a belt off the crankshaft. So it steals some of the engine's own power to spin its compressor. A turbocharger instead puts the hot exhaust gases to work. Those gases would normally just blow out the pipe. The turbo lets them spin a turbine on the way out, and that turbine drives the compressor for free.

steps.prompt: What spins the compressor in a turbocharger?
options: A belt connected to the engine
The exhaust gases leaving the engine
A small electric battery
steps.explain: The exhaust. A turbo captures the energy in gases that would otherwise be wasted out the pipe, using them to spin a turbine that drives the compressor.

steps.prompt: What is the cost of running a supercharger?
options: It draws some of the engine's own power to drive it
It uses up the exhaust gases
It has no cost at all
steps.explain: It taps the engine. A supercharger is belt-driven off the engine, so some of the power it helps create is spent driving the supercharger itself.

steps.prompt: Both devices push more air in. Remind yourself why that gives more power.
options: More air means more fuel can be burned each cycle
Air is the fuel
The compressor itself adds power directly
steps.explain: More air, more fuel, more power. The air is what lets the extra fuel burn. The compressor only supplies air; the burning fuel does the work.

steps.title: Same goal, different fuel source.
recap: Both force extra air in so more fuel can burn
A turbo is spun by waste exhaust gases
A supercharger is driven by the engine's own power

subject: HOW THINGS WORK
title: Most of Your Fuel Becomes Heat, Not Motion
blurb: An engine is mostly a heater that happens to turn the wheels.

steps.title: Most of your fuel becomes heat
steps.sub: You burn gas to move. But most of the energy never reaches the wheels. It leaves as heat, and the engine has to get rid of it fast.

steps.prompt: Of the energy in a tank of gas, roughly how much actually moves the car?
options: Most of it, around 80 percent
About half
Only a quarter or less
steps.explain: Only a quarter or less. A typical gasoline car engine turns roughly 20 to 35 percent of the fuel's energy into useful motion. The rest is mostly waste heat.

steps.title: Burning fuel makes heat first
steps.body: An engine works by burning fuel inside its cylinders. That burn is a controlled explosion that gets very hot. The hot gas expands and shoves a piston, and that push is the only part that becomes motion. Everything the explosion does that does not push the piston stays behind as heat in the metal and the exhaust.

steps.prompt: Where does the motion in an engine actually come from?
options: Hot expanding gas pushing a piston
The fuel spinning as it burns
Magnets pulling the crankshaft
steps.explain: The expanding gas. Burning fuel heats the gas, the gas expands, and that expansion pushes the piston. Only that push turns into motion.

steps.title: The leftover heat has to go somewhere
steps.body: All the heat that does not become motion would melt the engine if it stayed put. So coolant circulates through the engine, soaks up the heat, and carries it to the radiator, where airflow dumps it outside. The exhaust pipe carries away more heat in the hot gas. Without this constant dumping, the engine cooks itself.

steps.prompt: Why does an engine need coolant at all?
options: To lubricate the moving parts
Because most of the fuel's energy becomes heat that must be carried away
To add power to the explosion
steps.explain: Because most of the fuel becomes heat. Only a fraction becomes motion, so the engine must constantly carry the leftover heat away or it overheats.

steps.title: An engine is mostly a heater.
recap: Only about a quarter of fuel energy becomes motion
The rest leaves as waste heat
Coolant and exhaust dump that heat so the engine survives

subject: HOW THINGS WORK
title: Electric Motors Have Almost No Moving Parts
blurb: An engine is hundreds of parts in motion. A motor is basically one.

steps.title: A motor is almost all stillness
steps.sub: A gasoline engine has hundreds of parts moving in sequence. An electric motor does the same job with one spinning piece. Here is why.

steps.prompt: What spins the rotor in an electric motor?
options: Tiny explosions, like an engine
Magnetic force
Compressed air
steps.explain: Magnetic force. Electricity creates magnetic fields that push and pull the rotor around. No fuel, no fire, no pistons.

steps.title: An engine moves in steps
steps.body: A piston engine makes motion in a chain of events. Valves open, fuel and air come in, a piston squeezes them, a spark fires, the gas explodes and shoves the piston back, then the exhaust leaves. That cycle repeats many times a second across several cylinders, with valves, rods, and a crankshaft all moving in time. Lots of parts, all in motion.

steps.prompt: Why does a piston engine need so many moving parts?
options: It makes motion in a repeating chain of timed steps
Extra parts make it more powerful
The parts share the heat
steps.explain: The timed chain. Intake, compression, spark, push, and exhaust each need their own moving parts working in sequence.

steps.title: A motor just spins
steps.body: An electric motor skips the whole chain. A still outer part and a spinning inner part interact through magnetism. Send current through the coils and the magnetic fields push the rotor around continuously. The main moving part is the rotor and its shaft. Fewer parts means less to wear out, less to lubricate, and less to break.

steps.prompt: Which machine relies on a repeating chain of timed steps to make motion?
options: The electric motor
The piston engine
Neither, both spin freely
steps.explain: The piston engine. It cycles through intake, compression, spark, push, and exhaust. The motor just spins continuously from magnetic force.

steps.title: One spinning piece, pushed by magnetism.
recap: Engines make motion in a timed chain of many moving parts
Motors spin a rotor continuously using magnetism
Fewer moving parts means less to wear out

subject: HOW THINGS WORK
title: Horsepower and Torque Aren't the Same Thing
blurb: Torque is the twist. Horsepower is how fast that twist arrives.

steps.title: Twist versus rate
steps.sub: People use horsepower and torque as if they mean the same thing. They do not. One is force, the other is force over time.

steps.prompt: Which one most directly gets a heavy load moving from a standstill?
options: Torque
Horsepower
Neither, only weight matters
steps.explain: Torque. It is the twisting force at the wheels. More twist means more shove to break a heavy load loose and start it rolling.

steps.title: Torque is twisting force
steps.body: Torque is how hard the engine twists the shaft. Think of pushing on a long wrench: the harder you push, the more torque you apply, and the more stubborn a bolt you can turn. In a car, torque is the raw shove that gets you off the line and pulls you up a hill. It is a force, measured at one instant.

steps.prompt: What does torque describe?
options: How fast the engine delivers power
The twisting force the engine applies
How much fuel the engine burns
steps.explain: The twisting force. Torque is the raw shove, like the force on a wrench. It does not by itself say how fast that force is delivered.

steps.title: Horsepower adds time
steps.body: Horsepower is torque combined with how fast the shaft is spinning. The same twist applied faster, again and again each second, is more power. That is why horsepower rises with engine speed. Torque tells you how hard you can push; horsepower tells you how much work you can do in a given time, which sets top speed.

steps.prompt: What does horsepower add on top of torque?
options: How fast the twist is delivered
Extra fuel
More weight
steps.explain: The rate. Horsepower combines the twisting force with how fast the shaft spins, so it measures work done over time, not just raw force.

steps.prompt: A truck needs to pull a heavy trailer up a steep grade from a stop. Which matters most to get it moving?
options: Torque, the twisting force
Horsepower, the rate of work
Neither
steps.explain: Torque. Starting a heavy load against gravity needs raw twisting force. Horsepower matters more for how fast it can keep going once rolling.

steps.title: Force versus force over time.
recap: Torque is the twisting force, the raw shove
Horsepower is torque delivered fast, work over time
Torque starts heavy loads; horsepower sets how fast you go

subject: HOW THINGS WORK
title: A Jet Engine Pushes Air Backward to Go Forward
blurb: Jets don't push against the sky. They fling air out the back.

steps.title: Throw mass backward, move forward
steps.sub: It feels like a jet shoves against the air around it. It does not. It throws air out the back and rides the reaction.

steps.prompt: How does a jet engine push the plane forward?
options: It pushes against the surrounding air like an oar
It throws air and exhaust out the back
It pulls itself forward on the air ahead
steps.explain: It throws air out the back. The engine flings a fast stream of air and exhaust rearward, and the reaction to that throw pushes the plane forward.

steps.title: Every push has an equal push back
steps.body: Newton's third law says forces come in pairs. Push on something and it pushes back on you just as hard, in the opposite direction. When the engine hurls a stream of air backward, that air pushes the engine forward by exactly the same force. The plane moves not by leaning on the surrounding air, but by throwing mass the other way.

steps.prompt: Why does throwing air backward move the plane forward?
options: The air ahead gets thinner
Every push produces an equal push in the opposite direction
Hot air always rises
steps.explain: Equal and opposite. By Newton's third law, the force the engine puts on the air backward is matched by a forward force on the engine.

steps.title: How the engine throws the air
steps.body: A jet engine sucks in air at the front, squeezes it with spinning compressor blades, mixes in fuel and burns it, and the hot gas blasts out the back much faster than it came in. Speeding that air up is the throw. The bigger the difference between intake speed and exhaust speed, and the more air moved, the more thrust you get.

steps.prompt: Put the stages of a jet engine in order, front to back.
items: Intake
Compress
Burn
Exhaust

glosses.Exhaust: Hot gas blasts out the back

steps.prompt: A jet works in a vacuum-like high climb. Why does it still work even with little air to push against outside?
options: It pushes harder on the thin outside air
It moves forward by throwing its own exhaust stream backward
It cannot work without dense outside air
steps.explain: It throws mass backward. The forward force comes from accelerating air and exhaust rearward, not from leaning on the air around the plane.

steps.title: Forward by throwing backward.
recap: A jet does not push against the surrounding air
It accelerates air out the back and rides the reaction
Newton's third law turns the backward throw into forward thrust

subject: HOW THINGS WORK
title: Brakes Turn Your Speed Into Heat
blurb: Stopping doesn't erase your motion. It converts it into heat.

steps.title: Where your speed goes
steps.sub: When you brake, your motion does not just vanish. Energy never disappears. The brakes turn your speed into heat.

steps.prompt: When a car brakes to a stop, where does its motion energy go?
options: It disappears
It turns into heat in the brakes
It goes back into the fuel tank
steps.explain: Into heat. Energy cannot vanish. The brakes convert the car's motion energy into heat through friction.

steps.title: Friction is the converter
steps.body: Most brakes press a pad against a spinning disc attached to the wheel. The pad rubs hard on the disc, and that friction fights the spinning. Rubbing two surfaces together makes heat, the same way your hands warm up when you rub them. The harder and longer the rub, the more of the car's motion becomes heat, and the more it slows.

steps.prompt: What does the friction between pad and disc do to the car's motion energy?
options: Stores it in the pad
Converts it into heat
Sends it to the engine
steps.explain: Converts it to heat. The rubbing turns the energy of motion into heat in the disc and pad, which is how the car loses speed.

steps.title: Why brakes can overheat
steps.body: All that heat has to go somewhere, and it builds up in the discs. On a long downhill, braking again and again can heat the discs so much that the brakes fade and grip less. That is why heavy trucks use lower gears on descents, letting the engine help slow them so the brakes do not cook.

steps.prompt: Why might brakes fade after a long steep descent?
options: They run out of motion to absorb
The discs get too hot from converting so much speed into heat
The pads turn to liquid
steps.explain: Too much heat. Braking converts speed into heat, and repeated braking can overheat the discs until they grip less well.

steps.title: Speed in, heat out.
recap: Braking does not erase motion, it converts it
Friction turns motion energy into heat in the discs
Too much heat makes brakes fade

subject: HOW THINGS WORK
title: Why Lawnmowers and Chainsaws Smell So Bad
blurb: Two-stroke engines burn oil with the fuel on purpose.

steps.title: Why Lawnmowers and Chainsaws Smell So Bad
steps.sub: That blue haze and harsh smell are not a malfunction. They are how a two-stroke engine is built to run. In three minutes you'll see why.

steps.prompt: Your car has a separate tank of oil and never asks you to mix oil into the gas. Why might a chainsaw need oil mixed right into its fuel?
options: It has no separate way to oil its moving parts
Oil makes the fuel burn cooler
It is just to make more smoke
steps.explain: It has no separate oiling system. A simple two-stroke engine uses the fuel itself to carry lubricating oil to the moving parts, so the oil has to ride along in the gas.

steps.title: Two strokes, not four
steps.body: A car engine takes four strokes of the piston to make one power kick: intake, compression, power, exhaust. A two-stroke does the same job in two strokes, so it fires on every single turn of the crank. That is why it is light and revs hard for its size. The catch is it has no room for the separate valves and oil system a four-stroke uses.

steps.prompt: How many strokes does a two-stroke engine use to make one power kick?
options: Four
Two
One
steps.explain: Two. It fires on every crank turn instead of every other one, which packs more power into a small, light engine.

steps.title: The oil rides in the fuel
steps.body: Because there is no separate oil reservoir, you mix oil straight into the gas. The fuel and oil flow through the crankcase together, coating the bearings and piston as they pass. Then the mix gets burned in the cylinder. Burning oil is messy: it does not combust cleanly, so it throws out blue smoke and that sharp, oily smell.

steps.prompt: Where does the blue smoke and bad smell come from?
options: Oil being burned along with the fuel
The engine running too cold
Water in the gas tank
steps.explain: The oil. It is mixed into the fuel to lubricate the engine, then burned with it. Oil burns dirty, so you get blue smoke and a harsh smell.

steps.prompt: Why does a two-stroke need the oil mixed into its gas in the first place?
options: It has no separate oiling system, so the fuel carries the oil
The oil cools the spark plug
It runs better on dirty fuel
steps.explain: No separate oiling system. The simple two-stroke design has no oil reservoir, so the oil has to travel with the fuel to reach the moving parts.

steps.title: That's the two-stroke.
recap: Two strokes mean a power kick every crank turn
No separate oil system, so oil rides in the fuel
Burning that oil makes the smoke and smell

subject: HOW THINGS WORK
title: The Steam Engine Ran the Whole Industrial Revolution
blurb: Boiling water expands enormously, and that push built the modern world.

steps.title: The Steam Engine Ran the Whole Industrial Revolution
steps.sub: Water boils, it expands, and that simple fact moved factories, trains, and ships. In four minutes you'll see how a kettle became an engine.

steps.prompt: Boil a sealed pot of water and the steam has nowhere to go. What does steam want to do compared to the water it came from?
options: Take up much more space
Take up the same space
Shrink down smaller
steps.explain: It wants far more room. Water turning to steam expands hugely, on the order of 1,600 times its liquid volume. Trap that and it pushes hard on whatever holds it in.

steps.title: Steam pushes
steps.body: When water boils into steam, it expands roughly 1,600 times in volume. If the steam is trapped, all that expansion turns into pressure pushing outward. A steam engine aims that pressure at a piston in a cylinder. The steam shoves the piston, the piston moves, and you have turned heat into motion.

steps.prompt: What does a steam engine push with that trapped, expanding steam?
options: A piston in a cylinder
A spinning fan
A magnet
steps.explain: A piston. The steam's pressure shoves the piston down the cylinder, which is how the engine converts heat into mechanical motion.

steps.title: Back and forth into round and round
steps.body: A piston only goes back and forth. To do useful work like turning a wheel, that motion gets linked to a crank, which converts the straight push into rotation. Now the engine can spin a shaft. James Watt's improvements in the late 1700s made these engines efficient enough to power factories far from rivers, not just pump water out of mines.

steps.prompt: How does the piston's back-and-forth motion turn a wheel?
options: A crank converts the straight push into rotation
The steam spins the wheel directly
Gravity pulls the wheel around
steps.explain: A crank. It links the piston's straight motion to a rotating shaft, turning a simple back-and-forth into round-and-round.

steps.prompt: Put the steam engine's chain of events in order.
items: Heat boils water into steam
Steam expands and builds pressure
Pressure pushes the piston
A crank turns the push into rotation

glosses.Heat boils water into steam: Fire or fuel heats the boiler
glosses.Steam expands and builds pressure: Trapped steam wants far more room
glosses.Pressure pushes the piston: The shove moves the piston in the cylinder
glosses.A crank turns the push into rotation: Straight motion becomes a spinning shaft

steps.prompt: Roughly how much more space does water take up after it turns to steam?
options: About 1,600 times more
About 10 times more
About the same
steps.explain: About 1,600 times. That huge expansion is the source of the pressure that does all the work. Trap it, aim it at a piston, and you have an engine.

steps.title: That's the steam engine.
recap: Steam expands about 1,600 times and that builds pressure
Pressure pushes a piston, turning heat into motion
A crank turns the push into a spinning shaft

subject: HOW THINGS WORK
title: How a Small Pedal Lifts a Whole Car
blurb: Trapped liquid multiplies a little push into a big one.

steps.title: How a Small Pedal Lifts a Whole Car
steps.sub: A light press on a brake pedal stops two tons of car. A small jack lifts it off the ground. The trick is trapped liquid. Here's how it multiplies force.

steps.prompt: You squeeze a sealed bag of water. The water has nowhere to escape. What happens to the pressure inside?
options: It pushes equally on every part of the bag
It pushes only where you squeezed
It disappears
steps.explain: It pushes everywhere equally. Pressure in a trapped liquid spreads to every wall at once. That even spread is the whole secret behind hydraulics.

steps.title: Liquids barely squash
steps.body: Push on a gas and it compresses, soaking up your effort. Liquids barely squash at all. So when you push on a trapped liquid, the push passes straight through it rather than getting absorbed. Press on one end of a fluid-filled pipe and the other end feels it almost instantly. The liquid acts like a solid rod that can bend around corners.

steps.prompt: Why does a push pass through a trapped liquid instead of being absorbed?
options: Liquids barely compress
Liquids are very cold
Liquids are heavier than gas
steps.explain: They barely compress. Because the liquid does not squash, your push travels through it to the far end instead of getting soaked up.

steps.title: Two pistons, two sizes
steps.body: Connect a small piston and a large piston with fluid between them. The pressure is the same throughout the fluid. But force equals pressure times area, so the bigger piston has more area to push on. Make the large piston ten times the area of the small one and it pushes ten times harder. That is how a small pedal lifts a car.

steps.prompt: Why does the larger piston push with more force than the small one?
options: It has more area for the same pressure to act on
It is closer to the ground
It holds more fuel
steps.explain: More area. Pressure is equal across the fluid, and force is pressure times area, so the bigger piston turns the same pressure into a bigger force.

steps.title: Nothing is free
steps.body: You do not get the extra force for nothing. The small piston has to move a long way to push the large piston a short way. Trade distance for force. To lift a car an inch, you might pump the small piston many times. This is the same bargain every simple machine makes: more force, less distance.

steps.prompt: When the trapped liquid multiplies your force, what do you give up in return?
options: Distance, the small piston moves far to move the big one a little
Nothing, you get the force for free
The liquid gets used up
steps.explain: Distance. More force means less distance. The small piston travels a long way to move the large one a short way, so you trade one for the other.

steps.title: That's hydraulics.
recap: Trapped liquid spreads pressure evenly and barely compresses
A bigger piston turns equal pressure into bigger force
You trade distance for that extra force

subject: HOW THINGS WORK
title: Engines Need a Heavy Wheel to Run Smoothly
blurb: A flywheel stores motion between bangs so the engine never stalls.

steps.title: Engines Need a Heavy Wheel to Run Smoothly
steps.sub: An engine fires in bursts, not a steady stream. So why does it run smooth instead of lurching? A heavy wheel does the quiet work. Here's how.

steps.prompt: An engine only delivers a power burst now and then, with dead spots in between. What keeps it turning during the dead spots?
options: Stored spinning motion that carries it through
A second engine taking over
Nothing, it briefly stops each time
steps.explain: Stored spinning motion. A heavy spinning wheel keeps its momentum through the dead spots and drags the engine past them, so it never actually stalls.

steps.title: Power comes in bangs
steps.body: A piston engine does not push steadily. It fires, gets one shove from the burning fuel, then coasts through several dead strokes before the next fire. Left alone, the crank would speed up at each bang and slow down in between, lurching like a person being shoved every few seconds. That is rough, and at low speed it would just stall.

steps.prompt: Why would a bare engine lurch and risk stalling?
options: Power arrives in bursts with dead spots between them
The fuel is too cold
The pistons are too light
steps.explain: Power comes in bursts. With a shove then a gap, the crank speeds up and slows down, lurching and risking a stall in the dead spots.

steps.title: A heavy wheel evens it out
steps.body: Bolt a heavy wheel to the crankshaft and it stores energy as it spins. A spinning mass resists changes to its speed. So during a power bang it soaks up extra energy without speeding much, and during the dead spots it gives that energy back, keeping the crank turning. The result is a smooth, steady spin out of a series of jolts.

steps.prompt: How does a flywheel smooth out the engine?
options: It stores energy from each bang and releases it during the gaps
It blocks fuel between firings
It makes the engine fire more often
steps.explain: It stores and releases. The heavy wheel soaks up energy during a power stroke and gives it back during the dead spots, evening out the spin.

steps.prompt: Without a flywheel, what happens to the crankshaft's speed between firings?
options: It slows down in the dead spots and lurches
It stays perfectly steady
It spins faster and faster
steps.explain: It slows and lurches. Power comes in bursts, so without stored momentum the crank slows in every gap. The flywheel is what carries it through.

steps.title: That's the flywheel.
recap: Engines make power in bursts, not a steady push
A spinning mass resists changes to its speed
The flywheel stores energy from each bang to bridge the gaps

subject: HOW THINGS WORK
title: Why Your Car's Wheels Spin at Different Speeds
blurb: In a turn the outer wheel travels farther, and a differential lets it.

steps.title: Why Your Car's Wheels Spin at Different Speeds
steps.sub: Round a corner and your two driven wheels are forced to disagree. A clever gear named the differential lets them. Here's the problem it solves.

steps.prompt: Your car turns left. Think about the paths the two front wheels trace. Which wheel covers more ground?
options: The outer (right) wheel
The inner (left) wheel
They cover the same ground
steps.explain: The outer wheel. It swings around a wider arc than the inner wheel, so it travels farther in the same turn. To keep up over that longer path, it has to spin faster.

steps.title: The outer wheel goes farther
steps.body: When a car turns, both wheels on an axle sweep around the same center, but at different distances from it. The outer wheel is on a bigger circle, so it travels a longer path. The inner wheel is on a tighter circle and travels less. Same time, different distance, which means the wheels must roll at different speeds to avoid scrubbing and skidding.

steps.prompt: In a turn, why must the two wheels roll at different speeds?
options: The outer wheel covers a longer path in the same time
The inner wheel is heavier
The engine sends power to only one side
steps.explain: Longer path. The outer wheel sweeps a wider arc, so it must spin faster to cover the extra distance without dragging or skidding.

steps.title: Why a solid axle fails
steps.body: If both driven wheels were locked to one solid axle, they would be forced to spin at the same speed always. In a turn that is impossible without one wheel slipping. You would feel it skip and hop, the tires would scrub, and the handling would suffer. The car needs a way to drive both wheels yet still let them turn at their own speeds.

steps.prompt: What goes wrong if both driven wheels are locked to one solid axle in a turn?
options: One wheel must slip or skip because they can't spin at the same speed cleanly
The car speeds up
The engine stalls
steps.explain: A wheel slips. A solid axle forces equal speed, but the turn demands different speeds, so a tire has to scrub or hop. That is the problem the differential fixes.

steps.title: The differential splits the drive
steps.body: The differential is a set of gears between the two wheels. It takes the engine's drive and splits it to both sides, but it lets the two sides turn at different speeds. Going straight, both wheels turn together. In a turn, the gears let the outer wheel spin faster and the inner wheel slower, all while both keep getting power. That is the whole job, and where it gets its name.

steps.prompt: Trace the logic of why the differential exists.
items: The car enters a turn
The outer wheel must travel a longer path
A solid axle would force a wheel to slip
The differential lets the wheels spin at different speeds

glosses.The car enters a turn: Both wheels swing around one center
glosses.The outer wheel must travel a longer pat: It is on the wider arc
glosses.A solid axle would force a wheel to slip: Equal speed can't cover unequal paths
glosses.The differential lets the wheels spin at: Power to both, speeds free to differ

steps.prompt: Back to the start: in a left turn, which wheel spins faster?
options: The outer (right) wheel
The inner (left) wheel
Both spin the same
steps.explain: The outer wheel. It covers the longer arc, so it spins faster. The differential is what lets it do that while still being driven.

steps.title: That's the differential.
recap: In a turn the outer wheel travels a longer path
A solid axle would force a wheel to slip
The differential drives both wheels yet lets their speeds differ

subject: HOW THINGS WORK
title: Oil Isn't Fuel, It's What Keeps Metal Apart
blurb: Engine oil doesn't power anything. It floats metal parts on a film so they never touch.

steps.title: What oil actually does
steps.sub: An engine burns fuel to make power. So why does it also need a few quarts of oil, and what happens if it runs dry?

steps.prompt: What is the main job of the oil in a car engine?
options: It mixes with fuel to help it burn
It keeps moving metal parts from touching each other
It cools the engine and nothing else
steps.explain: Oil keeps metal apart. It does help carry away heat too, but its core job is to slip between moving parts so they ride on a film instead of grinding together.

steps.title: Metal on metal is the enemy
steps.body: Inside an engine, parts like the crankshaft and bearings spin against each other thousands of times a minute. If clean metal rubs on clean metal, the surfaces tear and weld microscopically, heat spikes, and parts wear out fast. Oil fixes this by filling the tiny gap with a slick layer the parts can slide on.

steps.prompt: Why is direct metal-on-metal contact bad inside an engine?
options: The metal gets too cold
The surfaces grind, heat up, and wear out fast
The metal turns to fuel
steps.explain: Grinding and heat. Bare metal surfaces tear at each other and overheat, so the parts wear quickly. The oil film stops them from ever touching.

steps.title: A film thinner than a hair
steps.body: A pump pushes oil through channels to every bearing and surface that moves. Spinning parts drag oil into the gap and float on a film far thinner than a human hair. The parts never touch when this works. Run the engine without oil and that film vanishes, metal seizes, and the engine can wreck itself in minutes.

steps.prompt: An engine loses all its oil but keeps running. What is most likely to happen?
options: It runs out of fuel and stalls
The parts seize as bare metal grinds and overheats
It runs cooler because there is less liquid inside
steps.explain: It seizes. With no film between them, the metal parts grind and overheat exactly as you would expect, and the engine can destroy itself fast.

steps.title: That's why engines need oil.
recap: Oil is not fuel; it separates moving parts
It floats metal on a film thinner than a hair
Lose the oil and the engine grinds itself apart

subject: HOW THINGS WORK
title: Rockets Work Better in Empty Space
blurb: A rocket doesn't push against air or ground. It throws mass out the back and gets shoved forward.

steps.title: What a rocket pushes on
steps.sub: A plane needs air. A car needs road. So what does a rocket push against once it leaves the atmosphere and there is nothing out there?

steps.prompt: In the vacuum of space, with no air to push against, what happens to a rocket's thrust?
options: It stops working with nothing to push on
It still works, and actually works a bit better
It works only if the rocket is spinning
steps.explain: It still works, and slightly better. A rocket does not push on the outside world at all. It pushes on its own exhaust, and air outside only gets in the way.

steps.title: Action and reaction
steps.body: A rocket burns fuel and blasts hot gas out the back at high speed. Newton's third law says every push has an equal push the other way. Throwing all that mass backward shoves the rocket forward. The rocket and its exhaust push on each other. The outside world is not part of the deal.

steps.prompt: Where does the forward push on a rocket come from?
options: The exhaust hitting the air or ground behind it
The rocket throwing exhaust backward, which shoves it forward
Heat rising and lifting the rocket up
steps.explain: From throwing exhaust backward. By Newton's third law, hurling mass out the back produces an equal forward push on the rocket itself, no outside surface required.

steps.title: Why vacuum helps
steps.body: On the launch pad, the rocket also has to shove the surrounding air aside, and air pressure presses back on the nozzle. In space there is no air to fight and no back-pressure on the exhaust. So the same engine produces somewhat more thrust in vacuum than at sea level.

steps.prompt: Put the steps of producing thrust in order:
items: Burn fuel to make hot, high-pressure gas
Force the gas out the nozzle at high speed
The escaping gas pushes back on the rocket
The rocket accelerates forward

glosses.Force the gas out the nozzle at high spe: The nozzle speeds the exhaust up

steps.prompt: Why does a rocket engine give a bit more thrust in space than at sea level?
options: Gravity is weaker up high
There is no air pushing back on the exhaust and nozzle
The fuel burns colder in space
steps.explain: No air pushing back. At sea level, air pressure presses on the nozzle and the exhaust must shove air aside. Remove the air and that resistance is gone.

steps.title: That's why space is no problem.
recap: A rocket pushes on its own exhaust, not on air or ground
Throwing mass backward shoves the rocket forward
No air means slightly more thrust, not less

subject: HOW THINGS WORK
title: Why Cars Need Gears at All
blurb: An engine only makes useful power in a narrow speed band. Gears keep it in the sweet spot.

steps.title: Why not just one gear
steps.sub: The engine spins, the wheels spin. So why does a car need a whole gearbox between them instead of a single direct connection?

steps.prompt: Why can't a car just bolt the engine straight to the wheels with no gears?
options: The engine would spin the wrong way
The engine only makes useful power in a narrow speed range
The wheels would spin too slowly to ever move
steps.explain: Narrow power band. An engine is weak at very low and very high speeds. Gears let it stay in its strong range whether the car is crawling or cruising.

steps.title: The engine has a sweet spot
steps.body: A gasoline engine does not make the same power at every speed. Down low it lacks force, and too high it strains and loses efficiency. There is a middle band where it makes strong, efficient power. Engineers call this the power band, and you want to keep the engine inside it.

steps.prompt: What is the engine's 'power band'?
options: The loudest speed the engine can reach
The middle speed range where it makes strong, efficient power
The point right before the engine stalls
steps.explain: The strong middle range. The engine is feeble at the extremes, so the goal is to keep it in that efficient band as much as possible.

steps.title: Gears trade speed for force
steps.body: But the wheels need to turn slowly with lots of force to get moving, then fast with less force to cruise. A low gear trades engine speed for turning force at the wheels, great for starting off. A high gear lets the wheels spin fast for a given engine speed, great for the highway. Either way the engine stays near its sweet spot.

steps.prompt: Order the gears a car uses as it speeds up from a stop:
items: Low gear to pull away from a stop
Middle gears as speed builds
High gear for cruising
Shift down again when slowing or climbing

glosses.Shift down again when slowing or climbin: Back to more force when needed

steps.prompt: You are starting from a dead stop on a steep hill. Which gear do you want?
options: A high gear for more force
A low gear, which trades engine speed for turning force
Any gear works the same at a stop
steps.explain: A low gear. It trades the engine's speed for extra turning force at the wheels, exactly what you need to get a heavy car moving uphill.

steps.title: That's why cars have gears.
recap: An engine makes useful power only in a narrow band
Wheels need lots of force at low speed, less at high speed
Gears match the two so the engine stays in its sweet spot

subject: HOW THINGS WORK
title: Your Fridge Is an Engine Run Backwards
blurb: A fridge doesn't make cold. It's a pump that moves heat out of the box and into your kitchen.

steps.title: There's no such thing as cold
steps.sub: A fridge keeps food cold, so it must be making cold inside. Except it isn't. It is doing something sneakier with heat.

steps.prompt: How does a refrigerator keep its inside cold?
options: It creates cold and pumps it into the box
It pumps heat out of the box and dumps it into the room
It seals the box so no heat can ever enter
steps.explain: It pumps heat out. Cold is just the absence of heat. The fridge moves heat from inside the box to the room, leaving the inside colder.

steps.title: Cold is missing heat
steps.body: You cannot add cold to anything. You can only take heat away. A fridge works by moving heat out of the food compartment and releasing it somewhere else, the coils on the back or bottom. That is why those coils feel warm and why the kitchen does not actually get cooler when you run the fridge.

steps.prompt: Why do the coils on the back of a fridge feel warm?
options: They are where the cold is generated
They release the heat pumped out of the inside
They are warmed by the kitchen lights
steps.explain: They dump the heat. The fridge carries heat from inside the box to those coils and releases it into the room, so they feel warm.

steps.title: A refrigerant carries the heat
steps.body: A special fluid called a refrigerant loops around the fridge. It absorbs heat inside the cold compartment and turns to gas. A compressor squeezes that gas, which makes it hot, and the outside coils let it dump that heat to the room and turn back to liquid. Then it expands, gets cold again, and repeats.

steps.prompt: Put the refrigerant's cycle in order:
items: Cold refrigerant absorbs heat inside the fridge and boils to gas
The compressor squeezes the gas and it gets hot
Outside coils release that heat and the gas turns back to liquid
The liquid expands, turns cold, and starts over

steps.prompt: Could you cool a kitchen by leaving the fridge door open?
options: Yes, the cold air would spread and cool the room
No, the fridge just moves heat around the same room and its motor adds more
Yes, but only if the fridge is empty
steps.explain: No. The fridge only moves heat from inside the box to the room. With the door open it pulls heat from the same room it dumps into, and the motor adds heat on top. The kitchen gets warmer.

steps.title: That's why it's a heat pump.
recap: A fridge moves heat out, it does not make cold
A refrigerant absorbs heat inside and releases it outside
Run the same cycle backward and you have an engine or a heater

subject: HOW THINGS WORK
title: Engines Turn Up-and-Down Into Round-and-Round
blurb: Pistons only go straight up and down. The crankshaft is the trick that turns that into spinning wheels.

steps.title: Straight line, spinning wheel
steps.sub: Burning fuel just shoves a piston in a straight line. Your wheels need to spin. How does one become the other?

steps.prompt: An explosion pushes the piston straight down its cylinder. How does that end up turning the wheels?
options: The piston itself spins in a circle
A crankshaft converts the straight push into rotation
The exhaust gases spin the wheels directly
steps.explain: A crankshaft. The piston only moves in a straight line. An offset crank turns that back-and-forth motion into spinning, like your leg pumping a bike pedal.

steps.title: The piston goes straight, nothing else
steps.body: Inside each cylinder, burning fuel expands and slams the piston down. The piston can only slide up and down its cylinder, in a straight line. On its own that motion gets you nowhere. Something has to convert that straight shove into the round motion a wheel needs.

steps.prompt: What kind of motion does a piston make by itself?
options: It spins in a circle
It slides straight up and down
It swings side to side
steps.explain: Straight up and down. A piston is trapped in its cylinder and can only slide in a line. The spinning has to come from somewhere else.

steps.title: The crank does the magic
steps.body: A connecting rod links the piston to the crankshaft. The crankshaft has an offset section, like the bent part of a bike pedal crank. As the piston pushes down, the rod shoves that offset around in a circle. Up-and-down at one end becomes round-and-round at the other. That spin runs out to the wheels.

steps.prompt: Everyday machine that turns the same straight push into spinning, just like a crankshaft?
options: A door hinge
Your legs pumping bicycle pedals
A light switch
steps.explain: Bike pedals. Your legs push down in near-straight strokes and the offset pedal crank turns it into a spinning wheel. Same trick the crankshaft uses.

steps.title: That's the piston-to-wheel trick.
recap: Pistons only move in a straight line
A connecting rod links the piston to the crankshaft
The crank's offset turns straight motion into rotation

subject: HOW THINGS WORK
title: Engines Have a Built-In Speed Limiter
blurb: Take the load off a running engine and it wants to speed up forever. Something has to say no.

steps.title: The engine that won't stop speeding up
steps.sub: An engine wants to go faster the more fuel it gets, and faster spinning often lets it burn even more. Left alone, that loop can run away. By the end you'll know what stops it.

steps.prompt: You disconnect a running engine from everything it was driving, so it spins free with no load. What happens to its speed?
options: It settles to a slow, steady idle
It tends to climb, and can climb dangerously high
It stops, because it has nothing to push
steps.explain: With no load eating its power, more of each combustion goes straight into spinning faster. Faster spinning can pull in more fuel and air, which makes more power, which spins it faster still. That loop can push the engine past the speed its own parts can survive.

steps.title: Why speed can run away
steps.body: An engine makes more power as it spins faster, up to a point. Normally a load, like wheels or a pump, soaks up that power and holds the speed steady. Remove the load and there is nothing to absorb the power, so it all goes into acceleration. Spin too fast and the parts can fly apart. Engineers call this overspeed, or a runaway.

steps.prompt: Why does losing the load let an engine overspeed?
options: Without a load there is nothing to absorb the power, so it all goes into acceleration
Losing the load makes the engine burn cleaner fuel
The load was the only thing making heat
steps.explain: Power has to go somewhere. With a load it goes into useful work and the speed holds. With no load it goes into raw acceleration, and the speed climbs until something limits it or breaks.

steps.title: The governor pushes back
steps.body: A governor is the limiter. It senses how fast the engine is turning and cuts back the fuel or air when speed gets too high. The classic version uses spinning weights that fly outward as they spin faster. As they swing out, they pull the fuel back. Speed up, less fuel. Slow down, more fuel. It steers the engine toward a target speed on its own.

steps.prompt: In a classic mechanical governor, what do the spinning weights do as the engine speeds up?
options: Fly outward and pull the fuel back
Pull inward and add more fuel
Stop the engine completely
steps.explain: Faster spin flings the weights outward. That outward motion is linked to the fuel control, so it trims fuel back as speed rises. It is a self-correcting loop: the very thing it is measuring, speed, is what it acts to limit.

steps.title: It's a feedback loop
steps.body: A governor is negative feedback. It senses the result, speed, and pushes against any change. Too fast, cut fuel. Too slow, add fuel. James Watt put a flyball governor on steam engines in the late 1700s. Modern engines often do it electronically: a sensor reads the speed and a controller adjusts fueling many times a second. Same idea, faster hands.

steps.prompt: A diesel generator is humming along under load. The load suddenly drops to zero. What keeps the engine from running away?
options: The fuel simply runs out
The governor senses the speed rising and cuts the fuel
Nothing, the engine always overspeeds when unloaded
steps.explain: Lose the load and the speed starts to climb. The governor catches that climb and pulls fuel back before the engine overspeeds. This matters most on diesels, which have no throttle plate limiting air, so fuel alone sets the speed. Without a governor a diesel can run away on its own.

steps.title: That's the governor.
recap: No load means power goes into pure acceleration.
A governor senses speed and trims the fuel.
It's negative feedback that keeps RPM in check.

subject: HOW THINGS WORK
title: Why an Engine Keeps Running When You Stop
blurb: An idling engine isn't resting. It's burning fuel just to stay alive.

steps.title: Stopped, but still running
steps.sub: You pull up to a light and the engine keeps going. It feels like it's doing nothing. It isn't. By the end you'll know why it can't just pause.

steps.prompt: Your car is stopped at a red light, engine idling. Is it burning fuel?
options: No, it's just sitting there
Yes, enough to keep itself turning over
Only if the air conditioning is on
steps.explain: It is burning fuel the whole time. An engine only keeps spinning because combustion keeps happening. Stop the burning and it stops turning. At idle it burns just enough to keep itself alive, even though the car isn't moving an inch.

steps.title: Combustion is what keeps it turning
steps.body: An engine runs by repeating a cycle: pull in air and fuel, squeeze it, burn it, push the exhaust out. The burn is what shoves the piston and turns the engine. Take away the burn and there is no push, so it coasts to a halt in a moment. To stay running it has to keep firing, even when you're not going anywhere.

steps.prompt: Why does an engine stop if combustion stops?
options: Because the wheels lock up
Because nothing is pushing the pistons anymore
Because the battery dies instantly
steps.explain: The push from each burn is the only thing keeping the pistons moving. No burn, no push, no motion. The engine can't glide through its cycle on its own, so it needs fuel firing continuously just to keep spinning.

steps.title: Idle is staying alive
steps.body: At idle the engine runs slow and does almost no useful work. So what's the fuel for? It overcomes the engine's own friction and the effort of pumping air in and out, just enough to keep turning. It also runs your accessories: the alternator charging the battery, the pump pushing heat or cold air into the cabin. Idle is the engine paying to stay ready.

steps.prompt: At idle, what is most of the fuel doing?
options: Driving the wheels forward
Overcoming the engine's own friction and keeping it turning
Cooling the engine down
steps.explain: At idle the engine isn't moving the car, so the fuel mostly fights its own internal friction and pumping losses to stay spinning. It's a baseline cost of being on and ready, plus running accessories like the alternator and cabin heat.

steps.prompt: Many new cars shut the engine off at a long stop and restart it when you lift off the brake. Why does that save fuel?
options: Restarting uses no fuel at all
An idling engine keeps burning fuel just to stay running, so turning it off stops that waste
The engine runs cooler when restarted
steps.explain: Idle isn't free. The engine burns fuel the whole time it sits running. If you'll be stopped a while, shutting it off stops that burn. Stop-start systems do exactly this, restarting fast when you're ready to go.

steps.title: That's idling.
recap: Combustion is the only thing keeping it turning.
Idle fuel just fights friction and runs accessories.
Shutting off at a stop saves that wasted burn.

subject: HOW THINGS WORK
title: Microwaves Don't Heat From the Inside Out
blurb: The myth says microwaves cook center-first. They don't. They jiggle water and only reach so deep.

steps.title: The inside-out myth
steps.sub: Almost everyone believes the microwave cooks the middle first and works outward. It's the opposite. By the end you'll know what microwaves actually grab onto, and how deep they reach.

steps.prompt: A microwave heats a thick bowl of soup. Where does the energy land first?
options: The center, then it spreads outward
The outer layer, within about an inch of the surface
Evenly everywhere at once
steps.explain: Microwaves only reach so far into food before they run out, roughly the first inch. The outer region absorbs most of the energy. The deep center isn't heated directly at all. The inside-out story is backwards.

steps.title: It jiggles water molecules
steps.body: A water molecule is lopsided: one end is slightly positive, the other slightly negative. A microwave is an electric field that flips back and forth billions of times a second. Each flip yanks the water molecules around, trying to make them line up. They twist and rub against each other, and that rubbing is heat. No flame, no element, just molecular friction.

steps.prompt: How do microwaves actually make food hot?
options: By blowing hot air over the surface
By flipping a field that twists water molecules, and the rubbing makes heat
By sending out infrared like a toaster element
steps.explain: The field flips billions of times a second, and the lopsided water molecules twist to follow it. That frantic twisting rubs molecules together and produces heat directly inside the food. It's friction at the molecular scale, not surface air or a glowing element.

steps.title: They only reach about an inch
steps.body: The catch is depth. As microwaves push into food, the outer water absorbs them, so less energy makes it deeper. Most is soaked up within roughly the first inch of the surface. Past that, the microwaves are nearly gone. So a thick item gets its outside heated directly, while the deep middle waits for heat to creep in from the edges.

steps.prompt: How does the deep center of a thick food get hot in a microwave?
options: Microwaves reach all the way to the center directly
Heat conducts inward from the heated outer layer
It stays cold no matter what
steps.explain: Microwaves are mostly absorbed in the outer inch, so they don't reach the deep center directly. That center heats the slow way, by conduction, as warmth moves inward from the hot outer layer. The microwave heats the outside; the food heats the inside.

steps.title: Why stirring and waiting help
steps.body: Because the energy lands unevenly and the center lags, microwaved food gets hot spots and cold spots. That's why instructions tell you to stir partway and let it stand a minute. Stirring mixes hot outer food into the cool middle. Standing gives conduction time to even things out. You're finishing the job the microwave couldn't.

steps.prompt: Reheated lasagna is scalding at the edges but cold in the middle. Why?
options: The microwave was set too low
Microwaves heat the outer inch directly, and the deep center has to wait for heat to conduct in
Cheese blocks microwaves
steps.explain: Microwaves are absorbed mostly in the outer inch, so the edges get hot fast while the center isn't heated directly. The middle relies on heat creeping inward by conduction, which is slow. Stir it and let it stand to fix the cold spot.

steps.title: That's the microwave.
recap: It twists water molecules, and the friction is heat.
It only reaches about the outer inch.
The center heats slowly by conduction.

subject: HOW THINGS WORK
title: Your WiFi Is Fine, Your Internet Is Down
blurb: WiFi and internet aren't the same thing. Knowing the difference tells you what actually broke.

steps.title: Two different things
steps.sub: The page won't load and you say the WiFi is down. Usually it isn't. WiFi and internet are separate links in a chain. By the end you'll know which one actually broke.

steps.prompt: Your phone shows full WiFi bars, but no website will load. What's most likely wrong?
options: The WiFi between phone and router
The connection from your router out to the internet
The phone's screen
steps.explain: Full bars mean your phone is talking to the router just fine. That's the WiFi part, and it's working. The break is further down the chain, between your router and the wider internet. WiFi healthy, internet down.

steps.title: WiFi is just the last hop
steps.body: WiFi is only the wireless jump between your device and your router, across one room or one house. That's all it is. Your signal bars measure that single short link. A strong link to the router tells you nothing about whether the router can reach the outside world. WiFi is the first step, not the whole trip.

steps.prompt: What do your WiFi signal bars actually measure?
options: How fast the internet is today
The quality of the link between your device and your router
How many websites are online
steps.explain: The bars only rate the short wireless hop to your router. That's the whole job of WiFi. They say nothing about whether the router can reach the internet, which is why you can have full bars and still load nothing.

steps.title: The whole chain
steps.body: Your data takes a path. Device to router over WiFi. Router to modem. Modem out through your internet provider's line to the global internet. The internet is that far end, the worldwide network. WiFi is only the first hop. Most outages happen out past your router, on the provider's side, even though your WiFi looks perfectly fine.

steps.prompt: In the chain from your device to the world, where does WiFi sit?
options: It's the whole path, start to finish
It's the first hop, from your device to your router
It's the provider's part of the network
steps.explain: WiFi is just the device-to-router hop. After that comes the modem, then the provider, then the global internet. Because WiFi is only one early link, a problem anywhere further down can leave your WiFi working while the internet is unreachable.

steps.prompt: Put the path of your data in order, from your phone to a far-off website.
items: Your device
WiFi hop to your router
Modem to your internet provider
The global internet

glosses.WiFi hop to your router: the short wireless link in your home
glosses.Modem to your internet provider: the line out of your house
glosses.The global internet: the worldwide network and the website

steps.prompt: If your WiFi truly went down, what would you see on your phone?
options: Full bars but no websites
Your phone can't connect to the router at all
Faster loading than usual
steps.explain: WiFi is the link to the router. If it's down, your device can't even reach the router, so you'd lose the connection entirely, not just websites. Full bars with dead pages is the opposite case: WiFi fine, internet down past the router.

steps.title: That's the difference.
recap: WiFi is just the hop to your router.
The internet is everything past your router.
Full bars and dead pages means the internet is down.

subject: HOW THINGS WORK
title: Why Your Touchscreen Ignores Gloves
blurb: Your phone doesn't feel your finger press. It feels your body's electric charge.

steps.title: It's not feeling pressure
steps.sub: You'd swear the screen feels your finger push. It doesn't. It's sensing something else entirely, which is why gloves fail and odd objects sometimes work. By the end you'll know what it's really detecting.

steps.prompt: Why won't your phone respond through an ordinary winter glove?
options: The glove isn't pressing hard enough
The glove blocks something electrical the screen needs from your finger
The glove is too cold
steps.explain: It's not about pressure. The screen senses the tiny electrical charge your body carries. An ordinary glove is an insulator, so it blocks that charge. Your finger could push as hard as it likes and the screen still wouldn't notice.

steps.title: The screen senses charge
steps.body: Most phone screens are capacitive. The surface holds a small electric charge spread across a fine grid of transparent conductors. Your body conducts electricity, so when your fingertip lands, it draws off a little of that charge at one spot. The screen notices where the charge changed and calls that point a touch. It feels charge, not force.

steps.prompt: What does a capacitive touchscreen actually detect?
options: How hard you press
Your fingertip drawing off a bit of the screen's electric charge
The heat of your finger
steps.explain: The screen holds a charge, and your conductive body pulls a little of it away where you touch. The screen reads that change in charge as a touch location. Pressure and heat have nothing to do with it, which is the whole surprise.

steps.title: Why a light tap works
steps.body: Because it senses charge and not force, you never have to mash the screen. A feather-light tap moves charge just as well as a hard jab. That's why phones feel so effortless compared to old machines. The screen isn't waiting for you to push it in. It's waiting for your charge to show up.

steps.prompt: Why does a light tap register just as well as a hard press?
options: The screen amplifies weak presses
It senses charge, not force, so how hard you press doesn't matter
Light taps move faster
steps.explain: Charge transfers whether you tap lightly or jab hard, so force isn't the trigger. The screen only cares that your conductive finger touched it and moved some charge. That's why it responds to the gentlest tap.

steps.title: Gloves, and stranger things
steps.body: An ordinary glove is an insulator, so it cuts the charge off and the screen sees nothing. Touchscreen gloves fix this with conductive thread in the fingertips that carries the charge through. The same logic explains the party trick where a peeled sausage works: it's wet and conductive enough to pass charge. Insulators fail, conductors work.

steps.prompt: Why does a special touchscreen glove work when a regular glove doesn't?
options: It's thinner, so you press harder
It has conductive thread that carries your body's charge through to the screen
It keeps your finger warmer
steps.explain: The screen needs charge to pass from your body to its surface. A regular glove insulates and blocks it. A touchscreen glove weaves conductive thread into the fingertips, giving the charge a path through. Same reason a conductive object can fake a touch.

steps.title: That's the touchscreen.
recap: It senses your body's charge, not pressure.
Light taps work because force doesn't matter.
Gloves insulate and block the charge unless they're conductive.

subject: HOW THINGS WORK
title: Incognito Mode Doesn't Hide You
blurb: Private browsing forgets your history. It does not hide you from anyone else.

steps.title: Incognito mode doesn't hide you
steps.sub: The name promises privacy. What it actually deletes is much smaller than you think. Here is the line.

steps.prompt: You browse a site in incognito mode at work. Who can still see that you visited it?
options: Nobody, that is the whole point
Only the website itself
Your employer's network and the website
steps.explain: Both. Incognito only controls what your own browser remembers. Anyone between you and the site, including your employer's network and the site's own servers, still sees the visit.

steps.title: It only erases local traces
steps.body: Incognito mode does one job. When you close the window, it throws away the history, cookies, and typed form data from that session. The point is the person who picks up your laptop next, not the wider internet. Nothing about how your traffic travels changes at all.

steps.prompt: What does incognito mode actually throw away when you close the window?
options: Your IP address
Local history, cookies, and form data
The record kept by the websites you visited
steps.explain: Just the local traces on your own device. It cannot reach out and erase records held by anyone else, because those were never on your machine to begin with.

steps.title: Everyone in the path still sees you
steps.body: Your request still leaves your device and travels to the site. Your internet provider sees where it goes. On a work or school network, the administrator sees it too. The website sees your IP address and can still identify you. Incognito does not encrypt anything or change your address. To hide the source, you would need something like a VPN.

steps.prompt: Why can your internet provider still see your activity in incognito mode?
options: Because incognito does not change how your traffic travels
Because incognito secretly logs you
Because the provider broke the encryption
steps.explain: Your traffic still leaves your device the same way and passes through the provider. Incognito never touched that path. It only cleaned up afterward on your end.

steps.prompt: A friend says incognito kept a site he visited off the company records. Is he right?
options: Yes, incognito hid it from everyone
No, the company network still saw the visit
Only if he closed the window fast
steps.explain: No. Remember, incognito only erased the traces on his own laptop. The company network sat in the path the whole time and logged the visit anyway.

steps.title: That's incognito.
recap: Incognito only forgets local history, cookies, and form data.
Your provider, your network admin, and the site still see you.
Hiding the source needs encryption or a VPN, not incognito.

subject: HOW THINGS WORK
title: Stop Charging Your Phone to 100%
blurb: Lithium batteries hate the top and the bottom. Live in the middle.

steps.title: Stop charging to 100%
steps.sub: A full battery feels like the goal. For a lithium battery, full is one of the worst places to sit. Here is why.

steps.prompt: Which state wears a lithium battery the most over time?
options: Sitting at a comfortable middle charge
Sitting pinned at fully charged
Charging is charging, it makes no difference
steps.explain: Sitting at full. A lithium cell held near 100 percent is under the most chemical stress, and that steady stress is what ages it faster.

steps.title: The edges are where wear happens
steps.body: A lithium-ion cell ages fastest at its two extremes. Held near full, especially when warm, it degrades faster. Run all the way down to empty, and that stresses it too. The damage is not from using the battery. It is from parking it at the top or the bottom.

steps.prompt: According to the lesson, what makes a high charge level worse for the battery?
options: Being held near full, especially when warm
Being used too often
Charging too slowly
steps.explain: Heat plus a high charge level is the bad combination. The cell sits under more chemical stress there, and warmth speeds that wear along.

steps.title: Living in the middle stretches its life
steps.body: Keep the charge in a middle band, often cited as roughly 20 to 80 percent, and you skip both stressful extremes. Doing this can meaningfully extend how long the battery holds a useful charge. This is why many phones now offer a setting that pauses or limits charging near full.

steps.prompt: Why does a phone setting that limits charging near full help the battery?
options: It charges faster
It keeps the cell out of the high-stress full zone
It uses less electricity
steps.explain: It keeps the cell away from the top of its range, where wear is fastest. Less time pinned at full means slower aging.

steps.prompt: You leave your phone plugged in overnight, sitting warm at 100 percent for hours. Why is that not ideal?
options: It overcharges and overflows
Full plus warm is the high-wear combination
It drains faster the next day
