steps.explain: Recall the bad combination: high charge plus heat. Hours parked at full and warm is exactly the condition that ages the cell fastest.

steps.title: That's battery care.
recap: Lithium cells age fastest at full and at empty.
Heat near full makes the wear worse.
Living in a middle band stretches usable life.

subject: HOW THINGS WORK
title: What's Actually Inside a QR Code
blurb: Three corner squares tell the scanner which way is up. The rest is built to survive damage.

steps.title: Inside a QR code
steps.sub: It looks like random noise. It is not. Two clever tricks make it fast to read and hard to break. Here they are.

steps.prompt: What are the three big squares in the corners of a QR code for?
options: They store the most important data
They are decoration for the brand
They tell the scanner where the code is and which way it faces
steps.explain: They are orientation markers. Before reading any data, the scanner finds those three squares to lock onto the code and figure out which way is up.

steps.title: The corner squares are finder patterns
steps.body: Three large squares sit in three of the four corners. They are called finder patterns. A scanner hunts for that distinctive shape first. Three corners, not four, is the trick: it tells the scanner the exact orientation, so you can scan a code sideways or upside down and it still works.

steps.prompt: Why are there finder patterns in only three corners, not all four?
options: To save ink
Three corners reveal the code's orientation
The fourth corner holds the data
steps.explain: Three marked corners and one blank one form an L shape. That asymmetry tells the scanner which way the code is turned, so any angle reads fine.

steps.title: It is built to survive damage
steps.body: A QR code does not just store your data once. It adds error correction, extra math woven through the pattern using a method called Reed-Solomon. If part of the code is scratched, smudged, or covered, the scanner reconstructs the missing bits from what is left. At the strongest setting, it can recover the data with up to about 30 percent of the code unreadable.

steps.prompt: A coffee ring covers a chunk of a QR code, yet it still scans. Why?
options: The scanner guesses randomly
Error correction rebuilds the missing data from the rest
QR codes ignore stains automatically
steps.explain: The extra error-correction data lets the scanner reconstruct what the stain hid. The information was spread out with redundancy on purpose.

steps.prompt: Put the steps a scanner takes in order, from first to last.
items: Find the three finder-pattern squares
Work out the code's orientation
Read the data modules
Use error correction to fix unreadable parts

glosses.Find the three finder-pattern squares: Locate the code in the image

steps.prompt: A logo is printed over the center of a QR code and it still scans. Which feature makes that possible?
options: The finder patterns
The error correction
The blank fourth corner
steps.explain: Error correction. Recall it can recover the data even with up to about 30 percent covered, so a logo over the middle is no problem. The finder patterns only handle locating and orienting.

steps.title: That's a QR code.
recap: Three corner squares are finder patterns for location and orientation.
Three corners, not four, reveal which way is up.
Error correction can survive up to about 30 percent damage.

subject: HOW THINGS WORK
title: The Padlock Doesn't Mean a Site Is Safe
blurb: The padlock means the line is encrypted. It says nothing about who is on the other end.

steps.title: The padlock isn't a safety badge
steps.sub: You learned to look for the padlock before typing a password. It means less than you were told. Here is what it really means.

steps.prompt: A phishing site that steals passwords shows a padlock in the address bar. Is that possible?
options: No, scam sites cannot get a padlock
Yes, the padlock is easy for anyone to get
Only if the browser is broken
steps.explain: Yes, easily. The padlock only certifies encryption, and basic certificates are free and quick to get. A scammer can have one in minutes.

steps.title: The padlock means encrypted, not trusted
steps.body: The padlock tells you one thing. The connection between your browser and the site is encrypted with HTTPS. Nobody on the network in between can read or change what you send. That protects your data on the wire. It says nothing about whether the site at the other end is honest.

steps.prompt: What does the padlock actually guarantee?
options: The site is a real, trustworthy company
The connection to the site is encrypted
Your password cannot be stolen
steps.explain: Only that the connection is encrypted. It protects data as it travels, but makes no promise about who runs the site or what they do with what you send.

steps.title: Scammers have padlocks too
steps.body: Certificates that enable HTTPS are cheap or free, from sources like Let's Encrypt. A phishing site can set one up and show a padlock just like your bank does. The encryption simply means your stolen password reaches the scammer privately. Browsers dropped older trust signals, so the padlock is now just a baseline, not a seal of approval.

steps.prompt: Why does a padlock on a phishing site give a false sense of security?
options: It means nobody is intercepting you, but the destination is still a scam
It means the site passed a fraud check
It means the site is brand new
steps.explain: The encryption is real, so the connection is private. But private to a scammer is no help. The padlock never checked who you were talking to.

steps.prompt: A site looks slightly off but shows a padlock. What has the padlock confirmed?
options: That the site is safe to trust
Only that the connection is encrypted
That the company is verified
steps.explain: Only that the connection is encrypted. Recall the padlock says nothing about honesty. Looking off is still a real warning sign, padlock or not.

steps.title: That's the padlock.
recap: The padlock means the connection is encrypted.
It does not mean the site is honest or legitimate.
Scam sites get padlocks too, because certificates are free.

subject: HOW THINGS WORK
title: GPS Doesn't Know Where You Are
blurb: Satellites don't track you. They just shout the time. Your phone does the math.

steps.title: GPS doesn't track you
steps.sub: It feels like satellites are watching your dot move. They are not. The whole system runs the other way. Here is how.

steps.prompt: When your phone shows your location, what did the GPS satellites send up to it?
options: A map with your dot on it
Just the time and where the satellite is
A direct ping that located your phone
steps.explain: Just the time and the satellite's own position. The satellites broadcast that endlessly to everyone. Your phone alone works out where you are from it.

steps.title: Satellites only broadcast the time
steps.body: GPS is one way. Each satellite carries an atomic clock and constantly broadcasts a message: here is the exact time, and here is where I am. That is it. The satellites never receive anything back. They do not know your phone exists. Nothing is being tracked from above.

steps.prompt: What is in the signal a GPS satellite broadcasts?
options: The exact time and the satellite's position
Your phone's location
A request for your phone to reply
steps.explain: The time and where the satellite is. The broadcast goes out to everyone and expects no reply. The satellite has no idea who is listening.

steps.title: Your phone does the math
steps.body: Your phone hears several satellites at once. For each, it sees how long the signal took to arrive. Since the signal travels at the speed of light, that delay is a distance. Knowing how far you are from several satellites whose positions you know lets you pin down one point. That is your location. This crossing of distances is called trilateration, and it usually needs at least four satellites.

steps.prompt: How does your phone turn a signal's delay into a distance?
options: It asks the satellite
The signal travels at the speed of light, so delay equals distance
It guesses from signal strength
steps.explain: The signal moves at a known, fixed speed, the speed of light. So a longer delay means the satellite is farther away. Time becomes distance directly.

steps.prompt: Put the steps of getting a GPS fix in order, from first to last.
items: Satellites broadcast time and position
Your phone receives several of these signals
Your phone turns each signal's delay into a distance
Your phone crosses the distances to find its location

steps.prompt: A friend worries the GPS satellites are tracking his movements. What do you tell him?
options: He is right, they watch every receiver
The satellites only broadcast and never receive, so they cannot track him
Only if he turns location on
steps.explain: Recall that GPS is one way. The satellites just shout the time to everyone and never hear back. They have no idea his phone exists, let alone where it is.

steps.title: That's GPS.
recap: Satellites only broadcast the time and their position.
Your phone turns each signal's delay into a distance.
Crossing several distances gives your location, with no tracking.

subject: HOW THINGS WORK
title: Why a Texted Code Beats a Better Password
blurb: A stolen password is half a key. Two-factor makes the other half live in your pocket.

steps.title: Why a texted code beats a better password
steps.sub: You can pick a longer password. Or you can make a stolen password worthless. One of those is much harder to beat.

steps.prompt: An attacker has your exact password but nothing else. You have two-factor turned on. Can they get in?
options: Yes, the password is the login
No, they are missing the second proof
Only if the password is short
steps.explain: No. With two-factor on, the password alone is just one of two locks. The attacker still needs the code that only your phone can produce, and they do not have your phone.

steps.title: Two factors means two different kinds of proof
steps.body: Logins can ask for three kinds of thing: something you know, something you have, and something you are. A password is something you know. A code from your phone is something you have. Two-factor means using two of these categories at once. The point is that they fail in different ways. A leaked password does not leak your phone.

steps.prompt: A password and a code texted to your phone count as two factors because they are:
options: Both things you memorize
Different kinds of proof
Both stored on the same device
steps.explain: Different kinds. One is something you know, the other is something you have. Using two categories is the whole idea, because a single theft rarely gets both at once.

steps.title: This is why a weak setup with 2FA beats a strong one without
steps.body: Most account break-ins start with a password that leaked in some other company's breach. The attacker types it into your account and walks in. That is the common case, and a longer password does not stop it once it has leaked. Two-factor does. Security agencies report that turning on a second factor blocks the large majority of these automated takeovers, even when the password is weak.

steps.prompt: Why does adding 2FA help more than just making your password longer?
options: Long passwords are easy to guess
A leaked password still gets the attacker in, but a second factor stops them
2FA replaces the password entirely
steps.explain: Because the usual attack is a leaked password, and length cannot un-leak it. The second factor is a separate wall the attacker has not climbed.

steps.title: SMS is the weakest second factor, and still worth it
steps.body: Texted codes can be intercepted, and attackers sometimes trick a phone company into moving your number to their SIM. So an app code or a physical security key is stronger. But do not let that talk you out of SMS. Any second factor beats none. A weak lock on the second door still stops the attacker who only had the first key.

steps.prompt: Your password leaks in a breach, but you use a code from an authenticator app. The attacker tries to log in. What happens?
options: They get in, the password is enough
They are stopped, they have no second factor
The app emails them the code
steps.explain: They are stopped. Remember, two factors means two different kinds of proof. The leaked password is something you know, but the app code is something you have, and that stayed on your phone.

steps.title: That's two-factor.
recap: Two factors means two different kinds of proof at once.
Most break-ins use leaked passwords, which a second factor blocks.
Even SMS beats no second factor.

subject: HOW THINGS WORK
title: The Cloud Is Just Someone Else's Computer
blurb: Your files are not floating. They are on a hard drive in a building with a roof and a power bill.

steps.title: The cloud is just someone else's computer
steps.sub: It sounds like the sky. It is actually a warehouse full of hard drives that you will never see. Let's land it.

steps.prompt: You upload a photo to the cloud. Where does the actual photo end up?
options: Spread through the air as a signal
On a physical drive in a building somewhere
Nowhere, it becomes a link
steps.explain: On a physical drive in a building. 'The cloud' is a friendly name for computers you do not own. Your photo is real data, and real data has to sit on a real disk.

steps.title: The cloud is computers you rent instead of own
steps.body: When you save to the cloud, your file goes to a company's computer, called a server. You are renting space on their machine instead of using your own drive. That is the entire trick. There is nothing in the sky. There is a powerful computer with your file on it, owned by someone else.

steps.prompt: What does 'storing a file in the cloud' actually mean?
options: The file is converted into radio waves
The file sits on a company's computer instead of yours
The file is deleted from everywhere
steps.explain: It sits on their computer. You rent the space; they own the hardware. The word 'cloud' just hides whose machine it is.

steps.title: Those computers live in data centers
steps.body: These servers are not one box on a desk. They sit in buildings called data centers: rows of racks packed with drives, fat network cables, backup power, and heavy cooling to stop it all from overheating. When you open a cloud file, your device sends a request over the internet to that building, and the building sends your data back.

steps.prompt: Why do data centers need heavy cooling systems?
options: To keep employees comfortable
Because racks of running servers produce a lot of heat
To freeze the data for storage
steps.explain: Because the servers run hot. Packed racks of computers throw off heat constantly, and without strong cooling the hardware would overheat and fail.

steps.title: Your file is usually kept in more than one place
steps.body: A single hard drive can die. If your only copy were on one disk, a failure would erase it. So providers keep several copies on different drives, often in different locations. When one drive fails, the others still have your file. That is why cloud storage usually survives hardware that does not.

steps.prompt: A drive in the data center dies overnight. Why is your cloud photo probably still fine?
options: The photo was never really stored on a drive
Providers usually keep multiple copies on different drives
The cloud rebuilds files from the air
steps.explain: Because there is more than one copy. Remember, your file lives on real drives, and one drive can fail. Keeping several copies is how the cloud survives a dead disk.

steps.title: That's the cloud.
recap: The cloud is renting space on someone else's computer.
Those computers sit in data centers with power and cooling.
Your file is usually copied across several drives.

subject: HOW THINGS WORK
title: Why 60Hz vs 120Hz Actually Matters
blurb: A higher refresh rate does not add detail. It adds smoothness, by drawing more often.

steps.title: Why 60Hz vs 120Hz actually matters
steps.sub: People say a 120Hz screen looks 'better.' Better how? Not sharper. Smoother. Here is the difference.

steps.prompt: You hold a 60Hz and a 120Hz phone side by side, both showing the same still photo. Which looks sharper?
options: The 120Hz one
Neither, they look the same
The 60Hz one
steps.explain: Neither. On a frozen image there is nothing to update faster, so they look identical. Refresh rate is about motion, not detail. The difference only shows up when things move.

steps.title: Your screen redraws itself constantly
steps.body: A screen does not hold a moving image. It paints a still picture, wipes it, and paints the next one, over and over, fast enough that your eye reads it as motion. Each painting is one frame. Refresh rate, measured in hertz, is just how many of these you get per second. 60Hz is sixty redraws a second. 120Hz is a hundred and twenty.

steps.prompt: What does a refresh rate of 120Hz mean?
options: The screen has 120 times more pixels
The screen redraws the image 120 times per second
The screen is 120 percent brighter
steps.explain: It redraws 120 times per second. Hertz counts redraws, not pixels or brightness. More redraws means more chances to update what is on screen.

steps.title: More redraws means smoother motion
steps.body: When you scroll, the text jumps from one position to the next on every frame. At 60Hz it jumps 60 times a second. At 120Hz it jumps twice as often, so each jump is smaller and the motion looks fluid instead of steppy. That is the whole feel of a high refresh rate. It does not add detail. It fills in the gaps between positions.

steps.prompt: Why does scrolling feel smoother on a 120Hz screen?
options: The text is rendered at higher resolution
The screen updates more often, so each step in the motion is smaller
The screen is brighter while moving
steps.explain: More frequent updates. More redraws per second means the moving content shifts in smaller, more frequent steps, which your eye reads as smooth.

steps.title: The content has to keep up too
steps.body: A 120Hz screen can show 120 frames a second, but only if something is feeding it that many. If a video was made at 30 frames a second, the screen just shows each frame longer, and you gain nothing. Fast refresh helps most where the device generates frames on the fly, like scrolling and games. That is also why phones drop the rate on a static screen, to save power.

steps.prompt: A friend says their new 120Hz phone makes photos look more detailed. What is the correction?
options: Refresh rate raises resolution
Refresh rate affects motion smoothness, not still detail
Photos only look good at 60Hz
steps.explain: Refresh rate is about motion, not detail. Remember, on a still image both rates look the same. Sharpness comes from resolution, which is a separate thing.

steps.title: That's refresh rate.
recap: A screen makes motion by redrawing frames many times a second.
More redraws means smoother motion, not more detail.
The benefit only shows when the content supplies enough frames.

subject: HOW THINGS WORK
title: What 'Pairing' Really Does
blurb: Connecting happens every time. Pairing happens once, and it is why reconnecting is automatic.

steps.title: What pairing really does
steps.sub: You pair earbuds once. After that they just connect. Those are two different events, and the first sets up the second.

steps.prompt: Why do you only pair earbuds the first time, but connect every day after?
options: Pairing wears out after one use
Pairing saves a shared secret, so later they just recognize each other
Connecting is the same as pairing, just faster
steps.explain: Pairing stores a shared secret on both devices. Once they each remember it, later sessions only need to recognize each other and reconnect. The hard part already happened once.

steps.title: Bluetooth is just radio in the open air
steps.body: Bluetooth devices talk over radio waves, which spread out in every direction. Anything nearby with the right antenna can hear them. So the devices face a problem: how do you know the signal is coming from your earbuds and not someone else's, and how do you keep a stranger from listening in? That problem is what pairing solves.

steps.prompt: Why do Bluetooth devices need a way to recognize and protect their link?
options: Radio waves spread out and others nearby can pick them up
Radio only works one direction at a time
Bluetooth uses wires that can be tapped
steps.explain: Because radio spreads in all directions. Nearby devices can hear the signal, so yours need a way to know they are talking to the right partner and keep others out.

steps.title: Pairing swaps a secret key, once
steps.body: When you pair, the two devices agree on a shared secret key and each saves it. That key does two jobs. It proves identity, because only the real partner has the matching key, and it scrambles the messages so an eavesdropper hears noise. This swap is the one-time setup. It is the part you have to approve.

steps.prompt: What do two devices end up with after pairing?
options: A physical wire between them
A shared secret key that each device stores
A brand new radio frequency no one else uses
steps.explain: A shared secret key, saved on both. That key lets them prove who they are and scramble what they send, which is exactly what the open-air radio problem needed.

steps.title: The PIN or code confirms it is the right device
steps.body: Sometimes pairing asks you to enter a PIN or confirm a number shown on both screens. That step is not busywork. It makes sure the device you are setting up the secret with is really yours, not an impostor sitting in range pretending to be your headphones. Once you confirm, the key is trusted and stored.

steps.prompt: Put the life of a Bluetooth link in order, from first time to everyday use.
items: Devices find each other over radio
You confirm with a PIN or matching code
They swap and save a shared secret key
Later, they recognize each other and reconnect automatically

glosses.Devices find each other over radio: Discovery: each is listening on the air
glosses.You confirm with a PIN or matching code: You verify it is the right device

steps.prompt: You tap 'forget this device' on your phone, then try to use the earbuds. Why won't they just connect?
options: The earbuds are broken
Forgetting deleted the stored secret key, so you must pair again
Bluetooth turned itself off
steps.explain: Forgetting erased the saved key. Remember, automatic reconnecting works because both sides stored that secret. With it gone, there is nothing to recognize, so you have to pair from scratch.

steps.title: That's pairing.
recap: Bluetooth is open-air radio, so devices must prove who they are.
Pairing swaps and saves a shared secret key, once.
That stored key is why reconnecting later is automatic.

subject: HOW THINGS WORK
title: Deleting a File Doesn't Erase It
blurb: Emptying the trash removes the signpost, not the data. That is why recovery works.

steps.title: Deleting a file doesn't erase it
steps.sub: You drag it to the trash and empty it. It feels gone. The data is usually still sitting right there. Here is why.

steps.prompt: You empty the trash. What most likely happens to the file's actual data on the disk?
options: It is wiped clean off the disk
It stays put, just marked as free space
It is shredded into random pieces
steps.explain: It stays put. Emptying the trash usually just marks that space as available. The data itself sits there untouched until something new gets written over it.

steps.title: The drive keeps an index, like a table of contents
steps.body: A drive does not hunt for your files by reading the whole disk. It keeps an index: a list of which files exist and exactly where on the disk each one's data lives. When you open a file, the system checks the index for the address, then jumps straight to that spot. The index is the map. The data is the territory.

steps.prompt: What is the index on a drive for?
options: It stores a backup copy of every file
It records which files exist and where their data sits
It speeds up the internet connection
steps.explain: It records what exists and where. The index is the map that points to each file's data, so the system can find it without scanning the entire disk.

steps.title: Deleting just removes the entry
steps.body: When you empty the trash, the fast thing to do is cross the file out of the index and mark its space as free. The system does exactly that. It does not bother scrubbing the actual data, because that would take time and there is no need. The file's data is now an orphan: still on the disk, but with no signpost pointing to it.

steps.prompt: When you empty the trash, what does the system actually do first?
options: Overwrites the file with zeros
Removes the file's entry from the index and frees its space
Copies the file to a hidden folder
steps.explain: It removes the index entry and frees the space. That is fast, and it is enough to make the file 'gone' from your view, even though the data has not moved.

steps.title: Recovery and real erasing both come from this
steps.body: Because the data is still there, recovery software can scan the disk, find the orphaned data, and rebuild the file, as long as nothing has been written over it yet. Once new files land on that space, the original is overwritten and gone for good. That is also why truly erasing something means overwriting it or destroying the drive, not just hitting delete.

steps.prompt: Put the life of a deleted file in order.
items: You empty the trash
The index entry is removed and the space is marked free
The data still sits on the disk, unreferenced
New data is written over that space
The original file is now unrecoverable

steps.prompt: You delete a tax document, then realize you needed it. Why is recovery software sometimes able to bring it back?
options: Deleting only hides the file in a secret folder
The data stays on disk until something overwrites it
The trash keeps a permanent copy
steps.explain: Because the data is still physically there. Remember, deleting just removed the index entry and freed the space. Until new data lands on that spot, the original can often be rebuilt.

steps.title: That's deletion.
recap: The drive finds files through an index, a map to the data.
Deleting removes the index entry, not the data itself.
The data survives until overwritten, which is why recovery works.

subject: HOW THINGS WORK
title: More Megapixels Won't Fix Your Photos
blurb: A phone with a huge megapixel number can still take worse photos than an old camera.

steps.title: More megapixels won't fix your photos
steps.sub: The number on the box is the easiest thing to sell and the easiest thing to misread. By the end you'll know what actually drives a sharp, clean photo.

steps.prompt: Two cameras. One has 12 megapixels, the other 48. Same scene, dim room. Which is more likely to give the cleaner photo?
options: The 48 megapixel one, always
It depends more on the sensor and light than the count
They will look identical
steps.explain: Megapixels only count the pixels. They say nothing about how much light each pixel gathers. In a dim room, the camera that collects more light per pixel usually wins, and that comes down to sensor size and the lens, not the headline number.

steps.title: A megapixel is just a count
steps.body: One megapixel is one million pixels. Pixels are the tiny points that capture the image. So a higher megapixel count means more points, which can mean more detail if everything else is good. But the count alone tells you nothing about how good each point is at its job, which is catching light.

steps.prompt: What does the megapixel number actually tell you?
options: How much light the camera collects
How many pixels make up the image
How good the lens is
steps.explain: It is only a count of pixels. It says nothing about light collection or lens quality. Treat it as one spec among many, not a quality score.

steps.title: Light lives on the sensor
steps.body: Every pixel sits on the sensor, a chip that collects light. A bigger sensor has more area, so it gathers more light overall. Cram more megapixels onto the same small sensor and each pixel shrinks, so each one catches less light. Less light per pixel means more grainy noise, especially in the dark. That is why a tiny sensor with a huge number can look worse than a bigger sensor with a modest one.

steps.prompt: You pack more megapixels onto the same small sensor. What happens to each pixel?
options: It gets smaller and catches less light
It gets bigger and catches more light
Nothing, pixels are a fixed size
steps.explain: Fixed area split into more pixels means each pixel is smaller. Smaller pixels gather less light, so noise tends to rise in dim scenes. The big number can actually hurt you.

steps.title: You rarely use them all
steps.body: Most photos end up on a screen or shared online, where a handful of megapixels is plenty. The extra resolution mostly helps if you crop hard or print large. So past a point, more megapixels add file size, not visible quality. What you feel as a better photo usually comes from good light and a good lens.

steps.prompt: A phone brags about 108 megapixels but the photos look grainy indoors. Best single explanation?
options: The count is too high to process
Tiny pixels on a small sensor catch little light each
The screen is showing it wrong
steps.explain: Same idea as before. A giant count on a small sensor means very small pixels, and small pixels struggle in low light. The headline number went up while the light per pixel went down.

steps.title: That's the real spec.
recap: Megapixels just count pixels, nothing more.
Sensor size and light drive low-light quality.
More pixels on a small sensor can look worse.

subject: HOW THINGS WORK
title: Why You Really Use Airplane Mode
blurb: Your phone isn't going to crash the plane. The real reasons are stranger and more practical.

steps.title: Why you really use airplane mode
steps.sub: The cabin announcement makes it sound like your phone could drop the plane. The honest reasons are less dramatic and more interesting.

steps.prompt: Why does leaving your phone on full power during a flight cause real problems?
options: It can directly crash the flight computers
It hunts hard for towers, draining battery and pinging many at once
It overheats and melts
steps.explain: The headline fear, crashing the plane, is not the real story. The practical issue is that your phone keeps shouting for a tower it can barely reach. That hunt drains your battery, and from the sky it can hit many towers at once, which is not how the network expects phones to behave.

steps.title: It's a radio kill switch
steps.body: Airplane mode does one simple thing. It shuts off your phone's radio transmitters, the parts that send signals out: cellular, Wi-Fi, and Bluetooth. The screen, camera, and saved files all still work. You are not turning the phone off. You are telling it to stop broadcasting.

steps.prompt: What does airplane mode actually switch off?
options: The whole phone
The radio transmitters that send signals out
Just the cell signal, never Wi-Fi
steps.explain: It cuts the transmitters: cellular, Wi-Fi, Bluetooth. The rest of the phone keeps running. That is why you can still watch a downloaded movie with airplane mode on.

steps.title: A phone shouts when it's lost
steps.body: When your phone can't find a strong tower, it does not give up quietly. It turns up its transmit power and keeps searching. That extra effort burns battery fast. At altitude it gets worse: instead of one nearby tower, your phone may reach many far-off towers at once, which the network was never built to handle cleanly.

steps.prompt: Why does a phone with a weak signal drain its battery faster?
options: It dims the screen less
It raises transmit power to keep hunting for a tower
It downloads more data
steps.explain: Weak signal makes the phone push harder, raising its transmit power to reach a distant tower. More power out means more battery gone. A phone stuck searching is a phone draining itself.

steps.title: The rule loosened
steps.body: Regulators long required airplane mode mostly as a precaution against possible interference, not because of proven crashes. As confidence grew, the rules eased. Many airlines now let you switch Wi-Fi and Bluetooth back on while airplane mode handles the cellular radio. So you can use in-flight Wi-Fi and wireless earbuds without breaking the rule.

steps.prompt: You forget airplane mode and land with a dead battery after a long flight. Most likely cause?
options: The plane's systems drained it
Your phone spent hours at high power hunting for towers
The cabin was too cold
steps.explain: Same idea from before. With no steady tower, the phone cranks up power and keeps searching for hours. That constant high-power hunt is what flattens the battery, not anything the plane did.

steps.title: That's the real reason.
recap: Airplane mode just cuts your radio transmitters.
A lost phone raises power and drains the battery.
Wi-Fi and Bluetooth are often allowed back on.

subject: HOW THINGS WORK
title: What a Website Cookie Actually Is
blurb: A cookie is just a little note your browser hands back each visit. That's how sites remember you.

steps.title: What a website cookie actually is
steps.sub: The word sounds friendly and vague. It is actually a precise, simple thing, and knowing it explains how sites remember you and how ads follow you.

steps.prompt: A site keeps you logged in across visits. How does it most likely remember it's you?
options: It stores your face
Your browser hands back a small text note it saved earlier
It guesses from your typing speed
steps.explain: The site asked your browser to keep a small note last time. On your return, the browser hands that note back, and the site reads it to recognize you. That note is a cookie. No magic, just a text label you carry.

steps.title: It's a note you carry
steps.body: A cookie is a small piece of text a website asks your browser to store on your device. Next time you visit that site, your browser sends the note back. The site reads it and knows something, like that you are logged in or what was in your cart. The cookie lives on your machine, not theirs.

steps.prompt: Where does a cookie actually live?
options: On the website's server
On your own device, stored by your browser
Floating on the internet
steps.explain: Your browser stores it on your device. The site only sees it again when your browser hands it back on a later visit. That hand-back is the whole mechanism.

steps.title: First-party versus follow-you
steps.body: A cookie set by the site you are visiting is first-party, and it is what keeps you logged in. But pages often embed content from other companies, like ad networks. Those can set third-party cookies. Because the same ad company appears on many sites, its cookie lets it recognize you across all of them. That is how an ad seems to follow you around the web.

steps.prompt: Why can a third-party cookie track you across many different websites?
options: It copies itself to every site
The same outside company is embedded on all of them and reads its own cookie
It follows your mouse
steps.explain: The advertiser's code sits on many sites. Each time, it reads the cookie it set before, so it links your visits together. Same company, same note, many pages. That is cross-site tracking.

steps.title: You hold the notes
steps.body: Because cookies sit on your device, you control them. Your browser settings let you see them and delete them. Clear them and sites forget you, which means logging in again but also shedding trackers. This is why a deleted cookie logs you out: the note that proved who you were is gone.

steps.prompt: You clear all cookies, then revisit a site you were logged into. What happens, and why?
options: Still logged in, cookies don't affect login
Logged out, because the note proving who you were is gone
The site crashes
steps.explain: The login cookie was the note your browser handed back to prove it was you. Delete it and there is nothing to hand back, so the site no longer recognizes you and you start fresh.

steps.title: That's the cookie.
recap: A cookie is a small text note your browser stores.
First-party notes remember you on one site.
Third-party notes let one company track you across many.

subject: HOW THINGS WORK
title: Why USB-C Plugs In Both Ways
blurb: The old USB never went in first try. USB-C fixed it with a simple trick: symmetry.

steps.title: Why USB-C plugs in both ways
steps.sub: The old plug fought you every time. The new one just works. The difference is one design choice, and it is easy to see.

steps.prompt: The old USB plug only worked one way up. What's the simplest reason?
options: It had contacts on only one side
It needed software to detect direction
The cable was too stiff
steps.explain: The old rectangular USB-A had its contacts on just one side. Flip it and the metal pins no longer line up with anything, so it does not work. There was a right way and a wrong way, and you could not see which from the outside.

steps.title: The old plug had a wrong way
steps.body: Look inside an old USB-A port and you see a plastic tongue with contacts on one face only. The plug matches that on a single side. So only one orientation connects. The other one just presses plastic against plastic. That is why it never seemed to go in on the first try.

steps.prompt: Why did the old USB-A only work one way up?
options: Its contacts were on only one side
It locked when inserted wrong
It charged in only one direction
steps.explain: Contacts on one side means only one orientation lines them up. Flip it and there is nothing to meet the pins. One working way, one dead way.

steps.title: USB-C is mirrored
steps.body: USB-C puts the same pins on both the top and the bottom, mirrored. So whichever way you flip it, a matching set of contacts meets the port. Both orientations are correct. There is no wrong way up. The plug is symmetric, and that symmetry is the entire trick.

steps.prompt: What lets USB-C go in either way up?
options: A sensor that detects orientation
Mirrored pins on both top and bottom
A spring that flips the plug
steps.explain: The pins are mirrored, so both faces carry a working set of contacts. Either orientation lines up. No sensor, no spring, just a symmetric layout.

steps.prompt: A friend says USB-C uses a chip to sense which way you inserted it. True or not, and why?
options: True, it senses direction
Not the reason, it works either way because the pins are mirrored
True, it flips the data inside
steps.explain: No sensing is needed for orientation. Both sides have matching contacts, so either way up simply connects. The symmetry does the job that a sensor would otherwise have to.

steps.title: That's the trick.
recap: Old USB-A had contacts on one side only.
USB-C mirrors its pins top and bottom.
Symmetry, not sensing, makes it reversible.

subject: HOW THINGS WORK
title: Modem vs Router: They're Not the Same Box
blurb: One box talks to your internet provider. The other shares that one connection with your devices.

steps.title: Modem vs router: not the same box
steps.sub: People use the words as if they mean one thing. They are two jobs, and knowing which does what makes home internet far less mysterious.

steps.prompt: Your internet is down. The provider says the line is fine to your house. Which box most likely shares that connection to all your devices?
options: The modem
The router
Neither, the devices connect directly
steps.explain: The modem brings the internet into the house. The router is the box that takes that single connection and shares it out to your phone, laptop, and TV, usually over Wi-Fi. If the line is fine but only one device works, suspect the router's sharing job.

steps.title: The modem talks to your provider
steps.body: The modem is the box wired to the line coming into your home, the cable or fiber or phone line. Its job is to connect you to your internet service provider. It translates the provider's signal into something your home gear can use. One job: bring the internet to the house.

steps.prompt: What is the modem's main job?
options: Sharing internet to all your devices
Connecting your home to your internet provider
Creating your Wi-Fi name
steps.explain: The modem links your home to the provider over the incoming line. That is the one thing it does. Sharing and Wi-Fi are someone else's job.

steps.title: The router shares it out
steps.body: The router takes that single connection from the modem and shares it among all your devices. It builds the local network in your home, including the Wi-Fi your phone and laptop join. Without a modem, a router has no internet to share. But it can still connect your devices to each other on the local network.

steps.prompt: You unplug the modem but leave the router on. What can the router still do?
options: Give every device full internet
Connect your devices to each other on the local network
Nothing at all
steps.explain: With no modem, there is no internet coming in to share. But the router still runs the local network, so devices can still talk to each other. The internet is gone, the local network is not.

steps.prompt: Order the path the internet signal takes to reach your phone.
items: Provider's line enters the home
Modem connects to the provider
Router shares the connection
Your phone joins over Wi-Fi

glosses.Provider's line enters the home: The cable or fiber from outside.
glosses.Modem connects to the provider: Brings the internet into the house.
glosses.Your phone joins over Wi-Fi: A device on the local network.

steps.prompt: Your provider hands you one box and calls it a 'gateway.' What's most likely inside?
options: Only a modem
Both a modem and a router combined
Only a router
steps.explain: Many providers combine both jobs in one unit. It connects to the provider like a modem and shares to your devices like a router. One box, two jobs, which is exactly why people confuse the terms.

steps.title: Two boxes, two jobs.
recap: The modem connects your home to the provider.
The router shares that connection to your devices.
A combined gateway does both jobs in one box.

subject: HOW THINGS WORK
title: How Headphones Cancel Noise With More Noise
blurb: Noise-cancelling headphones don't block sound. They add a second sound that erases the first.

steps.title: Fighting noise with noise
steps.sub: Noise-cancelling headphones don't wall sound out. They make their own sound on purpose. Two sounds meet and flatten each other.

steps.prompt: To cancel the drone of a plane, the headphones play...
options: Total silence, very loudly
A sound that is the mirror image of the drone
A higher pitch that drowns the drone out
steps.explain: A mirror image. Sound is a wave of pushing and pulling air. If the headphone pushes exactly when the noise pulls, the two cancel. You can't play silence, but you can play the opposite.

steps.title: Sound is moving pressure
steps.body: A sound is just air pressure going up and down very fast. A speaker pushes air to make a high-pressure peak, then pulls back to make a low-pressure dip. That pattern of peaks and dips races to your ear. Louder sound means bigger peaks and dips. Higher pitch means they come faster.

steps.title: Two waves add up
steps.body: When two sounds reach the same spot, their pressures add at every instant. Line up two matching waves peak on peak and you get a bigger wave, louder. But flip one upside down, so its peak lands on the other's dip, and they cancel. High plus low equals nothing. That flipped-over copy is the whole trick.

steps.prompt: Why does an upside-down copy of a sound cancel it?
options: It is louder, so it wins
Its peaks land on the original's dips, and they add to near zero
It travels faster and gets there first
steps.explain: Peaks meet dips. Because pressures add at each instant, a high pushing one way and an equal low pulling the other way sum to nothing. Same size, opposite shape, net flat.

steps.title: A mic listens, a speaker answers
steps.body: A tiny microphone on the headphone hears the noise coming in. A chip instantly builds the flipped copy and the speaker plays it into your ear, on top of the leaking noise. The two collide and mostly disappear. It works best on steady low hums like engines and fans. Sudden, sharp, high sounds change too fast to catch and flip in time.

steps.prompt: Which sound will active cancelling handle worst?
options: The steady drone of a jet engine
A constant fan hum
A sudden cough next to you
steps.explain: The cough. Steady hums are easy to predict and flip. A sharp, sudden, high sound changes too fast for the mic and chip to mirror in time, so it slips through.

steps.prompt: What is a sound wave, physically?
options: Air pressure rising and falling fast
A stream of tiny particles flying at your ear
A glow that your ear converts to sound
steps.explain: Pressure going up and down. That is why an opposite wave can cancel it: you push when it pulls, and the air ends up still.

steps.title: That's noise cancelling.
recap: Sound is air pressure rising and falling.
An upside-down copy meets the noise and cancels it.
A mic listens, a chip flips it, a speaker answers.

subject: HOW THINGS WORK
title: Your Phone Doesn't Store Your Fingerprint
blurb: Fingerprint unlock never saves a picture of your finger. It saves a math summary, locked on a chip.

steps.title: No photo of your finger
steps.sub: People picture a tiny image of their fingerprint sitting in the phone. That's not what's saved. And what is saved barely leaves the chip.

steps.prompt: When you set up fingerprint unlock, the phone stores...
options: A photo of your fingerprint
A set of numbers describing your fingerprint's features
Your fingerprint uploaded to the maker's servers
steps.explain: A set of numbers. The sensor measures where your ridges split and end, then boils that down to a math summary called a template. No picture is kept, and it stays on the phone.

steps.title: It reads features, not a picture
steps.body: Your fingerprint is full of ridges that split and stop. The sensor finds those distinctive points and where they sit relative to each other. It turns that into a list of numbers, a template. It does not keep a photo. From the numbers alone you cannot rebuild a usable image of your finger, which is part of the point.

steps.prompt: Why is a math template safer to store than a photo?
options: It takes up less space
You generally can't turn it back into a usable image of the finger
It can be changed whenever you want
steps.explain: It is one-way. The template throws away most of the picture and keeps only enough to recognize you. If it leaks, there is no photo of your fingerprint to steal from it.

steps.title: It lives on a locked chip
steps.body: The template is kept in a walled-off part of the phone's chip, separate from your apps and even from the main operating system. Apps never see it. On Apple phones this area is called the Secure Enclave, and the company says the data does not leave the device or get backed up to its servers. When you touch the sensor, the new scan is checked inside that wall, and only a yes or no comes out.

steps.prompt: Where does the fingerprint check actually happen?
options: On the company's servers, after upload
Inside a walled-off part of the phone's chip
Inside whichever app asked for it
steps.explain: Inside the locked chip. The scan is compared there and only a yes or no leaves. Apps and the main system never get the template, so they cannot hand it to anyone.

steps.prompt: Put one unlock in order, from finger to open phone.
items: You press the sensor and it scans your ridges
The scan becomes a math template
The chip compares it to the saved template
A close enough match unlocks the phone

glosses.A close enough match unlocks the phone: Yes or no comes out, never the data

steps.prompt: So what is actually saved when you enroll a finger?
options: A photo of the fingerprint
A math template, a summary of its features
Nothing, it re-reads from the cloud each time
steps.explain: A math template. Not a picture, and not in the cloud. Just enough numbers to recognize you, kept on the locked chip.

steps.title: That's fingerprint unlock.
recap: The sensor saves features as numbers, not a photo.
The template lives on a walled-off chip, not the cloud.
Matching happens inside; only a yes or no leaves.

subject: HOW THINGS WORK
title: Why Free Coffee-Shop WiFi Is Risky
blurb: On open WiFi, strangers can sometimes snoop or fake the hotspot. HTTPS and a VPN are why it still works out.

steps.title: The risk of free WiFi
steps.sub: Open coffee-shop WiFi isn't dangerous because it's free. It's risky because of two things: who can listen, and who you're really connecting to.

steps.prompt: On open WiFi with no password, your link to the router is...
options: Encrypted, the same as a home network
Not encrypted, so nearby devices may see traffic that isn't itself protected
Invisible to everyone but you
steps.explain: Not encrypted. A password also sets up encryption on the WiFi link. With no password, that link is open, so anything not protected another way can be seen by others nearby.

steps.title: Open means the air is shared
steps.body: WiFi is radio. On a passworded network, that radio link is scrambled so only your device and the router understand it. An open network skips that scrambling. So traffic that isn't protected some other way can be picked up by other devices in range. The danger isn't the price. It's that the link itself is wide open.

steps.prompt: Why is an open network easier to snoop on than a passworded one?
options: Open networks are slower
A password also encrypts the WiFi link, and open networks skip that
Open networks reach farther
steps.explain: The password does double duty. It controls access and scrambles the radio link. Without it, unprotected traffic on the shared air is readable by others in range.

steps.title: The hotspot might be fake
steps.body: Anyone can name a hotspot anything. Someone can set up their own network called something like the cafe's name. Your device connects, trusting the name, and now all your traffic flows through a stranger's gear. This is called an evil twin. The fix is not to trust the network at all, but to protect your traffic no matter what it rides over.

steps.title: HTTPS and a VPN do the protecting
steps.body: HTTPS scrambles what you send to and from a website, so even on open WiFi a snoop sees gibberish, not your password or messages. That is why the padlock matters most here. But others may still see which sites you reach. A VPN goes further: it encrypts all your device's traffic to a far server, hiding both the contents and the destinations from anyone on the local network.

steps.prompt: On open WiFi, what does HTTPS protect?
options: The fact that you used WiFi at all
The contents of your traffic with a website
Nothing, HTTPS only works at home
steps.explain: The contents. HTTPS scrambles what passes between you and the site, so a snoop gets gibberish. They may still see which site, which is where a VPN adds cover.

steps.prompt: Why does the WiFi password matter beyond keeping strangers off?
options: It also encrypts the radio link between you and the router
It makes the connection faster
It hides the network name
steps.explain: It encrypts the link too. That is the real gap with open WiFi: no password means no scrambling on the shared air, so unprotected traffic is exposed.

steps.title: That's open WiFi.
recap: No password means no encryption on the shared air.
A hotspot's name can be faked, so don't trust the network.
HTTPS hides your contents; a VPN hides contents and destinations.

subject: MATH & LOGIC
title: Why 23 People Beat the Odds
blurb: In a room of 23 people, it's more likely than not that two share a birthday.

steps.title: A room of 23
steps.sub: There are 365 days in a year, so a birthday match in a small group feels rare. With just 23 people, it is actually the more likely outcome. Here is why your gut is wrong.

steps.prompt: In a room of 23 people, the chance that two share a birthday is about...
options: Around 6 percent
Around 50 percent
Around 95 percent
steps.explain: It is just over 50 percent. More likely than not. The reason is that you are not looking for one match, you are looking across every possible pair at once.

steps.title: Count pairs, not people
steps.body: Your gut compares yourself to 22 others and thinks: slim chance. But the question is whether ANY two people match. With 23 people the number of pairs is 23 times 22 divided by 2, which is 253 pairs. That is a lot of chances for a match.

steps.prompt: Why does the chance climb so fast with only 23 people?
options: Each person is compared only to you
There are 253 possible pairs, not 23 comparisons
Birthdays cluster in summer
steps.explain: The match can happen between any two of them. Counting all pairs gives 253 chances, far more than the 22 your gut imagined.

steps.title: Flip the question
steps.body: The clean way to compute it: find the chance everyone's birthday is different, then subtract from 1. Person by person, the odds of staying all-different shrink fast. By 23 people that all-different chance drops below half, so the chance of a match rises above half.

steps.prompt: How do you find the chance of at least one shared birthday?
options: Add up each person's chance of matching
Subtract the chance everyone is different from 1
Divide 365 by the number of people
steps.explain: Computing every way a match could happen is messy. The chance that nobody matches is easy, so you take 1 minus that to get the chance of at least one match.

steps.prompt: How many distinct pairs are there among 23 people?
steps.explain: 253, from 23 times 22 divided by 2. That pile of pairs, not a one-to-one comparison, is why a match passes even odds at 23.

steps.title: Now you get it
recap: The match can be between any two people, so count pairs.
23 people make 253 pairs, far more chances than they feel.
Subtract the all-different chance from 1 to get the answer.

subject: MATH & LOGIC
title: The Coin Has No Memory
blurb: Five heads in a row doesn't make tails 'due.' The next flip is still 50/50.

steps.title: Heads again. And again.
steps.sub: Five heads in a row and tails starts to feel overdue. That feeling has a name, and it has cost people a lot of money. Let's dismantle it.

steps.prompt: A fair coin lands heads five times in a row. What is the chance the next flip is tails?
options: More than half; tails is due
Exactly half
Less than half; heads is hot
steps.explain: Still exactly half. The coin cannot remember its last five flips. Nothing about the past changes the odds of the next throw.

steps.title: The coin has no memory
steps.body: Each flip is independent. The coin does not know or care what came before. It has no way to keep score and no urge to balance out. Every single flip is a fresh 50/50, no matter how long the streak behind it.

steps.prompt: Why doesn't a streak of heads change the next flip?
options: Each flip is independent of the ones before
The coin slowly wears down toward tails
Long streaks are against the rules of chance
steps.explain: Independence means the past leaves no trace on the next flip. There is no memory and no force pulling things back toward balance.

steps.title: Rare to predict, not to continue
steps.body: Here is the trap. Five heads in a row was unlikely to call in advance: a one in thirty-two shot. But once those five have happened, they are done. The sixth flip is not part of that prediction anymore. It is just one new flip, 50/50.

steps.prompt: Why doesn't 'five heads was rare' make tails more likely now?
options: Because the rare run already happened; the next flip stands alone
Because rare events must reverse
Because the odds reset after five
steps.explain: The rarity was about predicting the whole run ahead of time. That run is locked in. The next flip is a separate, fresh 50/50.

steps.prompt: The belief that a streak makes the opposite result 'due' is called the...
options: Birthday paradox
Gambler's fallacy
Law of averages
steps.explain: The gambler's fallacy. It fails because independent flips have no memory, so nothing is ever owed or due.

steps.title: Now you get it
recap: Each fair flip is an independent 50/50.
The coin has no memory and nothing is ever due.
A run is rare to predict, but the next flip stands alone.

subject: MATH & LOGIC
title: Most People Have More Than the Average Number of Legs
blurb: One number can hide the whole story. A few outliers drag the mean away from the typical case.

steps.title: The average can lie
steps.sub: Most people have more than the average number of legs. That sounds like nonsense. It is true, and it reveals exactly how a single average can mislead you.

steps.prompt: Can most people have MORE than the average number of legs?
options: No, that is impossible by definition
Yes, if a few people drag the average below two
steps.explain: A small number of people have fewer than two legs, and almost no one has more. So the average sits just under two, while most people have exactly two, which is above that average.

steps.title: The mean gets dragged
steps.body: The mean adds everything up and divides by the count. That means every value tugs on it. A few people with one leg or none pull the leg average down below two. Most people have exactly two, so most people sit above an average that the outliers dragged down.

steps.prompt: Why does the average number of legs fall below two?
options: Many people have three legs
A few people with fewer than two legs pull the mean down
Two is not a whole number of legs
steps.explain: The mean is tugged by every value. A small group below two is enough to pull the average just under two, even though they are rare.

steps.title: The median holds steady
steps.body: The median is just the middle value when you line everyone up in order. It ignores how extreme the outliers are; it only cares about position. Line up everyone by leg count and the person in the middle has two. So the median is two: the truly typical number.

steps.prompt: What is the median number of legs?
options: Just below two
Exactly two
It cannot be found
steps.explain: Line everyone up in order and the middle person has two legs. The median ignores how far the outliers reach, so it lands on the typical value.

steps.prompt: A small town's mean income is far higher than its median. What does that tell you?
options: Almost everyone is rich
A few very high earners pull the mean up
The two numbers must be a mistake
steps.explain: When the mean sits well above the median, a few extreme values are stretching it upward. Most people earn near the lower median, not the inflated mean.

steps.prompt: Which measure gets pulled toward outliers?
options: The median
The mean
Neither one
steps.explain: The mean. Every value tugs on it, so extremes drag it around. The median just marks the middle, so it stays near the typical case.

steps.title: Now you get it
recap: The mean is tugged by every value, so outliers drag it.
The median marks the middle and resists outliers.
When mean and median split, ask what the average is hiding.

subject: MATH & LOGIC
title: Going From 1% to 2% Isn't a 1% Rise
blurb: A rate that climbs from 1% to 2% doubled. That's a 100% increase, not a 1% one.

steps.title: One little word changes everything
steps.sub: A headline says a rate went up 1%. Another says it went up 1 percentage point. They sound the same. They can mean wildly different things.

steps.prompt: An unemployment rate rises from 1% to 2%. By how much did it increase?
options: Both can be correct, depending on how you measure
steps.explain: Both. It went up 1 percentage point, the plain gap between 1 and 2. But relative to where it started, 2 is double 1, so it also rose 100%. Same change, two honest numbers.

steps.title: A percentage point is just the gap
steps.body: A percentage point is the simple subtraction. Go from 1% to 2% and you moved 1 percentage point. Go from 40% to 45% and you moved 5 percentage points. You are just counting the distance on the percent scale. It ignores where you started.

steps.prompt: A vote share goes from 40% to 45%. How many percentage points is that?
options: 5 percentage points
12.5 percentage points
45 percentage points
steps.explain: 5 points. A percentage point is just the gap: 45 minus 40. It doesn't care that 40 was the starting line.

steps.title: A percent change is relative
steps.body: A percent change asks how big the move was compared to where you started. From 1% to 2%, the move of 1 is the same size as the original 1, so that's a 100% increase. From 4% to 5%, the move of 1 is a quarter of the original 4, so that's a 25% increase. Same 1-point gap, very different percent.

steps.prompt: An interest rate goes from 4% to 5%. As a percent change, that's roughly...
options: A 1% increase
A 25% increase
A 100% increase
steps.explain: A 25% increase. The 1-point jump is a quarter of the starting 4, and a quarter is 25%. It's still only 1 percentage point, but relative to 4 it's a 25% rise.

steps.prompt: A rate climbs from 1% to 2%. Which statement is the relative, percent-change view?
options: It rose 1 percentage point
It rose 100%
It rose 2 points
steps.explain: It rose 100%. Relative to the starting 1, the jump of 1 is the whole thing again, so the rate doubled. The 1-percentage-point version is the plain gap, not the relative view.

steps.title: That's the difference.
recap: A percentage point is the plain gap between two rates.
A percent change measures the move relative to the start.
1% to 2% is 1 point but a 100% rise.

subject: MATH & LOGIC
title: A Million Seconds vs a Billion Seconds
blurb: A million seconds is about 12 days. A billion is about 32 years. Your gut can't feel the gap.

steps.title: Million, billion, same shape
steps.sub: The two words rhyme and sit side by side, so they feel like neighbors. They are not. One is a blink; the other is a lifetime.

steps.prompt: A million seconds is about 12 days. Roughly how long is a billion seconds?
options: About 4 months
About 3 years
About 32 years
steps.explain: About 32 years. A billion is a thousand times a million, so a billion seconds is a thousand times longer than 12 days. That lands at roughly 32 years, not months.

steps.title: A billion is a thousand millions
steps.body: The jump from million to billion is not one notch. A billion is a thousand millions. So whatever a million of something is, a billion of it is a thousand times more. The words are short and similar, which hides a gap of three zeros.

steps.prompt: How many times bigger is a billion than a million?
options: 10 times
100 times
1,000 times
steps.explain: A thousand times. A billion is a thousand millions. That single step adds three zeros, which is why the two numbers behave nothing alike.

steps.title: Turn them into time
steps.body: Counting one number per second, a million seconds runs out in about 12 days. A billion seconds takes about 32 years. The first is a long vacation. The second is most of a working life. Same gap of a thousand, but now you can feel it.

steps.prompt: Counting one per second, a million seconds is closest to...
options: About 12 hours
About 12 days
About 12 months
steps.explain: About 12 days. A million seconds is roughly 11.6 days. The billion version, a thousand times longer, stretches to about 32 years.

steps.prompt: Why is a billion seconds so much longer than a million seconds?
options: A billion is a thousand times a million
A billion is ten times a million
Seconds count differently at large numbers
steps.explain: A billion is a thousand millions. That factor of a thousand turns 12 days into about 32 years. The words look close; the sizes are a world apart.

steps.title: That's scale.
recap: A billion is a thousand times a million.
A million seconds is about 12 days.
A billion seconds is about 32 years.

subject: MATH & LOGIC
title: The Chessboard That Bankrupts a King
blurb: One grain on square one, then keep doubling. By square 64 you owe more rice than the world has ever grown.

steps.title: A modest-sounding reward
steps.sub: An old tale: a king offers a reward, and the inventor of chess asks only for rice on a chessboard. One grain on the first square, doubling each square. The king agrees. The king is doomed.

steps.prompt: One grain on square one, doubling each square across all 64. The final total is closest to...
options: A few truckloads of rice
A country's yearly harvest
More rice than the world has ever grown
steps.explain: More than the world has ever grown. The total is about 18 quintillion grains, far beyond any harvest in history. Doubling starts tiny and ends up astronomical.

steps.title: Doubling multiplies, it doesn't add
steps.body: Adding grows in a straight line: plus one, plus one. Doubling multiplies: each square is the whole pile so far, again. 1, 2, 4, 8, 16, 32. The early squares look harmless. But every step keeps multiplying, so the numbers don't just grow, they explode.

steps.prompt: What makes doubling different from adding the same amount each step?
options: Each step multiplies the running total instead of adding a fixed amount
It adds a slightly bigger number each time
Nothing; they end up the same
steps.explain: It multiplies. Adding crawls in a line. Doubling compounds the whole pile every step, so the total bends upward harder and harder.

steps.title: The end dwarfs the start
steps.body: With doubling, almost all the total hides in the last few steps. The 64th square alone holds 2 to the 63rd power grains, more than every earlier square combined. The whole board comes to about 18 quintillion grains, written as roughly 1.8 followed by 19 digits. No kingdom has that much rice.

steps.prompt: With doubling, where does almost all the total pile up?
options: Spread evenly across the board
In the first few squares
In the last few squares
steps.explain: In the last few squares. The 64th square alone beats every earlier square put together. That back-loading is what ambushes the king, and our intuition.

steps.prompt: Put the grain counts for the first five squares in order:
Why does the reward end up impossible to pay?
options: The squares add up slowly but there are many
Doubling multiplies the pile each step, so it explodes
Rice is expensive
steps.explain: Doubling multiplies. Each square is the whole pile again, so by square 64 the total reaches about 18 quintillion grains, more than the world has ever grown.

steps.title: That's exponential growth.
recap: Doubling multiplies the total, it doesn't add to it.
Almost all the total sits in the last few steps.
64 squares of doubling reach about 18 quintillion grains.

subject: MATH & LOGIC
title: Ice Cream Doesn't Cause Drowning
blurb: Ice cream sales and drownings rise together. Neither causes the other. Summer drives both.

steps.title: Two lines that rise together
steps.sub: Track ice cream sales and drownings across a year and they move in lockstep. Tempting story: ice cream is dangerous. The real story is the calendar.

steps.prompt: Ice cream sales and drownings rise and fall together all year. The best explanation is...
options: Ice cream makes people drown
A third thing, like warm weather, drives both
Drowning makes people buy ice cream
steps.explain: A third thing: summer. Heat sends people to buy ice cream and to swim, so both numbers climb together. Neither one is causing the other.

steps.title: Moving together isn't causing
steps.body: A correlation just means two things tend to move together. That's all it claims. It does not tell you which one, if either, is doing the pushing. Two lines can track perfectly because one causes the other, because they swap roles, or because something else moves them both.

steps.prompt: What does a correlation between two things actually tell you?
options: One of them causes the other
They tend to move together, nothing more
They are unrelated
steps.explain: Just that they move together. A correlation is a pattern, not a mechanism. By itself it never names the cause.

steps.title: Look for the hidden third thing
steps.body: Often a third factor quietly drives both. Here it's warm weather. It raises ice cream sales and it sends people swimming, which raises drownings. That hidden driver is called a confounder. Find it and the spooky link between ice cream and drowning dissolves.

steps.prompt: A hidden factor that drives both correlated things is called a...
options: Confounder
Coincidence
Constant
steps.explain: A confounder. It's the offstage cause moving both lines at once. Spot it and the apparent connection between the two often disappears.

steps.prompt: Why do ice cream sales and drownings track each other so closely?
options: Ice cream weighs swimmers down
Warm weather drives both at once
It's a pure fluke with no reason
steps.explain: Warm weather, the confounder. It pushes up ice cream sales and swimming together. The two lines move as one because something behind them does.

steps.title: That's the trap.
recap: Correlation means things move together, not that one causes the other.
A hidden confounder can drive both at once.
Always ask what third thing might explain the link.

subject: MATH & LOGIC
title: The 99% Accurate Test That's Usually Wrong
blurb: For a rare disease, a positive on a 99% accurate test is more likely a false alarm than the real thing.

steps.title: A scary result, a calmer reality
steps.sub: You take a test that's 99% accurate. It comes back positive. Sounds like near-certain bad news. For a rare disease, it usually isn't.

steps.prompt: A disease hits 1 in 1,000. A 99% accurate test says you're positive. What's the chance you actually have it?
options: About 99%
About 50%
Under 10%
steps.explain: Under 10%. The disease is so rare that the test's small error rate produces far more false alarms than true cases. The result feels certain but isn't.

steps.title: Rarity changes the math
steps.body: Picture 100,000 people. The disease hits 1 in 1,000, so about 100 are truly sick and 99,900 are healthy. That huge healthy group is the catch. Even a tiny error rate applied to 99,900 people produces a lot of wrong flags. The base rate, how common the disease is, sets the stage before the test even runs.

steps.prompt: In a group of 100,000 with a 1-in-1,000 disease, how many are actually sick?
options: About 100
About 1,000
About 10,000
steps.explain: About 100. One in a thousand of 100,000 is 100. The other 99,900 are healthy, and that enormous healthy crowd is where the false alarms come from.

steps.title: Count the positives
steps.body: The test catches 99% of the sick, so it flags about 99 of the 100 real cases. But it also wrongly flags 1% of the 99,900 healthy people, which is about 999 false alarms. Now add them up: roughly 99 true and 999 false. Out of every positive, only about 1 in 11 is real. Over 90% are false alarms.

steps.prompt: About 99 true positives and 999 false positives. So a positive result is real about...
options: 9 in 10 of the time
1 in 10 of the time
Always
steps.explain: About 1 in 10. There are roughly ten false alarms for every true case, so a positive is real less than 10% of the time. The flood of false positives swamps the few real ones.

steps.prompt: Why is a positive on this 99% accurate test usually a false alarm?
options: The test is secretly broken
The disease is so rare that false positives outnumber true ones
99% accuracy actually means 9% accuracy
steps.explain: The disease is rare. With only about 100 sick people but 99,900 healthy ones, the test's small error rate creates far more false alarms than real cases. Ignoring that base rate is the classic mistake.

steps.title: That's base rate neglect.
recap: A test's accuracy isn't the chance your positive is real.
For rare conditions, false alarms can outnumber true cases.
Always weigh how common the thing is first.

subject: MATH & LOGIC
title: Some Infinities Are Bigger Than Others
blurb: There are more decimals between 0 and 1 than there are whole numbers in all of counting.

steps.title: Some infinities are bigger
steps.sub: Infinity isn't one thing. There are bigger and smaller infinities, and you can prove it with a single clean trick.

steps.prompt: Which is the bigger collection: all the whole numbers, or all the decimals between 0 and 1?
options: Whole numbers, they go on forever
The decimals between 0 and 1
They're the same, both are infinite
steps.explain: The decimals win. 'Both infinite' feels right, but infinity comes in sizes. The decimals between 0 and 1 form a strictly larger infinity than every whole number combined. The rest of this shows why.

steps.title: Same size means you can pair them up
steps.body: To compare infinite sets you can't count them. Instead you pair them. Two sets are the same size if you can match each member of one to exactly one member of the other, with nothing left over on either side. Think of seats and people: if everyone has a seat and every seat is taken, the two groups are equal without counting either. Any infinity you can line up and pair with the whole numbers 1, 2, 3, ... is called countable.

steps.prompt: What makes two infinite sets count as the same size?
options: Both never end
You can pair their members one to one with none left over
They contain the same kinds of numbers
steps.explain: A perfect one-to-one pairing. Endlessness alone isn't enough, since all infinite sets share that. The test is whether you can match them up with nothing stranded, like seats and people.

steps.title: The list you can't finish
steps.body: Now try to list every decimal between 0 and 1, paired with 1, 2, 3, and so on. Cantor showed this fails. Take your list and build a new decimal: make its first digit differ from the first digit of decimal number one, its second digit differ from decimal number two, and so on down the diagonal. The result differs from every entry in at least one place, so it's not on your list. Your list was supposed to be complete and it isn't. No list can hold them all.

steps.prompt: Why does the new diagonal decimal prove the list is incomplete?
options: It's larger than every number on the list
It differs from each listed decimal in at least one digit, so it can't be any of them
It has infinitely many digits and the others don't
steps.explain: It's built to disagree with every row somewhere. Since it differs from entry one in digit one, from entry two in digit two, and so on, it can't equal any listed decimal. A complete list can't be missing a number, so no complete list exists.

steps.prompt: Put Cantor's diagonal argument in order.
items: Assume you can list every decimal between 0 and 1
Walk down the diagonal, digit by digit
Build a new decimal that differs from each row at that digit
Notice the new decimal is on no row, so the list was incomplete

glosses.Assume you can list every decimal betwee: Start by granting what you want to disprove.
glosses.Walk down the diagonal, digit by digit: First digit of row one, second of row two, and so on.

steps.prompt: Someone claims the whole numbers and the decimals between 0 and 1 are the same size. Using the pairing test, what's the flaw?
options: Both sets are infinite, so the claim is fine
No pairing can cover all the decimals, since the diagonal trick always builds one you missed
The decimals are finite, so they can't match
steps.explain: The pairing always fails. Same size requires a one-to-one match with none left over. The diagonal argument produces a decimal left out of any attempted match, so the decimals are a strictly bigger infinity.

steps.title: Bigger infinities are real.
recap: Same size means a one-to-one pairing with none left over.
Any list of the decimals from 0 to 1 misses one, by the diagonal trick.
So the reals are a strictly larger infinity than the whole numbers.

subject: MATH & LOGIC
title: Zero Had to Be Invented
blurb: For most of history there was no symbol for nothing, and math nearly stalled without it.

steps.title: Zero had to be invented
steps.sub: For most of history there was no symbol for nothing. Math limped along without it. Here's why nothing turned out to be a big deal.

steps.prompt: Roman numerals were used for centuries. How did they write zero?
options: With the letter O
They didn't, there was no symbol for zero
With a small dot
steps.explain: They didn't have one. Romans wrote I, V, X, and so on, but had no symbol for nothing. You don't need one to tally sheep. You need one badly the moment you want a place-value system.

steps.title: First job: holding a place
steps.body: In our number system, position carries meaning. The 2 in 25 means twenty; the 2 in 205 means two hundred. Without a symbol to mark the empty tens column, 25 and 205 blur together. Zero's first job is being that placeholder, a marker that says 'nothing here, but the column still counts.' Babylonian scribes used a gap or mark for this, but they treated it only as a spacer, not as a number you could add or multiply.

steps.prompt: Why does a place-value system need a placeholder symbol?
options: To make numbers look neater
So you can tell apart numbers like 25 and 205 by marking empty columns
Because every column must hold a different digit
steps.explain: To keep columns honest. Position decides a digit's value, so an empty column has to be marked or the number is ambiguous. The placeholder is what makes 205 readable as two hundred five, not twenty-five.

steps.title: Second job: being a number
steps.body: The bigger leap was treating zero as a number in its own right, something you can add, subtract, and reason about. Around 628 CE in India, Brahmagupta wrote down rules for arithmetic with zero: a number plus zero is unchanged, and a number minus itself is zero. That turned zero from a mere spacer into a working quantity. One rule stayed stubborn: dividing by zero never gives a sensible answer, so it's left undefined.

steps.prompt: What was the bigger leap beyond using zero as a placeholder?
options: Writing it as a round symbol
Treating zero as a number you can do arithmetic with
Using it to count backward
steps.explain: Making it a number you can compute with. A placeholder just holds a column. Treating zero as a real quantity, with rules for adding and subtracting it, is what unlocked algebra and beyond. Brahmagupta wrote those rules down.

steps.prompt: Put zero's rise in rough historical order.
items: Early systems like Roman numerals have no zero
Babylonians use a placeholder mark for empty columns
Indian mathematicians treat zero as a number with its own rules
Zero spreads to Europe through the Islamic world

glosses.Early systems like Roman numerals have n: Fine for tallying, weak for place value.
glosses.Babylonians use a placeholder mark for e: A spacer, not yet a number.

steps.prompt: A friend says zero is 'just a fancy way to write nothing.' What's the fuller picture?
options: They're right, it has only one job
Zero has two jobs: a placeholder that marks empty columns, and a number you can compute with
Zero is only useful for division
steps.explain: Two jobs, not one. As a placeholder it keeps place-value notation unambiguous. As a number it obeys arithmetic rules and powers algebra. The second role is the one that took centuries to accept.

steps.title: Nothing was a breakthrough.
recap: Many early systems had no symbol for zero.
As a placeholder, zero keeps place value clear.
As a number, it unlocked real arithmetic.

subject: MATH & LOGIC
title: The Numbers That Keep Your Passwords Safe
