blurb: Saving feels like loss. Reframe it: each dollar set aside is handed to a future you who will need it.

steps.title: A gift to someone who is you
steps.sub: Saving stings because spending feels good now and the payoff is far off. But the saver and the spender are the same person, just at different times. Every dollar you set aside is a gift to a future you who will badly need it.

steps.prompt: Why does saving feel like a loss even when you know it helps you later?
options: Because the future you is a stranger
Because your brain weighs a reward now more heavily than the same reward later
Because saving never actually helps
steps.explain: People naturally value rewards now over rewards later. The future payoff is real, but it feels faint next to the pull of spending today. That tilt is what makes saving feel like a sacrifice.

steps.title: Now feels louder than later
steps.body: People tend to value an immediate reward more than the same reward in the future. This is called present bias. It is why the snack today beats the savings goal years away, even when you know the goal matters more. The future you is not getting a fair vote.

steps.prompt: What is present bias?
options: Always preferring to save over spend
Valuing a reward now more than the same reward later
Forgetting about the future entirely
steps.explain: Present bias is the tilt toward immediate rewards. The future reward still counts, it just feels weaker now, which is why saving takes deliberate effort.

steps.title: Early dollars grow the most
steps.body: Invested money can compound, meaning its returns earn returns of their own. The longer a dollar sits, the more rounds of growth it gets. So a dollar saved early can outgrow a larger dollar saved late. The gift to your future self is worth more the sooner you send it.

steps.prompt: Why is a dollar saved early worth more than the same dollar saved later?
options: Early dollars are taxed less
It has more time to compound, earning returns on its returns
Money is worth more when you are young
steps.explain: Compounding rewards time. An early dollar gets more rounds of growth, so it can end up larger than a dollar saved later. Time is the ingredient you cannot add back.

steps.title: Take the choice away
steps.body: If saving depends on willpower every payday, present bias will win often. Automating it, like a recurring transfer that moves money the moment you are paid, removes the repeated decision. You do not have to feel like saving. It just happens before you can spend it.

steps.prompt: Put the path from a single saved dollar to future wealth in order.
items: You set aside a dollar instead of spending it
An automatic transfer moves it before you can touch it
It is invested and starts compounding
Returns earn their own returns over years
A future you withdraws a much larger sum

glosses.You set aside a dollar instead of spendi: The choice to save, fighting present bias.
glosses.It is invested and starts compounding: Money is put to work, not left idle.
glosses.Returns earn their own returns over year: Compounding builds on itself with time.
glosses.A future you withdraws a much larger sum: The gift arrives, grown by the years.

steps.prompt: What makes an early-saved dollar grow larger than a late-saved one?
options: It is more time spent compounding
Early dollars have higher interest rates
Banks reward young savers
steps.explain: More time means more rounds of returns earning returns. The early dollar simply gets more compounding behind it, which is why starting sooner beats saving more later.

steps.title: Now you get it
recap: Saving feels like loss because of present bias toward rewards now.
Early dollars compound the longest, so starting sooner wins.
Automating savings removes the willpower fight entirely.

subject: MONEY
title: The Real Price of Everything
blurb: The true cost of anything is not the money. It is the next-best thing you gave up to get it.

steps.title: Every yes is a no
steps.sub: You spend an hour, a dollar, a Saturday. The price tag tells you what you paid. It does not tell you what you missed. The real cost of any choice is the best thing you could have done instead.

steps.prompt: You spend a free evening watching TV. What did it cost you?
options: Nothing, it was free
The best other thing you could have done with that evening
Only the electricity
steps.explain: Free in dollars is not free in cost. That evening could have been a workout, a side project, or sleep. Whatever the best of those was, that is what watching TV actually cost you.

steps.title: Cost is the road not taken
steps.body: Every choice uses a resource you cannot use twice. Money, time, attention. The opportunity cost of a choice is the value of the next-best thing you gave up to make it. Not all the alternatives. Just the single best one you passed on.

steps.prompt: Opportunity cost counts which alternatives you gave up?
options: All of them added together
Only the single next-best one
The cheapest one
steps.explain: You can only do one other thing with that resource, so only the best forgone option counts. Adding up every alternative would double-count things you never could have done at once.

steps.title: Money is not the only price
steps.body: Opportunity cost is not just about cash. Time is the clearest case. A free seminar still costs you the hours you sat in it. Those hours had a next-best use. That is why a thing can be cheap to buy yet expensive to do.

steps.prompt: Why can a free activity still be costly?
options: Free things are secretly billed later
The time it takes had a next-best use
Nothing is ever truly free of tax
steps.explain: The dollars were zero, but the hours were not. Time spent here is time not spent on its best alternative, and that forgone use is the real cost.

steps.prompt: The opportunity cost of a choice is the value of what?
options: The money you handed over
The next-best thing you gave up
The worst option available
steps.explain: Price is what leaves your wallet. Opportunity cost is the best alternative you sacrificed. That is the figure that tells you whether a choice was actually worth it.

steps.title: Now you get it
recap: The real cost of a choice is the next-best thing you gave up.
Only the single best forgone option counts, not all of them.
Time and attention carry opportunity cost too, not just money.

subject: MONEY
title: Why You Finish the Bad Movie
blurb: Money already spent is gone either way. Letting it steer your next choice is the sunk cost trap.

steps.title: The ticket is already paid
steps.sub: The movie is bad. You paid for the ticket. You stay anyway, because leaving feels like wasting the money. But the money is gone whether you stay or go. So why is it deciding for you?

steps.prompt: You paid for a movie that turns out to be terrible. What should the ticket price tell you about staying?
options: Stay, or the money is wasted
Nothing, the money is gone either way
Stay if the ticket was expensive
steps.explain: The ticket cost the same whether you stay or leave. It is gone. The only thing left to decide is how to spend the next two hours, and the price already paid has no claim on that.

steps.title: Spent is spent
steps.body: A sunk cost is money, time, or effort you have already put in and cannot get back. It is the same no matter what you do next. Because it does not change with your choice, it should not weigh on your choice. Only future costs and benefits should.

steps.prompt: Why should a sunk cost not affect your next decision?
options: It is usually small
It is the same whatever you choose, so it cannot tip the choice
You will be refunded later
steps.explain: A good decision compares how your options differ from here forward. A sunk cost sits equally under every option, so it cancels out. It carries no information about which path is better.

steps.title: The trap has a name
steps.body: Throwing good resources after bad to justify what you already spent is the sunk cost fallacy. You finish the bad book, keep the failing project, stay in the long line, all because of what you put in. The feeling of waste is real, but it points backward at money that is already gone.

steps.prompt: What is the sunk cost fallacy?
options: Refusing to ever spend money
Continuing something to justify what you already put in
Always quitting when things get hard
steps.explain: The fallacy is letting past investment, which you cannot recover, pull you into spending more. The pull feels like avoiding waste, but it usually just adds to the loss.

steps.prompt: A sunk cost is best described as money or effort that is what?
options: Already spent and cannot be recovered
About to be spent next
Saved for emergencies
steps.explain: Sunk means already gone and unrecoverable. Since it does not change with what you decide next, the right move is to ignore it and weigh only what lies ahead.

steps.title: Now you get it
recap: A sunk cost is already spent and cannot be recovered.
It is the same under every option, so it should not sway your choice.
Pouring in more to justify the past is the sunk cost fallacy.

subject: MONEY
title: Why the Best Lawyer Still Hires a Typist
blurb: Even if you are better at everything, trading pays. Being relatively best at one thing beats being absolutely best at all.

steps.title: Better at everything, still trades
steps.sub: A top lawyer types faster than her assistant. So why pay someone to type? Because every hour she spends typing is an hour she is not practicing law. The math says she should let go of the thing she is merely good at.

steps.prompt: If the lawyer is faster at both law and typing, should she still hire a typist?
options: No, she does both better, so she should do both
Yes, because her time is far more valuable doing law
Only if she dislikes typing
steps.explain: Being faster at typing does not mean she should type. Each hour typing costs her an hour of high-value legal work. Handing off typing frees her for what she gives up the most by not doing.

steps.title: Two kinds of better
steps.body: Absolute advantage means you can simply produce more, or produce it with less. The lawyer has absolute advantage at both law and typing. But that is not what decides who should do what. What matters is opportunity cost: what you give up to do a task.

steps.prompt: What does absolute advantage describe?
options: Producing more, or with fewer resources, than the other person
Giving up the least to do a task
Always winning a negotiation
steps.explain: Absolute advantage is raw output. The lawyer has it at both jobs. But raw output alone does not tell you how to divide the work, because it ignores what each person sacrifices.

steps.title: Lower cost to whom
steps.body: You have comparative advantage in whatever you can make at the lowest opportunity cost. The typist gives up little by typing, since the alternative is low-value work. The lawyer gives up a fortune. So the typist should type and the lawyer should lawyer. Both end up ahead by trading.

steps.prompt: You have comparative advantage in a task when you can do it at the lowest what?
options: Effort
Opportunity cost
Hourly wage
steps.explain: Comparative advantage is about the lowest opportunity cost, not the fastest hands. The person who sacrifices the least by doing a task is the one who should do it, and trade lets each specialize there.

steps.prompt: Put the logic of comparative advantage in order.
items: One person is better at both tasks
Compare what each person gives up to do each task
Each specializes where their opportunity cost is lowest
They trade and both come out ahead

glosses.Compare what each person gives up to do: Look at opportunity cost, not raw speed

steps.prompt: Why should the faster-at-everything lawyer still hire a typist?
options: Typing is beneath her
Her opportunity cost of typing is huge, so she gains by specializing
The typist is actually faster
steps.explain: Speed at typing is beside the point. Every typing hour costs her a high-value legal hour. By trading, she does what she gives up the most by skipping, and both people are better off.

steps.title: Now you get it
recap: Absolute advantage is raw output. It does not decide who does what.
Comparative advantage is the lowest opportunity cost for a task.
Specialize where your cost is lowest and trade, and both sides gain.

subject: MONEY
title: Nobody Sets the Price
blurb: Prices are not dictated by sellers or buyers. They settle where what people will sell meets what people will pay.

steps.title: A price with no boss
steps.sub: It feels like the store sets the price. But charge too much and shelves stay full. Charge too little and they empty in an hour. The price that sticks is not chosen. It is found, where two crowds meet.

steps.prompt: Who ultimately decides the going price of a thing in a competitive market?
options: Sellers, since they set the tag
Buyers, since they hold the money
Neither alone, it settles where the two sides meet
steps.explain: A seller can ask any price, but cannot force a sale. A buyer can offer any price, but cannot force supply. The price that holds is the one where the amount offered matches the amount wanted.

steps.title: Buyers lean one way
steps.body: Demand describes how buyers respond to price. As a rule, the higher the price, the less people want to buy. Cheap, and a crowd lines up. Expensive, and most walk away. So the quantity buyers want falls as price rises.

steps.prompt: As price rises, the quantity buyers want to buy generally does what?
options: Rises
Falls
Stays fixed
steps.explain: Higher prices push buyers away, so quantity demanded drops. People buy less of a thing, or switch to something cheaper, when the price climbs.

steps.title: Sellers lean the other way
steps.body: Supply describes how sellers respond to price. Higher prices make selling more worthwhile, so sellers offer more. The two forces point in opposite directions. Where the amount sellers offer equals the amount buyers want, the price stops moving. That balance point is the equilibrium price.

steps.prompt: The equilibrium price is the price where what is true?
options: Sellers earn the most profit
Quantity supplied equals quantity demanded
Buyers pay the least possible
steps.explain: Equilibrium is where the two sides meet: the amount offered equals the amount wanted. At that price there is no leftover stock and no unmet line, so nothing pushes the price to move.

steps.prompt: The price is set too low, below equilibrium. Put what happens in order.
items: Buyers want more than sellers will supply
A shortage appears
Competition among buyers bids the price up
The price rises until supply and demand meet

glosses.Buyers want more than sellers will suppl: Too cheap, so demand outruns supply
glosses.Competition among buyers bids the price: People offer more to get scarce goods

steps.prompt: What happens to quantity demanded as the price of something goes up?
options: It generally falls
It generally rises
It does not change
steps.explain: Buyers retreat as prices climb, so quantity demanded falls. That downward pull from buyers, meeting the upward pull from sellers, is what locates the price.

steps.title: Now you get it
recap: Higher prices push buyers away and pull sellers in.
Price settles at equilibrium, where amount offered equals amount wanted.
Too low brings a shortage that bids the price back up.

subject: MONEY
title: Inflation Isn't Greedy Companies
blurb: Prices do not rise because businesses got greedy. Usually it is more money chasing the same goods.

steps.title: Blaming the wrong villain
steps.sub: Prices jump and the easy story is greed: companies decided to gouge you. But companies always wanted your money. If greed were the cause, why now, and why nearly everything at once?

steps.prompt: If companies got greedy this year, why not last year too?
options: They suddenly became greedier
Something changed in the economy, not in their character
They coordinate their greed by season
steps.explain: Firms were already trying to charge what the market would bear. Greed is a constant, not a trigger. A broad price rise points to something that changed across the whole economy, not a new mood among sellers.

steps.title: What inflation actually is
steps.body: Inflation is a sustained, general rise in prices across the economy. Not one product, not one month. The broad, ongoing climb in the cost of almost everything. One item getting pricier is just a price change. Inflation is the whole level lifting.

steps.prompt: Which of these best fits the definition of inflation?
options: One brand of coffee raises its price
A broad, sustained rise in prices across the economy
A holiday sale ending
steps.explain: Inflation is general and ongoing, not a single tag changing. A lone product or a one-time bump is a price change. Inflation is the overall level of prices drifting up over time.

steps.title: More money, same stuff
steps.body: The common explanation: the amount of money grows faster than the amount of goods to buy. More dollars chase the same shelves, so each dollar buys less and prices rise. The economist Milton Friedman put it bluntly, calling inflation always and everywhere a monetary phenomenon.

steps.prompt: In the common explanation, broad inflation tends to happen when what occurs?
options: Money grows faster than the goods available to buy
Companies hold a secret meeting
Buyers stop wanting things
steps.explain: When dollars multiply faster than goods, more money is bidding for the same stuff, so prices across the board climb. Each dollar is worth a little less. That broad mechanism is what greed cannot explain on its own.

steps.prompt: Which counts as inflation rather than just a price change?
options: One product gets more expensive once
Prices across the economy rise in a sustained way
A store runs out of one item
steps.explain: Inflation is broad and ongoing, the whole price level rising. A single product or a one-off jump does not qualify. Keeping that distinction clear is what stops you from blaming one greedy seller for an economy-wide trend.

steps.title: Now you get it
recap: Inflation is a broad, sustained rise in prices, not one product moving.
Greed is constant, so it cannot explain a sudden economy-wide jump.
The usual cause is more money chasing the same goods.

subject: MONEY
title: What GDP Quietly Ignores
blurb: GDP counts how much a country spends, not how well its people live. The two can move in opposite directions.

steps.title: A number everyone trusts too much
steps.sub: When the news says the economy grew, it usually means GDP went up. But GDP is a tally of spending, not a measure of how good life is. A country can post strong growth while ordinary people feel poorer.

steps.prompt: A hurricane wrecks a city, then billions get spent rebuilding it. What does GDP do?
options: Falls, because the city was damaged
Rises, because of all the rebuilding spending
Stays exactly the same
steps.explain: GDP counts the rebuilding as new production, so it rises. It does not subtract the homes and lives the storm destroyed. The number went up while the city is plainly worse off.

steps.title: GDP counts spending, not wellbeing
steps.body: GDP is the market value of all the goods and services a country produces in a year. That is it. It adds up transactions. It does not ask whether the spending made anyone happier, healthier, or safer. A dollar spent on a hospital bill and a dollar spent on a holiday count the same.

steps.prompt: What does GDP actually add up?
options: How happy and healthy people are
The market value of goods and services produced
How fairly money is shared out
steps.explain: GDP is a production and spending tally. Happiness and fairness are not in the formula, so a rising GDP tells you the economy is busier, not that life got better.

steps.title: Three big things it misses
steps.body: GDP ignores unpaid work, so caring for your own kids counts as zero while paid daycare counts as growth. It ignores who got the money, so it can climb even if the gains all go to a few. And it ignores leisure, health, and a clean environment unless they show up as a sale. The blind spots are not bugs. They are just outside what the number measures.

steps.prompt: You stop paying for daycare and care for your child yourself. What happens to GDP?
options: It rises, because childcare is valuable
It falls, because the paid transaction disappeared
It is unaffected by childcare either way
steps.explain: The same care now happens, but no money changes hands, so GDP drops. That is the blind spot: unpaid work is real work that the number cannot see.

steps.prompt: After a disaster, why can GDP rise even though people are worse off?
options: GDP secretly tracks suffering and inverts it
Rebuilding is spending, and GDP counts spending, not losses
Disasters always shrink the economy
steps.explain: GDP adds the rebuilding but never subtracts the destruction, because it counts transactions, not wellbeing. That gap between busier and better off is the whole point.

steps.title: Now you get it
recap: GDP measures spending and production, not how well people live.
It misses unpaid work, who gets the money, leisure, health, and the environment.
Rebuilding after a disaster can lift GDP while life gets worse.

subject: MONEY
title: Why Shared Things Get Wrecked
blurb: When a resource belongs to everyone and no one, each person grabs more until it collapses. The same logic runs overfishing and traffic.

steps.title: Everyone's, so no one's
steps.sub: Picture a shared pasture, an open fishing ground, a free road. No single owner, open to all. Each person has every reason to take a bit more. Add up all those reasons and the shared thing gets used into the ground.

steps.prompt: Herders share one open pasture. Each can add more cattle for free. What tends to happen?
options: Everyone restrains themselves to protect the grass
Each adds more cattle until the pasture is stripped bare
The grass grows faster under heavy use
steps.explain: Each herder keeps the full gain from one more cow, but the damage to the grass is shared by all. So everyone keeps adding, and the pasture collapses. Self-interest points straight at ruin.

steps.title: Private gain, shared cost
steps.body: The trap is in who gets what. When you take more from a shared resource, you pocket the whole benefit. But the cost, a little less for everyone, is split across the whole group. So even a reasonable person comes out ahead by taking more. Everyone reasons the same way, and the resource gets used faster than it can recover.

steps.prompt: Why does each person keep overusing a shared resource?
options: They get the full benefit but share the cost
They do not realize the resource is shared
Shared resources never run out
steps.explain: The benefit is yours alone, the cost is everyone's. That lopsided split makes taking more the rational move for each person, even though it wrecks the resource for all of them.

steps.title: It is not just pastures
steps.body: The same shape shows up everywhere shared. Fishers each catch all they can, and the fish stock crashes. Drivers each take the road, and the traffic that results slows everyone, including them. The fix is usually the same family of tools: ownership, quotas, fees, or community rules that make the shared cost land back on whoever causes it.

steps.prompt: What kind of fix does the lesson say usually helps?
options: Hoping people simply choose to take less
Rules, fees, or quotas that put the cost back on the user
Making the resource fully free to everyone
steps.explain: The problem is that taking more is free to the taker. Fixes work by making the user feel the cost, through ownership, quotas, fees, or agreed rules, so restraint becomes the rational choice.

steps.prompt: The core of the tragedy of the commons is that each user gets the benefit of taking more, but the cost is what?
options: Paid entirely by the user alone
Spread across everyone who shares the resource
Never paid by anyone at all
steps.explain: Private benefit, shared cost. That mismatch is why open-access resources get overused, and why every fix tries to put the cost back on the person who created it.

steps.title: Now you get it
recap: Shared, open resources get overused because no one owns the cost.
Each user keeps the full benefit but shares the damage with everyone.
Overfishing and traffic are the same trap. Rules and fees are the fix.

subject: MONEY
title: Why Free Stuff Has a Price
blurb: If a product costs you nothing, the cost is being paid somewhere else. Often it is your data, your attention, or a worse product.

steps.title: Nothing is actually free
steps.sub: Free apps, free email, free social networks. Running them costs real money, so someone is paying. If it is not you in cash, look for the bill in another form: your data, your attention, or a deliberately weaker product.

steps.prompt: A social app is free to use and has millions of users. Where does its money come from?
options: It does not need money to run
Mostly from advertisers paying to reach you
From a government that funds free apps
steps.explain: Servers and staff are not free, so the app needs revenue. With no price tag on users, the usual answer is advertisers, who pay for your attention and the data that helps them target it.

steps.title: You might be the product
steps.body: When a service is free and ad-funded, the real customers are the advertisers, and the thing being sold is access to you. Your attention and your data are the product. That is the meaning of the old line: if you are not paying for the product, you are the product. The price did not vanish. It just got charged to a different account.

steps.prompt: In a free, ad-funded service, who is the paying customer?
options: You, the user
The advertisers buying access to you
No one, it runs on goodwill
steps.explain: Advertisers pay the bills, so the service is built to serve them. You are not the customer. You are the inventory being sold, which shapes how the product treats you.

steps.title: Free shapes the product
steps.body: Who pays decides what gets built. If advertisers pay for your attention, the product is tuned to keep you scrolling, not to leave you satisfied and gone. And free can also mean a thinner version on purpose, missing the features that nudge you to a paid upgrade. Either way, free is a business model, not a gift.

steps.prompt: Why does an ad-funded free app tend to maximize your time on it?
options: Because more of your attention is what it sells
Because users demand to spend more time
Because longer use lowers its costs
steps.explain: Its revenue comes from selling your attention to advertisers, so more time on the app means more to sell. The product is optimized for the payer, not necessarily for you.

steps.prompt: The saying goes: if you are not paying for the product, you are the what?
options: The owner
The product
The competitor
steps.explain: When the service is free to you, you are usually what is being sold, to advertisers. The cost did not disappear. It moved to your data and attention.

steps.title: Now you get it
recap: Free products still cost money to run, so someone else is paying.
In ad-funded services, advertisers are the customer and you are the product.
Who pays shapes the product, often toward grabbing more of your attention.

subject: MONEY
title: The Minimum Wage Argument Nobody Wins
blurb: A higher wage floor raises pay for those who keep their jobs and can cost jobs for others. It is a tradeoff, not a free win or a sure disaster.

steps.title: Two true things at once
steps.sub: Raise the minimum wage and you help people who keep their jobs earn more. You may also make some jobs too costly to keep. Both can be true together. That is why the argument never really ends.

steps.prompt: The government sets a wage floor well above what some employers would pay. What does basic theory predict?
options: Employers happily pay more with no other change
Some employers want to hire fewer hours at the higher price
Wages everywhere instantly double
steps.explain: A floor above the going rate makes some labor more expensive than it produces. Basic theory says that at a higher price, buyers, here employers, tend to want less of it. The catch is that real-world effects are smaller and messier than the simple model.

steps.title: The winners are real
steps.body: Start with the clear gain. Every worker who keeps a job at the new, higher floor takes home more money. For low-paid workers that can be a meaningful raise they did not have to negotiate. This part is not in dispute. A higher floor lifts the pay of those who stay employed at it.

steps.prompt: Who clearly gains from a higher minimum wage?
options: Workers who keep their jobs at the new rate
Only the employers
Nobody gains at all
steps.explain: The undisputed winners are workers who stay employed at the higher floor. They earn more for the same work. The argument is not about them. It is about who might be left out.

steps.title: The cost can land on others
steps.body: Here is the other side. If a job produces less value than the new wage, an employer may cut hours, slow hiring, or not fill the role. The people most at risk are often the least experienced, the ones a first job would have helped most. The gain is concentrated and visible. The loss is spread out and hard to see, which is what makes the fight so stubborn.

steps.prompt: What is the possible downside the lesson describes?
options: Prices in shops always fall
Some jobs or hours can be cut for those who would have been hired
Experienced workers lose the most
steps.explain: If a role costs more than it produces, it may shrink or disappear. The risk falls hardest on less experienced workers, and because the loss is hard to see, it gets argued about endlessly.

steps.prompt: Why does the minimum wage argument never get settled?
options: Because it clearly helps everyone
Because it helps those who keep jobs but can cost others theirs
Because economists all agree it is harmless
steps.explain: It is a genuine tradeoff. Real winners and possible losers at the same time, with the loss harder to measure. That is why reasonable people land on different sides.

steps.title: Now you get it
recap: A higher minimum wage raises pay for workers who keep their jobs.
It can also price out some jobs, often for the least experienced.
It is a tradeoff with concentrated gains and spread-out costs, not a clean win.

subject: MONEY
title: Why the Second Slice Tastes Worse
blurb: Each extra unit of almost anything gives you less satisfaction than the one before. That rule quietly shapes how you spend.

steps.title: The first bite is the best
steps.sub: The first slice of pizza is heaven. The second is good. By the fourth you are slowing down. Nothing about the pizza changed. What changed is how much extra each slice was worth to you, and that pattern runs through almost everything you buy.

steps.prompt: You are hungry. Which slice of pizza gives you the most added enjoyment?
options: The first one
The fourth one
Every slice gives the same
steps.explain: The first slice meets the strongest hunger, so it delivers the most. Each one after meets a weaker need, so it adds less. The slices are identical. Your need for them is not.

steps.title: The extra is what shrinks
steps.body: Marginal utility is the extra satisfaction you get from one more unit of something. Diminishing marginal utility says that extra usually shrinks as you get more. The total can still rise, the fourth slice is not unpleasant, but each new unit adds less than the one before. The first of anything tends to be worth the most.

steps.prompt: What does diminishing marginal utility say shrinks as you get more?
options: The total satisfaction, which always falls
The extra satisfaction from each additional unit
The price you must pay
steps.explain: It is the marginal, the added, satisfaction that shrinks. Your total can keep rising while each new unit contributes less. Confusing total with extra is the usual mistake.

steps.title: Why this shapes spending
steps.body: Because each extra unit is worth less to you, you will pay less for it. That is why a sale moves the unit you would not buy at full price, and why one of almost anything satisfies more per dollar than a hoard of it. It also explains why spreading money across different things often beats piling it all into one.

steps.prompt: Why are you willing to pay less for the fifth unit than the first?
options: The fifth unit is lower quality
The fifth unit adds less satisfaction than the first
Prices legally drop with quantity
steps.explain: Same unit, but it meets a weaker need, so it is worth less to you. Since you value it less, you will only buy it at a lower price. That is the link to how people spend.

steps.prompt: Order these slices by how much extra enjoyment each adds, most first.
items: First slice
Second slice
Third slice
Fourth slice

glosses.First slice: Meets the sharpest hunger, adds the most
glosses.Second slice: Still good, but hunger is already easing
glosses.Fourth slice: Hunger nearly gone, adds the least

steps.prompt: As you consume more of something, the extra satisfaction from each new unit usually does what?
options: Stays exactly constant
Shrinks
Grows larger each time
steps.explain: The added satisfaction from each unit tends to shrink, even as your total rises. That shrinking extra is why the first of anything feels like the best deal.

steps.title: Now you get it
recap: Each extra unit usually adds less satisfaction than the one before.
Total can still rise. It is the marginal, added, value that falls.
Because later units are worth less, you will pay less for them.

subject: MONEY
title: The Costs You Make Other People Pay
blurb: Some prices leave out a real cost, and a bystander who never signed up ends up paying it.

steps.title: The bill goes to a stranger
steps.sub: A factory makes cheap goods and dumps smoke over the town. The buyer pays the sticker price. The neighbour pays with dirty air and never got a vote. That gap is an externality, and it is everywhere.

steps.prompt: A factory pollutes a river while making a product. Who pays for the pollution?
options: The factory, since it caused it
The buyer, in the price
People downstream who had no say
steps.explain: Unless someone forces the cost back onto the factory, it lands on people downstream. They did not buy the product and did not agree to the deal, yet they carry the damage. That spillover is the whole idea.

steps.title: A cost that escapes the price
steps.body: An externality is a cost or benefit from some activity that falls on a third party who never chose to take part. Pollution is the classic case. The buyer and seller settle on a price, but the smoke, noise, or runoff hits bystanders. Their loss is real, but it sits outside the deal, so nobody at the till is paying for it.

steps.prompt: What makes a cost an externality?
options: It is very large
It falls on a third party outside the deal
It is illegal
steps.explain: Size and legality are beside the point. What matters is that the cost lands on someone who was not part of the transaction and never agreed to bear it. That is what makes it spill over.

steps.title: Hidden costs mean too much gets made
steps.body: When a cost is left out of the price, the good looks cheaper than it truly is. So people buy more of it than they would if the full cost showed up at the till. The result is overproduction: society gets more of the polluting thing than is actually worth it, because the real bill is hidden from everyone making the choice.

steps.prompt: Why does a good with a negative externality get overproduced?
options: Its price leaves out a real cost, so it looks too cheap
Governments subsidise it
People enjoy pollution
steps.explain: The missing cost makes the good seem cheaper than it really is. A lower apparent price pulls in more buyers, so more gets made than is worth it once you count the harm to bystanders.

steps.title: Put the cost back on the maker
steps.body: One fix is to push the hidden cost back onto whoever creates it, often with a tax sized to the damage. Economists call this a Pigouvian tax, after Arthur Pigou. Now the price at the till includes the harm. The good stops looking artificially cheap, fewer people buy it, and production falls toward what it is actually worth.

steps.prompt: Before any fix, what happens to a good whose pollution cost is left out of its price?
options: It gets overproduced because it looks too cheap
It gets banned
It gets underproduced
steps.explain: With the cost hidden, the good looks cheaper than it is, so more gets bought and made than is worth it. A tax that adds the cost back is what pushes production toward the right level.

steps.title: Now you get it
recap: An externality is a cost or benefit that lands on a bystander outside the deal.
When a cost is hidden from the price, the good looks cheap and gets overproduced.
Taxing the maker for the damage puts the real cost back into the price.

subject: MONEY
title: How a Pin Factory Explains Wealth
blurb: Split one job into many tiny steps and output can multiply many times over. That is the engine of modern prosperity.

steps.title: One worker, or eighteen
steps.sub: Ask one person to make a pin from scratch and they might manage a handful a day. Split the job into tiny steps across a team and the same hands turn out thousands. Adam Smith noticed this in 1776, and it still explains why we are rich.

steps.prompt: Ten workers each make whole pins alone. Then they split the job into steps. What happens to total output?
options: It stays about the same
It rises a little
It jumps many times over
steps.explain: Output does not just nudge up, it leaps. By dividing the work, the same ten people can produce thousands of times more than they could making whole pins one at a time. That gap is what Smith set out to explain.

steps.title: Cut the job into steps
steps.body: Adam Smith opened The Wealth of Nations with a pin factory. Making a pin sounds simple, but he broke it into around eighteen separate operations: drawing the wire, straightening it, cutting, pointing, grinding, and so on. Instead of one person doing all of it, each worker does one step over and over. This is the division of labour.

steps.prompt: What did Smith mean by the division of labour in the pin factory?
options: Each worker repeats one small step of the job
Workers vote on how to make pins
One skilled worker makes the whole pin
steps.explain: The whole point is that the job is sliced up. Each person handles a single step rather than the entire pin. Smith counted around eighteen such steps, and that splitting is what unlocks the output.

steps.title: Why splitting works
steps.body: Smith gave three reasons the split raises output. First, doing one step all day makes you fast and skilled at it. Second, you waste no time switching tools and tasks. Third, a narrow, repeated step is easy to hand off to a machine. Together these turn a handful of pins per person into thousands.

steps.prompt: Which is one reason Smith gave for the output gain?
options: Workers are paid more
Time is saved by not switching between tasks
Pins become larger
steps.explain: Switching tasks costs time and focus. When you stick to one step, you skip all that, which is one of Smith's three reasons. The others were rising skill from repetition and the chance to use machines.

steps.title: The market sets the limit
steps.body: There is a catch Smith also named: you can only divide labour as far as the market is big enough to buy what you make. A tiny village cannot support a worker who does nothing but sharpen pin points all day. Bigger markets allow finer specialisation, which is part of why large, connected economies grow rich.

steps.prompt: In Smith's pin factory, how does each worker spend the day?
options: Making whole pins from start to finish
Repeating one small step of the process
Selling pins to customers
steps.explain: Each worker repeats a single step rather than building the whole pin. That division across around eighteen steps is exactly what lets a small team out-produce the same people working alone.

steps.title: Now you get it
recap: Smith split pin-making into many tiny steps, one per worker.
Splitting raises output through skill, saved time, and machines.
How far you can divide labour depends on how big the market is.

subject: MONEY
title: The One Dial That Moves the Economy
blurb: Central banks do not set wages or prices. They nudge the cost of borrowing, and the rest ripples out.

steps.title: One number, big ripples
steps.sub: A central bank cannot order shops to cut prices or bosses to raise pay. It has one main lever: the cost of borrowing money. Move that single dial and spending, saving, and prices all shift in response.

steps.prompt: The central bank lowers interest rates. What are people more likely to do?
options: Borrow and spend more
Save more and spend less
Nothing changes
steps.explain: Cheaper borrowing makes loans, mortgages, and credit less costly, so people and firms borrow and spend more. That is the lever working: change the price of money and behaviour follows.

steps.title: The rate is the price of money
steps.body: The central bank sets a short-term interest rate. Think of it as the price of borrowing money. The bank does not fix what bread costs or what a job pays. It only moves this one number. But almost every loan, mortgage, and business plan depends on the cost of borrowing, so that single dial reaches across the whole economy.

steps.prompt: What does the central bank actually set?
options: The price of everyday goods
A short-term interest rate, the cost of borrowing
Workers' wages
steps.explain: It sets the cost of borrowing, not prices or wages directly. Because so much activity runs on borrowed money, nudging that one rate is enough to steer spending across the economy.

steps.title: Raise the rate to cool things down
steps.body: When prices are rising too fast, the bank raises the rate. Borrowing gets expensive, so people and firms borrow and spend less. Weaker demand takes pressure off prices, which helps slow inflation. Lowering the rate does the reverse: cheaper borrowing wakes up spending. The usual aim is to keep inflation near a target, often about 2 percent.

steps.prompt: Why does the bank raise rates when inflation is high?
options: To make banks richer
To make borrowing costly, so spending and price pressure ease
To raise wages
steps.explain: Higher rates make borrowing expensive, which cools spending. Less demand means less upward pressure on prices, so inflation slows. The bank is steering demand, not setting prices itself.

steps.title: The effect arrives late
steps.body: The dial is slow. A rate change does not hit the economy at once. It works through loans coming up for renewal, business plans, and spending decisions, so the full effect can take many months to show up. That delay is why central banks try to act early, before a problem is obvious, rather than waiting.

steps.prompt: If the bank lowers the rate, what tends to happen to borrowing and spending?
options: Both fall
Both rise because borrowing is cheaper
Neither changes
steps.explain: A lower rate makes borrowing cheaper, so people and firms borrow and spend more. That is the lever in the other direction: the bank loosens to wake up a slow economy.

steps.title: Now you get it
recap: The central bank sets one number: the cost of borrowing.
Raise it to cool spending and inflation, lower it to revive them.
The effect spreads slowly, so banks try to act ahead of time.

subject: MONEY
title: Why Insurance Makes People Reckless
blurb: When someone else bears the risk, people take more of it. That is the hidden flaw in insurance, bailouts, and safety nets.

steps.title: Covered, so careless
steps.sub: Lock your bike when it is yours to lose. Insure it fully and you might leave it unlocked. Once someone else pays for the loss, you stop guarding against it. That shift has a name: moral hazard.

steps.prompt: You fully insure your phone against any damage at no extra cost. Do you treat it more or less carefully?
options: More carefully, to be safe
Less carefully, since a loss costs you nothing
Exactly the same
steps.explain: Once the loss is not yours to pay, the reason to be careful weakens. Many people get a little more reckless without even deciding to. That quiet shift in behaviour is moral hazard.

steps.title: Protection breeds risk
steps.body: Moral hazard is the tendency to take more risk when you are shielded from its cost. It is not about bad people. It is about incentives. When the downside lands on someone else, the careful choice stops paying off for you, so you take chances you would otherwise avoid. Insurance is the classic setting.

steps.prompt: What causes moral hazard?
options: People are naturally dishonest
The risk-taker does not bear the full cost of the risk
Insurance is too expensive
steps.explain: It comes from incentives, not character. When you do not pay the full cost of a bad outcome, taking the risk becomes cheap for you, so you take more of it.

steps.title: Bailouts have the same flaw
steps.body: The same logic scales up. If a bank expects to be rescued when its bets go bad, it has reason to make riskier bets. The reward is theirs, the loss gets covered by someone else. This is why bailouts and broad safety nets are often criticised: by removing the downside, they can quietly invite the very recklessness they had to clean up.

steps.prompt: Why can rescuing a failing bank create moral hazard?
options: It encourages future risky behaviour by removing the downside
It makes banks smaller
It raises interest rates
steps.explain: If the loss gets covered, the bank keeps the upside of risky bets without facing the full downside. That tilts it toward more risk next time, which is the hazard.

steps.title: Make them share the cost
steps.body: The cure is to put some of the cost back on the protected party. Insurers do this with deductibles and co-payments, so you still pay a slice of any loss. With your own money on the line again, the reason to be careful returns. The protection stays, but the recklessness it would otherwise invite shrinks.

steps.prompt: What is the root cause of moral hazard?
options: People are dishonest
The risk-taker is shielded from the cost of the risk
Markets are too small
steps.explain: When you do not bear the full cost of a bad outcome, caution stops paying off and you take more risk. That is why making people share the cost, through a deductible, pulls behaviour back.

steps.title: Now you get it
recap: Moral hazard is taking more risk when you are shielded from its cost.
It is driven by incentives, not bad character.
Making people share the cost, like a deductible, reins it in.

subject: MONEY
title: Why Progress Kills Jobs (and Makes Better Ones)
blurb: New industries grow by destroying old ones. It is painful, and it is how economies improve.

steps.title: Built by tearing down
steps.sub: The car did not just add to the economy. It wrecked the world of horses, stables, and blacksmiths. New industries rarely arrive in empty space. They grow by knocking down what came before. The economist Joseph Schumpeter called this creative destruction.

steps.prompt: A better technology arrives and the economy grows richer overall. What happens to existing jobs?
options: All jobs are safe because the economy grew
Some jobs are destroyed even as new ones appear
No new jobs are ever created
steps.explain: Growth and job loss happen together. The new technology creates work but also wipes out the old work it replaces. The economy can be richer overall while specific people lose their livelihoods. That tension is the heart of it.

steps.title: Progress is a churn
steps.body: Creative destruction means new methods and products advance by destroying the old ones. Joseph Schumpeter popularised the idea in his 1942 book Capitalism, Socialism and Democracy. Progress is not smooth addition. It is a churn in which entrepreneurs introduce something better, and the better thing kills off what it replaces.

steps.prompt: What does creative destruction describe?
options: New methods advancing by destroying older ones
Governments shutting down old factories
Economies that never change
steps.explain: It is the churn where the new replaces and destroys the old. Schumpeter saw this not as a flaw but as the actual mechanism by which an economy moves forward.

steps.title: Better overall, brutal up close
steps.body: When the car displaced the horse, demand for blacksmiths shoeing horses fell, while demand for mechanics rose. Society gained faster, cheaper transport. But that gain was no comfort to a blacksmith whose trade vanished. Creative destruction can lift living standards overall while still inflicting real, concentrated losses on particular workers.

steps.prompt: As cars displaced horses, what happened to the work?
options: All transport jobs disappeared
Demand for blacksmiths fell while demand for mechanics rose
Nothing changed for workers
steps.explain: The work did not vanish, it shifted. The horse trades shrank and new car trades grew. That swap is the pattern: old jobs destroyed, different new ones created.

steps.prompt: Put the creative destruction cycle in order, using the car as the example.
items: An entrepreneur introduces a better technology
The new product wins customers
Demand for the old industry collapses
Workers and capital shift to the new industry

glosses.The new product wins customers: People prefer it to the old way.

steps.prompt: Can an economy grow richer overall while still destroying jobs?
options: No, growth protects every job
Yes, new work appears while old work is destroyed
Only during a recession
steps.explain: That is exactly the deal. The new technology raises living standards overall, yet it kills the old jobs it replaces. The gains are spread wide while the losses hit specific people hard.

steps.title: Now you get it
recap: Creative destruction means the new advances by destroying the old.
Schumpeter saw this churn as how economies actually progress.
It lifts living standards overall while inflicting real local job losses.

subject: MONEY
title: What a Diploma Actually Sells
blurb: A degree is not just skills. Economists argue much of its value is proof you were capable before you ever enrolled.

steps.title: Buying a stamp, not just a skill
steps.sub: You assume college pays because it teaches you to do a job. But ask a graduate what their literature degree taught them about their office work. Often, little. So why does the degree still raise their pay? Part of the answer is not what you learned. It is what the degree proves about you.

steps.prompt: Suppose a degree taught you almost nothing useful for your job. Could it still raise your pay?
options: No, pay only rewards skills you actually use
Yes, because finishing it proves something employers want to know
Only if you lie about what you studied
steps.explain: Employers cannot see your ability directly before hiring you. A degree that is hard to finish acts as evidence you are capable and persistent, even if its content never comes up at work. That evidence has value on its own.

steps.title: Two stories about school
steps.body: There are two ways to explain why graduates earn more. The human capital story says school makes you more productive by teaching skills. The signaling story says school mostly reveals ability you already had. Both can be true at once. The debate is over how much each one matters.

steps.prompt: What does the signaling view say a degree mainly does?
options: Teaches the exact skills the job needs
Reveals ability the person already had
Lowers the cost of hiring nobody wants
steps.explain: Signaling is about information, not training. The degree sorts capable people from the rest by revealing what an employer cannot otherwise see. The human capital view is the rival idea that school builds the skills.

steps.title: A signal has to hurt the right people
steps.body: Economist Michael Spence showed a signal only works if it is harder for weak candidates to fake than for strong ones. A degree is grueling. Capable people get through it at lower cost in effort and failure than less capable people. So finishing it is believable proof. If anyone could get one easily, it would tell employers nothing.

steps.prompt: In Spence's model, what makes a signal believable?
options: It is cheap and easy for everyone to get
It is more costly for low-ability people to obtain
The government requires it
steps.explain: If a credential were easy for anyone, weak candidates would get it too, and it would prove nothing. The signal works precisely because it is more painful to earn for those who lack the underlying ability.

steps.prompt: Put the logic of educational signaling in order.
items: Employers cannot see ability before hiring
Capable people can finish a hard degree at lower cost
Finishing the degree sends a believable signal of ability
Employers pay graduates more even apart from skills learned

glosses.Capable people can finish a hard degree: The signal is costly, but less so for them
glosses.Finishing the degree sends a believable: Hard to fake, so it is trusted
glosses.Employers pay graduates more even apart: The proof itself is worth paying for

steps.prompt: Why can a degree raise pay even if it taught no job skills?
options: It signals ability the employer could not otherwise see
It makes you legally required to be hired
Pay is random anyway
steps.explain: The degree carries information. Because it is hard to finish, completing it tells employers you are capable, and that is worth paying for whether or not the coursework ever applies.

steps.title: Now you get it
recap: A degree may pay by teaching skills, by signaling ability, or both.
A signal only works if it is harder for weak candidates to earn.
That is why finishing a hard degree raises pay beyond the skills in it.

subject: MONEY
title: Why Some Things Only Government Builds
blurb: Streetlights and clean air help everyone whether they pay or not. So no business will sell them, and someone else has to step in.

steps.title: The thing you can't sell
steps.sub: A streetlight on your road makes everyone safer. So why doesn't a company install one and charge the neighbors? Because the moment it is on, everyone gets the light, payers and non-payers alike. There is no way to switch it off for the people who skip the bill.

steps.prompt: Could a business profitably sell streetlight service to one house on a shared street?
options: Yes, just bill that house and light their stretch
No, the light spills to everyone, so neighbors get it free
Only if the light is very dim
steps.explain: Once the light is on, it shines on the whole street. You cannot give it to one payer and withhold it from the rest. So neighbors get the benefit without paying, and no seller can make money.

steps.title: Two traits make a public good
steps.body: Economists call something a public good when it has two traits. First, non-excludable: you cannot easily stop non-payers from using it. Second, non-rival: one person using it does not leave less for the next. Clean air and national defense fit both. You cannot fence them off, and your share does not shrink anyone else's.

steps.prompt: Non-excludable means what?
options: It runs out when too many people use it
You cannot easily stop non-payers from using it
Only the government may own it
steps.explain: Non-excludable is about access, not scarcity. If you cannot keep non-payers out, you cannot make them pay. That is the trait that breaks the usual sell-it-for-money model.

steps.title: Why everyone waits for someone else
steps.body: If you will get the good whether or not you chip in, the smart move is to let others pay and ride along free. That is the free rider problem. When everyone reasons this way, too little gets funded, even though all of them want it. So private markets underprovide public goods, and government often steps in and funds them through taxes that everyone must pay.

steps.prompt: What is the free rider problem?
options: People enjoy a good without paying, so it gets underfunded
Free goods are always low quality
Riders crowd out drivers on the road
steps.explain: When you benefit without paying, you have little reason to contribute. If everyone free rides, the good never gets funded, even though the group wants it. That gap is why a forced contribution like tax can help.

steps.prompt: Put the public goods argument in order.
items: A good benefits everyone and cannot exclude non-payers
People realize they can get it without paying
Each waits for others to pay, so it stays underfunded
Government funds it through taxes everyone must pay

steps.prompt: Which pair of traits defines a public good?
options: Expensive and rare
Non-excludable and non-rival
Branded and patented
steps.explain: Non-excludable means non-payers cannot be kept out. Non-rival means your use does not shrink anyone else's. Together they are why a seller cannot make money on it, and why the free rider problem appears.

steps.title: Now you get it
recap: A public good is non-excludable and non-rival.
You cannot bill people who get it for free, so businesses won't make it.
Free riding leaves it underfunded, so government often pays via taxes.

subject: MONEY
title: Why a Coffee Price Hike Doesn't Stop You
blurb: Some things you buy at almost any price. Others you drop the moment they cost more. That gap decides who can raise prices.

steps.title: The price went up. You bought it anyway.
steps.sub: Your coffee shop raises the price twenty cents and you barely notice. But if your favorite soda jumped the same amount, you might grab the store brand instead. Same size hike, very different reaction. That difference has a name, and it decides who gets to raise prices.

steps.prompt: Which good can a seller raise the price on without losing many buyers?
options: One with many cheap, near-identical alternatives
One people are hooked on with no close substitute
Whichever one is more expensive to start with
steps.explain: If a substitute is one shelf over, buyers switch the instant you raise the price. If there is no real alternative and people are attached, they grumble and pay. The presence of substitutes, not the starting price, drives how buyers react.

steps.title: How much does demand bend
steps.body: Price elasticity of demand measures how much the amount people buy changes when the price changes. If a price rise barely dents sales, demand is inelastic, stiff. If a price rise sends buyers fleeing, demand is elastic, springy. What makes a good inelastic? Few substitutes, habit, or a price so small it hardly shows in your budget.

steps.prompt: Demand is inelastic when a price change causes the quantity bought to do what?
options: Change a lot
Change very little
Drop to zero
steps.explain: Inelastic means stiff. Buyers barely budge when the price moves. That happens when they have nowhere else to go or the item is a tiny part of their spending, so the hike is not worth reacting to.

steps.title: Who can raise prices and win
steps.body: Revenue is price times quantity sold. When demand is inelastic, raising the price loses you only a few buyers, so total revenue goes up. When demand is elastic, raising the price drives off so many buyers that total revenue falls. So a seller can profit from a hike only when buyers cannot easily walk away.

steps.prompt: If demand is elastic and you raise the price, total revenue tends to do what?
options: Rise, because each sale earns more
Fall, because too many buyers leave
Stay exactly the same
steps.explain: Elastic demand means buyers flee at the first sign of a hike. You earn more per sale but lose so many sales that the total drops. The higher price backfires when people have easy alternatives.

steps.prompt: A seller wonders whether to raise the price. Put the reasoning in order.
items: Check how easily buyers can switch away
Judge whether demand is inelastic or elastic
Predict how much quantity will drop after a hike
Raise the price only if revenue would rise

glosses.Raise the price only if revenue would ri: Inelastic goods can take the hike

steps.prompt: What makes a good's demand inelastic?
options: Many close substitutes nearby
Few substitutes, habit, or a tiny share of the budget
A high sticker price
steps.explain: Inelastic demand comes from having no easy escape: no close substitute, a strong habit, or a cost so small it barely registers. Those are the goods a seller can raise the price on and still keep buyers.

steps.title: Now you get it
recap: Elasticity measures how much quantity bought bends when price moves.
Few substitutes, habit, or low cost make demand inelastic.
Raising the price only grows revenue when demand is inelastic.

subject: MONEY
title: Trade Isn't One Side Winning
blurb: If I buy from you, it feels like you win and I lose. But a voluntary trade only happens because both sides expect to gain.

steps.title: Both sides walk away happy
steps.sub: You hand the cafe four dollars, they hand you a coffee. It feels like a tug of war: their gain, your loss. But stop and ask why you did it. You wanted the coffee more than the four dollars. They wanted the four dollars more than the coffee. Nobody got robbed.

steps.prompt: In a trade you chose to make, who comes out ahead?
options: The seller, since they got your money
The buyer, since they got the goods
Both, since each gave up something they valued less
steps.explain: Nobody forced the trade. You only paid because the coffee was worth more to you than the cash. They only sold because the cash was worth more to them than the coffee. Each side traded up. That is why both agreed.

steps.title: Fixed pie, or growing one
steps.body: A zero-sum deal is one where the total is fixed: whatever one side gains, the other loses, like splitting a pizza. Many people picture trade this way. But voluntary trade is usually positive-sum. The pie gets bigger because each person ends up holding something they value more than what they gave away.

steps.prompt: What does zero-sum mean?
options: Everyone ends up with nothing
One side's gain exactly equals the other side's loss
Both sides gain together
steps.explain: Zero-sum means the total never changes. One person's win is another's loss, dollar for dollar. The point of the lesson is that voluntary trade is not like this. It can make both sides better off at once.

steps.title: You and I value things differently
steps.body: Trade creates gains because people value the same thing differently. To the cafe, one more coffee is cheap to make and worth little kept on the shelf. To you, thirsty, it is worth more than your four dollars. You each give up the thing you value less and get the thing you value more. Both gain. That is gains from trade.

steps.prompt: What creates the room for both sides to gain from a trade?
options: They value the same item differently
One side is tricked
Prices are fixed by law
steps.explain: If you both valued the coffee identically, there would be no reason to swap. Because it is worth more to you than to them, and the cash is worth more to them than to you, the trade moves each thing to where it is valued most.

steps.prompt: Why is a voluntary trade usually not zero-sum?
options: Because the seller always loses
Because each side ends up with something it values more
Because money is destroyed in the deal
steps.explain: In a chosen trade, each person hands over what they value less for what they value more. Nobody's gain comes out of the other's pocket. Both move up, so the total value rises rather than staying fixed.

steps.title: Now you get it
recap: Zero-sum means one side's gain is the other's equal loss.
Voluntary trade is usually positive-sum: both sides gain.
It works because people value the same things differently.

subject: MONEY
title: The Same Dollar Pays Many People
blurb: One dollar can fund a chain of purchases as it changes hands. Why spending, not just printing, is what drives an economy.

steps.title: One dollar, many paydays
steps.sub: You spend a dollar at the bakery. The baker spends it on flour. The miller spends it on a coffee. That one dollar just paid three people. Money does not have to be printed to do more work. It only has to keep moving.

steps.prompt: You give a shop one dollar and it then circulates. How much total spending can that single dollar create?
options: Exactly one dollar, no more
Several dollars, as it is spent again and again
Nothing until more money is printed
steps.explain: The dollar does not vanish when you spend it. The shop spends it next, then their supplier, and so on. Each pass is fresh spending. So one dollar can add up to many dollars of activity as it moves.

steps.title: Money that moves does more
steps.body: Velocity of money is how often a unit of currency gets used over a period. A dollar that changes hands ten times funds ten purchases. A dollar that sits in a drawer funds none. So total spending in an economy depends on two things: how much money exists, and how fast it circulates.

steps.prompt: Velocity of money measures what?
options: How much money exists in total
How often a unit of money is used in a period
How fast prices rise
steps.explain: Velocity is about turnover, not quantity. It counts how many times the same money is spent. That is why a small money supply can still support a lot of activity if it moves quickly.

steps.title: When money stops moving
steps.body: If people get scared and hold their cash instead of spending, velocity falls. The same amount of money now funds fewer purchases, so total spending can drop even though no money disappeared. This is why economists say spending, not just the amount of money, drives the economy. Money parked is money doing no work.

steps.prompt: If people start holding money instead of spending it, what happens to total spending?
options: It rises, because savings grow
It can fall, because velocity drops
It cannot change unless money is destroyed
steps.explain: Held money funds nothing. When velocity falls, the same supply produces fewer transactions, so total spending can shrink. The money still exists, but it is no longer doing the work of changing hands.

steps.prompt: Trace one dollar through the economy. Put the chain in order.
items: You spend the dollar at the bakery
The baker spends it on flour
The miller spends it on coffee
One dollar has now funded three purchases

steps.prompt: Total spending in an economy depends on the amount of money and what else?
options: The color of the currency
How often the money changes hands
Only the number of banks
steps.explain: Spending depends on both quantity and turnover. The same dollars can do more work when they circulate faster, and less when people hold them. That turnover is velocity.

steps.title: Now you get it
recap: Velocity is how often a unit of money is spent in a period.
Total spending depends on both how much money exists and how fast it moves.
When people hoard cash, velocity falls and spending can drop.

subject: MONEY
title: Why Used Cars Are All Suspect
blurb: When the seller knows more than the buyer, the good stuff disappears and everyone assumes the worst.

steps.title: The seller knows, you don't
steps.sub: You are buying a used car. The seller has driven it for years. You have looked at it for ten minutes. That gap in knowledge does more damage to the market than you would think.

steps.prompt: Buyers can't tell good used cars from bad ones, so they offer a price in the middle. What happens to owners of genuinely good cars?
options: They happily sell at the middle price
They refuse to sell, since the price is too low for a good car
They raise their price and buyers pay it
steps.explain: A good car is worth more than the middling price, so its owner walks away. The cars left for sale skew toward bad ones, and buyers, sensing this, offer even less. The good cars get driven out.

steps.title: One side knows more
steps.body: Information asymmetry just means one party in a deal knows more than the other. The used car seller knows whether the car is solid or a wreck. You do not. When the hidden fact is about quality, the buyer has to guess, and guessing changes how much anyone will pay.

steps.prompt: What does information asymmetry mean?
options: Prices are different in different stores
One party in a deal knows more than the other
Buyers and sellers both lack information
steps.explain: It is a knowledge gap, not a price gap. One side holds facts the other cannot see, and that imbalance is what distorts the deal.

steps.title: Good stuff gets driven out
steps.body: Economist George Akerlof described this in 1970 as the market for lemons, where a lemon is a bad used car. Since buyers cannot tell good from bad, they pay only a middling price. Owners of good cars refuse and leave. That makes the average quality worse, so buyers pay even less, and more good cars exit. The bad drives out the good.

steps.prompt: In the market for lemons, why does average quality keep falling?
options: Cars physically wear out faster
Sellers of good cars leave because the price is too low, which lowers the price further
Buyers keep raising their offers
steps.explain: Each round of low prices pushes out the best remaining cars. The pool gets worse, prices drop again, and the spiral continues. Hidden quality plus a single price punishes honesty.

steps.title: How to break out
steps.body: The fix is to make the hidden quality visible in a way that is hard to fake. A seller of a good car offers a warranty, a service history, or an independent inspection. A bad-car seller cannot cheaply afford a warranty, so the warranty itself signals quality. Credible signals let good products survive the lemons trap.

steps.prompt: Why does offering a warranty signal a good car?
options: Warranties are required by law on all cars
A bad-car seller would lose money on a warranty, so only good-car sellers can afford one
Warranties make the car run better
steps.explain: The signal works because it is costly for the wrong type. A lemon would trigger expensive claims, so its seller avoids the warranty. That makes the warranty believable evidence of quality.

steps.prompt: When buyers can't judge quality and pay one middling price, what happens to the best products?
options: They sell fastest
They get driven out of the market
They set the price for everyone
steps.explain: The best products are worth more than the middling price, so their owners leave. The market fills with the worst, which is the whole point of the lemons problem.

steps.title: Now you get it
recap: Information asymmetry means one side knows more than the other.
When buyers can't see quality, good products get driven out.
Hard-to-fake signals like warranties let good products survive.

subject: MONEY
title: Price Is Not the Same as Value
blurb: Price is just where the last buyer and seller agreed. Value is what a thing is worth to you.

steps.title: Same tag, different worth
steps.sub: Two people see the same price on the same bottle of water. One walks past. The other, stuck in the desert, would pay ten times more. The price is identical. The value is not.

steps.prompt: If price and value were the same thing, why would anyone ever trade?
options: They wouldn't, no one would gain
Because both sides value the deal differently than the price
Trades only happen by mistake
steps.explain: A trade only makes sense if the buyer values the item more than the price and the seller values it less. Both walk away better off. That gap between price and personal value is what makes trade worth doing.

steps.title: Price is just an agreement
steps.body: Price is simply the number a buyer and seller settled on. It is the outcome of one negotiation between two parties. It is public, single, and the same for everyone looking at the tag. It records what the last deal closed at. It does not record what the thing is worth to you.

steps.prompt: What does a price actually record?
options: What the item is truly worth
What a buyer and seller agreed on
What it cost to make
steps.explain: A price is an agreement, not a verdict on worth. It tells you what one buyer and one seller settled on, nothing more about what the thing means to you.

steps.title: Value is personal
steps.body: Value is what a thing is worth to a specific person in a specific situation. Water in the desert is worth more to you than water at home, even at the same price. A tool you will use daily is worth more to you than to someone who will never touch it. Value sits inside you. Price sits on the tag.

steps.prompt: Why can the same item have different value to two people?
options: Prices change by the hour
Value depends on the person's situation and needs
One of them is wrong about the price
steps.explain: Value lives in the person, not the object. Your circumstances and needs decide what something is worth to you, so two people can rate the same item very differently at one price.

steps.prompt: A trade leaves both sides better off because the item is worth...
options: Exactly the price to both of them
More than the price to the buyer and less to the seller
Nothing to either of them
steps.explain: The buyer values it above the price, the seller values it below. Both gain from the gap between price and their own value. If price and value were identical, neither would bother.

steps.title: Now you get it
recap: Price is just what a buyer and seller agreed on.
Value is what a thing is worth to you, and it is personal.
Trade works because the two differ for each side.

subject: MONEY
title: The Stock Market Isn't the Economy
blurb: Stocks can soar while jobs vanish. The market prices future profits, not how people are doing today.

steps.title: Two different scoreboards
steps.sub: The news says the market hit a record. It also says layoffs are rising. Both can be true at once. The market and the economy are not the same scoreboard, and they do not always agree.

steps.prompt: Can the stock market rise in the same month that lots of people lose their jobs?
options: No, they always move together
Yes, the market looks ahead while jobs reflect now
Only if the numbers are wrong
steps.explain: The market prices what investors expect about future profits. If layoffs cut costs and investors expect higher profits later, stocks can climb even as workers are hurting today. They measure different things.

steps.title: Stocks price the future
steps.body: A stock price is a bet on a company's future profits. Investors are not paying for how things look today; they are paying for what they expect to earn later. So the market moves on expectations. Good news about the future can lift stocks even when the present is grim.

steps.prompt: What does a stock price mainly reflect?
options: How workers are doing right now
Investors' expectations about future profits
The company's past sales only
steps.explain: Stocks are forward-looking. The price is a guess about future earnings, which is why it can rise on hopeful news even while current conditions look bad.

steps.title: The economy is everyone
steps.body: The economy is jobs, wages, output, and spending across the whole population. The stock market is a set of large public companies, weighted toward the biggest. Most workers and small businesses are not in the index at all. So the market can boom while the typical person's situation barely moves, or gets worse.

steps.prompt: Why doesn't a rising index mean everyone is doing well?
options: Indexes cover every business and worker equally
Indexes are weighted toward big companies and leave out most workers
A rising index always lowers wages
steps.explain: The index tracks large public firms, not the whole population. Jobs and wages for most people sit outside it, so the market's gains can skip the typical worker entirely.

steps.title: Who actually gains
steps.body: When stocks rise, the gains flow to people who own stocks. Stock ownership is concentrated, so a soaring market mostly enriches owners. Someone with little or no stock feels almost none of it. That is another reason a record market can sit right next to widespread hardship.

steps.prompt: When the market rises, who feels it most?
options: Everyone equally
People who own stocks, since ownership is concentrated
Only people who are unemployed
steps.explain: Gains go to owners, and ownership is concentrated. So a rally lifts a relatively narrow group while people who own little stock barely notice.

steps.prompt: The stock market and the economy can diverge mainly because the market prices...
options: Today's jobs and wages
Expected future profits
The cost of making products
steps.explain: The market is a bet on future profits, while the economy is the present reality of jobs and wages. Pricing the future versus living the present is exactly why they can split apart.

steps.title: Now you get it
recap: Stocks price expected future profits, not today's conditions.
The economy is jobs and wages for everyone; the index is big firms.
Market gains flow to owners, who are a concentrated group.

subject: MONEY
title: Every Bubble Feels Reasonable From Inside
blurb: People don't join manias because they're stupid. The story makes sense right up until it doesn't.

steps.title: It made sense at the time
steps.sub: From outside, a bubble looks like obvious madness. From inside, every buyer had a reasonable-sounding story and watched prices prove them right, until the day they didn't.

steps.prompt: Why do smart, careful people buy into bubbles?
options: They suddenly become foolish
Rising prices and a believable story make buying feel rational
They know it's a bubble and don't care
steps.explain: Prices keep rising, which seems to confirm the story, and a convincing story explains why this time is different. Buying feels like the smart move, not a gamble. That is what makes bubbles so easy to join.

steps.title: There's always a story
steps.body: A bubble is when an asset's price climbs far above what it is worth. It never feels arbitrary from inside, because there is always a story: a new technology, a new era, a reason this asset is different. The story is believable, which is exactly why so many people accept the rising price.

steps.prompt: What makes a bubble feel reasonable from inside?
options: Prices are obviously too high
A believable story justifies the rising prices
Everyone admits it is a bubble
steps.explain: The story is the engine of belief. It explains away the high price, so buying feels grounded rather than reckless. Without a convincing story, fewer people would join.

steps.title: Rising prices recruit buyers
steps.body: Here is the loop. Prices rise. People who bought earlier look smart. That draws in new buyers afraid of missing out. Their buying pushes prices higher still, which seems to prove the story right and pulls in even more. The rise feeds on itself, and each new high makes the next buyer feel justified.

steps.prompt: How does a bubble feed itself?
options: Falling prices scare buyers away
Rising prices attract more buyers, which raises prices further
The government keeps setting higher prices
steps.explain: It is a self-reinforcing loop. Higher prices pull in more buyers, whose buying lifts prices again. Fear of missing out keeps the cycle spinning until buyers run out.

steps.title: It ends when buyers run out
steps.body: Many buyers are betting they can sell later to someone who will pay more. That only works while new buyers keep arriving.
