When they stop, there is no one to sell to, prices fall, and the rush to get out can make the fall sharp. The same story that seemed obvious now looks like a warning everyone missed.

steps.prompt: Why does a bubble eventually pop?
options: The story finally gets more convincing
New buyers stop appearing, so there is no one left to sell to
Prices are frozen by a committee
steps.explain: The whole thing rests on new buyers paying more. When they stop coming, sellers can't find takers, prices fall, and the exit rush can turn the drop into a crash.

steps.prompt: The reason bubbles feel rational from inside is that...
options: There is always a believable story justifying the price
Buyers know prices are too high
Prices never actually rise
steps.explain: A convincing story explains the rising price, so joining feels like sense, not folly. That is why intelligent people get swept in, and why the mania is only obvious after it bursts.

steps.title: Now you get it
recap: A bubble is a price far above value, backed by a believable story.
Rising prices recruit more buyers in a self-reinforcing loop.
It ends when new buyers stop arriving and prices fall.

subject: MONEY
title: Nobody Sets the Price - the Market Does
blurb: No committee decides what a stock costs. The price is just where millions of bets meet in the middle.

steps.title: Who picks the number?
steps.sub: A stock costs a precise amount that changes second by second. It feels like someone must be choosing it. No one is. The number is the result of a crowd, not a decision.

steps.prompt: Who decides what a share of a company costs right now?
options: The company's executives
A stock exchange committee
Whatever price a buyer and seller just agreed on
steps.explain: No one sets it. The price is simply the last amount a buyer and seller agreed on. The next trade can move it. It is a running tally of offers meeting, not a number anyone hands down.

steps.title: Buyers bid, sellers ask
steps.body: At any moment, buyers post what they are willing to pay and sellers post what they will accept. These are bids and asks. They are independent decisions made by many different people, each with their own reasons. Nobody coordinates them. They just stack up on two sides.

steps.prompt: What are buyers and sellers posting at any moment?
options: Prices they are willing to pay and prices they will accept
A single agreed price set in advance
Their opinions of the company
steps.explain: Buyers post bids, sellers post asks. They are separate, independent numbers. The market does not start with one price; it starts with two stacks of offers.

steps.title: A trade is where they meet
steps.body: A trade happens when a buyer's offer meets a seller's asking price. The moment they match, shares change hands at that number. The price you see quoted is just the last trade that went through. It is a record of where the two sides last met, not a target anyone aimed for.

steps.prompt: What is the price you see quoted for a stock?
options: The price the exchange wants
The last price at which a trade actually happened
The average of all offers ever made
steps.explain: The quote is the last completed trade. It marks where a buyer and seller just met. The next match can land somewhere else, which is why the number keeps moving.

steps.title: The crowd moves the number
steps.body: When more people want to buy than sell at the current price, buyers have to offer more to find a willing seller, so the price drifts up. When sellers outnumber buyers, they accept less, so it drifts down. No one chooses the direction. The price is the running balance of all those independent bets pressing on each other.

steps.prompt: Why does a price rise when more people want to buy than sell?
options: The exchange raises it on purpose
Buyers must offer more to find a willing seller
Sellers are forced to charge more by law
steps.explain: With more buyers than sellers, buyers compete and bid higher to get filled. The rise comes from that pressure, not from anyone deciding. The crowd does it.

steps.prompt: Put the steps of a single trade in order.
items: Buyers post bids and sellers post asks
A bid and an ask match
Shares change hands at that price
That price is reported as the latest quote

glosses.Buyers post bids and sellers post asks: Two separate stacks of offers form.
glosses.A bid and an ask match: A buyer's offer meets a seller's price.

steps.prompt: The quoted price of a stock is best described as...
options: A number set by the company
The last price a buyer and seller agreed on
A fixed value for the day
steps.explain: It is the last match between a bid and an ask. No authority sets it; it is just where the two sides most recently met, and the next trade can move it again.

steps.title: Now you get it
recap: No one sets a stock's price; buyers and sellers do.
The quote is just the last price a trade happened at.
More buyers than sellers pushes it up, and the reverse pushes it down.

subject: MONEY
title: You Can Make Money When Prices Fall
blurb: Short sellers borrow a stock, sell it high, and buy it back cheap. Betting against things is real, and risky.

steps.title: Profit on the way down
steps.sub: Most people think you only make money when prices rise. But there is a trade that pays off when a price falls. It is called short selling, and it flips the usual order: you sell first and buy later.

steps.prompt: A trader thinks a stock will crash. How could they profit from a price that is about to fall?
options: Buy now and hope it rises anyway
Sell shares now, buy them back cheaper later
There is no way to profit from a fall
steps.explain: You can sell high before buying low. The trick is selling something you do not yet own, then buying it back once it is cheaper. That reversed order is the whole idea of shorting.

steps.title: You borrow what you sell
steps.body: To sell a stock you do not own, you borrow it. Your broker lends you the shares, usually for a fee, and you sell them right away at today's price. Now you hold cash but you owe the shares back. To close the trade, you buy the shares again and return them to the lender.

steps.prompt: In a short sale, where do the shares you sell come from?
options: You already owned them
You borrow them, usually from a broker
The company prints new ones for you
steps.explain: You borrow the shares and sell them immediately. That borrowing is what lets you sell something before you own it, and it is why you owe shares back at the end.

steps.title: The loss has no ceiling
steps.body: When you buy a stock, the worst case is it goes to zero and you lose what you paid. Shorting is different. If the price rises after you sell, you must buy back at the higher price to return the shares. A price can keep climbing with no limit, so your loss has no ceiling. Your gain, meanwhile, is capped, because the price can only fall to zero.

steps.prompt: Why can a short seller's loss be unlimited?
options: Brokers charge endless fees
A rising price has no upper limit, and they must buy back
The company can sue them
steps.explain: A stock can rise forever in principle, and the short seller has to buy back to return the borrowed shares. The higher it goes, the more they pay. A buyer's loss stops at zero, but a short's loss does not.

steps.prompt: Put a short sale in order, from start to finish.
items: Borrow shares from a broker
Sell the borrowed shares at today's price
Wait for the price to drop
Buy the shares back cheaper
Return the shares to the lender

glosses.Borrow shares from a broker: You cannot sell what you do not have, so you borrow first.
glosses.Sell the borrowed shares at today's pric: You lock in the high price now.
glosses.Wait for the price to drop: Your bet is that it falls.
glosses.Buy the shares back cheaper: You close the gap by buying low.
glosses.Return the shares to the lender: You give back what you borrowed and keep the difference.

steps.prompt: Which trade can in theory cost you more than you put in?
options: Buying a stock for cash
Short selling a stock
Both lose the same amount at most
steps.explain: Shorting is the one with no ceiling on losses, because the price you must buy back at can keep rising. Buying caps your loss at what you paid, since the price stops at zero.

steps.title: Now you get it
recap: Shorting reverses the order: sell borrowed shares first, buy them back later.
You profit if the price falls and lose if it rises.
Losses have no ceiling because a price can climb without limit.

subject: MONEY
title: Some Things Are Hard to Sell at Any Price
blurb: An asset is only worth what you can actually sell it for right now. Some money gets trapped for months.

steps.title: Worth on paper, not in hand
steps.sub: A house might be worth half a million dollars. But you cannot turn it into cash this afternoon. The price you could get today, fast, can be very different from the number on paper.

steps.prompt: You need cash today and own a house valued at $500,000. How fast can you turn it into money?
options: Instantly, at full value
Fast, but only by accepting a lower price
Never, houses cannot be sold
steps.explain: To sell fast you usually have to drop the price until someone bites. The $500,000 is what it might fetch given time. Speed costs you, because few assets convert to cash quickly at full value.

steps.title: Liquidity is how fast you can cash out
steps.body: Liquidity is how quickly and easily you can sell something for cash without slashing the price. Cash itself is perfectly liquid. A popular stock is close behind, since buyers are always waiting. A house, a rare coin, or a stake in a private business is illiquid: finding the right buyer takes time.

steps.prompt: What does it mean for an asset to be liquid?
options: It is worth a lot of money
You can sell it fast for cash without a big price cut
It rises in value over time
steps.explain: Liquidity is about speed of conversion to cash, not size of value. A liquid asset finds a buyer quickly at close to its going price. Value and liquidity are two separate things.

steps.title: Speed costs you a discount
steps.body: When an asset is illiquid, getting cash fast means cutting the price until a buyer appears. Wait longer and you might get full value, but you cannot have both speed and the top price. A wide gap between what buyers offer and what sellers want is the telltale sign that liquidity is low.

steps.prompt: To sell an illiquid asset quickly, what usually has to give?
options: The price, you accept less
Nothing, speed is free
The buyer pays extra for the rush
steps.explain: With few buyers around, the only way to close fast is to make the deal attractive by dropping the price. That discount is the cost of liquidity you do not have.

steps.prompt: Which asset is the most liquid?
options: A rare painting
Cash
A house
steps.explain: Cash is liquidity itself, ready to spend instantly. The painting and the house both need a buyer and time, so they trade speed for price. That is what makes them illiquid.

steps.title: Now you get it
recap: Liquidity is how fast you can sell for cash without a big price cut.
Cash is most liquid; houses and collectibles are not.
Selling an illiquid asset fast means accepting a discount.

subject: MONEY
title: Someone Is Always Ready to Trade With You
blurb: Market makers stand between buyers and sellers, pocketing the tiny gap so your trade fills instantly.

steps.title: The trade fills in an instant
steps.sub: You tap sell and the shares are gone in a blink. But who was waiting on the other side at that exact moment? Often not another regular trader. It was a market maker, whose whole job is to be ready.

steps.prompt: You sell a stock at noon. How is a buyer ready that very second?
options: You wait until someone happens to want it
A firm stands ready to buy at any moment
The exchange buys it and keeps it
steps.explain: A market maker is always there, quoting prices to buy and sell. Without one, you might wait for a matching buyer. The market maker fills the gap so your trade clears instantly.

steps.title: They quote two prices at once
steps.body: A market maker posts two prices for the same asset. The bid is what they will pay to buy from you. The ask is the higher price at which they will sell to you. They hold both quotes constantly, so whether you want to buy or sell, there is always a price waiting.

steps.prompt: What is the bid price?
options: The price the market maker will buy from you at
The price the market maker will sell to you at
The average of recent trades
steps.explain: The bid is the buy side: it is what the market maker pays you. The ask is the sell side and is higher. Holding both at once is what lets them trade with anyone.

steps.title: The gap is how they get paid
steps.body: The ask is always a bit higher than the bid. That difference is the bid-ask spread. Buy from a market maker and sell back instantly and you lose the spread. For them, that small gap, earned again and again across many trades, is the pay for always standing ready to deal.

steps.prompt: How does a market maker earn money?
options: By betting the price will rise
By keeping the gap between the bid and the ask
By charging a yearly membership
steps.explain: They buy at the lower bid and sell at the higher ask, keeping the spread. Repeated over thousands of trades, that tiny gap is their reward for providing instant liquidity.

steps.prompt: Which is higher, the bid or the ask?
options: The bid
The ask
They are always equal
steps.explain: The ask, the sell price, sits above the bid, the buy price. If they were equal there would be no spread, and the market maker would have no reason to stand in the middle.

steps.title: Now you get it
recap: Market makers quote a bid and an ask at the same time.
The bid buys from you; the higher ask sells to you.
The bid-ask spread is their pay for instant liquidity.

subject: MONEY
title: Why 'Buy Low, Sell High' Is Terrible Advice
blurb: Nobody rings a bell at the bottom. The hard part is that low always feels like it is going lower.

steps.title: Obvious, and useless
steps.sub: Buy low, sell high. Of course. Everyone agrees. So why do so few people manage it? Because the advice skips the only hard part: knowing which moment is the low and which is the high.

steps.prompt: A price has been falling for weeks. How do you know it has hit the bottom?
options: A clear signal tells you it is the low
You cannot know until later, in hindsight
The lowest day always feels obvious
steps.explain: The bottom is only visible after the price turns back up. In the moment, a low looks like it could fall further. That uncertainty is exactly what 'buy low' ignores.

steps.title: Low and high are clear only later
steps.body: On a chart looking back, the bottom and the top are obvious. Live, they are not. Nobody rings a bell to mark the low. A falling price might be the bargain of the year or the start of a longer slide, and you cannot tell which until the price has already moved.

steps.prompt: Why is it hard to spot the bottom in real time?
options: Charts are usually wrong
The low is only clear after the price turns back up
Prices never actually have a bottom
steps.explain: You only confirm a bottom once the price rises again. While it is falling, every low could go lower. The clarity you see on an old chart simply is not there in the moment.

steps.title: Fear pushes you the wrong way
steps.body: There is a second trap. When prices are low they are usually falling, and falling prices feel frightening. Fear tells you to sell, not buy. So the very moment 'buy low' calls for courage is the moment your gut screams to get out. The advice fights your instincts.

steps.prompt: When a price is low and falling, what does fear usually push people to do?
options: Buy more, since it is cheap
Sell, the opposite of buying low
Ignore the price entirely
steps.explain: Falling prices trigger fear, and fear says sell. That is the reverse of buying low. The strategy asks you to act against your strongest instinct at the worst-feeling time.

steps.prompt: When can you be sure a price hit its lowest point?
options: The day it happens
Only afterward, once it has risen again
When the news announces it
steps.explain: Only hindsight confirms a bottom, after the price has climbed back. In the moment, the low and the next-lower low look the same. That is why 'buy low' is easier said than done.

steps.title: Now you get it
recap: The low and high are obvious only in hindsight.
A falling price might keep falling, so the bottom is never clear live.
Fear pushes you to sell exactly when 'buy low' says to buy.

subject: MONEY
title: Prices Are a Message, Not a Verdict
blurb: A rising price is not greed. It is a signal telling producers to make more and buyers to use less.

steps.title: A price is information
steps.sub: When a price jumps, it is tempting to blame greed. But a price is mostly a message. It tells everyone at once how scarce something is, and quietly nudges them to do something about it.

steps.prompt: A cold snap kills half the orange crop. What happens to the price of oranges?
options: It falls, fewer oranges to sell
It rises, the same buyers chase fewer oranges
It stays flat, prices are set in advance
steps.explain: Supply dropped but people still want oranges, so buyers compete for what is left and the price rises. That jump is not a trick. It is the market reacting to real scarcity.

steps.title: A high price shouts scarcity
steps.body: When demand outruns supply, the price climbs. That higher number is a signal everyone can read. It says this thing is scarce right now. No central planner had to announce it. The price itself carries the news to every buyer and seller at the same time.

steps.prompt: What is a rising price mainly telling the market?
options: That sellers are being greedy
That the thing is scarce relative to demand
That the government raised taxes
steps.explain: A rising price signals scarcity: more people want it than there is to go around. It spreads that information instantly, without anyone needing to declare a shortage.

steps.title: The signal makes people act
steps.body: The price does not just inform, it prods. A higher price tempts producers to make more, because each sale earns more. At the same time it pushes buyers to use less or find substitutes. Both responses add supply and trim demand, which eases the shortage. The price coordinates everyone without a single order being given.

steps.prompt: How does a higher price affect producers?
options: It makes them produce less
It tempts them to produce more
It has no effect on production
steps.explain: A higher price means more profit per sale, so producers want to make more. Meanwhile buyers pull back. Together those moves push supply up and demand down until the gap closes.

steps.prompt: Put the market's response to a shortage in order.
items: Demand exceeds supply at the current price
The price rises
Producers make more and buyers buy less
The shortage eases

glosses.Demand exceeds supply at the current pri: More people want it than there is to sell.
glosses.The price rises: Competition for the scarce good pushes the price up.
glosses.Producers make more and buyers buy less: The higher price changes both sides' behavior.
glosses.The shortage eases: Supply and demand move back toward balance.

steps.prompt: A higher price nudges buyers to do what?
options: Buy more, while it lasts
Buy less or switch to substitutes
Ignore the price
steps.explain: A higher price makes buyers economize, buying less or finding alternatives. That is the demand side of the adjustment, working alongside producers making more to close the gap.

steps.title: Now you get it
recap: A rising price signals that something is scarce.
It tempts producers to make more and buyers to use less.
Those responses ease the shortage with no central command.

subject: MONEY
title: You Can Sell Wheat You Haven't Grown Yet
blurb: A futures contract lets a farmer lock in next year's price today, selling a harvest before a seed goes in the ground.

steps.title: Sold before it grows
steps.sub: A farmer in spring does not know what wheat will sell for at harvest. Prices could crash. So she sells the crop now, at a price agreed today, for delivery months from now. The wheat does not exist yet. The deal already does.

steps.prompt: Why would a farmer agree to sell her crop now at a fixed price instead of waiting for harvest?
options: To gamble that prices will rise
To remove the risk that prices fall before she can sell
Because she is not allowed to sell at harvest
steps.explain: She is buying certainty. By fixing the price now, she stops worrying about a price crash at harvest. She trades the chance of a higher price for protection against a lower one.

steps.title: A promise about a future price
steps.body: A futures contract is an agreement to buy or sell something at a set price on a set future date. The farmer promises to deliver wheat in six months at today's agreed price. A buyer promises to take it and pay that price. Both are locked in. Neither can back out because the market moved.

steps.prompt: On the delivery date, the market price of wheat is far below the agreed price. What happens?
options: The buyer still pays the agreed price
The deal is cancelled because the price changed
The farmer must match the lower market price
steps.explain: The price was locked at the start. Both sides are obligated to the agreed price no matter where the market goes. That obligation is exactly what makes the contract useful as protection.

steps.title: This is hedging
steps.body: Locking in a price to protect against swings is called hedging. The farmer gives up the upside, a possible higher price, to escape the downside, a possible crash. The buyer, maybe a bakery, hedges the other way: it locks in a cost so flour stays affordable to plan around. Both sleep better.

steps.prompt: What does the farmer give up by hedging with a futures contract?
options: Nothing, it is pure protection
The chance to sell higher if prices rise
Ownership of her land
steps.explain: Protection has a cost. If prices soar by harvest, she still sells at the lower locked price and misses the gain. Hedging trades away the upside to remove the downside.

steps.prompt: Put the futures deal in order, from spring to harvest.
items: Farmer and buyer agree on a price for future delivery
Both are locked into that price
Months pass and the crop grows
On the delivery date, the trade settles at the agreed price

glosses.Farmer and buyer agree on a price for fu: The contract is struck before the crop exists
glosses.Both are locked into that price: Neither side can walk away if the market moves
glosses.Months pass and the crop grows: Market prices may rise or fall in the meantime
glosses.On the delivery date, the trade settles: The locked price holds regardless of the current market

steps.prompt: By harvest, wheat is selling far higher than the price the farmer locked in. How did she do?
options: She profits from the higher market price
She still sells at her locked price and misses the gain
The contract is void because prices rose
steps.explain: The lock cuts both ways. She is bound to the agreed price, so she misses the rally. That is the price of certainty: no crash can hurt her, but no rally can help her.

steps.title: That's a futures contract.
recap: A futures contract fixes a buy or sell price for a future date.
Producers use it to hedge, trading upside for protection.
Both sides are locked in no matter where the market goes.

subject: MONEY
title: Why Trading on Secrets Is Illegal
blurb: Markets only work if everyone plays with roughly the same information. Acting on private tips can land you in prison.

steps.title: Knowing too much
steps.sub: You hear, before anyone else, that a company is about to be bought at a huge premium. You buy the stock. The deal goes public, the price jumps, you cash out. Easy money. Also a crime.

steps.prompt: Why might trading on secret company news be against the law?
options: Because making money on stocks is illegal
Because the person on the other side is trading without the same information
Because secrets are always false
steps.explain: The person who sold you the stock did not know about the deal. You exploited an information gap they had no way to close. Markets are meant to be a fair contest, and this rigs it.

steps.title: Material and nonpublic
steps.body: Insider trading means trading on information that is both material and nonpublic. Material means it would matter to a reasonable investor: a pending buyout, a failed drug trial, a surprise loss. Nonpublic means it has not been released to everyone. Trade on news that is both, and you are breaking the law.

steps.prompt: Which trade counts as illegal insider trading?
options: Buying after reading the company's public earnings report
Buying after a friend at the firm secretly tells you about a coming buyout
Buying because you like the product
steps.explain: The buyout tip is material and nonpublic. The earnings report is public, so anyone can use it. Insider trading needs both conditions: important news that the rest of the market does not have.

steps.title: Trust keeps the market open
steps.body: If insiders could trade on secrets freely, ordinary people would assume the game is rigged and stop playing. Fewer buyers and sellers means a weaker market for everyone. The ban is less about punishing one clever trade and more about protecting the trust that makes the whole market function.

steps.prompt: According to the lesson, what is the deeper reason for banning insider trading?
options: To stop anyone from getting rich
To protect the trust that keeps people participating in the market
To make stocks move slower
steps.explain: Trust is the point. If outsiders believe insiders always win, they leave, and the market thins out for everyone. The rule guards confidence, not just fairness in a single deal.

steps.title: Insiders can still trade, carefully
steps.body: Being an insider is not itself illegal. Executives often own their company's stock and can buy and sell it. The line is the information: they must not trade on material news the public does not yet have, and they must follow disclosure rules. Trade on the secret, and it becomes a crime.

steps.prompt: An executive learns of a coming buyout before it is announced and buys shares that day. Is this allowed?
options: Yes, executives can trade their own stock anytime
No, the buyout news is material and not yet public
Yes, because they work there
steps.explain: Owning the stock is fine; trading on the secret is not. The buyout is material and nonpublic, so acting on it crosses the line, even for an insider who is otherwise allowed to trade.

steps.title: Now you get it
recap: Insider trading is trading on material, nonpublic information.
It exploits an information gap the other side cannot close.
The ban protects the trust that keeps the market working.

subject: MONEY
title: A Company's 'Worth' Is Mostly Imaginary
blurb: Market cap multiplies the last share price by every share, but you could never sell them all at that price.

steps.title: The trillion-dollar number
steps.sub: A headline says a company is worth two trillion dollars. That number is the share price times every share that exists. It sounds like cash in a vault. It is closer to a guess that only holds if nobody tests it.

steps.prompt: If you owned every share, could you sell them all and collect the full market cap in cash?
options: Yes, market cap is the real cash value
No, dumping all the shares would crush the price
Yes, but only on a weekday
steps.explain: Flooding the market with shares means far more sellers than buyers, so the price collapses as you sell. You would collect a fraction of the headline number. Market cap assumes a price that selling itself destroys.

steps.title: Price times shares
steps.body: Market cap is simple arithmetic: the current share price multiplied by the total number of shares. But the price comes from the most recent trade, which usually involves a tiny sliver of all the shares. The whole company is valued using the price of its last small transaction.

steps.prompt: Where does the price in the market cap formula come from?
options: The most recent trade, often a small number of shares
An average of every share ever sold
What the company says it is worth
steps.explain: It is the latest trade price. That single recent transaction, often tiny, gets stamped onto every share at once. The headline value rides on a sliver of real trading.

steps.title: Selling moves the price
steps.body: There are only so many buyers ready at the current price. Sell a few shares and the price barely moves. Try to sell a huge block and you run out of buyers, so the price drops to tempt more. This is liquidity. Market cap quietly assumes infinite buyers at one price, which never exists.

steps.prompt: Why does a very large sell order push the price down?
options: Selling is against the rules
You run out of buyers at the current price and must accept less
The company cancels the shares
steps.explain: Buyers at the current price are limited. Once you exhaust them, the price must fall to attract more. So big sales fetch less per share than the headline price implies.

steps.prompt: Why is it wrong to treat market cap as the cash you could pull out of a company?
options: Because selling all the shares would drive the price down well below the headline
Because market cap is always too low
Because cash is not allowed in markets
steps.explain: The headline price only holds for small trades. Sell everything and you flood the market, the price caves, and you collect far less. Market cap is a snapshot, not a withdrawable balance.

steps.title: That's market cap.
recap: Market cap is the last share price times all shares.
That price comes from one small recent trade.
Selling everything would crush the price, so it is not real cash.

subject: MONEY
title: Speculating and Investing Aren't the Same Thing
blurb: One bets on the next price move. The other buys a piece of a business. Confusing them is how people get burned.

steps.title: Two very different bets
steps.sub: Two people buy the same stock. One wants to own a slice of a profitable business for years. The other wants to flip it next week for a quick gain. Same ticker, completely different game.

steps.prompt: What is the core difference between an investor and a speculator?
options: Investors use more money
Investors buy value over time; speculators bet on the next price move
There is no real difference
steps.explain: It is about what you are betting on. The investor bets the business will create value over years. The speculator bets the price will move soon. The asset can be identical; the reasoning is not.

steps.title: Investing buys productive value
steps.body: When you invest, you buy something for the value it produces over time. A share of stock is partial ownership of a company. If the business earns profits and grows, your slice is worth more and may pay you income. Your return comes from the thing actually working, not just from someone paying more later.

steps.prompt: Where does an investor's return mainly come from?
options: The underlying business producing value over time
Selling to someone at a higher price next week
Luck alone
steps.explain: Investing leans on the asset doing real work. A share is a piece of a company, so if the company prospers, the investor benefits regardless of short-term price noise.

steps.title: Speculating bets on the price
steps.body: When you speculate, you are betting on the price moving, usually soon, not on the asset producing value. You buy hoping to sell higher to the next person. The underlying business almost does not matter. Because the payoff depends on price swings, speculation tends to carry higher risk.

steps.prompt: Why does the lesson say speculation tends to be riskier?
options: Speculators ignore the news
The payoff rides on price swings, not on an underlying stream of value
It is taxed more heavily
steps.explain: With no productive value to fall back on, the bet lives or dies on price movement alone. Prices are volatile and unpredictable in the short run, so the risk runs higher.

steps.prompt: Someone buys a stock purely hoping to sell it higher in a few days. Investing or speculating?
options: Investing, since it is a stock
Speculating, since the bet is on a short-term price move
Neither, it is just saving
steps.explain: The asset being a stock does not settle it. Betting on a quick price jump rather than the business creating value is speculation, which leans on volatile prices and runs higher risk.

steps.title: Now you get it
recap: Investing buys value an asset produces over time.
Speculating bets on a short-term price move.
Same stock, different game, and speculation runs riskier.

subject: MONEY
title: Free Money Disappears the Moment It Appears
blurb: If the same thing sells cheaper in one place, traders pounce until the gap closes. That is why bargains rarely last.

steps.title: The gap that closes itself
steps.sub: The same gold bar trades for less in one market than another. Buy low there, sell high here, pocket the difference for no risk. It sounds like free money. The catch is everyone else can see it too.

steps.prompt: A clear, risk-free price gap appears for the same asset in two markets. What happens next?
options: It sits there for anyone to take whenever they like
Traders rush in and the gap closes fast
Regulators freeze both markets
steps.explain: The moment a free gain is visible, traders pile in to grab it. Their buying and selling moves the prices together until the gap vanishes. Obvious bargains do not wait around.

steps.title: Buy cheap here, sell dear there
steps.body: Arbitrage means buying the same asset where it is cheap and selling it where it is expensive, at the same time, to capture the difference. If a stock is a dollar lower on one exchange, you buy there and sell on the other. The profit comes from the price gap, not from the asset going up.

steps.prompt: Where does an arbitrage profit come from?
options: The asset rising in value over time
The price difference between two markets for the same asset
Interest paid by the exchange
steps.explain: Arbitrage does not need the asset to rise. It harvests the gap between two prices for the same thing right now, which is why it is treated as low risk when it is clean.

steps.title: The act of grabbing it closes it
steps.body: Buying in the cheap market lifts that price. Selling in the expensive market pushes that price down. So the two prices move toward each other. As more traders do this, the gap shrinks until it disappears. The same asset ends up at nearly one price everywhere, the law of one price.

steps.prompt: How does arbitrage make the gap disappear?
options: Buying raises the cheap price and selling lowers the dear one, until they meet
It hides the prices from other traders
It freezes the cheaper market
steps.explain: The trades themselves move the prices together. Demand lifts the low side, supply drops the high side. The very act of capturing the gain erases the gain.

steps.prompt: Order what happens when a clean arbitrage appears.
items: Same asset trades cheaper in one market than another
Traders buy in the cheap market and sell in the expensive one
The cheap price rises and the expensive price falls
The gap closes and the free profit is gone

glosses.Same asset trades cheaper in one market: A visible, risk-free price difference opens up
glosses.Traders buy in the cheap market and sell: Everyone races to capture the same gap
glosses.The cheap price rises and the expensive: Their own trades push the prices together
glosses.The gap closes and the free profit is go: Prices converge to nearly one level

steps.prompt: Why do obvious, risk-free price gaps almost never last?
options: Traders rush in, and their trades push the two prices together
Markets are legally required to match prices
Nobody is ever watching the prices
steps.explain: The crowd chasing the same free gain moves the prices toward each other until the gap closes. The bargain destroys itself the instant people act on it.

steps.title: That's arbitrage.
recap: Arbitrage profits from a price gap for the same asset.
Buying cheap and selling dear pushes the prices together.
Acting on the gap closes it, so free money does not last.

subject: MONEY
title: There Are Always Two Prices, Not One
blurb: The price to buy and the price to sell are never the same. That hidden gap is a cost you pay on every trade.

steps.title: Two prices at once
steps.sub: You think a stock has a price. It doesn't. At any moment there's a price you can buy at and a lower price you can sell at. The gap between them never closes, and it quietly costs you on every trade.

steps.prompt: You buy a stock and sell it one second later, with no change in the market. What happens to your money?
options: You break even, since nothing moved
You lose a little, because buying and selling have different prices
You gain a little from trading activity
steps.explain: You lose a little. You bought at the higher price and sold at the lower one. The two prices are never equal, so a round trip costs you the gap even when the market sits still.

steps.title: Bid, ask, and the gap
steps.body: At any moment there are two quotes. The bid is the highest price someone will pay you to buy from you. The ask is the lowest price someone will sell to you. The ask is always higher than the bid. The difference between them is the bid-ask spread. You buy at the ask and sell at the bid, so you cross the gap each way.

steps.prompt: When you buy, which price do you pay?
options: The bid, the lower one
The ask, the higher one
The exact midpoint
steps.explain: You pay the ask, the higher price. To sell you'd get the bid, the lower one. Because the ask sits above the bid, you start every position already down by the spread.

steps.title: Busy markets have thin gaps
steps.body: The size of the spread depends on how heavily a thing trades. When lots of people are buying and selling, the bid and ask sit close together, so the spread is narrow. When few people trade something, the gap widens, because buyers and sellers are scarce and far apart. The spread is the price of getting in and out right now.

steps.prompt: Which is more likely to have a wide bid-ask spread?
options: A stock that trades millions of shares a day
An obscure asset almost nobody trades
Both have the same spread
steps.explain: The obscure, thinly traded asset. With few buyers and sellers, the closest bid and ask are far apart. Heavy trading packs orders tightly together, which narrows the gap.

steps.prompt: Why does an instant buy-then-sell lose money even when the market hasn't moved?
options: Taxes are charged on every trade
You buy at the ask and sell at the lower bid
Prices always fall after you buy
steps.explain: You buy at the ask and sell at the bid, and the ask is higher. The spread is a real cost you pay regardless of which way the market goes.

steps.title: Now you see the gap
recap: The bid is what you can sell at; the ask is what you can buy at.
The ask is always higher, and the gap is the bid-ask spread.
Busy markets have narrow spreads; thin ones have wide spreads.

subject: MONEY
title: Markets Run on Fear and Greed, Not Math
blurb: Prices swing far more than the facts do, because crowds panic and get euphoric in waves.

steps.title: The mood swings of the crowd
steps.sub: A company's value doesn't double in a week. But its stock price can. The gap between steady facts and wild prices is filled by something simpler than math: how the crowd feels right now.

steps.prompt: A company's actual business barely changes over a month. Can its stock price still swing wildly?
options: No, the price tracks the business closely
Yes, prices move on emotion more than on facts
Only if the company releases news
steps.explain: Yes. Prices move far more than the underlying facts do. Buyers and sellers react to hope and fear, so the price can lurch even when nothing real has changed.

steps.title: Price is a vote on feeling
steps.body: A price is just what the last buyer and seller agreed on. Those people are human, so their mood leaks into the number. When they feel greedy and hopeful, they bid prices up. When they feel afraid, they dump and prices fall. The facts about a company change slowly, but feelings change fast, so prices swing more than reality does.

steps.prompt: Why do prices swing more than the underlying facts?
options: Companies change their value daily
Buyers and sellers act on fast-changing emotion
Math errors move the market
steps.explain: Emotion moves fast while facts move slow. Each trade reflects how people feel right now, so the price overshoots what the slow-moving facts would justify.

steps.title: The crowd feeds on itself
steps.body: Feelings spread. When prices rise, people see others getting rich and pile in, which pushes prices even higher. That is greed feeding a bubble. When prices fall, fear spreads and people sell to escape, which drives prices lower still. This is herding: the move causes more of the same move. It is why markets overshoot in both directions.

steps.prompt: What does herding do to a price move?
options: It cancels the move out
It amplifies the move in the same direction
It has no effect on prices
steps.explain: It amplifies it. Rising prices pull in more buyers and falling prices trigger more sellers. The crowd reacts to the move itself, so the trend feeds on its own momentum.

steps.prompt: Why can a stock lurch even when nothing real about the company changed?
options: Because prices move on emotion, not just facts
Because the company hid bad news
Because math models broke down
steps.explain: Prices reflect the mood of buyers and sellers, and mood shifts fast. The facts can sit still while fear or greed jerks the price around.

steps.title: Now you read the crowd
recap: A price reflects how buyers and sellers feel, not just the facts.
Emotion changes faster than facts, so prices overshoot.
Herding amplifies moves: greed fuels bubbles, fear fuels crashes.

subject: MONEY
title: Markets Solve a Problem You Never Think About
blurb: A market's real job is matching strangers who want to trade, turning 'who has what I need?' into a single price.

steps.title: The hidden matching problem
steps.sub: You want bread. The baker wants shoes. You don't make shoes. So how does anyone ever trade? Markets exist to solve exactly this, and they do it so well you forget the problem was ever there.

steps.prompt: In a world with no money, what makes trading hard?
options: People don't want to trade
You must find someone who has what you want and wants what you have
There's nothing to trade
steps.explain: You need a perfect match on both sides. Without that, no trade happens. This double requirement is the core problem markets and money were built to solve.

steps.title: The double coincidence of wants
steps.body: Trade without money is barter. To barter, you must find someone who has what you want and also wants what you have. Both conditions at once. This is called the double coincidence of wants. It is rare and slow. You can spend all day looking for the one person who happens to want your exact thing and has your exact need.

steps.prompt: What is the 'double coincidence of wants'?
options: Both traders must want what the other has
Two people wanting the same item
Trading twice in one day
steps.explain: Each side must want what the other offers. Both conditions must hold at the same time, which is why barter is so slow and limiting.

steps.title: Money breaks the deadlock
steps.body: Money fixes this. You sell your thing for money to anyone who wants it, then spend that money on what you need from someone else. You no longer need one person to want your goods and supply yours. Money is a common medium everyone accepts, so it splits one hard double-match into two easy single trades.

steps.prompt: How does money make trade easier?
options: It makes goods more valuable
It removes the need for both sides to want each other's goods
It hides the price of things
steps.explain: Money is accepted by everyone, so you can sell to one person and buy from another. That breaks the double coincidence of wants into two simple trades.

steps.title: One number does the matching
steps.body: A market gathers many buyers and sellers so they don't have to hunt each other down. Out of all their bids and offers, one price emerges from supply and demand. That price is also a signal. A high price says a thing is scarce or wanted; a low price says the opposite. So the market answers 'who has what I need?' with a single number.

steps.prompt: Put the evolution of trade in order, from hardest to easiest.
items: Barter: find a perfect double match
Money: sell to anyone, buy from anyone
Market: many traders meet at one price

glosses.Barter: find a perfect double match: Both sides must want what the other has
glosses.Money: sell to anyone, buy from anyone: A common medium splits trade into two easy steps
glosses.Market: many traders meet at one price: Crowds and a single price remove the search

steps.prompt: Why is pure barter so slow?
options: Goods are too heavy to carry
You need someone who wants your goods and has what you want
There is never enough money
steps.explain: Barter needs a double coincidence of wants. Finding one person who matches you on both sides is rare, which is exactly the bottleneck money and markets remove.

steps.title: Now you see the problem
recap: Barter needs a double coincidence of wants, so it's slow.
Money lets you sell to one person and buy from another.
A market sets one price that signals scarcity and demand.

subject: MONEY
title: Ban Something and a Market Appears Anyway
blurb: Outlaw a product and demand doesn't vanish. It moves underground, usually more dangerous and more expensive.

steps.title: Demand doesn't take orders
steps.sub: Lawmakers ban a thing, expecting it to disappear. But people who wanted it still want it. So a new market grows in the dark, with worse prices and no safety net.

steps.prompt: A government bans a popular product. What usually happens to demand for it?
options: Demand disappears with the ban
Demand stays and is met illegally
Demand drops to zero overnight
steps.explain: Demand stays. People who wanted the thing still want it, and a ban doesn't change that. So sellers step in illegally to meet it. The market moves underground instead of vanishing.

steps.title: A ban moves the market, it doesn't kill it
steps.body: A market is just buyers meeting sellers. Banning the product removes neither side. The people who want it still want it, and someone will supply it for a profit. So a black market forms: an illegal market for the banned good. The classic case is US alcohol prohibition from 1920 to 1933, which created a huge illegal liquor trade.

steps.prompt: Why does a ban create a black market instead of ending trade?
options: The buyers and sellers still exist and still want to trade
Bans make products more popular
Governments secretly sell the goods
steps.explain: Both sides of the market remain. Banning the good doesn't remove the demand or the willing sellers, so trade simply continues out of sight.

steps.title: Risk makes it costlier and more dangerous
steps.body: Illegal trade carries risk, and risk has a price. Sellers can be arrested, so they charge more to make it worth their while. That pushes prices up. Worse, no one regulates quality, so the product may be unsafe. And since you can't call the police or use a court over a bad deal, disputes can turn violent.

steps.prompt: Why are black market goods often more expensive?
options: They are always higher quality
Sellers face legal risk and price it in
There are too many sellers
steps.explain: Sellers risk arrest, so they demand more money to take that risk. The legal danger gets baked into the price, on top of losing any safety guarantees.

steps.prompt: After a ban, what happens to the people who still want the product?
options: They stop wanting it
They keep wanting it, so the trade goes underground
They wait for the ban to lift
steps.explain: Their demand doesn't vanish. As long as buyers want it and sellers can profit, the market reappears illegally, just riskier and pricier than before.

steps.title: Now you get it
recap: A ban removes neither the buyers nor the sellers, so trade continues.
The market moves underground as a black market.
Legal risk raises prices and strips away safety and recourse.

subject: MONEY
title: A Barrel of Oil Is a Barrel of Oil
blurb: Some goods are interchangeable down to the unit, which is exactly what lets the whole world trade them on one price.

steps.title: When one unit equals any other
steps.sub: Nobody asks which exact barrel of oil they're buying. One is as good as another. That sameness sounds boring, but it's the trick that lets the entire planet trade these goods on a single price.

steps.prompt: Why can the world trade oil on one global price, but not, say, used cars?
options: Oil is cheaper than cars
Any barrel of a given grade is interchangeable; each used car is unique
Cars are illegal to trade globally
steps.explain: One barrel of a given grade is as good as any other, so a single price works. Each used car is different, so each needs its own inspection and price. That sameness is the key idea.

steps.title: Fungible means interchangeable
steps.body: A good is fungible when one unit can swap for another of the same kind and grade with no difference that matters. A dollar is fungible: any dollar equals any other. A barrel of crude oil of a set grade is fungible too. A specific painting is not, because each one is unique. Fungible goods are defined by their type, not their individual identity.

steps.prompt: Which of these is NOT fungible?
options: A single dollar bill
An original painting
A bushel of standard-grade wheat
steps.explain: The painting. Each one is unique, so it can't be swapped for another without changing what you have. Dollars and graded wheat are interchangeable unit for unit.

steps.title: Grades make sameness official
steps.body: Goods aren't naturally identical, so markets define standards. Crude oil comes in grades; wheat is sorted by type and quality. Once a grade is agreed, every unit of that grade counts as equal. This is what makes a commodity tradable. Buyers and sellers trust that a unit is a unit, without inspecting each one.

steps.prompt: What makes one unit of a commodity count as equal to another?
options: An agreed standard or grade
The seller's reputation
The color of the packaging
steps.explain: An agreed grade. Standards let everyone treat units as equivalent, so no one has to inspect each item. That trust is what turns a raw good into a tradable commodity.

steps.title: Sameness means one price
steps.body: Because each unit is interchangeable, you don't haggle over individual items. The whole grade trades at one market price. That is why you can see a single price for gold or oil that buyers and sellers around the world use. Fungibility is the quiet feature that makes a global market on one number possible.

steps.prompt: Put the chain that makes a global commodity price possible in order.
items: Agree a standard grade for the good
Each unit of that grade is interchangeable
The whole grade trades at one price

glosses.Agree a standard grade for the good: Define quality so units count as equal
glosses.Each unit of that grade is interchangeab: A unit is a unit, no inspection needed
glosses.The whole grade trades at one price: One number works for buyers everywhere

steps.prompt: What does it mean for a good to be fungible?
options: It is very valuable
One unit is interchangeable with another of the same grade
It can only be sold once
steps.explain: Fungible means any unit swaps for another of the same kind and grade. That interchangeability is exactly what lets a commodity trade on a single price.

steps.title: Now you get it
recap: Fungible means one unit is interchangeable with another.
Agreed grades make units count as equal.
Sameness lets a commodity trade on one global price.

subject: MONEY
title: Almost Nobody Can Time the Market
blurb: Miss the handful of best days over decades and your returns collapse. The catch: those days hide next to the worst ones.

steps.title: The ten days that decide everything
steps.sub: Over decades, a tiny number of trading days carry most of the gains. Step out of the market and you risk missing them. And they show up at the worst possible moment to predict.

steps.prompt: An investor sits out just the ten best market days across several decades. Roughly what happens to their total return?
options: Barely changes, ten days is nothing
Drops a little
Falls dramatically, often by half or more
steps.explain: It falls dramatically. Gains are not spread evenly across thousands of days. A small cluster of huge days does most of the work, so missing even a few of them guts the result.

steps.title: Returns hide in a few days
steps.body: Market gains are lumpy, not steady. Across decades of trading days, a small handful deliver an outsized share of the total return. Studies of long-run index returns show that missing just the best ten or so days over many years can cut your final result by half or more. Stay fully invested and you capture those days by default. Step out, and you are gambling that none of them happen while you are gone.

steps.prompt: Why does missing a few days hurt so much?
options: Returns are spread evenly, so every day matters equally
A small number of days deliver an outsized share of total returns
Trading fees pile up on the days you miss
steps.explain: Gains are lumpy. A few enormous days carry most of the decades-long return, so being absent for even a handful of them does real damage.

steps.title: The best days sit next to the worst
steps.body: Here is the trap. The biggest up days tend to cluster right beside the biggest down days, often within the same stretch of weeks. Many of the largest single-day jumps land during or just after a crash, exactly when a scared investor has already sold. So selling to dodge the worst days tends to make you miss the best ones too. To win at timing you would need two right calls: when to get out, and when to get back in.

steps.prompt: When do many of the market's best days tend to happen?
options: During calm, steadily rising stretches
During or just after sharp downturns
Only at the start of a new year
steps.explain: Big up days cluster around big down days. Many of the largest jumps come during or right after a crash, when someone who sold to avoid losses is sitting on the sidelines and misses the rebound.

steps.prompt: Why does staying fully invested beat trying to jump in and out?
options: It guarantees you avoid every down day
It captures the best days automatically, with no need to predict them
It removes all risk from investing
steps.explain: Staying in means you are present for the best days without having to forecast them. Timing requires two correct guesses; staying invested requires none.

steps.title: Time in the market beats timing it.
recap: A few days carry most of the long-run return.
The best days cluster right next to the worst.
Staying invested captures them without any guessing.

subject: MONEY
title: Most Trades Don't Send Money to the Company
blurb: Buy a stock and you usually pay another investor, not the business. The company got its cash years ago at the IPO.

steps.title: Who actually gets your money
steps.sub: You buy a share of a famous company. It feels like you handed cash to the business. You almost certainly didn't. You paid another investor who wanted out.

steps.prompt: You buy 10 shares of a big public company today. Where does your money go?
options: Straight to the company
To another investor selling those shares
Split between the company and the exchange
steps.explain: To another investor. You are buying used shares from someone who already owned them. The company is not on the other side of your trade and sees none of that cash.

steps.title: The stock market is mostly resale
steps.body: Day to day, the stock market is a resale market. Shares pass from one investor to another, like used cars changing owners. When you buy, your money goes to whoever sold, not to the business whose name is on the share. Ownership moves; the company's bank balance does not. This everyday trading is called the secondary market.

steps.prompt: In a normal stock trade, what changes hands?
options: New cash flows into the company
Ownership of existing shares moves between investors
The company prints fresh shares for you
steps.explain: Existing shares simply change owners. It is resale. The company already issued those shares long ago and gets nothing from your purchase.

steps.title: The company cashed in at the IPO
steps.body: So when does the company get money? Mainly when it sells its own shares for the first time, the initial public offering, or in later issuances. That is the primary market, and it usually happened years ago. After that, the shares trade among investors forever without sending another cent to the business. A rising price helps the company raise money more easily next time, but it does not deposit cash directly.

steps.prompt: When does a company actually collect cash from its shares?
options: Every time someone buys the stock
When it first sells its own shares, like at the IPO
Whenever the price goes up
steps.explain: When it sells its own shares, usually at the IPO or a later issuance, in the primary market. Ordinary buying and selling afterward never reaches the company.

steps.title: Buying stock is mostly buying used.
recap: Your trade pays another investor, not the company.
Daily trading is resale: the secondary market.
The company got its cash at the IPO or later issuances.

subject: SCIENCE
title: Why the sky is blue
blurb: Sunlight is every color. The air picks one to throw around.

steps.title: Why the sky is blue
steps.sub: Not because the air is blue, and not because of the oceans. The real reason is hiding in the sunlight itself.

steps.prompt: On a clear day, why is the sky blue?
options: The air itself is blue
Sunlight scatters, and blue scatters the most
It reflects the blue of the oceans
steps.explain: Sunlight is white, a mix of every color. The air scatters short blue wavelengths far more than the rest, so blue light bounces across the whole sky and into your eyes from every direction.

steps.title: White light, scattered
steps.body: Sunlight looks white but is really every color mixed together. When it hits the air, molecules scatter it in all directions. Short wavelengths, the blues, scatter much more strongly than long ones, the reds. So blue light gets flung all over the sky, and that is the color you see when you look up.

steps.prompt: Which part of sunlight does the air scatter the most?
options: The long red wavelengths
The short blue wavelengths
All colors equally
steps.explain: The short blue wavelengths. Scattering hits short wavelengths far harder than long ones, which is why blue dominates the daytime sky.

steps.title: Why sunsets go red
steps.body: At sunset the sun sits low, so its light slices through much more air to reach you. Along that long path almost all the blue has already been scattered away. What is left to come straight through is the red and orange. Same scattering, opposite result.

steps.prompt: Looking straight at the setting sun, why does it glow red?
options: The sun cools down in the evening
Over the long path through the air, the blue scattered away and left the red
Red light is simply brighter
steps.explain: The long path is the key. The same scattering that paints the midday sky blue strips the blue out of low-angle sunlight, leaving the reds to reach you directly.

steps.title: That's why the sky is blue.
recap: Sunlight is all colors mixed
Air scatters short blue wavelengths the most
Long path at sunset scatters blue away, leaving red

subject: SCIENCE
title: Why ice floats
blurb: Almost every solid sinks in its own liquid. Water breaks the rule.

steps.title: Why ice floats
steps.sub: Freeze almost any liquid and the solid sinks. Water does the opposite, and life in cold places depends on it.

steps.prompt: Most solids sink in their own melted liquid. What does ice do in water?
options: Sinks, like most solids
Floats
Dissolves away
steps.explain: Ice floats. Water is the rare exception: its solid form is less dense than its liquid, so it sits on top instead of sinking.

steps.title: Water expands when it freezes
steps.body: Most substances pack tighter and get denser as they solidify. Water reverses this. As it freezes, its molecules lock into an open, six-sided lattice that takes up more space than the jostling liquid did. More volume for the same mass means lower density, so ice floats.

steps.prompt: Why is ice less dense than the water it came from?
options: Its molecules pack together more tightly
It freezes into an open lattice that takes up more space
It is full of trapped air
steps.explain: The open lattice. Freezing spreads water molecules into a roomier structure, so the same mass fills more volume and density drops.

steps.title: Why it matters
steps.body: Because ice floats, a pond freezes from the top down. That floating lid of ice insulates the water underneath, so fish and plants can survive the winter below it. If ice sank, ponds would freeze solid from the bottom up and far less would live through the cold.

steps.prompt: A sealed glass bottle filled to the brim with water is left in the freezer. What happens?
options: Nothing, water keeps its volume
The water expands as it freezes and can crack the bottle
The water shrinks and pulls away from the glass
steps.explain: It expands. The same expansion that makes ice float has nowhere to go in a sealed bottle, so it pushes outward hard enough to crack the glass.

steps.title: That's why ice floats.
recap: Water expands as it freezes, unlike most substances
An open lattice makes ice less dense than liquid water
Floating ice insulates the water below it

subject: SCIENCE
title: How vaccines train your immune system
blurb: Protection without the disease. It's a training exercise.

steps.title: How vaccines train your immune system
steps.sub: A vaccine protects you from a disease without making you sick. The trick is that it teaches, rather than fights.

steps.prompt: A vaccine protects you without giving you the disease. How does it pull that off?
options: It kills germs in your blood on contact
It shows your immune system a harmless preview so it is ready
It swaps out your blood for clean blood
steps.explain: It is a preview. A vaccine presents a harmless piece or weakened form of the germ. Your immune system learns it in advance and remembers it, so the real thing meets a prepared defense.

steps.title: A training exercise
steps.body: A vaccine carries an antigen, a recognizable piece of a pathogen, with no ability to cause the disease. Your immune system treats it as a genuine threat. It builds antibodies that lock onto that exact shape, and it stores memory cells that remember the encounter.

steps.prompt: What does a typical vaccine actually contain?
options: The full, dangerous disease
A harmless antigen the immune system can learn from
A general-purpose antibiotic
steps.explain: A harmless antigen. It is a recognizable piece or weakened form of the pathogen that teaches the immune system without the power to make you ill.

steps.title: Why you don't get sick later
steps.body: The first time your body meets a new germ, building a defense takes several days, long enough to fall ill. After a vaccine, the memory cells already exist. Meet the real pathogen and the response is usually fast enough to stop it before it takes hold.

steps.prompt: Put the immune training in order, from the shot to lasting protection.
items: Vaccine introduces a harmless antigen
Immune system flags it as foreign
Antibodies are built to match it
Memory cells store the recipe
Real pathogen arrives and is cleared fast

glosses.Immune system flags it as foreign: Treats it as a real threat.

steps.prompt: Why does a brand-new infection often make you sick, but a matching vaccine prevents that?
options: Vaccines remove the germ from the world
The memory cells already exist, so the response is fast
The disease weakens over time on its own
steps.explain: Memory cells. They let your body skip the slow first-time response and react fast, the same prepared defense you just traced through the sequence.

steps.title: That's how vaccines work.
recap: A vaccine is a harmless antigen, not the disease
Your body builds antibodies and stores memory cells
Memory makes the real response fast enough to stop illness

subject: SPORTS
title: The offside rule, finally explained
blurb: One line you can't get behind, judged at one exact moment.

steps.title: The offside rule, finally explained
steps.sub: It is the rule everyone pretends to understand. Two ideas make the whole thing click, and you'll have them in a few minutes.

steps.prompt: The offside rule exists to stop one thing. What?
options: Players passing the ball backward
An attacker lurking behind the defense for easy goals
Goalkeepers leaving their box
steps.explain: It stops goal-hanging. Without offside, an attacker could just camp behind the defenders and wait for a long ball. The rule keeps attackers from getting too far ahead of the play.

steps.title: The line you can't get behind
steps.body: When a teammate plays the ball to you in the attacking half, you are offside if you are closer to the goal than both the ball and the second-to-last opponent. In practice that opponent is usually the last defender, since the goalkeeper is often the very last. Stay level with that defender or in front of the ball and you are fine.

steps.prompt: In the attacking half you receive a pass while standing closer to the goal than every opponent except the keeper. Offside?
options: Yes
No
Only if you score
steps.explain: Offside. You are beyond the second-to-last opponent when the ball is played, which is exactly the position the rule forbids.

steps.title: Judged at the moment of the pass
steps.body: Here is the part that confuses people. Offside is judged the instant the ball is played, not when you receive it. So you can be onside at the kick and then sprint behind the defense to collect it. What matters is where you were when your teammate struck the pass.

steps.prompt: A striker is exactly level with the last defender the moment the ball is kicked, then races clear onto it. Onside or offside?
options: Onside, level counts as onside
Offside, he finished behind the defender
Offside, he ran too early
steps.explain: Onside. Position is frozen at the moment of the pass, and level is allowed. Where he ends up after the ball is struck does not matter.

steps.title: That's offside.
recap: It stops attackers camping behind the defense
Offside = beyond the ball and the second-to-last opponent
Judged at the moment the ball is played; level is onside

subject: SPORTS
title: Why marathoners hit the wall at mile 20
blurb: The body has two fuel tanks. One runs dry right about there.

steps.title: Why marathoners hit the wall at mile 20
steps.sub: Runners dread the wall, and it tends to strike at a very specific point. The reason is simple fuel math.

steps.prompt: Marathoners often hit the wall near mile 20 of 26. Why right there?
options: They lose focus that far in
Their stored carb fuel runs low around then
The course is always uphill at mile 20
steps.explain: Fuel runs out. The body stores only so much fast-burning carbohydrate, enough for roughly 20 miles of hard running. When it runs low, the switch to fat for fuel is slower and pace falls apart.

steps.title: Two fuel tanks
steps.body: Your muscles run mainly on glycogen, carbohydrate stored in the muscles and liver. It burns fast and clean, but the tank is small, enough for roughly 18 to 22 miles for a typical runner, depending on fitness and fueling. You also carry far more energy as fat, but fat burns slower and cannot be released quickly enough to hold a fast pace.

steps.prompt: Why is glycogen the body's go-to fuel for fast running?
options: There is an unlimited supply of it
It burns fast, but the tank is small
It is the only fuel the body has
steps.explain: Fast but limited. Glycogen delivers energy quickly, which is what racing needs, but the store is small enough to run low before the finish.

steps.title: Hitting the wall
steps.body: When glycogen runs out, the body has to lean on fat, which cannot deliver energy fast enough to hold race pace. The legs feel heavy and the pace crashes. Runners hold it off by carb-loading beforehand, pacing evenly so they don't burn through early, and taking in carbs during the race.

steps.prompt: Why does taking energy gels during a marathon help hold off the wall?
options: They add carbohydrate to top up the fast-burning tank
They make fat burn instantly
They remove the need for any fuel
steps.explain: They refuel the carb tank. Gels add carbohydrate to your bloodstream and muscles so the glycogen does not run dry, the same fuel logic that explains the wall.

steps.title: That's the wall.
recap: Muscles prefer glycogen: fast-burning but limited
It lasts roughly 20 miles, then runs low
Fat is slower, so pace crashes unless you refuel

subject: SCIENCE
title: The Force That Isn't There
blurb: Nothing pushes you outward in a turn. Your body just wants to keep going straight.

steps.title: The force that isn't there
steps.sub: Take a sharp turn and something seems to shove you outward. It feels obvious. It's also not real. Here's what's actually happening.

steps.prompt: A car turns left and you slide toward the right door. What pushed you right?
options: An outward force from the turn
Nothing; you kept going straight while the car curved
The door pulled you toward it
steps.explain: Nothing pushed you. You were already moving forward. The car turned left under you, and your body tried to keep going straight, which left you pressed against the door on the outside of the turn.

steps.title: Things want to go straight
steps.body: A moving object keeps going in a straight line unless something forces it to change. This is Newton's first law. Nothing keeps you moving on its own, and nothing turns you on its own. So when a car curves, your body's plan is unchanged: straight ahead. The car veers off that line, but you don't, until something makes you.

steps.prompt: Why does your body keep heading straight when the car turns?
options: An outward force overpowers the car
A moving object continues straight unless a force changes it
The car is heavier than you
steps.explain: It is the first law. With no force redirecting you, your straight-line motion just continues. The turn is the car's, not yours, until the seat or door pushes you.

steps.title: The real force points inward
steps.body: To make you curve, something must pull you toward the center of the turn. For the car, that is friction from the tires gripping the road. That inward pull is the centripetal force, and it is the only real force turning you. The door pressing your shoulder is also inward. There is no outward force. The outward feeling is just your straight-line body meeting the inward-curving car.

steps.prompt: What actually makes a car follow a curve instead of going straight?
options: Friction from the tires pulling it inward
An outward centrifugal force
The weight of the passengers
steps.explain: Tire friction, pointing inward. That inward pull, the centripetal force, bends the path. The so-called outward force is just the absence of one acting on you.

steps.prompt: Swing a ball on a string in a circle, then the string snaps. The ball flies...
options: Straight outward, away from the center
Straight along the direction it was already moving
It drops straight down at once
steps.explain: Straight along its motion, not outward. The string was pulling it inward the whole time. Remove that pull and the first law takes over: it leaves on a straight line, the tangent, not a line aimed away from the center.

steps.prompt: Which way does the one real force in a turn point?
options: Outward, away from the center
Inward, toward the center
Forward, in the direction of travel
steps.explain: Inward. That is the centripetal force, the only real force bending your path. The outward shove you feel is your inertia, not a force.

steps.title: That's centrifugal force.
recap: Moving things go straight unless a force turns them.
The only real force in a turn points inward.
The outward push is your inertia, not a force.

subject: SCIENCE
title: A Hammer and a Feather Hit at the Same Time
blurb: Heavy things don't fall faster. Remove the air and everything speeds up identically.

steps.title: They hit at the same time
steps.sub: Drop a hammer and a feather and the hammer wins. Obvious, right? It's also the air's doing, not gravity's. Take the air away and it's a tie.

steps.prompt: Drop a hammer and a feather together in a room with no air at all. They...
options: Hammer lands first; it's heavier
Land at the same time
Feather lands first; it's lighter
steps.explain: Same time. The feather only loses on Earth because air slows it down. Remove the air and gravity speeds up both at exactly the same rate, no matter their weight.

steps.title: Air is what slows the feather
steps.body: A feather drifts down slowly because it has a lot of surface and very little weight, so the air pushes back hard compared to its size. A hammer barely notices the air. So in daily life heavy things often do land first. But that is the air sorting them, not gravity. The difference you see is a fight against air, not a difference in pull.

steps.prompt: Why does a feather fall slowly in normal air?
options: Gravity pulls less on light things
Air pushes back hard compared to its tiny weight
Feathers fall up slightly
steps.explain: Air resistance. With lots of surface and little weight, the air holds it back. Gravity is not weaker on it. The air is just a bigger obstacle for it than for a hammer.

steps.title: More weight, more sluggishness, even trade
steps.body: Here is the catch. A heavier object is pulled harder by gravity. But heavier things are also harder to get moving; they have more inertia. The extra pull is exactly matched by the extra sluggishness. So the two cancel, and every object speeds up at the same rate. Mass drops out. Without air, a bowling ball and a marble fall together.

steps.prompt: A heavier object is pulled harder by gravity. Why doesn't it fall faster in a vacuum?
options: The extra pull is canceled by its extra inertia
Gravity weakens for heavy things
It does fall faster; the vacuum changes nothing
steps.explain: The two effects cancel. More mass means more pull but also more resistance to being moved. They trade off exactly, so the acceleration is the same for everything.

steps.title: They proved it on the Moon
steps.body: The Moon has essentially no air. On Apollo 15 in 1971, astronaut David Scott dropped a hammer and a feather at the same time. With no air to slow the feather, they fell together and hit the ground at the same moment, on camera. It is the cleanest version of an idea Galileo argued for centuries earlier.

steps.prompt: So in everyday life, why does a hammer usually land before a feather?
options: Gravity pulls harder on the hammer
Air slows the feather far more than the hammer
The hammer is closer to the ground
steps.explain: Air resistance. Strip the air away, as on the Moon, and they land together. The everyday difference is the air at work, not gravity playing favorites.

steps.title: That's free fall.
recap: Without air, all objects fall at the same rate.
Extra weight brings extra inertia; they cancel out.
Apollo 15 proved it: hammer and feather, same time.

subject: SCIENCE
title: Astronauts Aren't Weightless Because There's No Gravity
blurb: They float because they're falling, not because gravity quit.

steps.title: Floating, explained
steps.sub: Astronauts on the space station drift around like gravity switched off. It didn't. Gravity up there is almost as strong as on the ground.

steps.prompt: The space station orbits a few hundred kilometers up. How strong is Earth's gravity there compared to the ground?
options: Basically zero
About 90 percent as strong
About half as strong
steps.explain: Gravity weakens with distance, but a few hundred kilometers is tiny next to Earth's radius of about 6,400 km. At the station's altitude, gravity is roughly 90 percent of its surface value. So 'no gravity' is wrong.

steps.title: Falling, but missing the ground
steps.body: If gravity is still pulling, why float? Because everything is falling together. The station, the astronauts, and their tools all fall toward Earth at the same rate. When everyone falls at the same rate, nothing presses on anything. That feeling of weight comes from the floor pushing back on you. Remove the push and you feel weightless.

steps.title: Why they don't crash
steps.body: The station is moving sideways very fast, around 28,000 kilometers per hour. It falls toward Earth, but it also moves forward so quickly that the ground curves away beneath it. It keeps falling and keeps missing. That endless miss is what we call an orbit.

steps.prompt: Why does an astronaut feel weightless?
options: There is no gravity in space
Everything around them is falling at the same rate, so nothing pushes on them
They are too far from Earth to be pulled
steps.explain: Weight is the sensation of a surface pushing back on you. In free fall, the floor falls with you and stops pushing, so you feel weightless even though gravity is still strong.

steps.prompt: Earlier: about how strong is gravity at the station's altitude?
options: Around 90 percent of surface gravity
Around 10 percent of surface gravity
Exactly zero
steps.explain: A few hundred kilometers barely changes your distance from Earth's center, so gravity stays near 90 percent of its surface strength. Floating is about free fall, not weak gravity.

steps.title: Free fall, not no gravity
recap: Gravity in orbit is roughly 90 percent of surface gravity.
You feel weight only when a surface pushes back on you.
An orbit is falling forward fast enough to keep missing Earth.

subject: SCIENCE
title: Cold Is Not a Thing
blurb: Cold doesn't flow in. Heat flows out. That's the whole trick.

steps.title: There is no cold
steps.sub: We talk about cold like it's a substance that creeps in through windows. It isn't. There is only heat, and whether it stays or leaves.

steps.prompt: A blanket keeps you warm. What is it actually doing?
options: Adding heat to your body
Blocking cold from reaching you
Slowing the heat from leaving your body
steps.explain: A blanket has no power source, so it can't add heat. And there is no cold to block. It traps the heat your body already makes and slows its escape.

steps.title: Heat only flows one way
steps.body: Heat is energy in moving particles. It always flows from warmer to cooler, never the other way on its own. When you feel cold, heat is leaving you and moving into the cooler air or object. Nothing cold is entering you. Cold is just the name we give to a place with less heat.

steps.prompt: A metal railing feels colder than a wooden one at the same temperature. Why?
options: The metal is actually colder
Metal pulls heat out of your hand faster
Metal holds more cold
