blurb: A black hole is not a cosmic vacuum cleaner. It is just gravity, same as anything else.

steps.title: Not a cosmic vacuum
steps.sub: Movies make black holes look like drains that swallow everything around them. They don't. A black hole pulls on you exactly the way any object of the same mass would.

steps.prompt: Imagine you swapped the Sun for a black hole of the exact same mass. What happens to Earth's orbit?
options: Earth gets sucked in within days
Nothing changes about the orbit; Earth keeps going around the same way
Earth flies off into space
steps.explain: Nothing about the orbit changes. Gravity depends on mass and distance, not on what the object is made of. Same mass at the same distance means the same pull, so Earth would keep orbiting exactly as before. It would just get very cold and dark.

steps.title: Gravity only cares about mass and distance
steps.body: Every object with mass pulls on every other object. The strength of that pull depends on two things: how much mass is involved, and how far apart you are. It does not depend on whether the mass is a star, a planet, or a black hole. A black hole with the Sun's mass pulls on far-away Earth with the Sun's gravity, no more.

steps.prompt: What does the strength of gravity's pull depend on?
options: Whether the object is a black hole or a star
The mass involved and the distance between the objects
The temperature of the object
steps.explain: Mass and distance. That is why a black hole and a star of equal mass pull identically from the same distance. The object's type does not enter into it.

steps.title: What makes a black hole special is being compact
steps.body: A black hole is not stronger gravity overall. It is the same mass packed into a tiny space. Because the mass is so concentrated, you can get extremely close to all of it. Gravity grows fast as you close in, so right next to a black hole the pull becomes enormous. With a star, you would hit its surface long before you got that close. A black hole has no surface to stop you, so you can reach the region where gravity is overwhelming.

steps.prompt: Why can a black hole's gravity feel so extreme when you get near it?
options: Its gravity is stronger than any star's at every distance
Its mass is packed so small that you can get extremely close to all of it
It actively reaches out and grabs things
steps.explain: It is compact. The same mass in a tiny volume lets you approach much closer than you ever could with a spread-out star, and gravity rises sharply as distance shrinks. From far away, though, it is just ordinary gravity.

steps.prompt: Earlier: swap the Sun for an equal-mass black hole. What happens to Earth's orbit?
options: It stays the same, because gravity depends on mass and distance
Earth spirals in quickly
Earth speeds up and escapes
steps.explain: It stays the same. Same mass at the same distance means the same gravitational pull, so the orbit is unchanged. The pull does not care that the object is now a black hole.

steps.title: Just gravity, concentrated
recap: Gravity depends on mass and distance, not on what the object is made of.
Swap the Sun for an equal-mass black hole and Earth's orbit would not change.
Black holes feel extreme only because their mass is packed small enough to get very close to.

subject: SCIENCE
title: On Venus, a Day Is Longer Than a Year
blurb: Venus spins so slowly that one rotation takes longer than one full lap around the Sun.

steps.title: A day longer than a year
steps.sub: A year is one trip around the Sun. A day is one spin on your axis. On Venus, the spin is so slow that the year finishes first.

steps.prompt: On Venus, which takes longer: one rotation on its axis, or one orbit around the Sun?
options: One orbit around the Sun takes longer
One rotation on its axis takes longer
They take exactly the same time
steps.explain: The rotation takes longer. Venus turns extremely slowly, so a single spin lasts longer than the time it needs to circle the Sun. By that measure, a day on Venus outlasts its year.

steps.title: Day and year are two different motions
steps.body: Two separate clocks are running on any planet. One is rotation, the planet spinning on its axis, which sets the length of a day. The other is the orbit, the planet looping around the Sun, which sets the length of a year. These are unrelated motions. There is no rule that says a spin has to be quicker than an orbit.

steps.prompt: What sets the length of a planet's year?
options: How fast it spins on its axis
How long it takes to orbit the Sun once
How far it is from the nearest star cluster
steps.explain: The orbit sets the year. A year is one full lap around the Sun. The spin sets the day. They are different motions, which is why one can outlast the other.

steps.title: Venus turns painfully slowly
steps.body: Venus is a sluggish spinner. One rotation takes about 243 Earth days. But its trip around the Sun takes only about 225 Earth days. So the orbit wraps up before a single spin does. That makes Venus the one planet where a day, measured as one rotation, is longer than its own year.

steps.prompt: Why is a day on Venus longer than a year on Venus?
options: It orbits the Sun unusually fast
It spins so slowly that one rotation takes longer than one orbit
It has no axis to spin on
steps.explain: Its spin is the slow part. One rotation takes roughly 243 Earth days while the orbit takes about 225, so the spin loses the race. The slow rotation, not a fast orbit, is the reason.

steps.prompt: Earlier: on Venus, which is longer, a rotation or an orbit?
options: The rotation (its day)
The orbit (its year)
They are equal
steps.explain: The rotation. Venus spins so slowly that one turn on its axis takes longer than one trip around the Sun, so its day outlasts its year.

steps.title: Slow spinner
recap: A day is one rotation; a year is one orbit. They are separate motions.
Venus rotates about once every 243 Earth days but orbits in about 225.
So one Venus day is longer than one Venus year.

subject: SCIENCE
title: The Northern Lights Are the Sun Hitting Air
blurb: Auroras are charged particles from the Sun crashing into the gases in our sky.

steps.title: The Sun, hitting our air
steps.sub: The northern lights look magical, but the recipe is plain. Particles from the Sun slam into the gas in our upper atmosphere and make it glow.

steps.prompt: Where does the glow of an aurora actually come from?
options: Sunlight reflecting off ice crystals
Gases in our atmosphere glowing after being hit by particles from the Sun
Heat rising from the ground at the poles
steps.explain: It is our own air glowing. The Sun throws out a stream of charged particles. When they hit the gas high in our atmosphere, that gas lights up, the same basic idea as a neon sign.

steps.title: The Sun is constantly throwing out particles
steps.body: The Sun does not just send light. It also blasts out a steady stream of charged particles, called the solar wind. These particles fly across space in all directions, and some of them reach Earth. On their own, out in space, they are invisible. They need something to hit before anything lights up.

steps.prompt: What is the solar wind?
options: A stream of charged particles thrown out by the Sun
Hot air blowing across the upper atmosphere
Light bouncing off the Moon
steps.explain: It is a stream of charged particles. The Sun sends them out constantly, and some reach Earth. They are invisible until they collide with something.

steps.title: Hitting air makes it glow
steps.body: When solar particles smash into gas atoms high in our atmosphere, they dump energy into those atoms. The atoms then release that energy as light. Different gases give off different colors. Oxygen tends to glow green, the most common aurora color. That is exactly how a neon sign works: energize a gas, and it shines in its own color.

steps.prompt: Why do auroras come in different colors?
options: Different temperatures of the same ice
Different gases give off different colors when energized
Different angles of moonlight
steps.explain: Different gases glow in different colors. The particles energize whatever gas they hit, and each gas releases its own color of light. Oxygen, for example, commonly glows green.

steps.prompt: Put the steps of making an aurora in order.
items: The Sun throws out charged particles
The particles travel to Earth
They collide with gas in the upper atmosphere
The energized gas gives off light

glosses.The Sun throws out charged particles: The solar wind leaves the Sun
glosses.The particles travel to Earth: Some of the stream reaches us

steps.prompt: Earlier: what is actually glowing during an aurora?
options: Reflected sunlight on clouds
Atmospheric gas, energized by particles from the Sun
The solar wind itself, glowing in space
steps.explain: Our atmospheric gas is glowing. The solar particles are invisible until they hit the gas and energize it. The gas then releases that energy as the light we see.

steps.title: Sun plus air equals glow
recap: The Sun blasts out charged particles called the solar wind.
Those particles hit gas in our upper atmosphere and energize it.
The gas releases that energy as light, with different gases glowing different colors.

subject: SCIENCE
title: Mars Is Red Because It's Rusty
blurb: The red planet is covered in iron oxide. That is the same stuff as rust.

steps.title: A rusty planet
steps.sub: Mars is famous for its red color. The reason is almost boringly ordinary. Its surface is coated in rust.

steps.prompt: Why does Mars look red?
options: Its surface is rich in iron that has rusted
It is hot enough to glow red
Red dust blows in from the Sun
steps.explain: It is rust. Mars has a lot of iron in its surface, and that iron has reacted with oxygen to form iron oxide, the reddish compound we call rust. The whole surface is tinted by it.

steps.title: Rust is iron plus oxygen
steps.body: Rust is not a special Martian thing. It is just iron that has reacted with oxygen, forming a compound called iron oxide. That compound has a reddish color. On Earth you see it on old nails and railings. The chemistry is the same anywhere: bring iron and oxygen together over time, and you get reddish iron oxide.

steps.prompt: What is rust, chemically?
options: Iron that has reacted with oxygen to form iron oxide
Iron that has melted and cooled
A coating of red dust with no chemistry involved
steps.explain: It is iron oxide, iron combined with oxygen. That reddish compound is the same whether it forms on an Earth railing or across the surface of Mars.

steps.title: Mars is iron-rich and rusted over
steps.body: The Martian surface holds a lot of iron, and much of it has oxidized into iron oxide dust. That fine reddish dust covers the ground and gets lifted into the thin air, tinting the whole planet. So when you look at Mars, you are essentially looking at a world coated in rust.

steps.prompt: Why is Mars covered in reddish color rather than just one rusty patch?
options: Only its poles have iron
Fine iron oxide dust covers the ground and gets lifted into the air
It is painted red by sunlight at sunset
steps.explain: Fine iron oxide dust is spread across the surface and stirred into the thin atmosphere. That tints the entire planet red, not just one spot.

steps.prompt: Earlier: what gives Mars its red color?
options: Iron oxide, which is rust
Glowing heat from its core
A red gas in its atmosphere
steps.explain: Iron oxide, also known as rust. The surface is rich in iron that has reacted with oxygen, and the reddish dust covers the planet.

steps.title: Rust, on a planetary scale
recap: Rust is iron oxide, formed when iron reacts with oxygen.
Mars has an iron-rich surface that has oxidized into reddish dust.
That dust coats the ground and the thin air, making the whole planet look red.

subject: SCIENCE
title: We Can Only See Part of the Universe
blurb: The 'observable universe' is not the whole thing. It is just how far light has reached us.

steps.title: A horizon, not an edge
steps.sub: People talk about the observable universe as if it were the entire universe. It is not. It is only the part we can see, limited by how far light has had time to travel to us.

steps.prompt: Why can't we see the whole universe?
options: The universe has a solid wall at its edge
Light from farther away has not had enough time to reach us yet
Telescopes are simply not powerful enough
steps.explain: It is a time limit. The universe has existed for a finite time, and light travels at a fixed speed. Light from things far enough away has not had time to reach us yet, so we cannot see them. That is the real limit, not telescope power.

steps.title: Light takes time to arrive
steps.body: Light is fast, but it is not instant. It travels at a fixed speed. When you look at a distant object, you are seeing light that left it long ago. The farther away something is, the longer its light takes to reach you. Look far enough and you are looking deep into the past.

steps.prompt: When you look at a very distant object, what are you seeing?
options: Exactly how it looks right now
Light that left it long ago, so an older view
A reflection of our own galaxy
steps.explain: An older view. Light takes time to travel, so the farther away something is, the further back in time you are seeing it. Distance in space is also a look into the past.

steps.title: The Big Bang sets a time budget
steps.body: The universe has been around for a finite time since the Big Bang, roughly 14 billion years. Light has only had that long to travel toward us. Anything so far away that its light has not finished the trip is simply invisible to us. That cutoff defines the observable universe. Beyond it, there is almost certainly more universe. Its light just has not reached us yet.

steps.prompt: What does the edge of the observable universe actually mark?
options: The physical end of all space
The limit of how far light has had time to travel to us
The boundary the most powerful telescope can resolve
steps.explain: It marks how far light has gotten in the time available. It is a horizon set by the age of the universe and the speed of light, not a wall and not a telescope limit. There is very likely more universe past it.

steps.prompt: Earlier: why is there a limit to how much universe we can see?
options: Light from farther away has not had time to reach us yet
The universe ends at a solid boundary
Our telescopes top out
steps.explain: Light has not had time. The universe is a finite age and light moves at a fixed speed, so light from far enough away simply has not arrived. The observable universe is the part whose light has made it to us.

steps.title: What we can see, not what exists
recap: Light travels at a fixed speed, so distant views are old views.
The universe is about 14 billion years old, limiting how far light has traveled to us.
The observable universe is that reachable region, not the whole universe or a physical edge.

subject: SCIENCE
title: Space Won't Make You Explode
blurb: Vacuum doesn't pop you like a balloon. You pass out from no oxygen first, with seconds to act.

steps.title: Space won't pop you
steps.sub: Movies show people bursting the instant they hit vacuum. Real bodies don't do that. The actual threat is quieter, faster, and inside your blood.

steps.prompt: An astronaut is suddenly exposed to vacuum with no suit. What happens first?
options: They explode from the inside pressure
Their blood instantly boils away
They stay intact but pass out within seconds from no oxygen
steps.explain: They pass out. Your skin holds your insides in just fine, so nothing bursts. The real problem is that without air pressure, oxygen leaves your blood fast, and you lose consciousness in seconds.

steps.title: Your skin holds you together
steps.body: Your body is built to contain about one atmosphere of internal pressure. Skin, muscle, and blood vessels are strong enough to hold that in even when the outside drops to nothing. You would swell a bit, but you would not burst. The balloon image is wrong. The container is tougher than the pressure trying to escape.

steps.prompt: Why doesn't a body burst in vacuum?
options: Space is too cold for anything to happen
Skin and tissue are strong enough to hold internal pressure in
There is no pressure inside the body to begin with
steps.explain: Your tissue holds the pressure. The body is engineered to contain about one atmosphere, so the outside vacuum cannot rip it open. You swell, you do not pop.

steps.title: The real killer is no oxygen
steps.body: With no outside pressure, your lungs can no longer hold oxygen in your blood. Oxygen actually starts leaving your bloodstream and venting out through your lungs. Your brain runs out of fuel in seconds. That short window of useful consciousness, just a few seconds, is the whole game. After that you are out, and damage builds the longer you stay.

steps.prompt: What is the most urgent danger of vacuum exposure?
options: The cold freezing you solid
Losing oxygen from your blood and passing out fast
Being crushed by the lack of pressure
steps.explain: Oxygen loss. Without pressure your blood can't keep its oxygen, so it vents out through your lungs and your brain starves within seconds. That is what drops you, not crushing or freezing.

steps.title: Don't hold your breath
steps.body: It feels right to clamp your mouth shut and hold air in. Do not. In a vacuum the air trapped in your lungs expands hard and can tear the tissue. The safer move is to let it out. Moisture on your tongue and eyes may also start to fizz, because boiling point drops as pressure drops. None of that is the explosion myth. It is just low pressure doing low-pressure things.

steps.prompt: Why is holding your breath in a vacuum a bad idea?
options: It wastes the oxygen you need
The trapped air over-expands and can damage your lungs
It makes you pass out faster
steps.explain: The trapped air expands. With no outside pressure pushing back, lung air swells and can tear tissue. Letting it out protects the lungs.

steps.prompt: A film shows a character's body bursting the moment an airlock opens. What's the accurate version?
options: Correct, the body bursts from internal pressure
The body stays intact and the person passes out within seconds from oxygen loss
The body freezes solid before anything else
steps.explain: It stays intact. Skin holds the pressure in, so there is no burst. The person swells slightly and loses consciousness in seconds as oxygen leaves the blood. That is the real story.

steps.title: That's vacuum exposure, for real.
recap: Your skin holds internal pressure in, so you don't explode.
Oxygen leaving your blood knocks you out within seconds.
Let your breath out, don't hold it, to protect your lungs.

subject: SCIENCE
title: How salt melts ice
blurb: Salt doesn't warm the road. It drops the freezing point so ice can't stay solid.

steps.title: Salt on ice
steps.sub: Trucks scatter salt before a freeze and the ice goes away. It looks like the salt is heating things up. It isn't. It's playing with the rules of freezing itself.

steps.prompt: You sprinkle salt on icy steps and the ice melts. What did the salt do?
options: Released heat that warmed the ice
Lowered the temperature at which water can stay frozen
Coated the ice so air couldn't reach it
steps.explain: It lowered the freezing point. Salt doesn't add warmth. It changes the deal, so water that was solid at this temperature can no longer stay frozen and turns to liquid.

steps.title: Pure water freezes at zero
steps.body: Pure water freezes at 0 degrees Celsius, which is 32 degrees Fahrenheit. At that point molecules lock into a solid crystal. That number is not fixed by magic. It depends on what is dissolved in the water. Change what is in the water and you change the temperature where it freezes.

steps.prompt: What does the freezing point of water depend on?
options: It is always exactly zero and never changes
What is dissolved in the water
How fast the water is moving
steps.explain: What's dissolved in it. Pure water freezes at zero, but mix something in and that number shifts. That is the lever salt pulls.

steps.title: Salt lowers the freezing point
steps.body: Dissolve salt in water and the freezing point drops below zero. This is called freezing point depression. The salt particles get in the way of water molecules trying to lock into a crystal, so the water needs to be even colder before it can freeze. The salt adds no heat at all. It just moves the goalpost.

steps.prompt: How does salt make ice melt without adding heat?
options: It chemically reacts and gives off warmth
It lowers the freezing point, so water can't stay solid at that temperature
It absorbs cold from the ice
steps.explain: It lowers the freezing point. Salt particles block crystal formation, so water needs a colder temperature to freeze. No heat is added; the freezing rule just changed.

steps.title: It works on the wet film
steps.body: Ice always has a thin film of liquid water on its surface, melting and refreezing at once. Salt dissolves into that film and makes a brine that freezes at a lower temperature. So the refreezing side stops winning, and the ice keeps melting. But if the air gets colder than the brine's new freezing point, salt stops helping. Road salt fades out near minus 10 degrees Celsius.

steps.prompt: Why does salt stop working when it gets very cold?
options: The salt freezes solid and can't dissolve
If the air is colder than the brine's lowered freezing point, the brine freezes anyway
Cold air blocks the salt from touching the ice
steps.explain: The brine freezes anyway. Salt only lowers the freezing point so far. Drop below that, around minus 10 Celsius for road salt, and even the salty water solidifies.

steps.prompt: A friend says salt melts ice by warming it up. What's the correct fix?
options: Right, salt gives off heat
No, salt lowers the freezing point so the ice can't stay solid
No, salt just makes the ice slippery
steps.explain: Salt lowers the freezing point. It adds no heat. By dropping the temperature at which water can stay frozen, it forces the ice to turn liquid.

steps.title: That's how salt beats ice.
recap: Pure water freezes at zero Celsius, but dissolved stuff changes that.
Salt lowers the freezing point with no heat added.
Below the brine's new freezing point, salt stops working.

subject: SCIENCE
title: Glass is not a slow liquid
blurb: Old windows are thicker at the bottom because of how they were made, not because glass flows.

steps.title: The old window myth
steps.sub: You've heard that medieval windows are thicker at the bottom because glass is secretly a slow liquid that oozes down over centuries. It's a great story. It's also wrong.

steps.prompt: An old cathedral window is thicker at the bottom than the top. Why?
options: The glass slowly flowed downward over centuries
The panes were made uneven and set thick-side down
The weight of the frame squashed it
steps.explain: It's how they were made. Old glass came out uneven, and glaziers usually set the thicker edge at the bottom. Glass does not flow at room temperature, not even over hundreds of years.

steps.title: Glass is a solid, just a messy one
steps.body: Glass is an amorphous solid. Its atoms are jumbled rather than stacked in a neat crystal grid like ice or salt. That disordered structure is why people call it liquid-like. But at room temperature it is genuinely solid. It holds its shape and does not flow in any amount you could ever measure, even across centuries.

steps.prompt: Why is glass sometimes called liquid-like?
options: Because it slowly pours over time
Because its atoms are disordered rather than in a neat crystal grid
Because it is made from melted sand
steps.explain: Its atoms are disordered. That jumbled arrangement resembles a liquid's, which is where the nickname comes from. But disordered structure is not the same as flowing.

steps.title: Old glass was made uneven
steps.body: Before modern flat-glass machines, panes were made by hand, often by spinning a blob into a disc. That left panes thicker in some spots than others. When fitting a window, glaziers usually put the heavier, thicker edge at the bottom for stability. That choice, not flowing glass, is why so many old windows are thick at the bottom.

steps.prompt: What's the real reason many old windows are thicker at the bottom?
options: Glass flowed down over centuries
Hand-made panes were uneven and set thick-edge down
Bottoms were reinforced for strength
steps.explain: Uneven hand-made panes. They came out thicker on one side, and the thick side was usually placed at the bottom. The flowing story was never the cause.

steps.title: The clincher
steps.body: If glass really flowed, every old pane would be thicker at the bottom. But plenty of old windows are thicker at the top or the sides instead. Flowing cannot explain that. Random placement of uneven panes can. Glass only flows when you heat it to high temperatures, which is exactly how it gets shaped in the first place.

steps.prompt: What fact disproves the flowing-glass story?
options: All old windows are perfectly even
Some old panes are thicker at the top or sides, not the bottom
Glass never changes shape at any temperature
steps.explain: Top-heavy panes exist. If gravity pulled flowing glass down, no pane would be thicker on top. Since some are, flowing can't be the cause.

steps.prompt: Someone insists glass is a liquid that flows over time. What's the accurate response?
options: True, it flows slowly forever
It's a solid at room temperature; old uneven panes explain the thick bottoms
It flows but only in cold climates
steps.explain: It's a solid. Its atoms are disordered, but it does not flow at room temperature. The thick-bottomed windows come from uneven hand-made panes set thick-side down.

steps.title: That's the glass myth, busted.
recap: Glass is an amorphous solid, disordered but not flowing.
Old panes were uneven and usually set thick-edge down.
Some old panes are thick on top, which flowing couldn't explain.

subject: SCIENCE
title: Diamond and pencil lead are the same stuff
blurb: Both are pure carbon. The arrangement of atoms, not the ingredients, makes one priceless and one smudgy.

steps.title: Same atoms, different worlds
steps.sub: A diamond ring and the smudge from a pencil seem like opposites. But strip them down and they're made of the exact same thing. The difference is purely in the arrangement.

steps.prompt: What's the main difference between diamond and pencil graphite?
options: They're made of completely different elements
Same element, carbon, but the atoms are arranged differently
Diamond has extra minerals mixed in
steps.explain: Same element. Both are pure carbon. What makes one a gem and the other a smear is how the carbon atoms are bonded together, not what they're made of.

steps.title: Both are pure carbon
steps.body: Diamond is carbon. Graphite, the stuff in a pencil, is also carbon. Despite the name, pencil lead has no metal lead in it at all. Two things made of the same single element but with different structures are called allotropes. Diamond and graphite are the classic example. Same ingredient, very different results.

steps.prompt: What is pencil lead actually made of?
options: The metal lead
Graphite, which is carbon
A mix of clay and metal
steps.explain: Graphite, which is carbon. The name lead is a leftover mistake. There's no metal lead in a pencil, just a form of pure carbon.

steps.title: Diamond is a rigid 3D cage
steps.body: In diamond, every carbon atom bonds tightly to four neighbors, locked into a three-dimensional network in all directions. There are no weak spots and no easy way for the atoms to shift. That rigid cage is why diamond is the hardest natural material known. Nothing slides; everything holds.

steps.prompt: Why is diamond so hard?
options: It is made of a harder element than graphite
Each carbon bonds to four neighbors in a rigid 3D network
It is squeezed tight by surrounding rock
steps.explain: The rigid 3D network. Four strong bonds per atom in every direction leave no weak spot, so nothing can slide. That makes diamond the hardest natural material.

steps.title: Graphite is slippery sheets
steps.body: In graphite, carbon atoms bond into flat sheets, strong within each sheet but only weakly held to the sheets above and below. Those layers slide apart with almost no effort. Drag a pencil across paper and whole sheets shear off and stick, leaving your mark. Same carbon as diamond, but stacked so it crumbles instead of cutting.

steps.prompt: Why does graphite leave a mark on paper?
options: It melts slightly from the friction
Its weakly held sheets slide apart and stick to the paper
It reacts chemically with the paper
steps.explain: The sheets slide off. Graphite's layers are only weakly bound to each other, so they shear away easily and transfer onto the paper as your mark.

steps.prompt: Put the reasoning in order, from shared ingredient to different result.
items: Both diamond and graphite are pure carbon
The carbon atoms are bonded in different arrangements
Diamond's 3D cage makes it the hardest material
Graphite's loose sheets slide off as a mark

steps.prompt: If diamond and graphite are both pure carbon, why is one so hard and one so soft?
options: Diamond has secret impurities that strengthen it
The atoms are arranged differently: a rigid cage versus loose sliding sheets
Graphite is an older, decayed form of diamond
steps.explain: It's the arrangement. Diamond locks each atom into a rigid 3D cage, while graphite stacks atoms in sheets that slide apart. Same carbon, opposite behavior.

steps.title: Same stuff, different builds.
recap: Diamond and graphite are both pure carbon.
Diamond's rigid 3D cage makes it the hardest material.
Graphite's loose sheets slide off, leaving a mark.

subject: SCIENCE
title: Rust is slow-motion burning
blurb: Iron rusting and iron burning are the same reaction. The only real difference is speed.

steps.title: Rust is fire in slow motion
steps.sub: A burning log and a rusting fence seem like opposites: one is dramatic, one is just decay. Chemically, they're the same event. Rust is just fire taking its time.

steps.prompt: What do iron rusting and iron burning have in common?
options: Nothing, they are unrelated processes
Both are iron combining with oxygen, just at different speeds
Both require an open flame to start
steps.explain: They're the same reaction. Both are iron joining with oxygen. Burning does it fast and hot; rusting does it so slowly you never see the heat. Speed is the difference.

steps.title: Burning is fast oxidation
steps.body: When something burns, it combines rapidly with oxygen and dumps out energy as heat and light. That's combustion. The fire you see is the reaction happening fast enough to glow. The key part is the oxygen: burning is really just a swift marriage of fuel and oxygen, releasing the energy stored in that fuel.

steps.prompt: What is burning, at its core?
options: Material breaking apart from heat alone
A rapid reaction with oxygen that releases heat and light
Water boiling out of a material
steps.explain: Rapid reaction with oxygen. Combustion is fuel combining fast with oxygen, releasing stored energy as heat and light. The oxygen is the partner that makes it go.

steps.title: Rust is the same, but slow
steps.body: When iron rusts, it also combines with oxygen. The product is iron oxide, which is what rust actually is. This is oxidation too, exactly like burning. But it happens so slowly that the heat trickles out unnoticed, with no flame and no glow. Same partners, same product type, just spread over months instead of seconds.

steps.prompt: What is rust, chemically?
options: Iron that has dried out
Iron oxide, formed when iron combines with oxygen
Dirt bonded to the metal surface
steps.explain: Iron oxide. Rust is iron joined with oxygen, the same kind of reaction as burning. It just happens slowly, so you get flakes instead of flames.

steps.title: Iron really can burn
steps.body: If rusting and burning are the same reaction, iron should be able to actually catch fire. It can. Steel wool, with its huge surface area exposed to air, will burn with real sparks and flame if you give it a spark. Spread thin enough, iron oxidizes fast enough to glow. The block of iron on a fence just has too little exposed surface to do it quickly.

steps.prompt: Why can steel wool burn with flames while an iron bar just rusts?
options: Steel wool is a different metal
Its large surface area lets it react with oxygen fast enough to glow
It is coated with a flammable chemical
steps.explain: Surface area. Thin strands expose far more iron to oxygen, so the same oxidation runs fast enough to burn. A solid bar reacts only at its skin, so it rusts slowly instead.

steps.prompt: Someone says rust and fire have nothing to do with each other. What's the correction?
options: Correct, they are unrelated
They are the same reaction, iron plus oxygen, differing only in speed
Rust is fire that has been put out by water
steps.explain: Same reaction. Both are iron combining with oxygen. Burning is fast and visible; rusting is slow and quiet. The chemistry is identical, just the pace differs.

steps.title: That's rust, the slow fire.
recap: Burning is rapid oxidation, releasing heat and light.
Rusting is the same oxidation, just slow and quiet.
Spread iron thin, like steel wool, and it truly burns.

subject: SCIENCE
title: Everything is chemicals
blurb: Why "chemical-free" is a marketing fairy tale.

steps.title: Everything is chemicals
steps.sub: Labels love the word "natural." Let's see what it actually buys you.

steps.prompt: A product is labeled "100% chemical-free." Could that ever be literally true?
options: Yes, if it's purely natural
No, not for any physical product
Only for water
steps.explain: No. A chemical is any substance made of matter. Water, salt, and air are all chemicals. A product with zero chemicals would have to contain nothing at all.

steps.title: A chemical is just matter
steps.body: In chemistry, a chemical is any substance with a definite composition. Water is a chemical. So is oxygen, salt, sugar, and the caffeine in your coffee. "Chemical" is not a synonym for "man-made" or "dangerous." It just means stuff made of atoms.

steps.prompt: By the chemistry definition, which of these is a chemical?
options: Only lab-made substances
Water, salt, and oxygen all count
Nothing found in nature
steps.explain: All matter is made of chemicals. Natural origin doesn't exempt something from being a chemical, because the word just means a substance made of atoms.

steps.title: The dose makes the poison
steps.body: Whether something harms you depends on how much you take in, not where it came from. Water can kill you if you drink far too much. Botulinum toxin is fully natural and one of the deadliest substances known. "Natural" tells you the source, not the safety.

steps.prompt: What mainly decides whether a substance harms you?
options: Whether it's natural or synthetic
The dose you're exposed to
Whether it has a chemical name
steps.explain: Toxicity scales with dose. Even water is harmful in extreme amounts, and plenty of natural substances are deadly in tiny ones. Origin alone tells you nothing.

steps.prompt: Is plain water a chemical?
options: No, it's natural
Yes, it's a substance made of atoms
Only bottled water is
steps.explain: Water is H2O, a substance with a definite composition. That makes it a chemical, natural source or not.

steps.title: Done
recap: A chemical is any substance made of matter.
"Natural" describes the source, not the safety.
The dose makes the poison, even for water.

subject: SCIENCE
title: What pH actually measures
blurb: Each step on the pH scale is a tenfold jump.

steps.title: What pH actually measures
steps.sub: pH isn't a vague acidity vibe. It's a precise count of acidic particles.

steps.prompt: How much more acidic is pH 4 than pH 5?
options: A little more
Twice as much
Ten times as much
steps.explain: Ten times. The pH scale is logarithmic, so each whole step is a factor of ten. From pH 5 to pH 4 means ten times the acid particle concentration.

steps.title: pH counts hydrogen ions
steps.body: pH measures how many hydrogen ions are floating in a solution. More hydrogen ions means more acidic and a lower pH. Fewer means more basic and a higher pH. The usual scale runs from 0 to 14, with 7 as neutral, the value of pure water.

steps.prompt: A solution has lots of hydrogen ions. Its pH is:
options: Low (acidic)
High (basic)
Exactly 7
steps.explain: More hydrogen ions means more acidic, which means a lower pH number. Pure water sits at 7 with a balanced amount.

steps.title: Each step is times ten
steps.body: The scale is logarithmic. Drop one pH unit and the hydrogen ion concentration multiplies by ten. So pH 3 is ten times more acidic than pH 4, and a hundred times more acidic than pH 5. Small number changes hide huge real differences.

steps.prompt: How much more acidic is pH 3 than pH 5?
options: 2 times
20 times
100 times
steps.explain: Two steps means ten times ten, which is a hundred. Because the scale is logarithmic, gaps that look small are actually enormous.

steps.prompt: Order these from most acidic to most basic.
items: Lemon juice (~pH 2)
Black coffee (~pH 5)
Pure water (pH 7)
Baking soda solution (~pH 9)

steps.prompt: What does a lower pH mean about hydrogen ions?
options: Fewer hydrogen ions
More hydrogen ions
No hydrogen ions
steps.explain: Lower pH means more hydrogen ions, which is what makes a solution acidic in the first place.

steps.title: Done
recap: pH counts hydrogen ions in a solution.
The scale runs 0 to 14, with 7 neutral.
Each step is a tenfold change.

subject: SCIENCE
title: How soap actually cleans
blurb: Soap doesn't kill dirt. It ferries it down the drain.

steps.title: How soap actually cleans
steps.sub: Water alone can't shift grease. Soap has a trick that fixes that.

steps.prompt: Why won't plain water rinse grease off your hands?
options: Water is too cold
Grease doesn't mix with water
Grease is too heavy
steps.explain: Grease and water don't mix. Water rolls off oily dirt instead of grabbing it, so the grease stays put. Soap is what bridges the two.

steps.title: A molecule with two ends
steps.body: Each soap molecule has two ends with opposite tastes. One end is attracted to water. The other end is attracted to oil and grease, and avoids water. That split personality is the whole secret.

steps.prompt: What makes a soap molecule special?
options: It has one end that loves water and one that loves grease
It dissolves grease into gas
It makes water hotter
steps.explain: Soap works because each molecule has two different ends: one that bonds with water and one that bonds with grease. That lets it link the two.

steps.title: Grease gets surrounded
steps.body: When you scrub, the grease-loving ends bury themselves in a drop of oil while the water-loving ends point outward. The grease ends up trapped in a tiny ball with a water-friendly shell. Now water can carry the whole package away.

steps.prompt: How does the grease leave your hands?
options: Soap burns it off
It's trapped in a ball with a water-friendly shell and rinsed away
It evaporates
steps.explain: Soap molecules wrap the grease so its outer surface likes water. The water can then rinse the bundled grease down the drain.

steps.prompt: Put soap's cleaning steps in order.
items: Soap meets greasy dirt
Grease-loving ends grab the grease
Grease gets wrapped in a water-friendly ball
Water rinses the ball away

steps.prompt: Why can't plain water clean grease on its own?
options: Grease doesn't mix with water
Water is too pure
Grease repels the drain
steps.explain: Grease and water don't mix, so water alone slides past it. Soap is what links the grease to the water.

steps.title: Done
recap: Soap molecules have a water-loving end and a grease-loving end.
They wrap grease in a water-friendly ball.
Water then rinses the whole thing away.

subject: SCIENCE
title: Why oil and water won't mix
blurb: Water isn't pushing oil away. It's pulling itself together.

steps.title: Why oil and water won't mix
steps.sub: Shake a bottle of dressing and it separates. Here's the real reason.

steps.prompt: Why do oil and water separate after you shake them?
options: Oil actively repels water
Water molecules cling to each other and squeeze oil out
Oil is always heavier
steps.explain: Water molecules attract each other strongly. They pull together and leave the oil with nowhere to go, so it clumps and separates out.

steps.title: Water sticks to itself
steps.body: Water molecules are polar, meaning they have a slightly positive side and a slightly negative side. Opposite sides attract, so water molecules cling tightly to each other. This strong self-attraction is why water beads up and forms drops.

steps.prompt: Why do water molecules cling to each other?
options: They are polar, with attracting positive and negative sides
They are heavier than oil
They are perfectly round
steps.explain: Water is polar. The positive side of one molecule attracts the negative side of the next, so they stick together strongly.

steps.title: Oil gets shut out
steps.body: Oil molecules are nonpolar, so they don't offer water anything to grab onto. Water would rather bond with more water than make room for oil. The water pulls together and pushes the oil into its own layer. It's exclusion, not repulsion.

steps.prompt: What's the best description of why oil separates out?
options: Oil and water shove each other apart
Water bonds with itself and excludes the oil
Oil dissolves and disappears
steps.explain: Water prefers bonding with itself over making room for nonpolar oil. The oil is squeezed out, which is exclusion rather than active repulsion.

steps.prompt: What property of water makes it cling to itself?
options: It is polar
It is nonpolar
It is colorless
steps.explain: Water is polar, with a positive and a negative side, so its molecules attract each other and stick together.

steps.title: Done
recap: Water is polar and clings tightly to itself.
Oil is nonpolar and gives water nothing to grab.
Water excludes oil rather than repelling it.

subject: SCIENCE
title: Why helium makes your voice squeaky
blurb: Helium doesn't raise your pitch. It changes your tone.

steps.title: Why helium makes your voice squeaky
steps.sub: Breathe in helium and you sound like a cartoon. The reason surprises people.

steps.prompt: What does helium do to your voice?
options: Raises the pitch of your vocal cords
Speeds up sound and shifts which tones stand out
Tightens your throat
steps.explain: Helium doesn't change your vocal cords. Sound travels faster through it, which boosts the higher tones in your voice and makes it sound squeaky.

steps.title: Your pitch barely changes
steps.body: Pitch comes from how fast your vocal cords vibrate. Helium doesn't change that vibration much, so the basic pitch of your voice stays roughly the same. The squeak isn't really about pitch at all.

steps.prompt: What sets the basic pitch of your voice?
options: The gas you breathe
How fast your vocal cords vibrate
The size of the room
steps.explain: Pitch is set by vocal cord vibration speed, and helium barely changes that. So the core pitch stays about the same.

steps.title: Sound moves faster in helium
steps.body: Helium is much lighter than air, and sound travels faster through it. In your throat and mouth, that faster sound boosts the higher tones that shape your voice. Those amplified high tones are what make you sound thin and squeaky.

steps.prompt: Why does helium make you sound squeaky?
options: Sound moves faster, boosting higher tones
It shrinks your vocal cords
It makes you talk faster
steps.explain: Sound travels faster through light helium, which amplifies the higher tones shaping your voice. That shift is what sounds squeaky.

steps.prompt: Order what happens when you breathe helium and speak.
items: You fill your mouth and throat with helium
Your vocal cords vibrate as usual
Sound travels faster through the helium
Higher tones get boosted and you sound squeaky

glosses.Sound travels faster through the helium: Helium is much lighter than air.

steps.prompt: Does helium change how fast your vocal cords vibrate?
options: Yes, it speeds them up a lot
No, it barely changes them
Yes, it stops them
steps.explain: Helium barely changes vocal cord vibration, so your basic pitch stays about the same. The squeak comes from sound moving faster through the gas.

steps.title: Done
recap: Your basic pitch comes from vocal cord vibration.
Helium barely changes that vibration.
Faster sound boosts higher tones, sounding squeaky.

subject: SCIENCE
title: Why stainless steel doesn't rust
blurb: No coating. Just a layer one atom thin that fixes itself.

steps.title: Steel that won't rust
steps.sub: Plain steel rusts in days. Stainless can sit in your sink for years. The trick is not paint.

steps.prompt: Why doesn't stainless steel rust?
options: It is coated in a clear plastic sealant
It has no iron, so it can't rust
Chromium in the metal forms an invisible protective layer
steps.explain: It is not coated, and it is still mostly iron. The protection comes from chromium mixed into the metal itself, which forms a barrier you cannot see.

steps.title: Chromium loves oxygen
steps.body: Stainless steel is iron with at least about 11 percent chromium mixed in. Chromium grabs oxygen even faster than iron does. So instead of the iron rusting, the chromium at the surface reacts with air first.

steps.title: A barrier you can't see
steps.body: That reaction makes a film of chromium oxide on the surface, only a few atoms thick. It is clear, so the metal still looks shiny. This film blocks oxygen and water from reaching the iron underneath. No oxygen at the iron means no rust.

steps.prompt: What actually keeps oxygen away from the iron?
options: A thick painted shell
A thin clear film of chromium oxide
The shine of the polished metal
steps.explain: The barrier is chromium oxide, only a few atoms thick and transparent. Shine is just how it looks, not what protects it.

steps.title: It heals itself
steps.body: Scratch stainless steel and you expose bare metal. But fresh chromium is now in contact with air, so a new oxide film forms almost at once. The barrier repairs itself as long as oxygen is around. A scratch in painted steel just rusts.

steps.prompt: You scratch a stainless knife. What happens to the protection?
options: It is gone for good at the scratch
A new oxide film forms there from the air
The whole blade starts rusting
steps.explain: The exposed chromium reacts with air and rebuilds the film. That self-healing is why a scratch does not doom the metal.

steps.prompt: Which element does the protecting?
options: Carbon
Chromium
Zinc
steps.explain: Chromium is the key ingredient. It reacts with oxygen first and forms the oxide film that shields the iron.

steps.title: Now you get it
recap: Stainless steel is iron plus chromium, not a coated metal.
Chromium forms a thin, clear oxide film that blocks oxygen.
Scratch it and the film reforms from the air.

subject: SCIENCE
title: Baking soda vs baking powder
blurb: One needs an acid to work. The other already has it.

steps.title: Two white powders
steps.sub: They look the same and both make batter rise. Swap one for the other and you get a flat or soapy mess. Here is the real difference.

steps.prompt: Can you just swap baking soda for baking powder one for one?
options: Yes, they are the same thing
No, they need different partners to make gas
Yes, but only in cookies
steps.explain: They are not the same. Baking soda needs an acid in the recipe to react. Baking powder already contains its own acid. Swapping them throws off that balance.

steps.title: Rising means gas
steps.body: Both work the same basic way. They make carbon dioxide gas inside the batter. The bubbles get trapped, the batter expands, and you get a light texture instead of a dense slab.

steps.title: Soda needs an acid
steps.body: Baking soda is pure sodium bicarbonate, a base. On its own it does almost nothing. It only fizzes when it meets an acid. So a recipe with baking soda must also have something acidic, like buttermilk, yogurt, lemon, or brown sugar.

steps.prompt: A recipe uses baking soda. What else must it contain to rise?
options: An acidic ingredient
Extra salt
More flour
steps.explain: Baking soda is a base and reacts only with an acid. Without an acid like buttermilk or lemon, it can't make gas.

steps.title: Powder brings its own acid
steps.body: Baking powder is baking soda already mixed with a dry acid. The acid and base sit together, inert, until liquid wakes them up. So baking powder works in recipes that have no acidic ingredient at all.

steps.prompt: Why can baking powder work in a plain recipe with no acid in it?
options: It reacts with flour
It already contains its own acid
It needs no reaction at all
steps.explain: Baking powder is soda plus a dry acid in one. The acid is built in, so the recipe doesn't have to supply one.

steps.prompt: Which one needs an acidic ingredient already in the recipe?
options: Baking powder
Baking soda
Both equally
steps.explain: Baking soda is just the base, so it depends on an acid in the recipe. Baking powder carries its own acid.

steps.title: Now you get it
recap: Both make carbon dioxide so batter rises.
Baking soda is pure base and needs an acid in the recipe.
Baking powder is soda plus a built-in dry acid.

subject: SCIENCE
title: What a catalyst really does
blurb: It speeds a reaction without being used up or becoming part of it.

steps.title: The helper that survives
steps.sub: Catalysts run your body, your car's exhaust, and most of industry. Most people picture them wrong. Let's fix that.

steps.prompt: What happens to a catalyst during a reaction?
options: It gets used up like a fuel
It becomes part of the product
It comes out unchanged and can be reused
steps.explain: A catalyst is not consumed. It helps the reaction along, then comes out the other side unchanged, ready to do it again.

steps.title: Every reaction has a hill
steps.body: To react, molecules must first get over an energy hill, called the activation energy. The taller the hill, the slower the reaction, because fewer molecules have enough energy to make it over.

steps.title: A catalyst lowers the hill
steps.body: A catalyst offers a different route with a smaller hill. Same starting materials, same products, just an easier path. More molecules can clear the lower barrier, so the reaction goes faster.

steps.prompt: How does a catalyst speed things up?
options: It adds energy to the molecules
It offers a path with a lower energy barrier
It changes what products you get
steps.explain: A catalyst lowers the activation energy by opening an easier route. It doesn't add energy and doesn't change the products.

steps.title: Why it isn't an ingredient
steps.body: A catalyst takes part in the steps but is released again at the end, unchanged. So it is not consumed and not part of the final product. A tiny amount can drive a huge amount of reaction over and over.

steps.prompt: Why can a small amount of catalyst do so much work?
options: It is extremely concentrated
It comes out unchanged and is reused again and again
It splits into many copies
steps.explain: Because a catalyst is regenerated each cycle, the same molecules keep working. A little drives a lot.

steps.prompt: The energy hill molecules must clear to react is called the what?
options: Boiling point
Activation energy
Bond strength
steps.explain: It is the activation energy. A catalyst works by lowering it, not by removing it.

steps.title: Now you get it
recap: Reactions must clear an activation energy hill.
A catalyst opens an easier path with a lower hill.
It is not used up and not part of the product.

subject: SCIENCE
title: Bananas are radioactive
blurb: They really emit radiation. The dose is the whole point.

steps.title: Yes, your fruit glows
steps.sub: Bananas give off radiation. So do you. The scary word matters far less than the size of the dose.

steps.prompt: Where does a banana's radiation come from?
options: Pesticide residue on the skin
A radioactive form of potassium inside it
Sunlight stored in the fruit
steps.explain: It is the potassium. Bananas are high in potassium, and a small fixed fraction of all potassium is a radioactive isotope called potassium-40.

steps.title: The potassium-40 inside
steps.body: All potassium in nature includes a tiny, fixed share of a radioactive isotope, potassium-40. About one part in nine thousand. Bananas are rich in potassium, so they carry some of this isotope, and it decays and emits radiation.

steps.title: Dose is what counts
steps.body: Radiation is everywhere in small amounts. What matters is the dose: how much energy your body absorbs. One banana gives a dose so tiny it is used as a casual yardstick, the banana equivalent dose, to make other doses feel less abstract.

steps.prompt: Why is a banana's radiation not a worry?
options: The radiation is a harmless type
The dose is extremely small
The potassium blocks it
steps.explain: The lesson is about dose. A banana delivers a vanishingly small dose, which is why it works as a friendly comparison unit.

steps.title: You don't stockpile it
steps.body: Your body holds a steady amount of potassium and dumps any excess. So eating bananas does not build up potassium-40 inside you. You hold a roughly constant level no matter how many you eat.

steps.prompt: Does eating more bananas pile up more potassium-40 in you?
options: Yes, it accumulates over time
No, your body holds potassium steady and excretes the rest
Only if you eat them daily
steps.explain: Your body regulates potassium tightly and excretes the excess, so the radioactive isotope does not accumulate from eating bananas.

steps.prompt: Which isotope makes bananas radioactive?
options: Carbon-14
Potassium-40
Uranium-238
steps.explain: It is potassium-40, the naturally radioactive form of potassium found in all potassium-rich foods.

steps.title: Now you get it
recap: Bananas emit radiation from potassium-40.
The dose is tiny, used as a casual comparison unit.
Your body keeps potassium steady, so it doesn't build up.

subject: SCIENCE
title: Why hard water ruins your kettle
blurb: The chalky crust isn't dirt. It's dissolved rock coming out of hiding.

steps.title: The crust in your kettle
steps.sub: Boil hard water for a while and a white crust builds up inside. It isn't grime. It comes from the water itself.

steps.prompt: What is the white scale inside a kettle?
options: Dirt and dust from the tap
Minerals that were dissolved in the water
Burnt residue from the metal
steps.explain: It is dissolved minerals. Hard water carries dissolved calcium and magnesium, and heating drives them out of the water as solid scale.

steps.title: Hard water is mineral water
steps.body: As rain soaks through rock like chalk and limestone, it dissolves calcium and magnesium. Water carrying a lot of these is called hard. The minerals are invisible and dissolved, not floating dirt.

steps.title: Heat forces them out
steps.body: Heating the water changes the chemistry so the dissolved calcium can no longer stay in solution. It comes out as solid calcium carbonate, the same stuff as chalk and limestone. That solid is limescale, and it sticks to the hot kettle.

steps.prompt: Why does scale build up faster in a kettle than in a cold pipe?
options: Kettles are dirtier
Heating forces the minerals out of the water as solid
Steam carries extra minerals in
steps.explain: Heat is the trigger. It pushes dissolved calcium out of solution as solid scale, so it piles up where the water gets hottest.

steps.title: It fights your soap too
steps.body: The same calcium and magnesium react with soap. Instead of lathering, the soap binds to the minerals and forms a dull scum. That is why soap struggles to foam in hard water and leaves a residue.

steps.prompt: Why won't soap lather well in hard water?
options: The water is too cold
The minerals react with the soap and make scum
There is too little soap
steps.explain: Calcium and magnesium grab the soap and form scum instead of suds, so you get poor lather and a residue.

steps.prompt: What is limescale mostly made of?
options: Calcium carbonate
Rust
Salt
steps.explain: Limescale is calcium carbonate, the same mineral as chalk and limestone, deposited when hard water is heated.

steps.title: Now you get it
recap: Hard water carries dissolved calcium and magnesium from rock.
Heating drops them out as calcium carbonate scale.
The same minerals react with soap and stop it lathering.

subject: SCIENCE
title: Splitting water with electricity
blurb: Push current through water and it falls apart into two gases.

steps.title: Water is not unbreakable
steps.sub: A water molecule is hydrogen bonded to oxygen. Electricity can tear them apart.

steps.prompt: Run current through water. What comes off the wires?
options: Just steam
Hydrogen and oxygen gas
Nothing, water is stable
steps.explain: Water is H2O. The molecule already contains hydrogen and oxygen, so splitting it releases those two gases. This is called electrolysis.

steps.title: Two wires, two jobs
steps.body: You dip two electrodes into water and connect a battery. At the negative electrode, hydrogen gas bubbles off. At the positive electrode, oxygen gas bubbles off. The electricity does the work of breaking the bonds.

steps.prompt: Which electrode produces hydrogen?
options: The positive one
The negative one
Both equally
steps.explain: Hydrogen forms at the negative electrode. Hydrogen ions carry positive charge, so they are pulled toward the negative electrode, where they pick up electrons and become hydrogen gas.

steps.title: Twice as much hydrogen
steps.body: Each water molecule has two hydrogen atoms and one oxygen atom. So you get about twice the volume of hydrogen as oxygen. Watch the bubbles and the hydrogen side fills up faster.

steps.prompt: Why do you collect more hydrogen than oxygen?
options: Hydrogen bubbles are bigger
Each water molecule has two H atoms but one O atom
Oxygen dissolves back into the water
steps.explain: The formula H2O sets the ratio. Two hydrogen atoms per one oxygen atom means roughly two volumes of hydrogen gas for every one volume of oxygen gas.

steps.title: The reverse of burning
steps.body: Burning hydrogen in oxygen makes water and releases energy. Electrolysis runs that backwards. You spend electrical energy to put the molecule back into hydrogen and oxygen. That is why it needs a power source.

steps.prompt: Which gas comes off the negative electrode?
options: Oxygen
Hydrogen
Steam
steps.explain: Hydrogen. Positive hydrogen ions are drawn to the negative electrode, where they gain electrons and bubble off as gas.

steps.title: You can unmake water
recap: Electrolysis splits water into hydrogen and oxygen.
Hydrogen forms at the negative electrode, oxygen at the positive.
You get about twice the volume of hydrogen, since water is H2O.

subject: SCIENCE
title: Fire needs three things
blurb: Fire is a reaction, not a thing. Take away any leg and it dies.

steps.title: Fire is an event
steps.sub: Flame is not a substance you can bottle. It is a reaction happening in the air.

steps.prompt: A candle burns under a glass. Why does it go out?
options: It runs out of wax instantly
It uses up the oxygen in the trapped air
The glass cools it down
steps.explain: The flame burns through the oxygen trapped under the glass. Remove the oxygen supply and the reaction cannot continue, so the flame dies.

steps.title: The fire triangle
steps.body: Fire needs three things at once: fuel to burn, oxygen to react with it, and enough heat to keep the reaction going. People call this the fire triangle. Remove any one side and the fire stops.

steps.prompt: What are the three sides of the fire triangle?
options: Fuel, oxygen, heat
Wood, smoke, ash
Spark, fuel, water
steps.explain: Fuel, oxygen, and heat. Each is required. The triangle is a reminder that taking away just one of the three is enough to put a fire out.

steps.title: Three ways to fight it
steps.body: Every way of stopping a fire removes one side. Water cools it, taking away heat. A blanket or foam smothers it, cutting off oxygen. Turning off a gas valve removes the fuel. Same triangle, three attacks.

steps.prompt: A fire blanket smothers a flame. Which side does it remove?
options: Fuel
Oxygen
Heat
steps.explain: A blanket seals the fire off from the air, cutting the oxygen supply. With no oxygen to react with, the fuel cannot keep burning.

steps.prompt: Which of the three does a glass over a candle take away?
options: Fuel
Heat
Oxygen
steps.explain: Oxygen. The glass traps a limited amount of air, and once the flame burns through that oxygen the reaction stops.

steps.title: Three legs, one fire
recap: Fire is a reaction needing fuel, oxygen, and heat.
Remove any one side and the fire goes out.
Water removes heat, smothering removes oxygen, cutting supply removes fuel.

subject: SCIENCE
title: Dissolving is not melting
blurb: Sugar in your tea did not vanish. It is hiding between the water molecules.

steps.title: Where did the sugar go?
steps.sub: Stir sugar into water and it disappears from view. It is still completely there.

steps.prompt: You weigh water, add sugar, and stir until it vanishes. The total weight now is:
options: Less, the sugar broke down
The same, the sugar is still there
More, dissolving adds mass
steps.explain: Dissolving does not destroy anything. The sugar molecules spread out among the water molecules but are all still present, so the total mass is unchanged.

steps.title: Spreading, not disappearing
steps.body: When sugar dissolves, the water pulls the sugar molecules apart from each other and surrounds them. The molecules slip into the gaps between water molecules. They are too small to see, but nothing is created or destroyed.

steps.prompt: After sugar dissolves, the sugar molecules are:
options: Turned into water
Spread out among the water molecules, still intact
Destroyed
steps.explain: The sugar molecules stay whole. Water just separates them and surrounds them, which is why you can taste the sweetness and recover the sugar by evaporating the water.

steps.title: Melting is different
steps.body: Melting means heating a solid until it turns to liquid on its own, like ice to water. Dissolving needs a second substance, the water, to do the spreading. The sugar is not melting. It is being carried apart by the water.

steps.prompt: What makes dissolving different from melting?
options: Dissolving needs another substance to spread into
Melting is faster
They are the same thing
steps.explain: Melting is a solid becoming liquid from heat alone. Dissolving requires a solvent like water to pull the particles apart and carry them. Different process, different cause.

steps.prompt: Dissolve 10 grams of sugar in water. How much sugar is in the cup now?
options: Less than 10 grams
Still 10 grams
Zero, it is gone
steps.explain: Still 10 grams. Dissolving only spreads the sugar out. It does not destroy any of it, so the mass is conserved.

steps.title: Hidden, not gone
recap: Dissolving spreads molecules out among the water; it destroys nothing.
The dissolved sugar is still there and weighs the same.
Melting is heat alone; dissolving needs a solvent like water.

subject: SCIENCE
title: Why metals conduct electricity
blurb: Metals hold a sea of loose electrons. Current is just that sea drifting.

steps.title: What is current, really?
steps.sub: Electricity flows through metal wires but not through wood or plastic. The difference is in the electrons.

steps.prompt: What actually moves when current flows through a metal wire?
options: The metal atoms slide along
Loose electrons drift through
Nothing moves, energy teleports
steps.explain: The atoms stay put in place. It is the free electrons that drift through the metal, and that drift is what we call an electric current.

steps.title: A sea of electrons
steps.body: In a metal, each atom lets go of one or more of its outer electrons. Those electrons are no longer tied to a single atom. They roam freely through the whole metal. People call this a sea of electrons shared across the structure.

steps.prompt: In a metal, the loose electrons belong to:
options: One specific atom each
No single atom, they roam the whole metal
The wire's plastic coating
steps.explain: The outer electrons leave their individual atoms and move through the entire metal. Because they are free to move, they can carry charge from one end to the other.

steps.title: Push them and they flow
steps.body: Connect a battery and you push on this electron sea. The free electrons drift toward the positive end. That coordinated drift is the current. Materials like plastic hold their electrons tightly, so nothing drifts and no current flows.

steps.prompt: Why does plastic not conduct electricity?
options: It has no atoms
Its electrons are held tightly and cannot drift
It is too cold
steps.explain: Plastic holds its electrons bound to their atoms. With no free electrons to drift, there is nothing to carry the current, so it acts as an insulator.

steps.prompt: An electric current in a metal is really:
options: Atoms moving
Free electrons drifting
Heat spreading
steps.explain: Free electrons drifting. The metal atoms stay fixed in place while the shared sea of electrons moves, and that movement is the current.

steps.title: It is all in the electrons
recap: Metal atoms release outer electrons into a shared sea.
Current is those free electrons drifting through the metal.
Insulators like plastic hold their electrons tight, so they cannot conduct.

subject: SCIENCE
title: Plastic is made from oil
blurb: Your phone case started as crude oil pumped out of the ground.

steps.title: From the ground to your hand
steps.sub: Most plastic begins as crude oil. Refining and chemistry turn it into the stuff all around you.

steps.prompt: Where does most plastic come from?
options: Crude oil and natural gas
Melted-down sand
Tree sap
steps.explain: Most plastic is made from fossil fuels, mainly crude oil and natural gas. They are rich in carbon and hydrogen, which is exactly what plastic is built from.

steps.title: Oil is carbon chains
steps.body: Crude oil is a mix of molecules made of carbon and hydrogen, called hydrocarbons. They are chains of carbon atoms. Refineries sort these chains by size and break the big ones into smaller, more useful pieces.

steps.prompt: Crude oil is mostly made of:
options: Carbon and hydrogen chains
Salt and water
Pure carbon only
steps.explain: Crude oil is a mix of hydrocarbons, molecules of carbon and hydrogen. Those carbon chains are the raw material that gets reshaped into plastic.

steps.title: Small units, long chains
steps.body: From the oil, chemists make small molecules called monomers, like ethylene. A monomer is one link. Join thousands of identical links end to end and you get a polymer, a long repeating chain. Polymer just means many parts.

steps.prompt: A polymer is:
options: A single small molecule
Many identical small units linked into a long chain
A type of metal
steps.explain: A polymer is many repeating units joined together. The small starting unit is the monomer; link enough of them and you build the long chains that make plastic.

steps.prompt: Put the journey from ground to plastic in order.
items: Pump crude oil
Refine and crack into small molecules
Make monomers like ethylene
Link monomers into polymers
Mold the polymer into a product

glosses.Pump crude oil: Extract the raw hydrocarbon from underground
glosses.Refine and crack into small molecules: Break big chains into smaller useful ones
glosses.Mold the polymer into a product: Shape the plastic into its final form

steps.prompt: What is the main raw material for most plastic?
options: Tree sap
Crude oil and natural gas
Sand
steps.explain: Crude oil and natural gas. They supply the carbon and hydrogen that get built into the long polymer chains of plastic.

steps.title: Oil, reshaped
recap: Most plastic starts as crude oil and natural gas.
Oil is carbon and hydrogen chains, refined into small monomers.
Linking monomers into long polymer chains makes plastic.

subject: SCIENCE
title: How carbon dating works
blurb: Living things take in a radioactive clock that starts ticking down the moment they die.

steps.title: A clock inside dead things
steps.sub: Tell an old bone's age without a label or a record. The trick is a built-in clock that every living thing carries and that starts running down at death.

steps.prompt: When does the carbon clock in a tree start ticking down?
options: The day the seed sprouts
The day the tree dies
It never changes once set
steps.explain: While alive, the tree keeps refilling its carbon-14 from the air, so the level holds steady. The countdown only begins at death, when the refilling stops.

steps.title: A rare carbon that decays
steps.body: Most carbon is stable. But high in the atmosphere, cosmic rays hit nitrogen and make a small amount of carbon-14, a radioactive version of carbon. It is unstable, so it slowly decays back into nitrogen. New carbon-14 keeps forming, so the air holds a roughly steady trace of it.

steps.title: Life keeps topping it up
steps.body: Plants pull in carbon from the air, animals eat plants, and so every living thing carries the same trace of carbon-14 as its surroundings. While you are alive, any carbon-14 that decays is replaced by fresh intake. The level stays matched to the atmosphere.

steps.prompt: Why does a living animal's carbon-14 level stay steady?
options: Its body makes new carbon-14
It keeps taking in fresh carbon to replace what decays
Carbon-14 does not decay inside a living body
steps.explain: Decay never stops, even in a living thing. The level holds steady only because eating and breathing keep replacing what is lost.

steps.title: Death starts the countdown
steps.body: When something dies, intake stops. Now carbon-14 only decays, and nothing refills it. It falls by half every 5,730 years, its half-life. Measure how much is left compared to a fresh sample, and you can count back to find the age. Once the trace runs too low, around 50,000 years, the method runs out.

steps.prompt: After one half-life, how much of the original carbon-14 is left?
options: None
Half
A quarter
steps.explain: A half-life is the time for half to decay. After 5,730 years half remains, after two half-lives a quarter, and so on. Counting the halvings gives the age.

steps.prompt: What event makes an organism's carbon-14 begin to drop?
options: Its birth
Its death
A change in the weather
steps.explain: Death. Intake stops, refilling ends, and from then on decay alone sets the clock. That is why the method dates the time since death.

steps.title: Now you get it
recap: Living things carry the same carbon-14 trace as the air.
At death, intake stops and carbon-14 only decays.
Its half-life of about 5,730 years lets you read the age.

subject: SCIENCE
title: Why acid eats metal
blurb: Acid doesn't melt metal away. It reacts with it, stealing electrons and fizzing off hydrogen gas.

steps.title: Not melting. Reacting.
steps.sub: Drop a reactive metal in acid and it fizzes, shrinks, and vanishes. It looks like melting. It is something stranger and more precise.

steps.prompt: A piece of metal fizzes in acid. What is in those bubbles?
options: Air being pushed out
A new gas made by the reaction
Steam from the heat
steps.explain: The bubbles are hydrogen gas, brand new, made by the reaction itself. The acid and metal are building something that was not there before.

steps.title: Dissolving is not reacting
steps.body: When sugar dissolves in water, the sugar is still sugar. Its molecules just spread out and hide among the water. Nothing is destroyed or created. You could get the sugar back by drying the water off. That is a physical change.

steps.prompt: What happens to sugar molecules when sugar dissolves in water?
options: They break into new substances
They stay intact and spread out
They turn into water
steps.explain: Dissolving leaves the molecules unchanged. They scatter through the water but are still sugar. Dry it out and the sugar returns.

steps.title: Acid strips electrons
steps.body: Acid on metal is different. The metal gives up electrons and turns into positive metal ions, which drift off into the liquid. That is a chemical change. The metal is no longer metal. You cannot just dry it out and get your nail back.

steps.prompt: What does the metal lose when acid attacks it?
options: Electrons, becoming positive ions
Heat, becoming colder
Nothing; it just spreads out
steps.explain: The metal hands over electrons and becomes positive ions in solution. Losing electrons is the heart of the reaction, and it changes the metal into a new substance.

steps.title: Where the bubbles come from
steps.body: Those stripped electrons go somewhere. The acid supplies hydrogen ions, and they grab the electrons and pair up into hydrogen gas. That gas is the fizz you see. What is left behind is a salt: the metal ions and the acid's leftover ions dissolved together.

steps.prompt: Put the reaction in order, from metal to fizz.
items: Metal gives up electrons
Metal becomes positive ions in solution
Hydrogen ions take the electrons
Hydrogen gas bubbles off

glosses.Metal becomes positive ions in solution: It leaves the solid and dissolves as ions

steps.prompt: What gas makes up the bubbles when a reactive metal meets acid?
options: Oxygen
Hydrogen
Carbon dioxide
steps.explain: Hydrogen. The acid's hydrogen ions take the electrons the metal gave up and pair into hydrogen gas, the fizz you can see.

steps.title: Now you get it
recap: Dissolving keeps molecules intact; reacting makes new substances.
Acid pulls electrons off the metal, turning it into ions.
Hydrogen ions take those electrons and bubble off as gas.
